topics.title: Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PCITT302**), the topic *Basic Operational Concepts* establishes the foundational model of how a stored-program digital computer interacts with its memory, input/output devices, and the external environment. It defines the **functional units** of a computer, the **interconnection structure** that links them, the **register-level data movement** during instruction execution, and the mechanisms for handling **interrupts** and **data transfers**.

At the architectural level (as described by M. Morris Mano and Hamacher et al., which are the standard reference texts for this module), a computer system is decomposed into four fundamental functional blocks:

1. **Input Unit** — Accepts coded information from external devices (keyboard, sensors, network).
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores both the program instructions and the data they manipulate. Typically implemented as a hierarchy of registers, cache, primary (RAM/ROM), and secondary (disk) memory.
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs the actual computational operations (addition, subtraction, logical AND, OR, comparison).
4. **Output Unit** — Returns processed results to the outside world (monitor, printer, actuators).

These four units are coordinated by a fifth, the **Control Unit (CU)**, which decodes instructions and generates the precise sequence of **control signals** required to direct data flow through the processor-memory-I/O fabric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Focus Point:** The phrase *"Basic Operational Concepts"* in Module 1 almost always requires you to draw a labeled functional block diagram of a computer, identify all registers (PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR), explain the single-bus / common-bus interconnection, and describe either the **Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle** OR the **Interrupt-driven execution model**. Memorize the register names — they are a guaranteed 3-mark question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Computer as a Smart Post Office

Imagine a **busy post office**:
- The **Postmaster (Control Unit)** reads each instruction from the top of a stack of orders.
- The **Sorting Counter (ALU)** does all the arithmetic and logical work.
- The **Storage Shelves (Memory)** hold all parcels and order slips.
- The **Delivery Bay (I/O)** receives new parcels and dispatches finished ones.
- The **Conveyor Belt (System Bus)** moves parcels between the shelves, the sorting counter, and the delivery bay.

Just as a post office cannot function if parcels are randomly dropped, a computer cannot function without a strict, well-defined **operational protocol** — the instruction set, the bus structure, and the fetch-execute cycle. The postmaster always *fetches* the next order, *decodes* it, *executes* it (sort, store, or dispatch), and *repeats*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** A stored-program computer stores its own instructions in memory, treating them as data. This single principle, first articulated by **John von Neumann (1945)**, is what makes the computer universal. The architecture derived from it is called the **Von Neumann Architecture**, and virtually every general-purpose CPU today (Intel, ARM, RISC-V) descends from it.

### 1.3 The Two Architectural Classes

| Property | Von Neumann Architecture | Harvard Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single shared memory for instructions and data | Separate memories for instructions and data |
| **Bus** | Single bus for both | Separate instruction & data buses |
| **Speed bottleneck** | Yes — *Von Neumann bottleneck* | No — simultaneous access possible |
| **Example** | Most general-purpose CPUs (x86, ARM in von-Neumann mode) | DSPs, microcontrollers (early), ARMv7-M (modified Harvard) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Von Neumann bottleneck** — the constraint that the CPU can only fetch one word (instruction or data) per memory cycle through a single shared bus — is the single most important performance limitation studied in this module.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Word Length (n)** — the number of bits the CPU processes as a single unit; typically **16, 32, or 64 bits**.
- **Address Space** = $2^N$ locations, where **N** = number of address bits.
- **Memory Capacity** = $2^N \times M$ bits, where **M** = word size in bits.
- **Clock Period (T)** — the duration of one clock cycle, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **Clock Frequency (f)** = $\frac{1}{T}$, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **GHz** for modern CPUs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Units in Depth

#### 2.1.1 Memory Unit
- Stores **binary information** in groups of bits called *words*.
- Each word is assigned a unique **address**.
- Operations: **READ** (data flows from memory to processor) and **WRITE** (data flows from processor to memory).
- Volatile memory (**RAM**) loses data when power is off; non-volatile memory (**ROM, Flash**) retains it.

#### 2.1.2 Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs arithmetic operations: **addition, subtraction, multiplication, division**.
- Performs logical operations: **AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shift, rotate**.
- Operates on operands fetched from **registers** (not directly from memory — this is a *register-transfer-level* concept).
- Sets **status flags** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) used for conditional branching.

#### 2.1.3 Control Unit
- The "brain" of the CPU. Decodes the **opcode** of an instruction.
- Generates **control signals** that orchestrate data movement (register reads, ALU activation, memory access).
- Implements either **hardwired control** (combinational logic) or **microprogrammed control** (a microcode ROM).

#### 2.1.4 Input/Output (I/O) Unit
- Provides the interface between the computer and the external world.
- Includes devices such as keyboards, displays, sensors, and network interfaces.
- Communicates with the processor via **I/O ports** using one of three schemes: **Programmed I/O, Interrupt-driven I/O, or Direct Memory Access (DMA)**.

#### 2.1.5 Processor (CPU)
- The combination of the **ALU + Control Unit + Registers**, all packaged in a single chip.
- Communicates with memory and I/O through the **system bus** (Address + Data + Control lines).

### 2.2 The Register Set — A Deep Look

Registers are the **fastest** storage elements in a computer, located physically inside the CPU. They hold operands, intermediate results, and control information.

| Register | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Program Counter | **PC** | Holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched |
| Instruction Register | **IR** | Holds the **currently executing instruction** |
| Address Register | **AR** | Holds the **memory address** to be accessed |
| Data Register (Memory Buffer Register) | **DR / MBR** | Temporarily holds the **data word** read from / written to memory |
| Accumulator | **AC** | A general-purpose register that is the **implicit operand** of most ALU operations |
| Temporary Register | **TR** | A scratchpad used by the CPU during complex operations (e.g., multiplication) |
| Input Register | **INPR** | Holds data coming in from an input device |
| Output Register | **OUTR** | Holds data going out to an output device |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 14-mark question on Basic Operational Concepts**, the most common pattern is: *"Explain the functional units of a computer and discuss the operations performed by the registers during a typical fetch-decode-execute cycle."* You must be able to trace the contents of **PC, AR, DR, IR, and AC** step-by-step.

### 2.3 The Common-Bus System (Interconnection)

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway. There are three types:

1. **Address Bus** — *Unidirectional* (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O). Carries the address of the location being accessed.
2. **Data Bus** — *Bidirectional*. Carries the actual data being transferred. Typical widths: **8, 16, 32, 64 bits**.
3. **Control Bus** — Carries timing and command signals: **Memory Read, Memory Write, I/O Read, I/O Write, Bus Request, Bus Grant, Interrupt Acknowledge**.

Because a CPU typically has many registers but only a few can be read from or written to at a time, the **common bus system** uses **multiplexers** to select which register is connected to the bus. A typical 4-bit selector (S1, S0) for an 8-register system (for read) and a separate 4-bit selector for write are used.

> [!NOTE]
> An alternative to multiplexers is the use of **three-state (tri-state) bus buffers**. A register outputs data on the bus only when its **enable** line is asserted; otherwise, its output is in a high-impedance (Hi-Z) state. This avoids the need for a large multiplexer and allows many registers to share the bus.

### 2.4 The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

The CPU continuously loops through three stages:

1. **Fetch Phase** — The instruction at the address held in **PC** is read from memory into **IR**. The **PC is then incremented** to point to the next instruction.
2. **Decode Phase** — The **Control Unit** examines the **opcode** in **IR** to determine what operation must be performed and which operands are required.
3. **Execute Phase** — The ALU performs the operation, the result is written back to a register or memory, and the cycle repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **branching** mechanism is what makes the computer programmable. When a **branch/jump** instruction is executed, the **PC is loaded with a new address** instead of being incremented, allowing the program to change its own flow of control.

### 2.5 Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)

An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that causes the CPU to **temporarily suspend** its current program, execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then **resume** the original program.

**Types of Interrupts:**
- **Hardware (External) Interrupts** — Generated by I/O devices (e.g., a key press, network packet arrival).
- **Software Interrupts (Traps / Exceptions)** — Generated by the program itself (e.g., division by zero, invalid opcode, system call).
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** — Cannot be ignored (e.g., power failure).
- **Maskable Interrupt** — Can be temporarily disabled using the **Interrupt Enable (IEN) flip-flop**.

**Interrupt Handling Sequence (Simplified):**
1. Device raises an **interrupt request (IRQ)**.
2. CPU finishes the **current instruction**.
3. CPU checks if interrupts are enabled (IEN flag) and if the device is not masked.
4. CPU saves the current **PC** (and sometimes status flags) to a known location (stack or specific memory location).
5. CPU loads PC with the **address of the ISR** (interrupt vector).
6. ISR executes, services the device, and executes a **Return from Interrupt (RTI)** instruction.
7. CPU restores the saved PC and resumes the original program.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are tested directly in numerical problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Addressable locations | $L = 2^{N}$ | $N$ = number of address bits |
| Total memory capacity | $C = 2^{N} \times W$ bits | $W$ = word size in bits |
| Memory capacity in bytes | $C_{B} = 2^{N} \times \dfrac{W}{8}$ | bytes |
| Clock frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clock}$ | seconds |
| Cycles per Instruction (CPI) | $CPI = \dfrac{T_{CPU} \times f}{I_{count}}$ | dimensionless |
| CPU Time via CPI | $T_{CPU} = I_{count} \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | seconds |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{I_{count}}{T_{CPU}}$ | instructions per second (IPS) |
| MIPS Rating | $MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | million instructions per second |
| Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $k$ = speedup of that fraction |
| Maximum Speedup | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | as $k \rightarrow \infty$ |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, all vertical bar operators have been replaced with $\vert$ and $\dfrac{}{}$ to preserve Markdown table integrity. In your answer script, write the absolute value as $\vert x \vert$.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Embedded Systems Design** — The same fetch-decode-execute loop and ISR structure underpins every microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, ESP32). Knowing register-level operations is essential for **firmware debugging**.
- **Performance Engineering** — The CPU performance equation $T = I \times CPI \times T$ is the foundation of compiler optimization, instruction-pipelining, and **CPU benchmarking** (e.g., SPEC, Geekbench).
- **Operating System Design** — The interrupt mechanism is the basis of **preemptive multitasking**, **system calls**, and **device drivers**. Every modern OS (Linux, Windows, Android) is built on top of the interrupt model discussed above.
- **Computer Security** — **Buffer overflow attacks** exploit the way the ISR saves PC onto the stack; understanding this flow is the first step toward understanding **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** and **stack canaries**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Capacity from Address and Word Size

**Problem Statement:** A computer has a **24-bit address bus** and a **32-bit word size**. Calculate the maximum memory capacity in bytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**

$$
N = 24 \text{ address bits}, \qquad W = 32 \text{ bits per word}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of addressable locations.**

Each unique combination of the 24 address lines corresponds to one memory word. The total number of distinct addresses is:

$$
L = 2^{N} = 2^{24}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
2^{24} = 2^{4} \times 2^{20} = 16 \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total memory capacity in bits.**

$$
C = L \times W = 2^{24} \times 32 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
C = 2^{24} \times 2^{5} \text{ bits} = 2^{29} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes** (1 byte = 8 bits).

$$
C_{B} = \dfrac{2^{29}}{2^{3}} = 2^{26} \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ MB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C = 64 \text{ MB}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CPU Execution Time from Instruction Mix

**Problem Statement:** A processor runs at **$f = 2.5 \text{ GHz}$**. It executes a benchmark consisting of three instruction classes with the following characteristics:

| Class | Instruction Count $I_i$ | CPI |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | $5 \times 10^{8}$ | 1 |
| Load/Store | $2 \times 10^{8}$ | 4 |
| Branch | $1 \times 10^{8}$ | 2 |

Calculate the **total execution time**, the **average CPI**, and the **MIPS rating**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the total instruction count.**

$$
I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = (5 + 2 + 1) \times 10^{8} = 8 \times 10^{8} \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the average CPI** (weighted by instruction frequency).

$$
CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (I_i \times CPI_i)}{I_{total}}
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{8})(1) + (2 \times 10^{8})(4) + (1 \times 10^{8})(2)}{8 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{8} + 8 \times 10^{8} + 2 \times 10^{8}}{8 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{8}}{8 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{15}{8} = 1.875
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the clock period.**

$$
T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.4 \text{ ns per cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the total CPU execution time.**

$$
T_{CPU} = I_{total} \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clock}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = 8 \times 10^{8} \times 1.875 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = 8 \times 1.875 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-1} = 6.0 \times 10^{-1} = 0.6 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the MIPS rating.**

$$
MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{1.875 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
MIPS = \dfrac{2.5}{1.875} \times 10^{3} = 1.333 \times 10^{3} = 1333.33 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Final Summary:**

- $CPI_{avg} = 1.875$
- $T_{CPU} = 0.6 \text{ s}$
- $MIPS = 1333.33$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Amdahl's Law for System Speedup

**Problem Statement:** A program spends **40%** of its time in a function that can be **parallelized** with a speedup of **$k = 8$**. The remaining **60%** is sequential. Calculate the overall speedup and the maximum theoretical speedup.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
f = 0.40, \qquad (1-f) = 0.60, \qquad k = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.60 + \dfrac{0.40}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.60 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1}{0.65} \approx 1.538
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum theoretical speedup** (as $k \to \infty$).

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{0.60} \approx 1.667
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with an *infinitely fast* parallel implementation, the program cannot run more than **1.667 times faster** because the sequential 60% portion is the bottleneck. This is the **fundamental limit** of parallelization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Amdahl's Law tells us that the **sequential portion** of a program dominates its performance. This is why even modern multi-core CPUs (16, 32, 64 cores) often yield only a **2$\times$–4$\times$** speedup for general-purpose workloads. KTU examiners love to test this concept numerically.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Given Quantity | Find | Key Equation | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address bus size $N$, word size $W$ | Memory capacity | $C = 2^{N} \times W$ | Forgetting to convert bits to bytes |
| $f$, $I_{count}$, $CPI$ | CPU Time | $T = I \times CPI / f$ | Mixing $f$ in Hz vs MHz |
| Instruction mix and CPI per class | Average CPI | $CPI_{avg} = \sum I_i CPI_i / \sum I_i$ | Not weighting by $I_i$ |
| $f$ and $CPI$ | MIPS | $MIPS = f / (CPI \times 10^{6})$ | Forgetting the $10^6$ factor |
| Fraction enhanced $f$, speedup $k$ | Overall speedup | $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$ | Setting $k=1$ mistakenly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Computer

The following Mermaid diagram represents the canonical **single-bus, Von Neumann architecture** studied in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Devices] -->|INPR| cpuA[CPU Block]
    cpuA -->|OUTR| outputA[Output Devices]
    cpuA <-->|Address + Data + Control Bus| busA[System Bus]
    busA <--> memA[Memory Unit]
    busA <--> ioA[I/O Interface]
    classDef block fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F172A
    classDef bus fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef io fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    class cpuA,memA block
    class busA bus
    class inputA,outputA,ioA io
```

**Description of the Diagram:**
- The **CPU Block** is the central element containing the **ALU**, **Control Unit**, and **register file** (PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR).
- The **System Bus** is the **shared communication channel**, comprising three sub-buses:
  - **Address Bus** (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O)
  - **Data Bus** (bidirectional)
  - **Control Bus** (read/write/interrupt signals)
- The **Memory Unit** stores both instructions and data.
- **I/O Devices** communicate with the bus through special **INPR** (input) and **OUTR** (output) registers inside the CPU.

### 4.2 Detailed Register-Level Schematic

This expanded diagram shows the **internal CPU registers** and their interconnections through the **common bus** using a multiplexer-based selection scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pc[PC Program Counter] --> muxA[MUX for Bus Read]
    ar[AR Address Register] --> muxA
    dr[DR Data Register] --> muxA
    ac[AC Accumulator] --> muxA
    ir[IR Instruction Register] --> muxA
    tr[TR Temporary Register] --> muxA
    inpr[INPR Input Reg] --> muxA
    outr[OUTR Output Reg] --> muxA
    muxA --> commonBus[Common System Bus]
    commonBus --> aluA[ALU]
    aluA -->|Status Flags Z N C V| cuA[Control Unit]
    cuA -->|Control Signals| muxA
    cuA -->|Control Signals| aluA
    cuA -->|Control Signals| memA2[Memory]
    classDef reg fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0F172A
    classDef ctrl fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#7F1D1D
    class pc,ar,dr,ac,ir,tr,inpr,outr reg
    class cuA,aluA,muxA,commonBus,memA2 ctrl
```

**Key Observations:**
- The **MUX for Bus Read** selects exactly one register at a time to drive the common bus, using selector inputs **S2 S1 S0** (3 bits for 8 registers).
- The **ALU** has two inputs: one from the bus, one internally latched (e.g., AC). The result can be routed back to AC or to memory via the bus.
- The **Control Unit** decodes the **IR** and drives the multiplexer selectors and ALU function code.

### 4.3 Interrupt-Driven Execution Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant IODev as I/O Device
    participant MEM as Memory
    Note over CPU: Executing main program
    CPU->>MEM: Fetch instruction
    MEM-->>CPU: Instruction
    CPU->>CPU: Decode & Execute
    IODev->>CPU: Interrupt Request (IRQ)
    CPU->>CPU: Finish current instruction
    CPU->>CPU: Check IEN flag (interrupt enabled)
    CPU->>MEM: Save PC to stack
    CPU->>MEM: Load ISR address into PC
    CPU->>CPU: Execute ISR
    CPU->>IODev: Service the device
    CPU->>MEM: RTI: Restore PC from stack
    Note over CPU: Resumes main program
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The CPU runs the main program normally.
- When an I/O device needs attention, it raises an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)**.
- The CPU completes the current instruction, saves context (PC, flags), and jumps to the **ISR**.
- After the ISR finishes, the CPU **restores context** and resumes the main program — at the exact point where it was interrupted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always mention that the **current instruction is completed** before the CPU responds to an interrupt. Examiners deduct marks for claiming the CPU "stops in the middle" of an instruction.

### 4.4 Three-State Bus Buffers (Alternative to MUX)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regA1[Register A] --> bufA1[Tri-State Buffer]
    regA2[Register B] --> bufA2[Tri-State Buffer]
    regA3[Register C] --> bufA3[Tri-State Buffer]
    bufA1 --> busA3[Common Bus]
    bufA2 --> busA3
    bufA3 --> busA3
    enA1[Enable A] --> bufA1
    enA2[Enable B] --> bufA2
    enA3[Enable C] --> bufA3
    classDef buf fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#3B0764
    classDef en fill:#FFF7ED,stroke:#9A3412,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#7C2D12
    class bufA1,bufA2,bufA3 busA3 buf
    class enA1,enA2,enA3 en
```

**Description:**
- Each register has its own **tri-state buffer** on the bus.
- Only the register whose **Enable** line is asserted drives the bus; all others are in **Hi-Z** (high-impedance).
- This approach **scales better than a multiplexer** when the number of registers is large.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer questions typical of KTU ESE Module Internal Assessment or Part A of the End Semester Exam.

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following registers: Program Counter (PC), Instruction Register (IR), and Accumulator (AC). State the role of each in the fetch-decode-execute cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Program Counter (PC)** — A register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched. It is automatically **incremented** after each instruction fetch, except when a branch or jump instruction is executed, in which case it is loaded with a new address. *(1 mark)*
- **Instruction Register (IR)** — A register that holds the **currently executing instruction** after it has been fetched from memory. The opcode field of the IR is decoded by the Control Unit to determine which operation to perform. *(1 mark)*
- **Accumulator (AC)** — A general-purpose register that is the **default operand and result location** for most ALU operations. In a single-accumulator architecture, the AC holds one of the inputs to the ALU and receives the result of the operation. *(1 mark)*

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Von Neumann bottleneck? How does the Harvard architecture address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Von Neumann bottleneck** is the performance limitation that arises in a **Von Neumann architecture** because the CPU can only access **one memory location at a time** through a **single shared bus**, since instructions and data reside in the same memory. As a result, the CPU alternates between fetching an instruction and fetching its data, causing idle time and limiting throughput. *(1.5 marks)*

The **Harvard architecture** addresses this by using **physically separate memories and separate buses for instructions and data**. This allows the CPU to fetch an instruction and read/write a data word **simultaneously**, eliminating the bottleneck. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions follow the KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **a (7 marks) + b (7 marks)** within one question, with two alternative full-question choices (A or B).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic operational concepts** of a computer system. Identify the function of each unit. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A computer system consists of **four main functional units**: Input, Memory, ALU, and Output — coordinated by a fifth, the **Control Unit**.

- **Input Unit:** Accepts programs and data from external devices (keyboard, mouse, scanner). Converts them to binary form understandable by the CPU. *(1 mark)*
- **Memory Unit:** Stores both **instructions and data** in binary. Provides **READ** and **WRITE** operations. Includes Main Memory (RAM, ROM) and Storage (disk). *(1 mark)*
- **ALU:** Performs all arithmetic (+, –, $\times$, /) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, compare) operations. Operates on data fetched from registers. Sets condition flags. *(1 mark)*
- **Output Unit:** Sends processed results to the outside world (monitor, printer). *(0.5 marks)*
- **Control Unit:** Directs all operations. Fetches instructions, decodes them, and generates the control signals that orchestrate data movement among the units. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram:** *(see Section 4.1 for the Mermaid diagram; in the answer script, draw a rectangular block diagram showing CPU in the center, connected to Memory and I/O via a shared system bus, with Input devices on the left and Output devices on the right).* *(1.5 marks)*

**Interconnection via the System Bus:** Address bus (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O), Data bus (bidirectional), Control bus (read/write/IRQ signals). *(1 mark)*

**(b)** Explain the **register set** of a basic computer. List and describe the function of any **five internal CPU registers**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The register set is a small, high-speed storage area inside the CPU used for temporary data, control, and addressing.

- **PC (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction. Incremented automatically; altered by branch/jump. *(1.4 marks)*
- **AR (Address Register):** Holds the memory address currently being referenced (for read/write). *(1.4 marks)*
- **IR (Instruction Register):** Holds the instruction after it is fetched; opcode decoded by Control Unit. *(1.4 marks)*
- **DR (Data Register / MBR):** Temporarily buffers data being transferred to/from memory. *(1.4 marks)*
- **AC (Accumulator):** The primary ALU operand register; also holds the result of ALU operations. *(1.4 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **fetch-decode-execute cycle** with a register-transfer-level (RTL) notation. Clearly state the role of PC, AR, IR, and DR. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The fetch-decode-execute cycle is the **fundamental operation loop** of any stored-program computer. It comprises three phases:

**Phase 1 — Fetch:** The address in **PC** is transferred to **AR**, and a memory **READ** is issued. The instruction is loaded into **DR**, then transferred to **IR**. The **PC is incremented** to point to the next instruction. *(2 marks)*

In RTL notation:

$$
AR \leftarrow PC
$$
$$
DR \leftarrow M[AR]
$$
$$
IR \leftarrow DR
$$
$$
PC \leftarrow PC + 1
$$

**Phase 2 — Decode:** The **opcode** portion of **IR** is interpreted by the Control Unit, which determines the operation type (arithmetic, memory access, branch) and prepares the required control signals. *(1.5 marks)*

**Phase 3 — Execute:** The operation specified by the instruction is carried out:
- For memory-reference instructions: a second memory access is performed using **AR** and **DR**.
- For arithmetic/logic instructions: the **ALU** operates on the **AC** and the operand, storing the result back in **AC**.
- For branch instructions: the **PC** is loaded with the new target address. *(2 marks)*

**Role Summary:**
- **PC:** Tracks the *next* instruction's address. *(0.5 marks)*
- **AR:** Specifies the *current* memory address. *(0.5 marks)*
- **IR:** Holds the *current* instruction. *(0.5 marks)*
- **DR:** Buffers the *current* data word. *(0.5 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **concept of interrupts**. Differentiate between **maskable and non-maskable interrupts**. How does the CPU handle an interrupt request? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

An **interrupt** is a signal that causes the CPU to **temporarily halt** the execution of the current program, execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** to service the requesting device, and then **resume** the original program. *(1 mark)*

**Types of Interrupts:**
- **Hardware Interrupts:** Triggered by external I/O devices (keyboard, disk controller, timer). *(1 mark)*
- **Software Interrupts (Exceptions/Traps):** Triggered by the program (e.g., division by zero, invalid memory access, system call). *(1 mark)*

**Maskable vs Non-Maskable:**
- **Maskable Interrupt:** Can be *temporarily ignored* by clearing the **Interrupt Enable (IEN)** flag. Used for routine I/O events. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI):** *Cannot* be ignored; used for critical events like power failure, memory parity error. *(1.5 marks)*

**Interrupt Handling Sequence:**
1. The I/O device raises the **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line. *(0.25 marks)*
2. The CPU **finishes the current instruction** (it does not interrupt mid-instruction). *(0.25 marks)*
3. The CPU checks the **IEN flag**; if set, it proceeds. *(0.25 marks)*
4. The CPU **saves the current PC** (and possibly status flags) to the **stack** or a dedicated memory location. *(0.25 marks)*
5. The CPU **loads PC with the ISR's address** (from an interrupt vector table). *(0.25 marks)*
6. The **ISR executes**, services the device, and ends with a **Return from Interrupt (RTI)** instruction. *(0.25 marks)*
7. The CPU **restores the saved PC** and resumes the main program. *(0.25 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to complete the current instruction:** Many students write that the CPU "stops immediately" when an interrupt occurs. This is wrong; the CPU always **finishes the current instruction** first. *(–0.5 to –1 mark deduction)*
> 2. **Confusing PC, AR, IR, and DR:** Examiners check that you can clearly distinguish what each register holds. Mixing up **IR** (instruction) and **DR** (data) is a common error. *(–0.5 mark deduction)*
> 3. **Not mentioning the IEN flag:** When discussing maskable interrupts, you **must** mention the **Interrupt Enable flag** to receive full marks. *(–0.5 mark deduction)*
> 4. **Skipping the context save/restore:** The interrupt handling sequence must include **saving PC** to the stack and **restoring it** on RTI. Skipping this step loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong formula for memory capacity:** Writing $C = 2^{N} \times N$ instead of $C = 2^{N} \times W$ is a classic error. Always verify: **capacity = (number of locations) $\times$ (word size in bits)**. *(–1 mark deduction)*
> 6. **Mixing Von Neumann and Harvard:** Many students claim "Harvard has one memory." This is **wrong** — Harvard has **two separate memories** (instruction + data). *(–1 mark deduction)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

- [ ] A computer has **four functional units** (Input, Memory, ALU, Output) **plus** the Control Unit.
- [ ] The **Von Neumann architecture** uses a **single shared memory** for instructions and data → causes the **Von Neumann bottleneck**.
- [ ] The **Harvard architecture** uses **separate memories and buses** for instructions and data.
- [ ] **Bus types:** Address (unidirectional), Data (bidirectional), Control (command/timing).
- [ ] **Six core CPU registers** to know: **PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR** (+ INPR, OUTR for I/O).
- [ ] **Fetch phase:** $AR \leftarrow PC$; $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$; $IR \leftarrow DR$; $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$.
- [ ] **Decode phase:** Control Unit interprets opcode in IR.
- [ ] **Execute phase:** ALU performs the operation; result written back to register or memory.
- [ ] **Interrupt** = external event halting the CPU to service a device; CPU **finishes current instruction first**.
- [ ] **Maskable interrupt** can be disabled via the **IEN flag**; **NMI cannot**.
- [ ] ISR must **save PC** before execution and **restore PC** via RTI before returning.
- [ ] **Memory capacity** = $2^{N} \times W$ bits = $2^{N} \times \dfrac{W}{8}$ bytes.
- [ ] **CPU Time** = $I_{count} \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ = $\dfrac{I_{count} \times CPI}{f}$.
- [ ] **MIPS** = $\dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$; max speedup $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
- [ ] **Common bus** interconnects registers via **multiplexers** OR **tri-state buffers**.
- [ ] **Tri-state** = output is either **0, 1, or Hi-Z** (high-impedance / disconnected).

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In any 14-mark question on this topic, **always draw the block diagram first**, label it clearly, and then proceed with the explanation. A well-labeled diagram alone can fetch **3 to 4 marks** even if your written explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Locations and Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Locations and Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Memory Location** is the smallest addressable physical unit in a computer's memory system, uniquely identified by a binary number known as a **Memory Address**. A **Memory Address** is a numerical label (expressed in binary, hexadecimal, or decimal) assigned by the CPU to each memory location so that data can be precisely stored and retrieved.

In modern computer systems, memory is typically **byte-addressable**, meaning that every individual 8-bit byte possesses its own unique address. The collection of all valid memory addresses that a processor can generate is called the **Address Space**, which is mathematically bounded by the width of the system's address bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *memory location* refers to a physical storage cell, whereas a *memory address* is a logical reference number pointing to that cell. They are NOT the same thing — the address is a *label*, the location is the *cell itself*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office System

Imagine a massive post office building in a city:

- **Memory** → The entire post office building with millions of PO boxes.
- **Memory Location** → One individual PO box (a small physical compartment).
- **Memory Address** → The unique PO box number written on the front of that compartment (e.g., "Box 12,451").
- **Address Bus** → The postal clerk's form — if the form has 16 blank spaces for digits, the clerk can address up to **2¹⁶ = 65,536** boxes.
- **Data Bus** → The size of the parcel that can be inserted/retrieved from the PO box in a single visit.

Just as no two PO boxes can share the same number, **no two memory locations can share the same address**. The CPU places an *address* on the address bus, and the memory hardware responds by accessing the *location* carrying that label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Address** = logical/electrical reference (a binary number on the bus).
> - **Location** = physical storage cell (a capacitor, flip-flop, or transistor group in DRAM/SRAM).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Memory Metrics (as per IEEE / Industry convention):**
> - **1 Byte (B)** = 8 bits
> - **1 Word** = 2 bytes (16-bit), 4 bytes (32-bit), or 8 bytes (64-bit) depending on processor architecture
> - **1 Kibibyte (KiB)** = 2¹⁰ bytes = 1024 bytes
> - **1 Mebibyte (MiB)** = 2²⁰ bytes = 1,048,576 bytes
> - **1 Gibibyte (GiB)** = 2³⁰ bytes
> - **1 Tebibyte (TiB)** = 2⁴⁰ bytes

These **binary multiples** (KiB, MiB, GiB) are now the standard in IEEE 1541 and should be used in KTU answers, although "KB", "MB", "GB" are still widely accepted in informal notation.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Memory Location** | The smallest addressable physical storage unit. |
| **Memory Address** | A unique binary number identifying a memory location. |
| **Address Space** | Total range of addresses generable by the CPU. |
| **Address Bus** | Physical wires carrying the address from CPU to memory. |
| **Data Bus** | Physical wires transferring data to/from memory. |
| **Byte Addressability** | Every byte has its own address. |
| **Word Addressability** | Every word (multi-byte unit) has its own address. |
| **Physical Address** | Real location in main memory hardware. |
| **Logical Address** | Virtual address generated by the executing program. |
| **Effective Address** | Final address used after all address translations. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classification of Memory Addresses

Modern processors deal with **three different kinds of addresses** simultaneously, and KTU frequently tests the ability to distinguish between them:

### (a) Physical Address
The **actual, real-world address** of a memory location in the physical RAM chips. The Memory Management Unit (MMU) translates all other addresses into physical addresses before the memory controller accesses the RAM.

### (b) Logical Address (Virtual Address)
The address **generated by the CPU** during program execution. Each program "believes" it has its own private contiguous memory starting from address **0**. Logical addresses are independent of physical hardware.

### (c) Effective Address
The address that **results after applying the addressing mode arithmetic** to the address field in the instruction. It is the final reference passed to the MMU for translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Address Translation Pipeline (KTU Favourite):**
> Program generates **Logical Address** → CPU addressing-mode arithmetic computes **Effective Address** → MMU translates to **Physical Address** → RAM hardware accesses the **Memory Location**.

## 2.2 Address Space Mathematics — The Cornerstone Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula (MUST memorize for every KTU exam):**
> $$\text{Address Space (in locations)} = 2^{n}$$
> where **n** = number of address bits (i.e., width of the address bus).

> **Derived Memory Capacity Formula:**
> $$\text{Total Memory Capacity} = 2^{n} \times w \text{ bits}$$
> where **w** = width of each memory location in bits.

### Worked Insight — Why 2ⁿ?

If the address bus has **n** parallel wires, each wire can be **0 (low) or 1 (high)** independently. By the fundamental rule of combinatorics, the number of unique binary patterns of n bits equals **2ⁿ**. Since each unique pattern represents one addressable location, the address space is **2ⁿ locations**.

## 2.3 Byte Addressability vs Word Addressability

### Byte Addressable Memory
- Most modern systems (x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS).
- Address increments by **1** for each successive byte.
- A 32-bit address bus yields **2³² = 4 GiB** of byte-addressable memory.
- **Memory addresses:** 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, …, 4,294,967,295.

### Word Addressable Memory
- Each **word** (e.g., 16-bit or 32-bit) gets a single address.
- Address increments by **1** for each successive word.
- A 16-bit address bus with 32-bit words yields **2¹⁶ = 65,536 words = 256 KiB**.
- **Memory addresses:** 0, 1, 2, 3, …, 65,535.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse "Address Space = 2ⁿ" with "Memory Capacity = 2ⁿ". The address space counts *locations*, while the capacity depends on the *width* of each location. Always multiply by word size when asked for total capacity in bits/bytes.

## 2.4 Byte Ordering — Endianness

Endianness defines the **byte order** in which a multi-byte datum is stored in byte-addressable memory.

### Big-Endian (Motorola, PowerPC, Network Byte Order)
- The **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
- Reading memory in ascending order naturally produces the "human-readable" form.
- Example — Storing the 32-bit value `0x12345678` at base address **4000**:

| Address | 4000 | 4001 | 4002 | 4003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x12 | 0x34 | 0x56 | 0x78 |

### Little-Endian (x86, x86-64, most ARM in default mode)
- The **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
- Requires byte-reversal during human reading.
- Same example `0x12345678` at address **4000**:

| Address | 4000 | 4001 | 4002 | 4003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 |

## 2.5 Memory Alignment

A multi-byte datum is said to be **aligned** if its starting address is a multiple of its size in bytes. For example, a 4-byte integer stored at address 1000 is aligned; the same integer at address 1001 is **misaligned**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why alignment matters (KTU 2024 high-yield):**
> - Aligned accesses may be completed in a **single bus cycle** (one memory transaction).
> - Misaligned accesses require **two bus cycles** plus byte-shuffling logic, **degrading performance** by up to 2×.
> - Some RISC architectures (older SPARC, MIPS) **trap (hardware exception)** on misaligned access.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address Space | $N = 2^{n}$ | Locations generable with *n* address bits | locations |
| Memory Capacity (bit) | $C_{bits} = 2^{n} \times w$ | Total capacity when each location is *w* bits | bits |
| Memory Capacity (byte) | $C_{B} = 2^{n} \times w \mid 8$ | Total capacity in bytes | Bytes |
| Last Address | $A_{last} = 2^{n} - 1$ | Highest valid address (zero-based indexing) | — |
| Address Range | $[0,\ 2^{n} - 1]$ | Valid address range | — |
| Aligned Address Test | $A \bmod k = 0$ | A is *k*-byte aligned if divisible by *k* | — |
| Endianness Offset | $byte_{i} = A_{base} + \big(N - 1 - i\big)$ | Byte *i* address in big-endian (0 = MSB) | — |
| Endianness Offset (LE) | $byte_{i} = A_{base} + i$ | Byte *i* address in little-endian (0 = LSB) | — |
| Address Bus Width | $n = \log_{2} N$ | Bits required to address *N* locations | bits |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Code generators emit aligned load/store instructions to maximize CPU throughput.
- **Operating Systems:** Virtual memory managers exploit the gap between logical and physical addresses to enable process isolation, paging, and swapping.
- **Network Engineering:** Big-endian is the standard "Network Byte Order" (RFC 1700), requiring explicit conversions at the socket API.
- **Embedded Systems:** Engineers in low-power IoT devices deliberately choose byte vs word addressability based on RAM cost, energy budget, and peripheral compatibility.
- **Reverse Engineering / Forensics:** Byte-order analysis is the first step in interpreting binary dumps (e.g., analyzing firmware from a captured device).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Deriving Total Addressable Memory

**Given Problem:** A processor has a **24-bit address bus** and uses **byte addressability**. Find:
1. The total address space.
2. The highest valid memory address.
3. The total memory capacity in **bytes**, **kilobytes**, and **megabytes**.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Identify the address-bus width.**

From the problem statement, the number of address bits is:
$$n = 24 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master address-space formula.**

The total number of unique addressable byte-locations is:
$$N = 2^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute n = 24.**

$$N = 2^{24}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 2.**

We know that $2^{10} = 1024$ and $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$.
So $2^{24} = 2^{20} \times 2^{4} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 16 = 16{,}777{,}216$.

$$\boxed{N = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the highest valid address (zero-based indexing).**

In zero-based memory addressing, the first location is **0**, the second is **1**, …, the last is **N − 1**:

$$A_{last} = 2^{n} - 1 = 2^{24} - 1 = 16{,}777{,}215$$

In hexadecimal: $2^{24} - 1 = \text{0xFFFFFF}$.

**Step 6 — Convert to memory capacity units.**

Since each location is **1 byte** (byte-addressable system), the total capacity is:

$$C = 2^{24} \text{ bytes} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes}$$

Dividing by $2^{10} = 1024$ to convert to KiB:

$$C = \frac{16{,}777{,}216}{1024} = 16{,}384 \text{ KiB}$$

Dividing by $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ to convert to MiB:

$$C = \frac{16{,}384}{1024} = 16 \text{ MiB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Memory Capacity} = 16 \text{ MiB} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Address Mapping with Endianness

**Given Problem:** A 32-bit value `0xA5B6C7D8` is stored in memory starting at base address `0x00001000`. Show the byte-by-byte memory map under both **big-endian** and **little-endian** schemes.

### Step 1 — Break the 32-bit value into its 4 bytes.

The 32-bit value `0xA5B6C7D8` decomposes as:

$$\text{0xA5B6C7D8} = \underbrace{\text{0xA5}}_{MSB}\,\underbrace{\text{0xB6}}_{}\,\underbrace{\text{0xC7}}_{}\,\underbrace{\text{0xD8}}_{LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply big-endian convention.**

Big-endian places the **MSB at the lowest address**:

| Address | 0x1000 | 0x1001 | 0x1002 | 0x1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xA5 | 0xB6 | 0xC7 | 0xD8 |

**Step 3 — Apply little-endian convention.**

Little-endian places the **LSB at the lowest address**:

| Address | 0x1000 | 0x1001 | 0x1002 | 0x1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xD8 | 0xC7 | 0xB6 | 0xA5 |

**Step 4 — Generalized offset formula.**

For a *k*-byte datum stored at base address $A_b$ with byte index $i$ (where $i = 0$ is the MSB):

$$\text{Address of byte } i = A_{b} + \big(k - 1 - i\big) \quad \text{(Big-Endian)}$$

$$\text{Address of byte } i = A_{b} + i \quad \text{(Little-Endian)}$$

Verifying for $i = 0$ (the MSB 0xA5) in big-endian:

$$A_{MSB} = \text{0x1000} + (4 - 1 - 0) = \text{0x1000} + 3 = \text{0x1003} \;\; \text{❌ INCORRECT}$$

Correction: The MSB index convention. In the formula above, *i = 0* should be the **MSB only if the formula represents the MSB's position**, but a clearer convention is:

Let $j = 0$ denote the **lowest-addressed byte**:

- Big-Endian: lowest address = MSB → byte at address $A_b + j$ is the MSB.
- Little-Endian: lowest address = LSB → byte at address $A_b + j$ is the LSB.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Finding the Address-Bus Width Required

**Given Problem:** A system must support **8 GiB** of byte-addressable memory. Find the minimum address-bus width required.

### Step 1 — Express the target capacity as a power of 2.**

We know that $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$.
Therefore:

$$8 \text{ GiB} = 8 \times 2^{30} = 2^{3} \times 2^{30} = 2^{33} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the equation for total capacity.**

For byte-addressable memory, capacity in bytes equals address space:
$$C = 2^{n} = 2^{33}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for n.**

Taking $\log_{2}$ of both sides:

$$n = \log_{2}(2^{33}) = 33 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the alternative formula.**

$$N = C = 8 \text{ GiB} = 8{,}589{,}934{,}592 \text{ locations}$$

$$n = \log_{2} N = \log_{2}(8{,}589{,}934{,}592) = 33 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the highest valid address.**

$$A_{last} = 2^{33} - 1 = 8{,}589{,}934{,}591 = \text{0x1FFFFFFFF}$$

$$\boxed{n_{min} = 33 \text{ address lines}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Common KTU Scenarios

| Scenario | n (bits) | Byte-Addr Locations | Word Size | Total Capacity | Highest Address |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8086 (16-bit bus) | 20 | $2^{20}$ | 1 B | 1 MiB | 0xFFFFF |
| 80836 (24-bit bus) | 24 | $2^{24}$ | 1 B | 16 MiB | 0xFFFFFF |
| 80386 (32-bit bus) | 32 | $2^{32}$ | 1 B | 4 GiB | 0xFFFFFFFF |
| 64-bit modern CPU | 48 (effective) | $2^{48}$ | 1 B | 256 TiB | 0xFFFFFFFFFFFF |
| Hypothetical 16-bit word | 16 | $2^{16}$ | 2 B | 128 KiB | 0xFFFF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy and Address Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete journey of an address from program generation to physical RAM access — a high-frequency KTU diagram topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Instruction] --> B[CPU generates Logical Address]
    B --> C[Addressing Mode Arithmetic]
    C --> D[Effective Address Computed]
    D --> E[MMU Address Translation]
    E --> F[Physical Address Generated]
    F --> G[Address Bus Activated]
    G --> H[Memory Controller Selects RAM Chip]
    H --> I[Target Memory Location Accessed]
    I --> J[Data Transferred via Data Bus]
    J --> K[CPU Register Updated]
    K --> L[Next Instruction Fetch Cycle]

    classDef cpu fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D cpu
    class F,H,I,J mem
    class G,K,L bus
```

## 4.2 Memory Address Space Structure Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the structural organization of a byte-addressable memory array, showing the relationship between addresses, locations, and the address bus width.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADDR_BUS [ADDRESS BUS n BITS]
        A0[Line 0 LSB] --- A1[Line 1] --- A2[Line 2] --- A3[Line 3] --- AN[Line n-1 MSB]
    end

    subgraph MEM_ARRAY [MEMORY ARRAY]
        L0[Location 0 0x00] --- L1[Location 1 0x01] --- L2[Location 2 0x02] --- LDOTS[...] --- LLAST[Location 2n-1 LAST]
    end

    subgraph DATA_BUS [DATA BUS w BITS]
        D0[Line 0] --- D1[Line 1] --- DW[Line w-1]
    end

    ADDR_BUS -->|selects one| MEM_ARRAY
    MEM_ARRAY -->|reads or writes| DATA_BUS

    classDef busline fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef loc fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    class A0,A1,A2,A3,AN,D0,D1,DW busline
    class L0,L1,L2,LDOTS,LLAST loc
```

## 4.3 Address Translation Pipeline Diagram

The diagram below shows the **three-tier address translation** which is a common KTU module question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Program Code] -->|generates| LA[Logical Address 32 bits]
    LA -->|Addressing Mode Computation| EA[Effective Address]
    EA -->|MMU Page Table Lookup| PA[Physical Address 30 bits]
    PA -->|RAM Chip Select Signal| LOC[Memory Location in DRAM]
    LOC -->|Data Returned| CPU[CPU Register]

    classDef prog fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef addr fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#00695c,color:#000

    class P,CPU prog
    class LA,EA,PA addr
    class LOC hw
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — CPU, Address Bus, Data Bus, and Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_BLOCK [CPU SUBSYSTEM]
        REG[General Purpose Registers]
        MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]
        MDR[Memory Data Register MDR]
        PC[Program Counter]
    end

    subgraph BUS_SYSTEM [BUS SYSTEM]
        ABUS[Address Bus Unidirectional]
        DBUS[Data Bus Bidirectional]
        CBUS[Control Bus Read/Write Signals]
    end

    subgraph MEM_BLOCK [MAIN MEMORY]
        DEC[Address Decoder]
        RAM[RAM Array 2n Locations]
    end

    REG --> MAR
    PC --> MAR
    MAR --> ABUS
    ABUS --> DEC
    DEC --> RAM
    RAM --> DBUS
    DBUS --> MDR
    MDR --> REG
    CBUS --> RAM

    classDef cpuunit fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef busunit fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef memunit fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    class REG,MAR,MDR,PC cpuunit
    class ABUS,DBUS,CBUS busunit
    class DEC,RAM memunit
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Memory Location
(b) Memory Address
(c) Address Space

#### Model Answer (Expected length: 3–4 lines per term):

**(a) Memory Location:** A memory location is the smallest physical storage unit within a computer's main memory that can be individually identified and accessed by the CPU. *Example:* A single byte in DRAM storing the ASCII value `0x41` (character 'A').

**(b) Memory Address:** A memory address is a unique binary number assigned to each memory location, used by the CPU to read from or write to that specific cell. *Example:* Address `0x00000500` in a byte-addressable system refers to the byte located at position 1280 in RAM.

**(c) Address Space:** Address space is the total range of unique memory addresses that a processor can generate, determined by the width of the address bus. *Example:* A processor with a 16-bit address bus has an address space of $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ byte-locations (64 KiB).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Big-Endian | Little-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| MSB Position | Stored at the **lowest** memory address | Stored at the **highest** memory address |
| LSB Position | Stored at the **highest** memory address | Stored at the **lowest** memory address |
| Natural Reading | Matches the human/hex reading order | Requires byte reversal for natural reading |
| Used By | Motorola 68k, PowerPC, SPARC, network protocols | Intel x86, x86-64, most ARM (default) |
| Advantage | Easier human debugging of memory dumps | Easier arithmetic on multi-byte integers |

**Example:** Storing the 16-bit value `0x1234` at base address `0x1000`:

| Address | 0x1000 | 0x1001 |
|---|---|---|
| Big-Endian | 0x12 (MSB) | 0x34 (LSB) |
| Little-Endian | 0x34 (LSB) | 0x12 (MSB) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the address translation process from a **logical address** generated by a program to the **physical address** in main memory. Discuss the role of the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A computer system uses a **32-bit address bus** with **byte addressability**.
- (i) Calculate the total addressable memory in **bytes**, **KiB**, and **MiB**. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Find the **highest valid memory address** in hexadecimal. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the system is upgraded to a **36-bit address bus**, what is the new maximum addressable memory? **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Program generates Logical Address:** When a high-level program runs, the compiler assigns symbolic or virtual addresses starting from `0x00000000` (logical address space).

2. **CPU Issues the Address:** The CPU places the logical address on the internal address lines and forwards it to the **MMU**.

3. **MMU Performs Translation:** The MMU uses a **segmentation** or **paging** data structure (segment table or page table stored in main memory) to translate the logical address into a physical address.

4. **Page Table Lookup:** For paging-based systems, the logical address is split into a **page number** (high-order bits) and a **page offset** (low-order bits). The page number indexes the page table to obtain the corresponding **frame number** in physical RAM.

5. **Physical Address Formation:** The physical address is constructed by concatenating the **frame number** with the **page offset**.

6. **Address Bus Activation:** The physical address is placed on the external address bus, and the memory controller decodes it to select the correct RAM chip and row/column.

7. **Role of MMU:** The MMU enables **virtual memory**, **process isolation**, **memory protection**, and **demand paging** — all transparently to the running program.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.3 of these notes for the address translation pipeline.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Total Memory Calculation:**

Address bus width: $n = 32$ bits.

Total byte-addressable locations:
$$N = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to KiB (divide by $2^{10} = 1024$):
$$\frac{2^{32}}{2^{10}} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ KiB}$$

Converting to MiB (divide by $2^{20}$):
$$\frac{2^{32}}{2^{20}} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ MiB}$$

**(ii) Highest Valid Address in Hex:**

$$A_{last} = 2^{32} - 1 = 0xFFFFFFFF$$

**(iii) Upgrade to 36-bit Address Bus:**

$$N_{new} = 2^{36} = 2^{32} \times 2^{4} = 4 \text{ GiB} \times 16 = 64 \text{ GiB}$$

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the concepts of **byte addressability** and **word addressability** in memory systems. A 16-bit processor has a 20-bit address bus. Calculate the total memory in bytes for both byte-addressable and word-addressable configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **big-endian** and **little-endian** byte ordering schemes. Given the 32-bit hexadecimal value `0xDEADBEEF` stored at memory address `0x00002000`, show the complete byte-by-byte memory layout for both schemes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Byte Addressability:**
- Every individual byte (8 bits) has a unique address.
- A 20-bit address bus yields $2^{20}$ unique byte locations.

**Word Addressability:**
- Each machine word (in this case, 16 bits = 2 bytes) has a single address.
- A 20-bit address bus yields $2^{20}$ unique word locations, but each word is 2 bytes wide.

**Calculations:**

**Byte-Addressable Memory:**
$$C_{byte} = 2^{20} \times 1 \text{ byte} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MiB}$$

**Word-Addressable Memory:**
$$C_{word} = 2^{20} \text{ words} \times 2 \text{ bytes/word} = 2^{21} \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MiB}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Big-Endian:** MSB at the lowest memory address.

**Little-Endian:** LSB at the lowest memory address.

**Decomposition of `0xDEADBEEF`:**
- Byte 1 (MSB) = `0xDE`
- Byte 2 = `0xAD`
- Byte 3 = `0xBE`
- Byte 4 (LSB) = `0xEF`

**Big-Endian Memory Layout (base `0x00002000`):**

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xDE | 0xAD | 0xBE | 0xEF |

**Little-Endian Memory Layout (base `0x00002000`):**

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xEF | 0xBE | 0xAD | 0xDE |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (examiner feedback):**
> 1. **Confusing address space with memory capacity.** If the question asks for "memory in bytes" and the bus is 32-bit with word size 16 bits, students write `2³²` instead of `2³² × 2 bytes = 8 GiB`. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting zero-based addressing.** Highest address is $2^{n} - 1$, not $2^{n}$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Reversing endianness definitions.** Big-endian ≠ "small end first" — it means **big end (MSB) first at low address**. **Lose 2 marks** if reversed.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to KiB/MiB when asked.** Writing `2²⁰ bytes` instead of `1 MiB` for a byte-addressable system costs **1 mark** in descriptive questions.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram** for the 7-mark "explain address translation" sub-question. **Lose 2 marks** since the diagram carries its own weight.
> 6. **Confusing physical and logical addresses** in MMU-based questions. The logical address goes INTO the MMU; the physical address comes OUT. **Lose 1–2 marks** if these terms are swapped.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - A **memory location** is a physical storage cell; a **memory address** is a numerical label for that cell.
> - Address Space = $2^{n}$ where *n* = number of address bits.
> - Total Memory Capacity (in bytes) = $2^{n} \times \big(w \mid 8\big)$, where *w* = location width in bits.
> - Highest valid address = $2^{n} - 1$ (zero-based).
> - **Logical Address** = generated by program. **Physical Address** = actual RAM location. **Effective Address** = post-addressing-mode result.
> - **Big-Endian** = MSB at low address. **Little-Endian** = LSB at low address.
> - Byte-addressable = 1 byte per address. Word-addressable = multi-byte word per address.
> - **Aligned access** = starting address is a multiple of the datum size; **misaligned** = otherwise.
> - Standard capacity units use powers of 2: $1 \text{ KiB} = 2^{10} \text{ B}$, $1 \text{ MiB} = 2^{20} \text{ B}$, $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ B}$.
> - Typical processors: 8086 (20-bit), 80386 (32-bit), modern x86-64 (48-bit effective, 64-bit logical).
> - The MMU is the hardware bridge between logical and physical addresses — it is **transparent** to the running program.
> - **Network Byte Order** is **Big-Endian** (RFC 1700).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instructions and Instruction Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Instructions and Instruction Sequencing # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Machine Instruction? A **machine instruction** is a binary code that directs the processor to perform a specific operation. It is the smallest, atomic unit of work understood directly by the CPU hardware. Every program written in any high-level language (C, Java, Python) is ultimately translated by the compiler into a long, ordered sequence of such machine instructions before execution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCITT302 Module 1):** > An *instruction* is a binary pattern stored in memory that specifies a single operation the processor must execute. The complete set of such patterns recognized by a processor is called its **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the ISA is treated as the **contractual interface** between hardware and software — defining what the CPU *can* do, in what format it accepts commands, and how it interacts with memory and I/O. --- ## 1.2 Instruction Format — The Anatomy of a Single Instruction Every machine instruction, regardless of complexity, is logically divided into discrete **fields** (groups of bits). The most common internal structure is: $$ \underbrace{\boxed{\text{Opcode}}}_{\text{Operation}} \quad \underbrace{\boxed{\text{Operands / Addresses}}}_{\text{Data References}} \quad \quad \underbrace{\boxed{\text{Mode Bits}}}_{\text{Addressing Mode}} $$ | Field | Full Name | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Opcode** | Operation Code | Specifies *what* operation to perform (ADD, LOAD, JUMP, etc.) | | **Operands** | Source / Destination Fields | Specify *where* the data is located (registers, memory, constants) | | **Mode Bits** | Addressing Mode Field | Specify *how* to interpret the operand fields (direct, indirect, immediate) | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Instructions as a Restaurant Order 🍽️ Imagine a well-organized restaurant kitchen: - **The Menu (Instruction Set)** = the fixed list of dishes the chef knows how to cook (the ISA). - **A Customer Order Ticket (A Single Instruction)** = a slip of paper that says *"Cook pasta, with these specific ingredients, deliver to table 7."* - **The Chef (CPU)** reads the ticket in order and executes it. - **The Waiter (System Bus)** carries ingredients (data) from the pantry (memory) to the chef's counter (registers). Just as a kitchen cannot cook a dish that isn't on the menu, **a CPU cannot execute an instruction that isn't part of its ISA.** Also, the order in which tickets are processed is critical — the *sequence* of instructions determines whether the meal makes sense. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** The ISA is to a programmer what a recipe book is to a chef — it defines the *vocabulary* of legal operations, but the *sequence* in which those operations are invoked is what produces meaningful behaviour. --- ## 1.4 What is Instruction Sequencing? **Instruction sequencing** refers to the step-by-step process by which the CPU: 1. **Fetches** the next instruction from memory, 2. **Decodes** it to determine the required action, 3. **Executes** the operation, and 4. **Repeats** the cycle continuously until a HALT instruction or reset. The processor also needs to know **where to find the next instruction**, which is why a special register called the **Program Counter (PC)** always holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched. > [!TIP] > The default sequencing of the PC is **PC ← PC + 1** (or +2, +4 depending on instruction size) — this is called *straight-line sequencing*. Only *control-flow instructions* (jumps, branches, calls) alter this default order. --- ## 1.5 Categories of Machine Instructions KTU Module 1 typically classifies instructions into three broad categories: | Category | Examples | Function | |---|---|---| | **Data Transfer Instructions** | `LOAD`, `STORE`, `MOVE`, `PUSH`, `POP` | Move data between registers, memory, and I/O without modification | | **Data Processing / Arithmetic-Logic Instructions** | `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`, `SHIFT`, `COMPARE` | Perform computation on data already inside the CPU | | **Control Transfer Instructions** | `JUMP`, `BRANCH`, `CALL`, `RETURN`, `HALT` | Alter the default sequential flow of execution | > [!IMPORTANT] > A **fourth category** often added in advanced treatments is **I/O and System Control Instructions** (e.g., `IN`, `OUT`, `NOP`, interrupt-related opcodes), but for KTU Module 1 the three-category model is sufficient. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Instruction Length and Word Size Relationship The length of a single instruction is constrained by the underlying **word length** of the processor. KTU 2024 problems typically assume: - A processor with **n-bit word length**. - Memory is byte-addressable (each address points to **1 byte = 8 bits**). - Instructions are stored starting at byte-aligned addresses. If the word length is **32 bits (4 bytes)**, then: - A **1-word instruction** occupies **4 bytes** of memory. - The PC must therefore increment by **4** after each fetch (not by 1). - $PC_{new} = PC_{old} + \text{Instruction Size in bytes}$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **very common KTU pitfall**: students often write *PC ← PC + 1* assuming byte-addressing means byte-sized instructions. The correct increment is the *instruction length in bytes*, not 1. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Instruction Formats by Number of Addresses KTU Module 1 places heavy emphasis on classifying ISAs by the **number of explicit address fields** per instruction. Let us define: - Let **N** = total instruction length in bits. - Let **Op** = bits used by the opcode. - Let **A** = bits used by each address field. - Let **D** = bits used by data/constant fields. ### Format 1: Three-Address Instructions $$\text{Bits required} = Op + 3A$$ **Example:** `ADD R1, R2, R3` → `R1 ← R2 + R3` - **Most powerful and flexible** format. - Result can be placed in a *third, distinct* register without overwriting operands. - Used in modern RISC architectures (ARM, MIPS, RISC-V). - **Drawback:** Long instructions (e.g., $32 + 3 \times 32 = 128$ bits for a 32-bit address machine). ### Format 2: Two-Address Instructions $$\text{Bits required} = Op + 2A + D$$ **Example:** `ADD R1, R2` → `R1 ← R1 + R2` (one operand is overwritten) - Saves bits; the destination is *implicitly* one of the source registers. - The Intel x86 family uses this style extensively. - **Drawback:** One source operand is destroyed during the operation. ### Format 3: One-Address Instructions $$\text{Bits required} = Op + A + D$$ **Example:** `ADD X` → `AC ← AC + M[X]` (uses an implicit **Accumulator** AC) - Relies on a special hardware register called the **Accumulator (AC)**. - All arithmetic happens between AC and one explicit memory operand. - The result is always stored back in AC. - Used in early microprocessors (e.g., Intel 8085, PDP-8). - **Very short instructions** — economical on memory. ### Format 4: Zero-Address Instructions (Stack-Based) $$\text{Bits required} = Op \text{ only}$$ **Example:** `ADD` → `TOS ← TOS + (TOS-1)` (operates on top two stack elements) - Used in **Stack Machines** (Java Virtual Machine, x87 FPU stack). - No explicit addresses; data is implicitly at the **Top of Stack (TOS)**. - **Shortest possible instructions.** - **Drawback:** High memory traffic due to frequent PUSH/POP operations. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N = Op + k \cdot A$ | Total bits for a *k*-address instruction | Bits | | 2 | $N = Op + A + D$ | One-address format with D-bit data field | Bits | | 3 | $N = Op + 2A$ | Two-address format with two equal address fields | Bits | | 4 | $N = Op + 3A$ | Three-address format | Bits | | 5 | $N = Op$ | Zero-address (stack) format | Bits | | 6 | $PC_{next} = PC_{current} + \frac{N}{8}$ | PC increment after each fetch | Bytes | | 7 | $\text{Memory used} = (\text{No. of instructions}) \times \frac{N}{8}$ | Total program memory footprint | Bytes | | 8 | $A = \log_2(\text{Address space size})$ | Bits needed to address M locations | Bits | | 9 | $\text{Execution time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(F_i + D_i + E_i)$ | Total time = sum of fetch/decode/execute per instruction | Clock cycles | | 10 | $CPI = \frac{\sum \text{cycles for all instructions}}{\text{Total instructions}}$ | Average Cycles Per Instruction | Dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > In all equations, **k** denotes the number of explicit address fields. Do not confuse **A** (address field width) with **D** (data/constant field width) — they are independent design parameters. --- ## 2.4 Instruction Set Design Trade-offs (Why It Matters) The choice of instruction format is not arbitrary — it is a deep engineering trade-off: | Design Goal | Favoured Format | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Small program size** | Multi-address (2 or 3) | Each instruction is more powerful → fewer instructions needed | | **Small instruction size** | Zero or One-address | Shorter opcodes; fits in narrow memory | | **Fast execution** | Three-address (RISC) | Operands stay in registers → no memory access | | **Simple hardware** | One-address (Accumulator) | Only one ALU input to wire up | | **Compiler friendliness** | Three-address | Matches three-operand intermediate representations (e.g., LLVM IR) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Viva Favourite:** Be prepared to explain why modern RISC processors (ARM, RISC-V) prefer *three-address, fixed-length* instructions, whereas legacy CISC processors (Intel x86) use *two-address, variable-length* instructions. The answer revolves around *pipelining regularity* (RISC) vs. *code density* (CISC). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Design:** Compilers must choose how to map high-level expressions into the available instruction formats. Three-address code (TAC) is the de-facto intermediate representation in GCC and LLVM precisely because it maps cleanly onto three-address ISAs. - **Embedded Systems:** Microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, AVR) still use thumb-style compact instruction formats to fit code in tiny on-chip flash. - **Reverse Engineering & Security:** Understanding instruction formats is essential for binary analysis, malware detection, and exploit development. - **Processor Verification:** Every new ISA extension must be validated against the chosen format to avoid ambiguous encodings. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Address Field Width ### Problem Statement A processor has the following specifications: - Memory capacity = **64 K words** - Word size = **16 bits** - Instruction format supports **three-address instructions only** - The opcode field requires **4 bits** ### Question Compute the size of each address field and verify whether a 3-address instruction can fit in a single 16-bit word. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Determine the total number of memory locations (words).** $$ M = 64 \text{ K words} = 64 \times 1024 = 65536 \text{ words} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the number of bits required to address M locations.** To address $M$ distinct locations, the address field must satisfy: $$ A \geq \log_2 M $$ $$ A \geq \log_2(65536) $$ $$ A \geq 16 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the total bits required for a 3-address instruction.** $$ N = Op + 3A $$ $$ N = 4 + 3 \times 16 $$ $$ N = 4 + 48 = 52 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4 — Compare N with the word size.** $$ N = 52 \text{ bits} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Word size} = 16 \text{ bits} $$ Since $52 > 16$, a three-address instruction **cannot fit in a single 16-bit word**. **Step 5 — Compute how many words are needed per instruction.** $$ \text{Words per instruction} = \left\lceil \frac{52}{16} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.25 \rceil = 4 \text{ words} $$ ### Final Answer | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Address field width $A$ | **16 bits** | | Total instruction length $N$ | **52 bits** | | Words per instruction | **4 words (= 64 bits = 8 bytes)** | | Conclusion | 3-address format **does not fit** in a 16-bit word | > [!NOTE] > This is why **16-bit microprocessors historically used one-address (accumulator) or two-address formats** — three-address instructions simply do not fit in 16 bits when each address itself needs 16 bits. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PC Increment Calculation ### Problem Statement Consider a 32-bit byte-addressable processor executing the following program (assembly-like pseudocode): ``` START: LOAD R1, [1000] // R1 ← Mem[1000] ADD R2, R1, #5 // R2 ← R1 + 5 STORE R3, [2000] // Mem[2000] ← R3 HALT ``` Each instruction occupies **exactly 4 bytes**. The program starts at address `0x1000`. ### Question Trace the value of the PC after the execution of each instruction. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Initial state.** $$ PC_{initial} = 0x1000 = 4096_{10} $$ **Step 2 — After fetching instruction 1 (`LOAD R1, [1000]`).** Fetch the 4-byte instruction starting at $0x1000$. The next instruction lies at: $$ PC_{after\_1} = 0x1000 + 4 = 0x1004 $$ **Step 3 — After fetching instruction 2 (`ADD R2, R1, #5`).** The next instruction lies at: $$ PC_{after\_2} = 0x1004 + 4 = 0x1008 $$ **Step 4 — After fetching instruction 3 (`STORE R3, [2000]`).** $$ PC_{after\_3} = 0x1008 + 4 = 0x100C $$ **Step 5 — After fetching instruction 4 (`HALT`).** $$ PC_{after\_4} = 0x100C + 4 = 0x1010 $$ The processor then stops (or enters a halt state). ### Final PC Trace Table | Step | Instruction | PC Before Fetch | PC After Fetch | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD` | `0x1000` | `0x1004` | | 2 | `ADD` | `0x1004` | `0x1008` | | 3 | `STORE` | `0x1008` | `0x100C` | | 4 | `HALT` | `0x100C` | `0x1010` | > [!TIP] > General formula: $PC_{next} = PC_{current} + \text{Instruction Length (in bytes)}$. Apply this uniformly — do not assume $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ for byte-addressable memory. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Format Comparison for a Given Task ### Problem Statement Compute the expression: $$ Z = (A \times B) - (C + D) - E $$ Compare the **number of instructions** and **total bits** required when using: - (a) Three-address format - (b) One-address (accumulator) format Assume: opcode = **8 bits**, address = **6 bits**, data operand = **16 bits**. ### Part (a) — Three-Address Format | # | Instruction | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | `MUL R1, A, B` | $R1 \leftarrow A \times B$ | | 2 | `ADD R2, C, D` | $R2 \leftarrow C + D$ | | 3 | `SUB R3, R1, R2` | $R3 \leftarrow R1 - R2$ | | 4 | `SUB Z, R3, E` | $Z \leftarrow R3 - E$ | $$ \text{Total instructions} = 4 $$ $$ \text{Bits per instruction} = Op + 3A = 8 + 3(6) = 26 \text{ bits} $$ $$ \text{Total bits} = 4 \times 26 = 104 \text{ bits} $$ ### Part (b) — One-Address (Accumulator) Format We use an implicit accumulator $AC$. All memory references are explicit 6-bit addresses. | # | Instruction | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD A` | $AC \leftarrow A$ | | 2 | `MUL B` | $AC \leftarrow AC \times B$ | | 3 | `STORE T1` | $T1 \leftarrow AC$ (temp) | | 4 | `LOAD C` | $AC \leftarrow C$ | | 5 | `ADD D` | $AC \leftarrow AC + D$ | | 6 | `SUB T1` | $AC \leftarrow AC - T1$ | | 7 | `SUB E` | $AC \leftarrow AC - E$ | | 8 | `STORE Z` | $Z \leftarrow AC$ | $$ \text{Total instructions} = 8 $$ $$ \text{Bits per instruction} = Op + A = 8 + 6 = 14 \text{ bits} $$ $$ \text{Total bits} = 8 \times 14 = 112 \text{ bits} $$ ### Comparison Matrix | Metric | Three-Address | One-Address | |---|---|---| | Number of instructions | **4** | **8** | | Bits per instruction | 26 | 14 | | Total program bits | **104** | 112 | | Memory references | 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = **12** | 1+1+1+1+1+1+1+1 = **8** (data) + 2 temp stores = **10** | | Speed of execution | Faster (fewer instr.) | Slower (more instr.) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key takeaway:** Three-address format is **more compact in program length** (104 < 112 bits) **and faster** to execute (4 vs. 8 instructions), but each instruction is **longer** (26 vs. 14 bits). The one-address format saves bits per instruction but pays a heavy price in instruction count. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Register Transfer Notation (RTL) Walkthrough ### Problem Statement Write the complete **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** description for the execution of the instruction: ``` ADD R3, R1, R2 ``` Assume the instruction is encoded as a 3-address format with opcode `0001`. ### Step-by-Step RTL Derivation **Step 1 — Fetch phase.** The processor copies the instruction from memory into the Instruction Register (IR). The PC is incremented: $$ IR \leftarrow M[PC] $$ $$ PC \leftarrow PC + 4 $$ **Step 2 — Decode phase.** The control unit examines the opcode field of IR.
