Recognizing `0001` as `ADD`: $$ \text{Opcode Decoder Output} \leftarrow \text{"ADD with 3-address format"} $$ The address fields are extracted: $$ R1_{field} \leftarrow IR[11:6] $$ $$ R2_{field} \leftarrow IR[5:0] \text{ (or wherever the bit slice lies)} $$ *(Bit positions depend on the encoding; the *concept* is that the decoder separates opcode from operand fields.)* **Step 3 — Operand fetch phase.** The operand values are read from the register file: $$ A \leftarrow R[R1_{field}] $$ $$ B \leftarrow R[R2_{field}] $$ **Step 4 — Execute phase.** The ALU performs the addition: $$ ALU_{result} \leftarrow A + B $$ **Step 5 — Store result phase.** The result is written back to the destination register: $$ R[3] \leftarrow ALU_{result} $$ ### Compact Combined RTL $$ \boxed{ \; R[3] \leftarrow R[1] + R[2]; \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 4 \;} $$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU exam answers, always present RTL in **two parts** — the *operation* (what happens to data) and the *sequencing* (what happens to the PC). Both must be explicitly shown. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Instruction Execution Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute) The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete instruction processing cycle** as taught in KTU Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: Power ON or Reset]) --> B[PC -> MAR
Address bus carries PC value] B --> C[Memory Read: MDR - M MAR
Instruction fetched from memory] C --> D[MDR -> IR
Instruction placed in Instruction Register] D --> E[PC - PC + 4
Program Counter incremented] E --> F[Decoder examines Opcode in IR] F --> G{Opcode Type?} G -->|Data Transfer| H[Activate Data Path
Move data between Reg and Mem] G -->|Data Processing| I[Read Operands from Register File
into ALU inputs A and B] G -->|Control Transfer| J[Evaluate Branch Condition
Update PC if branch taken] H --> K[Update Status Flags Z N C V] I --> L[ALU performs operation
ADD SUB AND OR SHIFT] L --> K J --> K K --> M{More instructions to execute?} M -->|Yes| B M -->|No - HALT or Reset| N([End: Processor Stops]) style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style N fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#D4F4C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read this diagram:** > - The **blue boxes** are the *Fetch* phase. > - The **green box** is the *Decode* phase. > - The **orange diamonds** are *decision* points. > - The **purple box** is the *Execute + Flag Update* phase. > The cycle then loops back to fetch the next instruction, producing a **continuous flow** of execution. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Single-Cycle Processor for Instruction Sequencing The diagram below shows the major hardware blocks involved in executing a single instruction. Each block performs one part of the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR PC[Program Counter PC] -->|Address Bus| MAR[Memory Address Register MAR] MAR -->|Address Lines| MEM[(Main Memory
Instructions and Data)] MEM -->|Data Bus| MDR[Memory Data Register MDR] MDR -->|Copy| IR[Instruction Register IR] IR -->|Opcode Bits| DEC[Instruction Decoder
and Control Unit] IR -->|Operand Field Bits| RF[Register File
R0 to R31] DEC -->|Read Enable| RF RF -->|Operand A| ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU] RF -->|Operand B| ALU DEC -->|ALU Operation Select| ALU ALU -->|Result| RF ALU -->|Flags Z N C V| FLG[Status Flags Register] DEC -->|PC Source Select| PCMUX[PC Multiplexer] PCMUX -->|Next PC Value| PC DEC -->|Branch Target| PCMUX ALU -->|Branch Condition Result| PCMUX style PC fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style MEM fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DEC fill:#D4F4C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ALU fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style FLG fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style PCMUX fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### How Each Block Participates in Sequencing | Hardware Block | Role in Instruction Sequencing | |---|---| | **PC (Program Counter)** | Holds the address of the *next* instruction to fetch | | **MAR** | Latches the address before presenting it to memory | | **Main Memory** | Stores both instructions and data; supplies them on request | | **MDR** | Temporarily holds data coming from / going to memory | | **IR** | Holds the currently executing instruction in a stable form | | **Decoder / Control Unit** | Interprets the opcode and orchestrates all other units | | **Register File** | Provides fast operand storage directly accessible to the ALU | | **ALU** | Performs arithmetic, logic, and shift operations | | **Status Flags** | Record outcomes (zero, negative, carry, overflow) for branches | | **PC Multiplexer** | Chooses the next PC value — either `PC+4` or a branch target | > [!NOTE] > This single-cycle diagram is the **conceptual skeleton** for every KTU Module 1 question on "how an instruction is executed." Memorize the *signal flow* — examiners reward diagrams that show correct connections between PC, MAR, Memory, MDR, and IR. --- ## 4.3 Instruction Format Visual Map (Three-Address) The following diagram shows the bit-level layout of a generic three-address instruction. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph bit32["32-bit Three-Address Instruction Word"] direction LR OP["Opcode Field
Bits 31 to 24
Width = 8 bits"]:::opcode DEST["Destination Address
Bits 23 to 16
Width = 8 bits"]:::dest SRC1["Source 1 Address
Bits 15 to 8
Width = 8 bits"]:::src1 SRC2["Source 2 Address
Bits 7 to 0
Width = 8 bits"]:::src2 end classDef opcode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef dest fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef src1 fill:#D4F4C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef src2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!TIP] > In KTU exam diagrams, **clearly label each field, its bit range, and its width**. Examiners award partial marks for correctly identifying field boundaries even if the rest of the answer has minor errors. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "Instruction Format". List the three basic fields present in a typical machine instruction with a one-line description of each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) **Definition (1 Mark):** An instruction format is the internal layout of bits within a single machine instruction. It defines the position, width, and meaning of every field such that the hardware can decode the instruction unambiguously. **Three Basic Fields (2 Marks):** 1. **Opcode (Operation Code) Field:** Specifies the operation to be performed (e.g., ADD, LOAD, JUMP). It identifies *what* the CPU must do. 2. **Operand / Address Field:** Specifies the location of the data (register, memory address, or constant) on which the operation acts. It identifies *where* the data is. 3. **Mode / Function Field (if present):** Specifies the addressing mode (immediate, direct, indirect) or a sub-variant of the operation. > [!NOTE] > Allocating **1 mark** for the definition and **2 marks** for the three-field enumeration with brief descriptions is the typical KTU valuation pattern. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between three-address, two-address, and one-address instruction formats. Give one example of an assembly instruction for each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) | Format | Implicit Assumption | Example | Effect | |---|---|---|---| | **Three-Address** | Result has its own destination | `ADD R1, R2, R3` | `R1 ← R2 + R3` | | **Two-Address** | Destination = first source | `ADD R1, R2` | `R1 ← R1 + R2` | | **One-Address** | Implicit accumulator (AC) is always involved | `ADD X` | `AC ← AC + M[X]` | **1 mark** for the three-format table, **1 mark** for the implicit-assumption explanation, and **1 mark** for the three correct examples. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** For each Part B question, students are given *internal choice* — meaning they can answer either Choice A *or* Choice B. Both choices test the *same module* but approach the topic from different angles. The (a) sub-part carries 7 marks and the (b) sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### Choice A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Question:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-address, two-address, and one-address instruction formats** with neat diagrams. For each format, derive the total number of bits per instruction given: - Memory size = **16 K words** - Word size = **12 bits** - Opcode size = **4 bits** **(b) [7 Marks]** A program consisting of **1000 instructions** runs on each of the three formats above. Calculate the **total program size in bytes** for each format. Comment on which format is most memory-efficient. --- #### Part (a) Model Solution [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Compute address field width (A).** Number of memory locations: $$ M = 16 \text{ K} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ words} $$ Address field width: $$ A = \log_2(16384) = 14 \text{ bits} $$ **(1 mark)** for $A = 14$ bits. **Step 2 — Three-address format total bits.** $$ N_3 = Op + 3A = 4 + 3(14) = 4 + 42 = 46 \text{ bits} $$ **(1 mark)** for $N_3 = 46$ bits. **Step 3 — Two-address format total bits.** $$ N_2 = Op + 2A = 4 + 2(14) = 4 + 28 = 32 \text{ bits} $$ **(1 mark)** for $N_2 = 32$ bits. **Step 4 — One-address format total bits.** $$ N_1 = Op + A = 4 + 14 = 18 \text{ bits} $$ **(1 mark)** for $N_1 = 18$ bits. **Step 5 — Diagrams (3 marks).** Draw three box diagrams showing the field layout: - **3-address:** `[Opcode: 4][Addr1: 14][Addr2: 14][Addr3: 14]` - **2-address:** `[Opcode: 4][Addr1: 14][Addr2: 14]` - **1-address:** `[Opcode: 4][Addr: 14]` ``` Three-Address: | Opcode(4) | Addr1(14) | Addr2(14) | Addr3(14) | = 46 bits Two-Address: | Opcode(4) | Addr1(14) | Addr2(14) | = 32 bits One-Address: | Opcode(4) | Addr(14) | = 18 bits ``` > [!NOTE] > Award **1 mark each** for the three field-layout diagrams and **1 mark** for labelling the field widths correctly. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Convert bits to bytes for each format.** For 1000 instructions: $$ \text{Size}_3 = 1000 \times 46 \text{ bits} = 46000 \text{ bits} = \frac{46000}{8} = 5750 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Size}_2 = 1000 \times 32 \text{ bits} = 32000 \text{ bits} = \frac{32000}{8} = 4000 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Size}_1 = 1000 \times 18 \text{ bits} = 18000 \text{ bits} = \frac{18000}{8} = 2250 \text{ bytes} $$ **(3 marks)** — **1 mark** per correct size calculation. **Step 2 — Comparison Table (2 marks).** | Format | Bits/Instr | Total Size (1000 instr.) | Bytes/Instr | |---|---|---|---| | Three-address | 46 | 5750 bytes | 5.75 | | Two-address | 32 | 4000 bytes | 4.00 | | One-address | **18** | **2250 bytes** | **2.25** | **Step 3 — Conclusion (2 marks).** The **one-address format is the most memory-efficient** in terms of bits per instruction. However, as discussed earlier, it requires *more instructions* to express the same task. Therefore: - *Bits per instruction:* One-address is smallest. - *Program size in instructions:* One-address is largest. - *Overall memory footprint:* Depends on the trade-off; for the same 1000-instruction program, one-address wins. --- ### Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Question:** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** of a single instruction. Show clearly the role of the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Memory Address Register (MAR)**, **Memory Data Register (MDR)**, and **Instruction Register (IR)**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following two-instruction program running on a byte-addressable processor with **32-bit instructions**: ``` Address 0x0000: LOAD R1, [0x1000] // R1 ← M[0x1000] Address 0x0004: ADD R2, R1, #10 // R2 ← R1 + 10 Address 0x0008: HALT ``` Trace the **PC, MAR, MDR, and IR** values through the complete execution of this program. Assume `M[0x1000] = 25`. --- #### Part (a) Model Solution [7 Marks] **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Use the diagram shown in Section 4.2 of these notes (or draw an equivalent labelled block diagram showing PC → MAR → Memory → MDR → IR → Decoder). **Fetch Phase (2 marks):** 1. `MAR ← PC` — address of next instruction is loaded into MAR. 2. `MDR ← M[MAR]` — memory contents at that address are fetched into MDR. 3. `IR ← MDR` — the fetched instruction is placed into the Instruction Register. 4. `PC ← PC + 4` — Program Counter is incremented to point to the next instruction. **Decode Phase (1 mark):** 5. The **Instruction Decoder** examines the opcode field of IR. 6. The decoder generates control signals that activate the appropriate data path (e.g., ALU inputs, register read/write enables). **Execute Phase (1 mark):** 7. Based on the decoded opcode, the corresponding operation is performed: - For `LOAD`: read memory at the operand address, write the value into the destination register. - For `ADD`: read two source registers, perform addition in the ALU, write result to the destination register. - For `HALT`: stop the processor. > [!NOTE] > The complete cycle is then **repeated** for the next instruction at the updated PC value. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution [7 Marks] **Initial state:** $$ PC = 0x0000, \quad MAR = ?, \quad MDR = ?, \quad IR = ?, \quad M[0x1000] = 25 $$ **Instruction 1: `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` located at `0x0000`** | Cycle Step | Operation | PC | MAR | MDR | IR | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Fetch 1a | `MAR ← PC` | `0x0000` | `0x0000` | ? | ? | | Fetch 1b | `MDR ← M[MAR]` | `0x0000` | `0x0000` | `LOAD instr` | ? | | Fetch 1c | `IR ← MDR` | `0x0000` | `0x0000` | `LOAD instr` | `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` | | Fetch 1d | `PC ← PC + 4` | `0x0004` | `0x0000` | `LOAD instr` | `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` | | Exec 1a | Read effective address `0x1000` | `0x0004` | `0x1000` | `LOAD instr` | `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` | | Exec 1b | `MDR ← M[0x1000] = 25` | `0x0004` | `0x1000` | `25` | `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` | | Exec 1c | `R1 ← MDR = 25` | `0x0004` | `0x1000` | `25` | `LOAD R1, [0x1000]` | **State after Instruction 1:** `R1 = 25`, `PC = 0x0004`. **(2 marks)** **Instruction 2: `ADD R2, R1, #10` located at `0x0004`** | Cycle Step | Operation | PC | MAR | MDR | IR | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Fetch 2a | `MAR ← PC` | `0x0004` | `0x0004` | `25` | `LOAD instr` | | Fetch 2b | `MDR ← M[MAR]` | `0x0004` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `LOAD instr` | | Fetch 2c | `IR ← MDR` | `0x0004` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `ADD R2, R1, #10` | | Fetch 2d | `PC ← PC + 4` | `0x0008` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `ADD R2, R1, #10` | | Exec 2a | Read `R1 = 25`, immediate = `10` | `0x0008` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `ADD R2, R1, #10` | | Exec 2b | `ALU: 25 + 10 = 35` | `0x0008` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `ADD R2, R1, #10` | | Exec 2c | `R2 ← 35` | `0x0008` | `0x0004` | `ADD instr` | `ADD R2, R1, #10` | **State after Instruction 2:** `R1 = 25`, `R2 = 35`, `PC = 0x0008`. **(2 marks)** **Instruction 3: `HALT` located at `0x0008`** | Cycle Step | Operation | PC | IR | |---|---|---|---| | Fetch 3a | `MAR ← PC` | `0x0008` | ... | | Fetch 3b | `MDR ← M[MAR] = HALT` | `0x0008` | ... | | Fetch 3c | `IR ← MDR` | `0x0008` | `HALT` | | Fetch 3d | `PC ← PC + 4` | `0x000C` | `HALT` | | Exec 3 | Stop processor | `0x000C` | `HALT` | **Final state:** `R1 = 25`, `R2 = 35`, `PC = 0x000C`, processor halted. **(1 mark)** **Compact final result table (2 marks):** | After Instruction | PC | R1 | R2 | Processor State | |---|---|---|---|---| | `LOAD` | `0x0004` | 25 | — | Running | | `ADD` | `0x0008` | 25 | 35 | Running | | `HALT` | `0x000C` | 25 | 35 | Halted | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for "Instruction Sequencing" Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting to update the PC after each fetch.** Many students show the fetched instruction correctly but leave PC unchanged. The fetch phase *always* includes a `PC ← PC + k` step. **(–1 to –2 marks)** > > 2. **Confusing MAR and MDR.** MAR holds the *address*; MDR holds the *data*. Examiners treat this as a fundamental error. **(–1 mark minimum)** > > 3. **Wrong PC increment.** For a 32-bit instruction on a byte-addressable machine, the increment is **4**, not 1. **(–1 mark)** > > 4. **Missing the `HALT` PC update.** Some students assume the processor stops at the HALT instruction and never show the final `PC ← PC + 4` step. **(–1 mark)** > > 5. **Confusing "Instruction Format" with "Addressing Mode."** These are separate concepts. The *format* is the bit-layout; the *addressing mode* is the *interpretation* of the operand. Examiners deduct for mixing them up. **(–1 to –2 marks)** > > 6. **Omitting diagrams.** A well-labelled block diagram is worth at least **2–3 marks** in any 14-mark question on this topic. Always include one. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ ### Key Definitions to Memorize - **Instruction:** A binary-coded command stored in memory that directs the processor to perform one specific operation. - **Instruction Format:** The internal bit-layout of an instruction (opcode + operand fields + mode bits). - **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA):** The complete set of machine instructions recognized by a processor. - **Program Counter (PC):** A register that always holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. - **Instruction Sequencing:** The process by which the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions one after another in a controlled order. ### Essential Formulas (Revise Before Exam) - Total instruction length: $N = Op + k \cdot A$ (for k-address format) - Address field width: $A = \log_2(\text{Memory size in words})$ - PC update: $PC_{next} = PC_{current} + \text{Instruction size in bytes}$ - Average execution time per instruction: $T_{avg} = CPI \times T_{clock}$ - CPI (Cycles Per Instruction): $CPI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i}{n}$ ### Format Comparison — Quick Reference | Format | Bits/Instr | # Instr. for $Z = A \cdot B - (C + D) - E$ | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **3-Address** | Longest | Fewest (4) | RISC (MIPS, ARM, RISC-V) | | **2-Address** | Medium | Medium | x86 CISC family | | **1-Address** | Short | More (with temp stores) | Early 8/16-bit microcontrollers | | **0-Address (Stack)** | Shortest | Most | Java VM, x87 FPU | ### Key Hardware Registers and Their Roles | Register | Role | |---|---| | **PC** | Holds address of next instruction | | **MAR** | Latches memory address for read/write | | **MDR** | Latches data being read from / written to memory | | **IR** | Holds the currently executing instruction | | **AC (Accumulator)** | Implicit operand for 1-address machines | | **Status Flags (Z, N, C, V)** | Record ALU outcomes for conditional branches | ### High-Yield Exam Tips 🎯 1. **Always show PC update in fetch phase.** Even for HALT. 2. **Byte-addressable ≠ byte-sized instructions.** Compute the actual size. 3. **Use clear, labelled diagrams** in every long-answer question. 4. **For 1-address problems, introduce the accumulator $AC$ explicitly** in the very first line. 5. **For 3-address problems, state that all three operands are explicit registers** to avoid confusion with implicit-operand formats. 6. **Practice the "trace table" format** for register-transfer questions — KTU examiners award full marks for well-structured tables.

topics.title: Instruction Set Principles Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Instruction Set Principles Modes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) — The Heart of the Machine > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCITT302 / Module 1):** > An **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** is the *complete specification* of the programmer-visible interface between the hardware and the lowest-level software. It defines the **data types, registers, addressing modes, instruction formats, opcodes, interrupt/exception model, and the I/O architecture** that the machine exposes to the programmer. In simple terms, the **ISA is the contract** between hardware and software. The same binary program (compiled for ARM, for instance) cannot run on an x86 processor because their ISAs differ — even if the underlying transistors are equally powerful. The ISA is, therefore, the **boundary of portability**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of a processor as a *kitchen* and the ISA as its **menu**. > - Each **menu item** = an *instruction* (e.g., "ADD", "LOAD", "BRANCH"). > - **Ingredients you can specify** = *operands* and *addressing modes* (e.g., "from the top shelf" = direct, "from the recipe book page 5" = indirect). > - **Waiter** = the *instruction decoder / control unit*. > - **Chef's pantry layout** = the *microarchitecture* (a private implementation detail). > > Just as a customer cannot walk into the kitchen to cook his own dish, a programmer cannot directly manipulate micro-architectural details like pipeline stages or cache lines — they must use only what the ISA *menu* exposes. ## 1.3 Instruction Set Principles — The Four Pillars The design of any modern instruction set rests on four fundamental principles: | # | Principle | Meaning | |---|-----------|---------| | 1 | **Orthogonality** | Every operation should be usable with every addressing mode and every data type, without arbitrary restrictions. | | 2 | **Completeness** | The ISA should provide a sufficient set of primitives so that *all* higher-level operations can be synthesized. | | 3 | **Regularity** | Instructions of the same class (e.g., all ALU ops) should follow a consistent encoding pattern. | | 4 | **Efficiency** | Frequently used operations must be encoded compactly and execute quickly. | ## 1.4 Instruction Formats — The Shape of a Command Every machine instruction is a binary word composed of: - **Opcode field** → specifies *what* operation to perform. - **Address/operand fields** → specify *where* the operands are and *how many* of them. The four canonical formats (by operand count) are: 1. **Three-Address Instructions** — e.g., `ADD R1, R2, R3` (R1 ← R2 + R3). Used in classic RISC machines. 2. **Two-Address Instructions** — e.g., `ADD R1, R2` (R1 ← R1 + R2). Used in x86 family. 3. **One-Address Instructions** — e.g., `ADD M` (AC ← AC + M[addr]). Used in simple accumulator machines. 4. **Zero-Address Instructions** — e.g., `ADD` (operates on top of stack). Used in Java VM, x87 FPU. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Instruction Set Design Decisions (The "ISA Design Space") A computer architect chooses between two extremes when designing an instruction set: > [!IMPORTANT] > **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer):** Many addressing modes, variable-length instructions, multi-cycle complex instructions (e.g., Intel x86, Motorola 68000). The motivation is to *reduce the semantic gap* between high-level languages and machine code. > > **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):** Few addressing modes, fixed-length instructions, load/store architecture, single-cycle execution (e.g., ARM, MIPS, RISC-V). The motivation is to *simplify the hardware* so the clock rate can be pushed higher. ## 2.2 Addressing Modes — The "How to Find the Operand" Problem An **addressing mode** is the mechanism by which the processor computes the *effective address* of an operand from the bits encoded in the instruction. The effective address is the final, physical address the hardware actually places on the memory bus. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** For a displacement-mode instruction like `OP Rd, X(Rs)`, the Effective Address is computed as: > $$ EA = \text{Contents}(Rs) + X $$ > where $X$ is a constant (sign-extended) embedded in the instruction and $Rs$ holds a base address. Mastering this single equation is sufficient to solve ~80% of the addressing-mode numerical problems in KTU exams. ## 2.3 The 8 Canonical Addressing Modes (KTU Frequently Asked) | # | Mode | Syntax (Generic) | Effective Address Calculation | Use Case | |---|------|------------------|-------------------------------|----------| | 1 | **Immediate** | `#N` | Operand is the constant $N$ itself | Loading constants, e.g., `MOV R1, #5` | | 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | `(addr)` | $EA = addr$ (address field of instruction) | Static global variables | | 3 | **Indirect** | `@(addr)` or `@R` | $EA = \text{Mem}[addr]$ (one extra memory fetch) | Pointer dereferencing in C | | 4 | **Register** | `R` | Operand is in the named register | ALU operations, fastest mode | | 5 | **Register Indirect** | `(R)` | $EA = \text{Contents}(R)$ | Pointer traversal (`*p++`) | | 6 | **Displacement (Indexed/Base+Offset)** | `X(R)` | $EA = \text{Contents}(R) + X$ | Array access, struct field access | | 7 | **Relative (PC-Relative)** | `X(PC)` | $EA = PC + X$ | Branch targets, position-independent code | | 8 | **Auto-increment / Auto-decrement** | `(R)+` / `-(R)` | $EA = \text{Contents}(R)$, then $R \leftarrow R \pm 1$ | Stack operations, `*p++` loops | ## 2.4 Formula Sheet & Memory Reference Count > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory-Reference Cheat Code (used heavily in KTU problems):** > When the processor executes an operand-fetch, the *total number of memory references* for a single operand can be 0, 1, or 2. The number depends on the mode. | Mode | Memory References to Fetch Operand | |------|------------------------------------| | Immediate | **0** (value is in the instruction itself) | | Register | **0** (value is in the register file) | | Direct / Displacement / Relative | **1** (one fetch from computed EA) | | Register Indirect | **1** (one fetch from address held in register) | | Indirect | **2** (one fetch for the address, one for the value) | ## 2.5 Why Addressing Modes Matter in Engineering - **Compiler writers** rely on the rich set of addressing modes to generate tight code (e.g., `*p++` becomes a single auto-increment instruction). - **OS kernel designers** use absolute and register-indirect modes to manipulate the page tables and stack. - **Embedded systems engineers** exploit the small, fixed set of ARM/MIPS modes to write deterministic, predictable real-time code. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Effective Address Computation (KTU Standard Problem) > **Problem:** Given a processor whose PC = `2000` (decimal) and the following register contents: > $R1 = 400$, $R2 = 1000$, $X = 500$ > Compute the **Effective Address (EA)** for each of the following addressing-mode instructions: > (i) Direct address `500` > (ii) Immediate operand `500` > (iii) Register indirect `(R1)` > (iv) Indexed (Displacement) `500(R2)` > (v) Relative `500(PC)` **Solution — Step by Step:** **(i) Direct (Absolute) Addressing** The address field of the instruction itself is the EA. $$ EA = 500 $$ **(ii) Immediate Addressing** There is **no memory reference**; the operand value is the constant itself. $$ \text{Operand Value} = 500 $$ **(iii) Register Indirect `(R1)`** The address field points to a register, and the *contents* of that register are the EA. $$ EA = \text{Contents}(R1) = 400 $$ **(iv) Indexed / Displacement `500(R2)`** The base register is `R2`, the displacement is `500`. The EA is their sum. $$ EA = \text{Contents}(R2) + 500 = 1000 + 500 = 1500 $$ **(v) PC-Relative `500(PC)`** The displacement is added to the current value of the Program Counter. $$ EA = PC + 500 = 2000 + 500 = 2500 $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Memory-Reference Counting for an Instruction Sequence > **Problem:** A CPU executes the following hypothetical instruction (single operand in mode $M$): > `LOAD X(R1)` where `R1 = 1000`, `X = 4`, and `M[1004] = 750`. > The instruction itself is stored at memory location `200`. > Calculate (a) Effective Address, (b) Total memory references. **Solution:** (a) Effective Address (Base-Register Displacement mode): $$ EA = \text{Contents}(R1) + X = 1000 + 4 = 1004 $$ (b) Memory references: - **1** reference to fetch the instruction from `M[200]`. - **1** reference to fetch the operand from `M[1004]`. - **Total = 2 memory references.** --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Among Instruction Formats | Format | Instruction Length | Memory Used by Program | Cycles per Instruction | Example ISA | |--------|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|-------------| | 3-Address | Longest | Lowest (small code size) | Fewest (parallelism-friendly) | ARM, MIPS | | 2-Address | Medium | Medium | Medium | x86 (legacy) | | 1-Address | Short | High (needs accumulator) | Many | PDP-8 (vintage) | | 0-Address (Stack) | Shortest | Highest (many `PUSH`/`POP`) | Many | JVM, x87 | > [!NOTE] > **Derivation of the "3-address advantage" :** If a C statement `a = b + c + d` is to be executed, a 3-address machine needs only **2 instructions**: > `ADD a, b, c` ; `ADD a, a, d` > A 1-address (accumulator) machine needs **3 loads, 2 adds, 1 store = 6 memory references** for the same statement. This is why modern load/store RISC ISAs use the 3-address form for ALU ops. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Why RISC Beats CISC in Cycles per Instruction (CPI) For a program of $N$ instructions, the total execution time is: $$ T = N \times CPI \times T_{clock} $$ - **CISC machine:** Tries to minimize $N$ by using complex instructions (low $N$), but each instruction has high, variable CPI (CPI ≈ 4–10) and a long $T_{clock}$ (since the control unit is microcoded). - **RISC machine:** Has higher $N$ (more, simpler instructions), but CPI ≈ **1** and $T_{clock}$ is short (hard-wired control). The product $N \times CPI$ is roughly the same in both designs, but the **shorter $T_{clock}$** of RISC wins in raw speed. This is the central insight of the RISC philosophy. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Instruction Execution Pipeline (Top-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fetch Instruction
from Memory] --> B[Decode Opcode
and Read Registers] B --> C[Compute Effective Address
EA via Addressing Mode] C --> D[Fetch Operand
from EA] D --> E[Execute ALU Operation
in Datapath] E --> F[Store Result
to Destination] F --> G[Update PC
and Repeat] style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the **Effective Address calculation** is a distinct, addressable stage. This is the stage that physically consults the addressing-mode logic (the `Addr` field of the decoded instruction is added to a base register, fed through the index mux, etc.). ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Addressing-Mode Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR IR[Instruction Register IR] --> DEC[Opcode Decoder] IR --> ADDR[Address Field ADDR] ADDR --> SIGN[Sign Extender] SIGN --> MUX[Addressing Mode MUX] REG0[R0] --> MUX REG1[R1] --> MUX REG2[R2] --> MUX PC[PC Register] --> MUX MUX -->|mode select| EA[Effective Address EA] DEC --> MUX EA --> MEM[Address Bus to Memory] style MUX fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000 style EA fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The decoder examines the opcode, then drives the *mode-select* lines of the MUX. The MUX picks the correct source — base register, PC, sign-extended displacement, or immediate — to form the final EA. This is the **hardware reality** behind the abstract addressing modes listed in Section 2.3. ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stack-Based (Zero-Address) Instruction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[PUSH A] --> STK[Stack Top
TOS = A] P2[PUSH B] --> STK2[Stack Top
TOS = B
Below = A] P3[ADD] --> POP1[Pop B to ACC] POP1 --> POP2[Pop A to Operand] POP2 --> ALU[ACC = A + B] ALU --> PUSH[PUSH Result] PUSH --> STK3[New TOS = A + B] style STK fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style ALU fill:#ea9999,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison — RISC vs. CISC ISA ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RISC[RISC Architecture] R1[Fixed Length 32-bit Instr] R2[Load-Store Design] R3[3-Address Format] R4[Hardwired Control] R5[Large Register File 32x32] end subgraph CISC[CISC Architecture] C1[Variable Length 1-15 bytes] C2[Register-Memory Mixed] C3[2 or 1-Address Format] C4[Microcoded Control] C5[Smaller Register File 8x32] end R1 --- C1 R2 --- C2 R3 --- C3 R4 --- C4 R5 --- C5 style RISC fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13 style CISC fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#7f6000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]`** > **Q.** *Define an instruction set. List any four instruction formats.* **Model Answer:** An instruction set is the complete collection of machine-language commands (opcodes) that a processor is designed to execute. It is the programmer-visible interface of the computer. Four instruction formats are: 1. **Three-address format** — `OP dst, src1, src2` 2. **Two-address format** — `OP dst, src` (dst is implicit second source) 3. **One-address format** — `OP addr` (uses an implicit accumulator) 4. **Zero-address format** — `OP` (operates on top-of-stack) --- > **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]`** > **Q.** *What is an addressing mode? Why is "register indirect" mode preferred over "indirect" mode in high-level language implementations?* **Model Answer:** An addressing mode is the rule by which the Effective Address (EA) of an operand is computed from the address bits in the instruction. Register-indirect mode is preferred because: 1. It requires **only one memory reference** (to fetch the operand), while indirect mode needs **two** (one to fetch the address, another to fetch the value). 2. The address can be **modified quickly** by simply updating the register (no need to modify the instruction in memory — useful for relocatable code and pointer-based data structures like linked lists). --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Module Internal Choice: Option A` > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`** > > **(a)** Explain the different types of addressing modes used in instruction sets with suitable examples. Show how the effective address is calculated in each case. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A processor has PC = 2500. The contents of registers are: R1 = 1500, R2 = 800. A displacement-mode instruction specifies X = 200. Calculate the effective address for: (i) Direct, (ii) Indexed `X(R1)`, (iii) Relative `X(PC)`, (iv) Register indirect using R2. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** The eight standard addressing modes with EA formulas are: - **Immediate:** Operand is part of the instruction. $EA$ = N/A; value = constant in IR. - **Direct (Absolute):** $EA = A$ (where A is the address field). - **Indirect:** $EA = \text{Mem}[A]$. - **Register:** Operand is in a register; no memory access. - **Register Indirect:** $EA = \text{Contents}(R)$. - **Displacement (Indexed):** $EA = \text{Contents}(R) + A$. - **Relative:** $EA = PC + A$. - **Auto-inc / Auto-dec:** $EA = \text{Contents}(R)$, then $R \leftarrow R \pm 1$. (Examples: `MOV R1, #5` for immediate; `LOAD [1000]` for direct; `LOAD (R1)` for register-indirect; `LOAD 4(R2)` for indexed; `BEQ 8(PC)` for relative; `LOAD (R1)+` for auto-increment.) **Model Solution — Part (b):** | Sub-part | Mode | Formula | Computation | Result | |----------|------|---------|-------------|--------| | (i) Direct | Absolute | $EA = A$ | $EA = 200$ | **EA = 200** | | (ii) Indexed `X(R1)` | Displacement | $EA = \text{Contents}(R1) + X$ | $EA = 1500 + 200$ | **EA = 1700** | | (iii) Relative `X(PC)` | PC-relative | $EA = PC + X$ | $EA = 2500 + 200$ | **EA = 2700** | | (iv) Register Indirect | `@R2` | $EA = \text{Contents}(R2)$ | $EA = 800$ | **EA = 800** | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Module Internal Choice: Option B` > **`[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`** > > **(a)** Compare RISC and CISC architectures in detail. State the merits of each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Consider the evaluation of the arithmetic statement: $X = (A + B) \times (C + D)$. > Show the sequence of three-address, two-address, one-address, and zero-address instructions required to evaluate it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** | Feature | RISC | CISC | |---------|------|------| | Instruction size | Fixed (typically 32 bits) | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) | | Addressing modes | Few (3–5) | Many (12+) | | Memory access | Load/Store only | Register–Memory mixed | | Control logic | Hardwired (fast) | Microcoded (slower) | | CPI | ≈ 1 | 2–15 | | Clock period | Short | Longer | | Code size | Larger | Smaller | | Example | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V | Intel x86, Motorola 68k | **Merits of RISC:** Simpler hardware → higher clock; pipelining-friendly; lower power; predictable timing. **Merits of CISC:** Smaller programs; smaller memory footprint; less work for the compiler of the 1970s. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Three-Address (RISC-style, 4 instructions):** ``` ADD T1, A, B // T1 ← A + B ADD T2, C, D // T2 ← C + D MUL X, T1, T2 // X ← T1 × T2 ``` **Two-Address (x86-style, 4 instructions):** ``` MOV T1, A ADD T1, B MOV T2, C ADD T2, D MUL T1, T2 MOV X, T1 ``` **One-Address (Accumulator, 7 instructions):** ``` LOAD A // ACC ← A ADD B // ACC ← A + B STORE T // T ← ACC LOAD C ADD D MUL T STORE X ``` **Zero-Address (Stack, 9 instructions):** ``` PUSH A PUSH B ADD // TOS = A + B PUSH C PUSH D ADD // TOS = C + D MUL // TOS = (A+B) × (C+D) POP X ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Always show the formula first** (e.g., $EA = \text{Contents}(R) + X$) **before** substituting numerical values. Marks are awarded for the *method*, not just the final number. > 2. **Confusing register-indirect with indirect:** In *indirect* mode, the address field of the instruction points to a memory location whose *contents* are the EA. In *register-indirect*, the instruction points to a *register* whose contents are the EA. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 3. **Forgetting PC auto-increment:** When computing PC-relative EA, remember the PC is typically *already incremented* past the current instruction. KTU papers often specify whether to use "old PC" or "new PC" — read carefully. > 4. **Stack machine answers:** When asked for zero-address code, students frequently forget the `POP X` at the end. The result on the Top-of-Stack is *useless* until it is popped to a named variable. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)** > > - [ ] **ISA** = programmer-visible contract: opcode set, data types, registers, addressing modes, memory model. > - [ ] **4 design principles:** Orthogonality, Completeness, Regularity, Efficiency. > - [ ] **4 instruction formats:** 3-addr, 2-addr, 1-addr, 0-addr (Stack). > - [ ] **3-address** is the most powerful and parallel-friendly (RISC). > - [ ] **1-address** uses an implicit accumulator; needs more memory references per statement. > - [ ] **0-address** uses a stack; needs many `PUSH`/`POP` instructions. > - [ ] **8 canonical addressing modes** — know EA formula for each. > - [ ] **Master equation:** $EA = \text{Contents}(R_{base}) + \text{Displacement}$. > - [ ] **Memory references:** Immediate/Register = 0; Direct/Indexed = 1; Indirect = 2. > - [ ] **PC-relative** is used for branches → position-independent code. > - [ ] **Register-indirect** is the workhorse of pointer dereferencing in C. > - [ ] **RISC** = fixed length, load/store, hardwired, low CPI, high clock. > - [ ] **CISC** = variable length, microcoded, complex instructions, compact code. > - [ ] **Total time** $T = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ — RISC wins by making $T_{clock}$ tiny. > - [ ] In numerical KTU problems, **always** show the formula → substitute → answer, in that order.

topics.title: Classifying Instruction Set Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classifying Instruction Set Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** is the systematic categorization of processor instruction sets based on the **internal storage model** used to hold operands during computation, the **number of explicit addresses** contained per instruction, and the **type of operands** the instructions manipulate. It defines the boundary between the *hardware implementation* and the *software-visible programming model* of a CPU.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCITT302 - Module 1)** framework, the classification is primarily based on three orthogonal axes:

1. **Operand Storage Architecture** – *Where* are the operands kept? (Stack, Accumulator, or Registers).
2. **Number of Explicit Addresses per Instruction** – *How many* memory/register locations are named in a typical arithmetic instruction?
3. **Nature of Operations & Operand Types** – *What kinds* of data do instructions operate on (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, FP, character)?

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Chef's Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a chef (the CPU) cooking a dish (computation):
>
> - In an **Accumulator-based kitchen**, there is *one* special mixing bowl (the accumulator). The chef must scoop ingredients from the pantry (memory) into the bowl, mix, and put the result back. Only one bowl exists, so the chef is constantly swapping things in and out.
> - In a **Stack-based kitchen**, ingredients are placed on a tall pile (the stack). The chef only ever grabs from the *top* of the pile — this is fast, but remembering which ingredient is below becomes tricky.
> - In a **Register-based kitchen**, the chef has *many* labeled mixing bowls (R1, R2, R3…). This is the most flexible and is what modern processors (ARM, RISC-V, x86) use.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters

The choice of ISA class directly determines:
- **Instruction length** (in bits)
- **Number of memory accesses** per instruction (a critical performance metric)
- **Compiler complexity** required to generate code
- **Clock cycles per instruction (CPI)** and overall **MIPS / throughput**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Emphasis):**
> The most cited classification metric is the **maximum number of memory operands per ALU instruction**. Stack architectures implicitly have **0**; Accumulator has **1**; Register-based can have **0, 1, 2, or 3** depending on sub-class.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primary Operand-Storage Classes

#### 2.1.1 Stack Architecture
- Operands are *implicitly* on the **Top-Of-Stack (TOS)**.
- No explicit operand field is needed in the instruction.
- All ALU operations (ADD, MUL, SUB) are **zero-address** instructions.
- Example (evaluate $C = A + B$):
  - `PUSH A`
  - `PUSH B`
  - `ADD`  → TOS now contains $A+B$
  - `POP C`
- **Used in:** Java Virtual Machine (JVM), x87 FPU stack, early Forth machines, Burroughs B5000.

#### 2.1.2 Accumulator Architecture
- One special **implicit register** called the **Accumulator (ACC)** holds one operand and receives the result.
- All ALU operations are **one-address** instructions.
- Example (evaluate $C = A + B$):
  - `LOAD A`     → ACC = A
  - `ADD B`      → ACC = ACC + B
  - `STORE C`    → C = ACC
- **Used in:** Early von Neumann machines (EDSAC, IAS), 6502, PDP-8.

#### 2.1.3 Register-Based (General-Purpose Register) Architecture
- Operands are explicitly named **general-purpose registers (GPRs)**.
- Sub-classified by **maximum number of memory operands per ALU instruction**:
  - **Register-Register (Load/Store)** → 0 memory operands, e.g., ARM, RISC-V, MIPS, SPARC, PowerPC.
  - **Register-Memory** → 1 memory operand, e.g., Intel x86, Motorola 68000.
  - **Memory-Memory** → 2 or 3 memory operands, e.g., VAX.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The reason modern desktop CPUs prefer Register-Memory (x86) while mobile/embedded prefer Register-Register (ARM/RISC-V) lies in code density vs. pipeline simplicity. Register-Register allows uniform instruction length → easier pipelining → higher clock speeds.

### 2.2 Comparison Table — Key Architectural Parameters

| Parameter | Stack | Accumulator | Register-Register | Register-Memory | Memory-Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Explicit addresses/instr.** | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 3 |
| **Memory operands per ALU instr.** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **Total instrs for $A=B+C$** | 4 | 3 | 4 (with loads) | 3 | 3 |
| **Memory accesses for $A=B+C$** | 7 | 6 | 6 | 4 | 3 |
| **Instruction size (typical)** | 8 bits | 12–16 bits | 32 bits | 8–32 bits (variable) | 8–32 bits (variable) |
| **Compiler complexity** | High | High | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Pipelining friendliness** | Low | Low | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |
| **Representative ISA** | JVM | PDP-8 | MIPS / ARM | x86 | VAX |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations a KTU examiner can spring on you:**

**Total Memory Accesses for an Expression** (with $n$ operands and $m$ operators in a Load-Store architecture):

$$
\text{MemAccess}_{\text{LS}} = 2n \;(\text{loads}) + 1 \;(\text{store}) + 0 \;(\text{ALU}) = 2n + 1
$$

**Total Instructions for an Expression** (with $m$ operators in a Load-Store architecture):

$$
\text{Instr}_{\text{LS}} = 2n \;(\text{loads}) + m \;(\text{ALU}) + 1 \;(\text{store}) = 2n + m + 1
$$

**Bits Required to Encode $k$ Registers** in an instruction format:

$$
\text{RegField}_{\text{bits}} = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil
$$

**Instruction Format Size** for a 3-register Load/Store ISA:

$$
\text{InstrSize} = \text{Opcode}_{\text{bits}} + 3 \times \text{RegField}_{\text{bits}} + \text{Immediate}_{\text{bits}}
$$

> **Example:** For a Load-Store ISA with **8 registers** and **6-bit opcode** and **16-bit immediate**:
>
> $$\text{InstrSize} = 6 + 3 \times \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil + 16 = 6 + 3(3) + 16 = 31 \text{ bits}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Code-generator phase of GCC/LLVM uses this classification to choose *register allocation* strategy.
- **Performance Modeling:** Architects use instruction-mix statistics (e.g., **45% loads, 20% stores, 15% ALU, 20% branches** — SPEC benchmarks) to design cache hierarchies.
- **Reverse Engineering & Emulation:** Tools like QEMU and Ghidra rely on the precise ISA class to translate machine code to micro-ops.
- **Embedded Systems:** Picking between ARM (RISC, Load-Store) and 8051 (CISC, Register-Memory) determines power budget by **30–60%** for the same workload.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Expression $A = (B + C) \times D$ Across All 5 Architectures

Let us evaluate the same high-level statement across every class so a KTU student can see the *mechanical* difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Memory variables $A, B, C, D$. Each architectural model must produce $A = (B + C) \times D$ in memory.

#### (a) Stack Architecture (0-address ALU)

```
PUSH B
PUSH C
ADD         ; TOS = B + C
PUSH D
MUL         ; TOS = (B + C) * D
POP  A
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**
1. `PUSH B` reads B from memory and pushes it on the stack.
2. `PUSH C` reads C and places it on top of B.
3. `ADD` pops top two elements, adds them, and pushes the result $B+C$.
4. `PUSH D` reads D and places it on top.
5. `MUL` pops top two, multiplies, pushes $(B+C) \times D$.
6. `POP A` stores TOS into memory location A.

**Memory Accesses:**

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{Stack}} = \underbrace{4}_{\text{PUSHes}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{POPs}} = 6
$$

**Instructions issued:** 6

#### (b) Accumulator Architecture (1-address ALU)

```
LOAD  B
ADD   C
MUL   D
STORE A
```

**Memory Accesses:**

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{Acc}} = \underbrace{3}_{\text{LOAD/ADD/MUL reads}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{STORE write}} = 4
$$

Wait — corrected accounting (the *operand* memory access only, not ACC's internal fetch):

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{Acc}} = 3 \;(\text{mem reads}) + 1 \;(\text{mem write}) = 4
$$

**Instructions issued:** 4

#### (c) Register-Register (Load/Store) — Assume registers R1, R2, R3

```
LOAD   R1, B
LOAD   R2, C
ADD    R3, R1, R2
LOAD   R1, D
MUL    R3, R3, R1
STORE  A, R3
```

**Memory Accesses:**

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{LS}} = 4 \;(\text{LOADs}) + 1 \;(\text{STORE}) = 5
$$

**Instructions issued:** 6

#### (d) Register-Memory — 1 memory operand permitted per ALU

```
LOAD   R1, B
ADD    R1, C        ; R1 = R1 + C  (1 mem op + 1 reg)
MUL    R1, D        ; R1 = R1 * D
STORE  A, R1
```

**Memory Accesses:**

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{RM}} = 3 \;(\text{LOAD/ADD/MUL reads}) + 1 \;(\text{STORE write}) = 4
$$

**Instructions issued:** 4

#### (e) Memory-Memory — All operands from memory

```
ADD    R_temp, B, C
MUL    A,    R_temp, D
; (or in 3-op form, if hardware supports)
```

**Memory Accesses:**

$$
\text{Accesses}_{\text{MM}} = 3 \;(\text{reads}) + 1 \;(\text{write}) = 4
$$

**Instructions issued:** 2 *(most compact)*

### 3.2 Derivation — Why Register-Register Won Modern Computing

Let $T$ = total cycles to evaluate $A=(B+C)\times D$, $C_m$ = memory access cycles, $C_r$ = register access cycles, $C_a$ = ALU cycles.

$$
T_{\text{Stack}} = 6 C_m + 4 C_a
$$

$$
T_{\text{Acc}} = 4 C_m + 3 C_a
$$

$$
T_{\text{LS}} = 5 C_m + 5 C_a + 0 \cdot C_r
$$

$$
T_{\text{RM}} = 4 C_m + 3 C_a
$$

$$
T_{\text{MM}} = 4 C_m + 2 C_a
$$

**Step-by-step logical conclusion:**

1. If $C_m \gg C_a$ (which is **always** true in real systems, $C_m / C_a \approx 100$ with cache misses), minimizing memory accesses dominates.
2. The **Memory-Memory** architecture has the *fewest instructions* but **only 2 instructions** to evaluate — but each is a complex multi-cycle operation that internally fetches operands, breaking the pipeline.
3. **Register-Register** has *more* instructions but each is *fixed-format* and *single-cycle*, so the **CPI stays close to 1**, making pipelining trivial.
4. Therefore, in the modern era where **clock speed >> memory speed**, Register-Register maximizes throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Register-Register (Load-Store) is the dominant choice for pipelined, superscalar processors because the **fixed instruction length** allows parallel decode across multiple stages.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ISA Classification Decision Tree

| Decision Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Are operands implicitly on a stack? | Stack Architecture | Go to next question |
| Is one operand implicit in an accumulator? | Accumulator Architecture | Go to next question |
| Are operands only in registers (no memory operand in ALU instr)? | **Register-Register (Load-Store)** | Go to next question |
| Is exactly **one** memory operand allowed in ALU instructions? | **Register-Memory** | **Memory-Memory** |
| Is instruction length fixed (e.g., always 32 bits)? | RISC-leaning | CISC-leaning (x86, VAX) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ISA Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Set Architecture] --> B[Based on Operand Storage]
    A --> C[Based on Memory Operands per ALU Instr]
    A --> D[Based on Instruction Format]

    B --> B1[Stack]
    B --> B2[Accumulator]
    B --> B3[Register Based]

    C --> C1[Zero Address]
    C --> C2[One Address]
    C --> C3[Two Address]
    C --> C4[Three Address]

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> C4

    B3 --> E1[Register Register]
    B3 --> E2[Register Memory]
    B3 --> E3[Memory Memory]

    C1 --> F1[JVM]
    C2 --> F2[PDP-8]
    E1 --> F3[MIPS, ARM, RISC-V]
    E2 --> F4[x86, M68K]
    E3 --> F5[VAX]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Flow of Instruction Decoding (Load-Store ISA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[Instruction Register] --> DCD[Opcode Decoder]
    DCD --> RF[Register File Access]
    DCD --> EX[ALU Execution Unit]
    DCD --> LSU[Load Store Unit]
    RF --> EX
    RF --> LSU
    EX --> WB[Write Back to Register File]
    LSU --> MEM[Main Memory]
    MEM --> LSU
    LSU --> WB
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Operand Flow Comparison Across Architectures (for $A=B+C$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StackArch[Stack Architecture]
        S1[Memory B] --> S2[Stack Push]
        S3[Memory C] --> S2
        S2 --> S4[ADD Operation]
        S4 --> S5[Stack Top holds B+C]
        S5 --> S6[Pop to Memory A]
    end

    subgraph AccArch[Accumulator Architecture]
        A1[Memory B] --> A2[ACC Register]
        A2 --> A3[ADD C from Memory]
        A3 --> A4[Memory A]
    end

    subgraph RegReg[Register Register Load Store]
        R1[Memory B] --> R2[Reg R1]
        R3[Memory C] --> R4[Reg R2]
        R2 --> R5[ALU Add]
        R4 --> R5
        R5 --> R6[Reg R3]
        R6 --> R7[Memory A]
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The RISC vs CISC Spectrum with Real ISAs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure Stack JVM] --> B[Accumulator PDP-8]
    B --> C[Memory Memory VAX]
    C --> D[Register Memory x86]
    D --> E[Register Register ARM]
    E --> F[Pure RISC RISC-V MIPS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Moving right along the spectrum, the architecture becomes *more pipelinable* and *more compiler-friendly* but typically *less code-dense*. Modern x86 internally translates CISC instructions into RISC-like micro-ops, blurring the distinction in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Classify Instruction Set Architectures based on the internal storage of operands. List any two real-world examples for each class.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary classes based on internal operand storage are:

1. **Stack Architecture** — Operands are implicitly on the Top-Of-Stack (TOS). ALU instructions are 0-address. *Example: Java Virtual Machine (JVM), x87 floating-point unit.*
2. **Accumulator Architecture** — One implicit register (the Accumulator) holds one operand and receives the result. ALU instructions are 1-address. *Example: PDP-8, 6502 microprocessor.*
3. **General-Purpose Register Architecture** — Operands are in explicitly named registers. Sub-classes are Register-Register (Load/Store), Register-Memory, and Memory-Memory. *Example: MIPS (Load/Store), x86 (Register-Memory), VAX (Memory-Memory).*

(Each class with examples = 1 mark, 3 classes = 3 marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is meant by a "Register-Register" (Load/Store) architecture? Why is it preferred for pipelined processors?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Register-Register** or **Load-Store** architecture is one in which *all* arithmetic/logic operations use **only registers as operands** — no memory operand is permitted inside an ALU instruction. Memory is accessed exclusively via dedicated `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions.

It is preferred for pipelined processors because:
1. **Fixed instruction length** (e.g., 32 bits) simplifies instruction fetch and decode stages.
2. **Single-cycle ALU operations** with predictable register access latency → **CPI ≈ 1**.
3. **No memory access inside ALU stages** → pipeline stalls are minimized and **hazards** become easier to detect.
4. It enables **superscalar** and **out-of-order** execution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare the **Stack, Accumulator, and General-Purpose Register** architectures. Tabulate your answer based on: (i) number of addresses per instruction, (ii) number of memory accesses, (iii) number of instructions issued, and (iv) any two real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the expression $X = (A - B) \times C + (D / E)$ on a **Register-Register (Load-Store)** architecture. Assume registers R1, R2, R3, R4, R5 are available. Calculate the total number of **instructions issued** and **memory accesses**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | Stack | Accumulator | Register (GPR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Addr/instr | 0 | 1 | 2 or 3 |
| (ii) Mem accesses for $A=B+C$ | 7 (6 mem + 1 store) → typically 6 mem + 1 store = 7 | 4 (3 reads + 1 write) | 5 (4 reads + 1 write) |
| (iii) Instructions for $A=B+C$ | 4 (PUSH, PUSH, ADD, POP) | 3 (LOAD, ADD, STORE) | 6 (with 4 LOADs and 1 STORE and 2 ALU) |
| (iv) Real-world examples | JVM, x87 FPU | PDP-8, 6502 | MIPS, x86, ARM, VAX |

(Marking: 1 mark per correct row × 4 rows = 4 marks; +1 mark for correct sub-categorization of GPR into Load/Store, Register-Memory, Memory-Memory; +2 marks for accurate real-world examples with one-liner justification.)

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the operators and operands.**
- Operands: $A, B, C, D, E$ → 5 operands.
- Operators: 1 subtraction, 1 multiplication, 1 division, 1 addition → 4 operators.

**Step 2: Generate the Load-Store code.**

```
LOAD   R1, A           ; R1 = A
LOAD   R2, B           ; R2 = B
SUB    R3, R1, R2      ; R3 = A - B
LOAD   R1, C           ; R1 = C
MUL    R3, R3, R1      ; R3 = (A-B) * C
LOAD   R1, D           ; R1 = D
LOAD   R2, E           ; R2 = E
DIV    R4, R1, R2      ; R4 = D / E
ADD    R3, R3, R4      ; R3 = (A-B)*C + D/E
STORE  X, R3           ; X = R3
```

**Step 3: Count instructions issued.**

$$
N_{\text{instr}} = \underbrace{5}_{\text{LOADs}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{ALU ops}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{STORE}} = 10 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 4: Count memory accesses.**

$$
N_{\text{mem}} = \underbrace{5}_{\text{LOAD reads}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{STORE write}} = 6 \text{ memory accesses}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the general formula from §2.3 and verify.**

Using the formula $\text{Instr}_{\text{LS}} = 2n + m + 1$ where $n=5$ (operands) and $m=4$ (operators):

$$
\text{Instr}_{\text{LS}} = 2(5) + 4 + 1 = 15
$$

The discrepancy with the 10 we obtained arises because *some operands are reused* (C is loaded once, etc.). The formula is a *worst-case upper bound* when every operand is unique. **Always count explicitly for the KTU exam.**

(Marking: 2 marks for code correctness, 2 marks for formula usage, 2 marks for numerical calculation, 1 mark for final boxed answer.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **three sub-classes** of General-Purpose Register (GPR) architectures. For each, give one real-world example and one advantage / one disadvantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a **6-bit opcode** and supports **16 general-purpose registers**. Calculate the instruction length (in bits) for: (i) Register-Register (3-address), (ii) Register-Memory (2-address with one memory operand), and (iii) Stack-based (1-byte opcode + 6-bit function). Comment on which is most pipelinable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

The three sub-classes of GPR architectures are:

1. **Register-Register (Load-Store):**
   - *Definition:* ALU instructions may use only registers; memory is accessed only via LOAD/STORE.
   - *Example:* MIPS, ARM, RISC-V.
   - *Advantage:* Fixed instruction format → simple pipelining → high clock speeds.
   - *Disadvantage:* More instructions for the same task → larger code size.

2. **Register-Memory:**
   - *Definition:* ALU instructions may have one memory operand and one register operand.
   - *Example:* Intel x86, Motorola 68000.
   - *Advantage:* Compact code — fewer instructions for the same task.
   - *Disadvantage:* Variable instruction length complicates the fetch/decode pipeline.

3. **Memory-Memory:**
   - *Definition:* ALU instructions may have two or three memory operands.
   - *Example:* VAX.
   - *Advantage:* Very compact code.
   - *Disadvantage:* Multiple memory accesses per instruction → slow, difficult to pipeline.

(Marking: ~2 marks per sub-class, divided between definition + example + pro/con.)

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** 6-bit opcode, 16 registers (so register field = $\lceil \log_2 16 \rceil = 4$ bits), and 16-bit immediate where applicable.

**(i) Register-Register (3-address):**
- Fields: opcode (6) + rs (4) + rt (4) + rd (4) + shamt (assumed 5) + funct (assumed 9) ... 

Let us use the standard MIPS-style breakdown with 5-bit shamt and 6-bit funct:
- Total = 6 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 5 + 6 = **27 bits** (padded to 32 in real MIPS).
- Without shamt/funct split (simpler RISC-V style): 6 + 3×4 + 12 immediate = **30 bits**.

**(ii) Register-Memory (2-address):**
- Fields: opcode (6) + reg (4) + addr/memory (16 bits) = **26 bits** (variable length, may need padding).
- A simpler form: opcode (6) + reg (4) + reg (4) + 16-bit displacement = **30 bits**.

**(iii) Stack-based (1-byte opcode + 6-bit function):**
- An *assumed* typical RISC-like stack ISA: 8-bit opcode + 6-bit function field = **14 bits** per instruction (very compact).

**Comment on Pipelining:**

> The **Register-Register (Load-Store)** architecture is the **most pipelinable** because:
> 1. It uses a **fixed instruction length** (e.g., 32 bits in MIPS, 32 bits in RISC-V).
> 2. The opcode and register fields are at **fixed bit positions**, allowing the IF/ID stages to decode in a single cycle.
> 3. No memory operand appears in the ALU stage, so the EX stage is **deterministic** with no memory stalls.

(Marking: 2 marks per sub-part numerical calculation, 1 mark for pipelinability comment.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "number of addresses" with "instruction length."** A 0-address stack instruction is *not* 0 bits — it still has an opcode.
> 2. **Forgetting to count memory access for `STORE`.** Many students miss the 1 write access and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong field-width formula.** Always compute $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ — for 16 registers, it is **4**, not 5.
> 4. **Assuming the Load-Store formula is exact.** The formula $2n + m + 1$ is a *worst-case upper bound*; with operand reuse, the actual count is lower. KTU expects you to *count explicitly* for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up the addressing terminology.** A 2-address instruction (e.g., `ADD A, B`) means *destination is one of the sources*; a 3-address instruction (e.g., `ADD R1, R2, R3`) has a *separate* destination register.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three main ISA classes**: Stack, Accumulator, Register-based. (Memorize their **0-, 1-, 3-address** ALU formats.)
- **Register-based has 3 sub-classes**: Load-Store (0 mem ops), Register-Memory (1 mem op), Memory-Memory (2+ mem ops).
- **Memory-Memory = most compact code**, **Load-Store = most pipelinable**.
- **Modern desktop CPUs (x86)** are CISC externally but RISC internally (micro-ops).
- **Modern mobile CPUs (ARM, RISC-V)** are natively Load-Store.
- **Total memory accesses** for an $n$-operand expression on Load-Store = $2n + 1$ (worst-case, no reuse).
- **Total instructions** for an $n$-operand, $m$-operator expression on Load-Store = $2n + m + 1$ (worst-case).
- **Register field bits** = $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ where $k$ is the number of registers.
- **KTU 2024 favorite question types**: (i) Compare all 3 architectures in a table, (ii) Generate code for a given expression on a named architecture, (iii) Calculate instruction length given opcode/reg/immediate field widths.
- **Real-world examples to remember**: JVM (Stack), PDP-8 (Accumulator), MIPS/ARM/RISC-V (Load-Store), x86 (Register-Memory), VAX (Memory-Memory).
- **Key performance metric**: Number of *memory accesses* per high-level operation — fewer is faster, but pipelinability matters equally.
- **Exam tip**: Always *count explicitly* in code-generation questions rather than blindly using the formula — KTU rewards step-by-step working.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Addressing** is the mechanism by which a Central Processing Unit (CPU) specifies, calculates, and accesses the physical or virtual location of an instruction operand (data) stored in the main memory hierarchy. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, memory addressing encompasses three fundamental concepts:

- **Effective Address (EA)**: The final, computed binary address of an operand that is sent to the memory unit for execution.
- **Logical Address (Virtual Address)**: The address generated by the program (CPU) at the software level, independent of the actual RAM hardware configuration.
- **Physical Address**: The real, hardware-level address of a memory cell in the main RAM modules that the Memory Address Register (MAR) actually places on the address bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Highlight)**
> The *effective address* is the address computed by the CPU's Address Generation Unit (AGU) after applying the addressing mode logic. It is the address that is finally latched into the **Memory Address Register (MAR)** for memory access.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a massive library with **2^32 (4.29 billion)** uniquely numbered lockers, each holding one byte of data.

- **Logical Address** = The locker number you *write down* on a piece of paper when you are in the reading room (your program). It starts from locker #0 and counts upward.
- **Effective Address (EA)** = The locker number you *finally arrive at* after following instructions such as "go 10 lockers past the one mentioned in your notebook" or "go to the locker whose number is stored in locker #500." This involves the **addressing mode**.
- **Physical Address** = The actual, real-world locker number in the physical building after the librarian (the **MMU - Memory Management Unit**) has translated your paper number into a real locker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Picture**: A single instruction (e.g., `ADD A, B`) does not just contain the operation; it must specify *where* operand `B` lives in memory. The method of specifying "where" is the **Addressing Mode**, and the *resulting computed location* is the **Effective Address**.

## 1.3 Memory Address Types — Hierarchical View

| Address Type | Generated By | Visibility | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Logical Address** | Program / Compiler | Software / User | High-level programs |
| **Effective Address (EA)** | CPU (Address Generation Unit) | Hardware internal | Final computed address |
| **Physical Address** | MMU / Memory Hardware | Physical RAM | Hardware bus signals |

> [!TIP]
> The number of distinct memory locations a CPU can directly reference is determined solely by the **width of its address bus (N bits)**, giving a maximum addressable space of **2^N bytes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Address Categories (Conceptual Logic Steps)

1. **Instruction Fetch Address**: The address of the next instruction to be fetched from memory. Incremented by the Program Counter (PC).
2. **Operand Address (Effective Address)**: The address of the data on which the instruction operates. Computed by the Addressing Mode logic.
3. **Branch Target Address**: The destination address of a branch/jump instruction.

## 2.2 Memory Capacity and Bus Width Relationship

The address bus width (`N`) directly defines the maximum memory that the CPU can reference. For a memory system with `N` address lines:

- Number of unique addressable memory locations = **2^N**
- If each location holds `B` bytes, total memory capacity = **2^N × B bytes**
- The last valid address = **2^N − 1**
- The first address = **0**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Max memory locations | $L = 2^N$ | locations | N = number of address lines |
| 2 | Total memory capacity | $M = 2^N \times B$ | bytes | B = bytes per location |
| 3 | Last valid address | $A_{max} = 2^N - 1$ | decimal | Highest addressable location |
| 4 | EA (Direct) | $EA = \text{Address field of instruction}$ | — | Operand is at given address |
| 5 | EA (Indirect) | $EA = M[\text{Address field}]$ | — | Address field points to pointer |
| 6 | EA (Register) | $EA = R_i$ | — | Operand is in register |
| 7 | EA (Register Indirect) | $EA = M[R_i]$ | — | Register holds pointer |
| 8 | EA (Displacement) | $EA = A + (R_i)$ | — | A = constant, R = base/index reg |
| 9 | EA (Relative) | $EA = PC + A$ | — | Offset A added to PC |
| 10 | EA (Indexed) | $EA = A + (R_i)$ | — | A = base, R = index |
| 11 | Memory size (KB) | $M = 2^N / 1024$ | KB | For reporting size |
| 12 | Number of chips | $n = \lceil M_{total} / M_{chip} \rceil$ | chips | For memory interfacing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` in formulas (not `|`)** — this is the KTU 2024 typographical convention. The `|` symbol breaks markdown table parsers.

## 2.4 Addressing Modes — The Complete List

KTU 2024 syllabus mandates detailed coverage of the following addressing modes:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is part of the instruction itself.
2. **Direct (Absolute) Addressing** — Instruction contains the memory address of the operand.
3. **Indirect Addressing** — Instruction contains the address of a memory location that contains the address of the operand.
4. **Register Addressing** — Operand is held in a CPU register specified by the instruction.
5. **Register Indirect Addressing** — Instruction specifies a register whose contents are the effective address.
6. **Displacement Addressing** — Combines a constant with a register. Three subtypes:
   - **Relative** (PC + offset)
   - **Base-Register** (Base + offset)
   - **Indexed** (Index + offset)
7. **Stack Addressing** — Operand is on the top of the stack (implicit).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design**: Compilers choose addressing modes to balance code size vs. execution speed.
- **Cache Performance**: Different addressing modes affect memory access patterns, influencing cache hit rates.
- **Operating Systems**: Virtual memory systems rely on logical-to-physical address translation (paging/segmentation).
- **Embedded Systems**: Fixed memory maps use direct addressing for predictable timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Capacity from Address Bus Width

**Problem**: A CPU has a 24-bit address bus and is connected to byte-addressable memory. Calculate:
- (a) Maximum number of addressable bytes
- (b) Total memory capacity in KB
- (c) Range of valid addresses (first and last)
- (d) Maximum address in hexadecimal

### Step-by-Step Solution

Given: Number of address lines, $N = 24$ bits. Memory is byte-addressable, so $B = 1$ byte.

**Step (a) — Number of addressable locations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2^N \\
  &= 2^{24} \\
  &= 2^4 \times 2^{20} \\
  &= 16 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \\
  &= 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: Each of the 24 address lines can be 0 or 1, giving $2^{24}$ unique binary combinations.

**Step (b) — Total memory in KB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{bytes} &= L \times B = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 1 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes} \\
M_{KB}    &= \frac{M_{bytes}}{1024} = \frac{16{,}777{,}216}{1024} = 16{,}384 \text{ KB} \\
M_{MB}    &= \frac{M_{KB}}{1024} = \frac{16{,}384}{1024} = 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Range of valid addresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{first} &= 0_{(10)} = 000000_{(16)} \\
A_{last}  &= 2^{24} - 1 = 16{,}777{,}215_{(10)} = \text{FFFFFF}_{(16)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — Hex conversion of the maximum address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
16{,}777{,}215 \div 16 &= 1{,}048{,}575 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F}) \\
1{,}048{,}575 \div 16 &= 65{,}535 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F}) \\
65{,}535 \div 16     &= 4{,}095 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F}) \\
4{,}095 \div 16       &= 255 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F}) \\
255 \div 16           &= 15 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F}) \\
15 \div 16            &= 0 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{F})
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading the remainders from bottom to top: $\text{FFFFFF}_{(16)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Address (EA) Calculation for Different Modes

**Problem**: A CPU uses the following values during instruction execution:
- PC = `2000`
- R1 = `4000`
- R2 = `1500`
- Memory contents: M[`4000`] = `1200`, M[`1500`] = `800`, M[`500`] = `900`, M[`900`] = `600`

Compute the Effective Address (EA) and the final operand for the following instructions:

### Case (i): Direct Addressing — `LDA 500`

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= 500 \quad (\text{address field of instruction}) \\
\text{Operand} &= M[EA] = M[500] = 900
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: The instruction itself contains the literal address `500`. CPU goes to memory location 500 and reads the operand.

### Case (ii): Indirect Addressing — `LDA @500` or `LDA (500)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= M[500] = 900 \\
\text{Operand} &= M[EA] = M[900] = 600
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: The instruction points to location 500. CPU reads location 500 → gets pointer `900`. CPU then reads location 900 → gets operand `600`. **Two memory references are required**.

### Case (iii): Register Indirect — `LDA (R1)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= (R1) = 4000 \\
\text{Operand} &= M[EA] = M[4000] = 1200
\end{aligned}
```

*Logic*: The instruction names register R1. R1 already contains `4000`. CPU uses that as the EA.

### Case (iv): Indexed Addressing — `LDA 100(R1)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= 100 + (R1) = 100 + 4000 = 4100 \\
\text{Operand} &= M[EA] = M[4100]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: A constant `100` is added to the contents of R1 to form the EA. Common in array traversals.

### Case (v): PC-Relative Addressing — `LDA +50(PC)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= PC + 50 = 2000 + 50 = 2050 \\
\text{Operand} &= M[EA] = M[2050]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: Constant `50` is added to the Program Counter. Common in branch instructions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Endianness and Byte Addressing

**Problem**: A 32-bit word `0x12345678` is stored in memory starting at address `1000`. Show the memory layout in both **Big-Endian** and **Little-Endian** formats, assuming byte-addressable memory.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Given: Word value = `0x12345678`, stored at address `1000`.

**Big-Endian layout** (MSB at lowest address — used by Motorola, SPARC, network byte order):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address 1000} &\rightarrow 0x12 \quad (\text{MSB}) \\
\text{Address 1001} &\rightarrow 0x34 \\
\text{Address 1002} &\rightarrow 0x56 \\
\text{Address 1003} &\rightarrow 0x78 \quad (\text{LSB})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: The most significant byte (`0x12`) is stored at the lowest memory address. This matches the human reading order (left-to-right).

**Little-Endian layout** (LSB at lowest address — used by Intel x86, x86-64):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address 1000} &\rightarrow 0x78 \quad (\text{LSB}) \\
\text{Address 1001} &\rightarrow 0x56 \\
\text{Address 1002} &\rightarrow 0x34 \\
\text{Address 1003} &\rightarrow 0x12 \quad (\text{MSB})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: The least significant byte (`0x78`) is stored at the lowest memory address. This simplifies multi-byte arithmetic in hardware.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Addressing Mode Comparison

| Addressing Mode | EA Calculation | Memory Refs | Speed | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Immediate | Operand in instruction | 0 | Fastest | Constants, small values |
| Direct | EA = Address field | 1 | Fast | Static variables |
| Indirect | EA = M[Address] | 2 | Slow | Pointers, indirection |
| Register | EA = R_i | 0 | Fastest | Temporary values |
| Register Indirect | EA = (R_i) | 1 | Fast | Pointer dereferencing |
| Relative (PC) | EA = PC + offset | 1 | Fast | Branch instructions |
| Indexed | EA = A + (R_i) | 1 | Fast | Array access |
| Stack | EA = TOS (implicit) | 1 (if any) | Fast | Function calls, expressions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical-to-Physical Address Translation Flow

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Program Counter PC"] --> nodeB["Instruction Fetch Unit"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Instruction Register IR"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Addressing Mode Decoder"}

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Effective Address. Explain the Direct and Indirect addressing modes with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer**:

The **Effective Address (EA)** is the final binary address of the operand in memory that is generated by the CPU's Address Generation Unit after applying the addressing mode logic. It is the value that gets loaded into the Memory Address Register (MAR) for memory access.

**Direct Addressing Mode**:
- The instruction's address field contains the effective address itself.
- **EA = Address field** of the instruction.
- Only **one memory reference** is needed to fetch the operand.
- Example: Instruction `LDA 2000` means "load accumulator with the contents of memory location 2000."

**Indirect Addressing Mode**:
- The instruction's address field contains the address of a memory location, which in turn contains the effective address.
- **EA = M[Address field]** of the instruction.
- **Two memory references** are required.
- Example: `LDA @2000` means "go to address 2000, read the pointer stored there, then go to that address to get the operand."

**Comparison Diagram**:

| Mode | Steps | Memory Refs | Speed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direct | Instruction → Memory[2000] → Operand | 1 | Faster |
| Indirect | Instruction → Memory[2000] → Pointer → Memory[Pointer] → Operand | 2 | Slower |

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A computer has 32 address lines. Calculate the maximum memory size it can address in bytes, KB, MB, and GB. Also find the range of valid addresses.**

**Model Answer**:

Given: Number of address lines, $N = 32$ bits. Byte-addressable memory.

**Step 1 — Number of addressable locations**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2^{32} \\
  &= 2^2 \times 2^{30} \\
  &= 4 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \\
  &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total memory**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{bytes} &= 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\
M_{KB}    &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{10}} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ KB} \\
M_{MB}    &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{20}} = 2^{12} = 4{,}096 \text{ MB} \\
