- ✅ **RISC = Reduced Instruction Set Computer** — small, simple, fixed-length instruction set.
- ✅ **Origin:** John Cocke (IBM 801, 1975); Patterson (Berkeley RISC); Hennessy (MIPS).
- ✅ **Five Golden Rules:** Fixed 32-bit length, Load/Store, large register file, hardwired control, deep pipelining.
- ✅ **Load/Store architecture** is the *defining* feature of RISC — memory accessed only via `LOAD`/`STORE`.
- ✅ **Hardwired control** (FSM) beats microcode in speed.
- ✅ **Pipeline stages (5):** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB.
- ✅ **Ideal CPI for RISC = 1.0.**
- ✅ **Pipeline Speedup (ideal):** $S = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}$; maximum = $k$.
- ✅ **CPU Time Formula:** $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$.
- ✅ **Register Windows** (SPARC): Overlapping parameter registers eliminate stack push/pop on `CALL`/`RETURN`.
- ✅ **CISC vs RISC Trade-off:** CISC = compact code, complex hardware; RISC = larger code, simpler hardware, faster execution.
- ✅ **Modern x86** is *externally CISC* (legacy) but *internally RISC* (µop pipeline) — a hybrid model.
- ✅ **Common hazard penalties:** `LOAD-USE` stall = 1 cycle; branch misprediction = 2 cycles (unpredicted).
- ✅ **RISC examples:** ARM, MIPS, SPARC, RISC-V, PowerPC.
- ✅ **CISC examples:** x86, x86-64, VAX, Motorola 68k.
- ✅ **Branch prediction, forwarding, and hazard detection** are essential to keep CPI near 1.0 in real RISC pipelines.
- ✅ **Code-size caveat:** RISC programs are typically 1.2× to 1.4× larger than CISC equivalents, but execute faster due to pipelining.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Single Cycle and Multi Cycle Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Single Cycle and Multi Cycle Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Cycle Processor:** A processor design in which **every instruction completes its entire execution in exactly one clock cycle**. The clock period is set equal to the execution time of the *slowest* (longest-latency) instruction in the instruction set, ensuring that even the most complex instruction (typically `LW` - Load Word) finishes before the next rising edge of the clock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Cycle Processor:** A processor design in which **each instruction is decomposed into multiple micro-operations (steps/stages)**, with each step executing in one clock cycle. The clock period is set equal to the *longest* delay path among all functional units in any single step (not the longest instruction). Different instructions therefore consume a different number of clock cycles, yielding a **CPI > 1** on average.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Cycle Analogy — "The Marathon Walker":**
> Imagine a delivery person who **must complete a single delivery round in exactly 60 minutes, no matter what**. Whether delivering a small letter or a heavy package, they reserve 60 minutes. The clock is fixed at the *worst-case* delivery time. Simple jobs (like dropping a letter) finish in 5 minutes, but the person still *waits* until the 60-minute mark before starting the next task. **Wasted time, but predictable rhythm.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Cycle Analogy — "The Efficient Runner":**
> Now imagine the same delivery person is told *"Each task takes as long as it needs — short jobs finish faster, long jobs take more time."* The letter delivery takes 5 minutes (5 clock cycles), while the heavy package takes 30 minutes. The person is *always busy*. The cycle time (1 minute) is set by the longest *single step*, not the longest *entire job*.

## 1.3 Why Two Designs? The Core Engineering Trade-off

| Parameter | Single-Cycle | Multi-Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Cycle Time (T<sub>clk</sub>) | **Long** (fixed to slowest instr.) | **Short** (fixed to longest functional unit path) |
| CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) | **Exactly 1** | **> 1** (varies per instruction type) |
| Functional Unit Reuse | None (separate HW per stage) | **Yes** (one ALU, one Memory) |
| Hardware Area | **Large** (duplicated units) | **Small** (shared units) |
| Control Logic | Combinational | **Finite State Machine (FSM)** |
| Power Efficiency | Poor (idle gates waste power) | Better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Multi-Cycle design is the **conceptual precursor to the Pipelined Processor** covered in Module 3. Understanding multi-cycle decomposition is *mandatory* before tackling pipelining hazards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Single-Cycle Datapath — Operational Logic

The single-cycle datapath executes **all five classical MIPS instruction classes** in one cycle:
- R-type (e.g., `add`, `sub`, `and`, `or`)
- Load Word (`lw`)
- Store Word (`sw`)
- Branch (`beq`)
- Jump (`j`)

### Operational Flow (Single Cycle — Step by Step)

1. **Instruction Fetch (IF):** PC supplies address to **Instruction Memory**; PC is updated to `PC + 4` via an adder.
2. **Instruction Decode / Register Fetch (ID):** Read two source registers (`rs`, `rt`) from the **Register File**; sign-extend the 16-bit immediate to 32 bits.
3. **Execution (EX):** The **ALU** performs the operation — R-type arithmetic, address calculation for `lw`/`sw`, or branch comparison for `beq`.
4. **Memory Access (MEM):** For `lw`/`sw` only — read or write **Data Memory**.
5. **Write-Back (WB):** For R-type and `lw` — write the result back into the destination register (`rd` or `rt`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** In the single-cycle design, **all** these steps happen in parallel within the *same* clock cycle. The clock period must be long enough to accommodate the **longest path**, which is typically:
> $$\text{Instruction Memory} \rightarrow \text{Register File} \rightarrow \text{ALU} \rightarrow \text{Data Memory} \rightarrow \text{Register File (Write)}$$

## 2.2 The Multi-Cycle Datapath — Operational Logic

The multi-cycle datapath **reuses** the same physical hardware (one ALU, one Memory, one Register File) across **multiple clock cycles**. Each MIPS instruction is broken into 3–5 micro-operations.

### The Five Canonical Steps (Multi-Cycle)

| Step | Mnemonic | Operation | Active Functional Units |
|------|----------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **IF** | Instruction Fetch & PC + 4 | Instruction Memory, Adder, PC |
| 2 | **ID** | Instruction Decode & Register Fetch | Register File, Sign-Extend |
| 3 | **EX** | Execution / Effective Address | ALU |
| 4 | **MEM** | Memory Access (for `lw`/`sw`) | Data Memory |
| 5 | **WB** | Write-Back (for R-type, `lw`) | Register File |

### Cycle Count Per Instruction Class

| Instruction Type | # of Cycles | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| R-type (e.g., `add`) | **4** | IF → ID → EX → WB |
| Load Word (`lw`) | **5** | IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB |
| Store Word (`sw`) | **4** | IF → ID → EX → MEM |
| Branch (`beq`) | **3** | IF → ID → EX |
| Jump (`j`) | **3** | IF → ID → EX |

## 2.3 Control Logic: Combinational vs. FSM

- **Single-Cycle:** Control signals are generated by a single **combinational decoder** based on the opcode — the same control word drives the entire datapath every cycle.
- **Multi-Cycle:** Control signals are generated by a **Finite State Machine (FSM)**. The current state, combined with opcode bits, determines the *next* state and *next* set of control signals. One control word is generated *per cycle*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | CPU Execution Time (fundamental performance equation) |
| 2 | $T_{clk}^{single} = \max_{i \in I} \left( t_{IF} + t_{ID} + t_{EX} + t_{MEM} + t_{WB} \right)_{i}$ | Single-cycle clock period = sum of *all* delays of *longest* instruction |
| 3 | $T_{clk}^{multi} = \max_{u \in U} \left( t_{u} \right)$ | Multi-cycle clock period = max delay of *any single functional unit* |
| 4 | $CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i} \left( \frac{IC_i}{IC_{total}} \times CPI_i \right)$ | Weighted average CPI across instruction mix |

Where:
- $IC$ = Total Instruction Count
- $I$ = Set of all instruction types
- $U$ = Set of all functional units (ALU, Memory, Reg. File)
- $t_u$ = Propagation delay of functional unit $u$
- $IC_i$ = Count of instructions of type $i$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The multi-cycle paradigm is the **direct intellectual ancestor of pipelining**. Every pipelined processor (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series, ARM Cortex) inherits the multi-cycle decomposition of instructions into stages. Understanding cycle-by-cycle control is essential for diagnosing pipeline hazards, branch mispredictions, and CPI stalls in real silicon.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Performance Comparison (Classic KTU Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (standard KTU-style):** Given the following instruction mix and functional unit delays, derive the **average CPI** and **total CPU time** for both a single-cycle and multi-cycle implementation.

### Given Data

**Instruction Mix (per 100 instructions):**
- 40% R-type (CPI<sub>multi</sub> = 4)
- 25% Load Word (CPI<sub>multi</sub> = 5)
- 15% Store Word (CPI<sub>multi</sub> = 4)
- 15% Branch (CPI<sub>multi</sub> = 3)
- 5% Jump (CPI<sub>multi</sub> = 3)

**Functional Unit Delays (ns):**
- Instruction Memory: 200 ns
- Data Memory: 200 ns
- Register File (read or write): 100 ns
- ALU + Sign-Extend + Adders: 200 ns
- Other mux/setup overhead: 50 ns

**Total Instructions:** $IC = 1{,}000{,}000$ (1 million)

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation for the Single-Cycle Processor

**Step 1: Identify the Longest Instruction Path**

A `Load Word` instruction exercises *all* datapath elements: it reads instruction memory, reads the register file, uses the ALU, reads data memory, and writes back to the register file.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk}^{single} &= t_{IM} + t_{RF\_read} + t_{ALU} + t_{DM} + t_{RF\_write} + t_{mux} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 200 + 100 + 50 \\
&= \mathbf{850 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each term is the propagation delay of one stage; the longest path belongs to `lw` because it traverses the most hardware.

**Step 2: Compute Total CPU Time**

For single-cycle, $CPI = 1$ for *every* instruction (by definition).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU}^{single} &= IC \times CPI \times T_{clk} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1 \times 850 \text{ ns} \\
&= \mathbf{8.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ ns} = 850 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Even though R-type instructions finish their actual work in ~650 ns, they wait the full 850 ns clock period. The processor is *idle* for unused portions of the cycle.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation for the Multi-Cycle Processor

**Step 1: Identify the Clock Period**

The multi-cycle clock period equals the **maximum delay of any single functional unit in any single step**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk}^{multi} &= \max(t_{IM}, t_{DM}, t_{RF}, t_{ALU}, t_{mux}) \\
&= \max(200, 200, 100, 200, 50) \\
&= \mathbf{200 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* In any given step, only *one* functional unit is in the critical path. The longest is 200 ns (shared by IM, DM, and ALU).

**Step 2: Compute Average CPI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.05 \times 3) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.15 \\
&= \mathbf{4.05 \text{ cycles/instruction}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each instruction class contributes its cycle count weighted by its frequency in the mix.

**Step 3: Compute Total CPU Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU}^{multi} &= IC \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk}^{multi} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 4.05 \times 200 \text{ ns} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 810 \text{ ns} \\
&= \mathbf{8.1 \times 10^{8} \text{ ns} = 810 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The 4.05× higher CPI is more than offset by the 4.25× shorter clock period (850/200 = 4.25).

---

### 3.1.3 Speedup Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{T_{CPU}^{single}}{T_{CPU}^{multi}} = \frac{850 \text{ ms}}{810 \text{ ms}} \approx \mathbf{1.05 \times}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The multi-cycle design is ~5% faster despite a much higher CPI. This is because the *clock period reduction* dominates. As instruction-mix changes (e.g., more `lw`), the speedup grows because the single-cycle clock is *dominated* by `lw`, while multi-cycle amortizes the cost.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Single-Cycle | Multi-Cycle | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware Units | 1 IM + 1 DM + 1 ALU + 1 Adder + 1 RegFile | 1 IM (shared) + 1 DM (shared) + 1 ALU (shared) + 1 Adder + 1 RegFile | **Multi** (smaller area) |
| Extra Registers | None | Several internal registers (IR, A, B, ALUOut, MDR) | **Single** (simpler) |
| Clock Period | Long (sum of delays) | Short (max single-unit delay) | **Multi** |
| CPI | 1 (always) | 3–5 (varies) | **Single** |
| Average Throughput | Lower | Higher (typically) | **Multi** |
| Control Unit | Combinational | FSM (sequential) | **Single** (simpler logic) |
| Determinism | Yes — every instr. takes 1 cycle | No — variable cycles | **Single** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Cycle Datapath — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the **MIPS single-cycle datapath**, showing all functional units active *simultaneously* within a single clock period. Each unit is dedicated — none are shared across cycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCnode[PC Program Counter]
    IMemNode[Instruction Memory]
    Add1Node[Adder PC plus 4]
    RegFileNode[Register File]
    SignExtNode[Sign Extend Unit]
    ALUNode[ALU]
    DMemNode[Data Memory]
    Add2Node[Adder Branch Target]
    Mux1Node[Mux RegDst]
    Mux2Node[Mux ALUSrc]
    Mux3Node[Mux MemtoReg]
    Mux4Node[Mux PCSrc Branch]
    Mux5Node[Mux Jump]

    PCnode -->|address| IMemNode
    PCnode -->|PC| Add1Node
    Add1Node -->|PCplus4| Mux4Node
    IMemNode -->|instruction bits| Mux1Node
    IMemNode -->|rs rt rd| RegFileNode
    RegFileNode -->|ReadData1 ReadData2| ALUNode
    RegFileNode -->|ReadData1 ReadData2| Mux1Node
    SignExtNode -->|extended immediate| Mux2Node
    Mux1Node -->|WriteReg| Mux3Node
    Mux2Node -->|Operand2| ALUNode
    ALUNode -->|ALUResult| DMemNode
    ALUNode -->|ALUResult| Mux3Node
    DMemNode -->|ReadData| Mux3Node
    Mux3Node -->|WriteData| RegFileNode
    RegFileNode -->|ReadData1 ReadData2| Add2Node
    SignExtNode -->|shifted immediate| Add2Node
    Add2Node -->|BranchTarget| Mux4Node
    Mux4Node -->|next PC| Mux5Node
    Mux5Node -->|next PC| PCnode
```

## 4.2 Multi-Cycle Datapath — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the **MIPS multi-cycle datapath**, where functional units (ALU, Memory, Register File) are **shared and reused** across multiple clock cycles. Internal registers (`IR`, `A`, `B`, `ALUOut`, `MDR`) hold intermediate results between cycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCnode2[PC Program Counter]
    IMemOrDMem[Shared Memory IM and DM]
    Add1Node2[Adder PC plus 4]
    RegFileNode2[Register File]
    SignExtNode2[Sign Extend Unit]
    ALUNode2[ALU]
    IRreg[IR Instruction Register]
    Areg[A Register]
    Breg[B Register]
    ALUOutReg[ALUOut Register]
    MDRreg[MDR Memory Data Register]

    PCnode2 -->|address| IMemOrDMem
    PCnode2 -->|PC| Add1Node2
    Add1Node2 -->|PCplus4| PCnode2
    IMemOrDMem -->|instruction| IRreg
    IRreg -->|opcode rs rt rd| RegFileNode2
    IRreg -->|immediate| SignExtNode2
    RegFileNode2 -->|ReadData| Areg
    RegFileNode2 -->|ReadData| Breg
    Areg -->|operand| ALUNode2
    Breg -->|operand| ALUNode2
    SignExtNode2 -->|extended| ALUNode2
    ALUNode2 -->|result| ALUOutReg
    ALUOutReg -->|address| IMemOrDMem
    IMemOrDMem -->|data| MDRreg
    Areg -->|write data| RegFileNode2
    MDRreg -->|write data| RegFileNode2
    ALUOutReg -->|write data| RegFileNode2
    Breg -->|write data| IMemOrDMem
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Control Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Instruction] --> IFStep[Step 1 Instruction Fetch]
    IFStep --> IDStep[Step 2 Decode and Register Fetch]
    IDStep --> ExeStep[Step 3 Execute via ALU]
    ExeStep --> Decision{Instruction Class}
    Decision -->|R type| WB_R[Step 4 Write Back to Register]
    Decision -->|Load lw| MEM_LW[Step 4 Access Data Memory]
    MEM_LW --> WB_LW[Step 5 Write Back Loaded Data]
    Decision -->|Store sw| MEM_SW[Step 4 Write to Data Memory]
    Decision -->|Branch beq| Done_B[Step 4 Update PC and Complete]
    Decision -->|Jump j| Done_J[Step 4 Update PC and Complete]
    WB_R --> End[End of Instruction]
    WB_LW --> End
    MEM_SW --> End
    Done_B --> End
    Done_J --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

### **Q1. [3 Marks]**
**Distinguish between a single-cycle and a multi-cycle processor with respect to clock period, CPI, and hardware sharing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single-Cycle | Multi-Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | Fixed to longest instruction latency | Fixed to longest functional-unit delay in a single step |
| CPI | 1 (constant) | 3–5 (varies with instruction class) |
| Hardware Sharing | None (separate units for each stage) | Functional units (ALU, Memory, Reg. File) are reused |

A single-cycle processor completes every instruction in one clock cycle but suffers from wasted time on simple instructions. A multi-cycle processor breaks instructions into micro-operations, allowing reuse of hardware and a shorter clock period.

---

### **Q2. [3 Marks]**
**What is the role of a Finite State Machine (FSM) in the control unit of a multi-cycle processor?**

**Model Answer:**

In a multi-cycle processor, the control unit is implemented as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)**. The FSM determines, for each clock cycle, which control signals must be asserted based on:
1. The **current state** of the processor, and
2. The **opcode** of the instruction being executed (input from the IR).

The FSM moves to the next state at every clock edge, ensuring that the datapath performs the correct micro-operation per cycle. This sequential control enables instruction-dependent cycle counts (e.g., `beq` takes 3 cycles, `lw` takes 5).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the datapath of a single-cycle MIPS processor with a neat block diagram. List the major functional units and describe the data flow for an R-type instruction.**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Functional Units:**
1. **Program Counter (PC)** – Holds the address of the current instruction.
2. **Instruction Memory** – Outputs the 32-bit instruction at address PC.
3. **Register File** – Two read ports, one write port; 32 registers × 32 bits.
4. **ALU** – Performs arithmetic/logic operations.
5. **Data Memory** – Read/Write 32-bit words.
6. **Sign-Extend Unit** – Extends 16-bit immediate to 32 bits.
7. **Adders** – PC + 4, branch target calculation.
8. **Multiplexers** – Select among data sources (RegDst, ALUSrc, MemtoReg, PCSrc, Jump).

**Data Flow for R-type (e.g., `add $rd, $rs, $rt`):**

1. **IF:** PC addresses Instruction Memory; instruction is fetched; PC is updated to PC+4.
2. **ID:** Register File reads `$rs` and `$rt`; opcode `000000` is decoded.
3. **EX:** ALU performs `ReadData1 + ReadData2` (for `add`); Mux `ALUSrc = 0` selects the register value.
4. **MEM:** No memory access (R-type skips this stage); Mux output is forwarded.
5. **WB:** Mux `MemtoReg = 0` selects the ALUResult; Mux `RegDst = 1` selects `rd` as the destination; Register File writes the result into `$rd`.

**Key Control Signals for R-type:** `RegDst = 1, ALUSrc = 0, MemRead = 0, MemWrite = 0, MemtoReg = 0, RegWrite = 1, Branch = 0, Jump = 0`.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
**A processor has the following instruction mix. Compute the average CPI for a multi-cycle implementation and the total execution time for IC = 1,000,000 instructions, given that the clock period is 250 ns.**

| Instruction | Frequency | Cycle Count |
|---|---|---|
| R-type | 35% | 4 |
| Load | 25% | 5 |
| Store | 15% | 4 |
| Branch | 20% | 3 |
| Jump | 5% | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Average CPI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= (0.35 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.05 \times 3) \\
&= 1.40 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.15 \\
&= \mathbf{3.95 \text{ cycles/instruction}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CPU Execution Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= IC \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 3.95 \times 250 \text{ ns} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 987.5 \text{ ns} \\
&= \mathbf{987.5 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $CPI_{avg} = 3.95$, $T_{CPU} = 987.5$ ms.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
**Compare the single-cycle and multi-cycle MIPS datapaths in terms of: (i) memory organization, (ii) ALU usage, (iii) write-back strategy, and (iv) control unit implementation.**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Single-Cycle | Multi-Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Memory Organization** | Separate Instruction Memory and Data Memory (Harvard-style split) | Single unified memory used for both instruction fetch and data access |
| **(ii) ALU Usage** | Dedicated ALU used once per instruction; idle for portions of the cycle | One ALU shared across multiple steps; computes PC+4, effective address, branch comparison, and arithmetic operations in different cycles |
| **(iii) Write-Back Strategy** | Direct write to Register File in the same cycle as memory access | Intermediate results held in internal registers (`ALUOut`, `MDR`); write-back occurs in a dedicated final cycle |
| **(iv) Control Unit** | Purely combinational — one control word generated from opcode, asserted for the whole cycle | Sequential FSM — control signals change every cycle based on current state and opcode bits |

**Conclusion:** The multi-cycle design trades a more complex (but cheaper) control unit and additional internal storage registers for a shorter clock period and lower hardware area.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
**For a single-cycle processor, the following delays are given: Instruction Memory = 200 ns, Register File read = 100 ns, ALU = 200 ns, Data Memory = 200 ns, Register File write = 100 ns, Mux/Setup = 50 ns. (i) Determine the clock period. (ii) If the same processor is redesigned as a multi-cycle design, what is the new clock period? (iii) If 30% of instructions are R-type (4 cycles), 30% are loads (5 cycles), 20% are stores (4 cycles), and 20% are branches (3 cycles), what is the speedup of multi-cycle over single-cycle for IC = 10⁶ instructions?**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Single-Cycle Clock Period**

The longest instruction is `lw`, which uses the full datapath:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk}^{single} &= t_{IM} + t_{RF\_read} + t_{ALU} + t_{DM} + t_{RF\_write} + t_{mux} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 200 + 100 + 50 \\
&= \mathbf{850 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Multi-Cycle Clock Period**

The clock period is the maximum delay of any single functional unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk}^{multi} &= \max(200, 200, 100, 200, 50) = \mathbf{200 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Speedup Calculation**

**Single-Cycle CPU Time** (CPI = 1):

$$
T_{CPU}^{single} = 10^6 \times 1 \times 850 \text{ ns} = 850 \text{ ms}
$$

**Multi-Cycle Average CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= (0.30 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.50 + 0.80 + 0.60 \\
&= \mathbf{4.10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multi-Cycle CPU Time:**

$$
T_{CPU}^{multi} = 10^6 \times 4.10 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 820 \text{ ms}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{T_{CPU}^{single}}{T_{CPU}^{multi}} = \frac{850}{820} = \mathbf{1.037 \times}
\end{aligned}
$$

The multi-cycle design is approximately **3.7% faster** for this instruction mix.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the clock period formulas:** Many students compute the multi-cycle clock period as the *sum* of all unit delays instead of the *maximum* of any single unit's delay. **The multi-cycle clock is bounded by one step's worst path, not the whole instruction's worst path.**
>
> 2. **Assuming CPI = 1 for multi-cycle:** This is the most common error. In multi-cycle, CPI is *always* > 1, and varies per instruction class. The correct formula uses a **weighted average** based on instruction mix.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the *longest* instruction for single-cycle clock:** The clock must accommodate `lw` (Load), not R-type. Students who compute the single-cycle clock using R-type lose full marks on clock-period questions.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Harvard vs. unified memory:** Single-cycle uses *separate* I and D memories (parallel access in one cycle); multi-cycle uses *unified* memory (serial access). Mixing this up costs marks in datapath diagrams.
>
> 5. **Ignoring internal registers in multi-cycle:** When drawing the multi-cycle datapath, students frequently forget the intermediate latches (`IR`, `A`, `B`, `ALUOut`, `MDR`). These registers are *mandatory* for holding values between cycles.
>
> 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables:** Always escape absolute-value bars as `\vert` or `\mid` in your exam answers if typeset in markdown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Single-cycle:** Every instruction completes in **exactly 1 clock cycle**; clock period = sum of *all* datapath delays of the *longest* instruction (typically `lw`).
- [ ] **Multi-cycle:** Each instruction decomposes into **3–5 micro-operations**; clock period = max delay of *any single* functional unit.
- [ ] **CPI:** Single-cycle = 1 (constant); Multi-cycle = variable (3 for branches, 4 for R-type/SW, 5 for LW).
- [ ] **Memory:** Single-cycle = separate I and D memories; Multi-cycle = unified shared memory.
- [ ] **ALU:** Single-cycle = one use per instruction; Multi-cycle = reused for PC+4, address calc, branch compare, and arithmetic.
- [ ] **Hardware Area:** Multi-cycle is **smaller** (shared units); Single-cycle is **larger** (duplicated units).
- [ ] **Control Unit:** Single-cycle = combinational decoder; Multi-cycle = **Finite State Machine (FSM)**.
- [ ] **Internal Registers:** Multi-cycle uses `IR`, `A`, `B`, `ALUOut`, `MDR` to hold intermediate values between cycles.
- [ ] **Performance Equation:** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ — always use the correct $CPI$ and $T_{clk}$ for each design.
- [ ] **Speedup:** Typically $1.05\times$ to $1.10\times$ in favor of multi-cycle for balanced mixes; the gap widens with `lw`-heavy workloads.
- [ ] **Bridge to Pipelining:** Multi-cycle decomposition is the **conceptual foundation** of the 5-stage MIPS pipeline (covered in Module 3).
- [ ] **Key Insight:** Multi-cycle trades a **higher CPI for a much shorter clock period** — the net effect is usually a faster, smaller, and more power-efficient processor than single-cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining - Basic and Intermediate Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pipelining - Basic and Intermediate Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the computation into a series of independent stages, each stage handling a portion of the instruction's processing. A pipeline is analogous to an assembly line where instructions enter at one end and exit (after completion) at the other, with each stage performing a specific sub-operation simultaneously on different instructions.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, pipelining is taught as the **core technique for Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** without requiring multiple processing cores. The canonical reference pipeline used in KTU boards is the **classic 5-stage RISC pipeline**, which decomposes instruction execution into five sequential sub-operations:

1. **IF** — Instruction Fetch
2. **ID** — Instruction Decode / Register Fetch
3. **EX** — Execute / Effective Address Calculation
4. **MEM** — Memory Access
5. **WB** — Write Back

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **laundry assembly line** with 4 workers and 4 stages: **Wash → Dry → Fold → Pack**.

* **Non-Pipelined (Sequential):** Worker 1 washes, dries, folds, and packs one shirt completely before starting the next. If each stage takes 30 minutes, one shirt takes 2 hours. Four shirts take 8 hours.
* **Pipelined:** Once Worker 1 finishes washing Shirt 1, they immediately start washing Shirt 2 while Worker 2 dries Shirt 1. Now every 30 minutes a finished shirt emerges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pipelining does **NOT** reduce the latency of a single instruction — it reduces the **total time to complete a batch of instructions** by increasing **throughput**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following bolded metrics are universally tested in KTU examinations:

* **Clock Cycle Time ($t_{clk}$ or $\tau$):** The time taken by the slowest pipeline stage, governed by propagation delays of combinational logic and the inter-stage pipeline register setup time.
* **Pipeline Throughput:** Measured in **Instructions Per Cycle (IPC)** — ideally **1 IPC** for a fully utilized balanced pipeline.
* **Pipeline Speedup Formula:** $S_n = \dfrac{T_{non-pipeline}}{T_{pipeline}}$
* **Pipeline Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Busy period}}{\text{Total period}} \times 100\%$
* **Pipeline Depth ($k$):** Number of stages. For KTU, the canonical depth is **$k = 5$** unless explicitly stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Pipelined Datapath

A pipelined datapath is constructed by inserting **pipeline registers (latches)** between combinational logic blocks. Each register holds the intermediate results for one instruction while the next instruction enters the previous stage in the *same* clock cycle.

#### Stage-by-Stage Logic

* **IF (Instruction Fetch):**
  * **Why:** Fetches the next instruction from memory using the Program Counter (PC).
  * **How:** Sends PC to instruction memory; PC is updated (typically $PC \leftarrow PC + 4$).
  * **Boundary Condition:** PC may be overwritten by a branch target via the PCSrc mux.

* **ID (Instruction Decode / Register Fetch):**
  * **Why:** Decodes opcode, reads source registers from the register file.
  * **How:** The fixed-format RISC fields allow parallel decoding and register fetch in a *single* stage. Sign-extension of immediates occurs here.
  * **KTU Highlight:** This "decode-and-read" combination is what makes RISC pipelines fast and uniform.

* **EX (Execute / Effective Address Calculation):**
  * **Why:** Performs ALU operations (R-type) or computes the branch target / load-store address.
  * **How:** Operands are fed to the ALU along with the extended immediate. Branch outcome and target are resolved.

* **MEM (Memory Access):**
  * **Why:** Performs read/write for Load/Store instructions.
  * **How:** Data memory is addressed; for R-type and I-type ALU operations, this stage is a *pass-through* (no-op).

* **WB (Write Back):**
  * **Why:** Writes the result (from ALU or memory) back to the destination register.
  * **How:** Selects between ALU result and memory-loaded data via a mux, then writes to the register file.

### 2.2 Pipeline Registers (Inter-Stage Buffers)

To prevent data from one instruction's stage from corrupting the next instruction's stage, **four sets of pipeline registers** are inserted:

| Register | Holds Between Stages | Key Contents |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IF/ID** | IF → ID | PC+4, Fetched Instruction bits |
| **ID/EX** | ID → EX | Read data 1 & 2, Sign-extended immediate, Destination register, Control signals |
| **EX/MEM** | EX → MEM | ALU result, Register data 2 (for stores), Destination register, Branch flags |
| **MEM/WB** | MEM → WB | Memory read data, ALU result, Destination register |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sequential Execution Time | $T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot \tau$ | Time for $N$ instructions, $k$ stages, cycle $\tau$ | Time |
| Ideal Pipelined Time | $T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau$ | Time for $N$ instructions in a $k$-stage ideal pipeline | Time |
| Ideal Speedup | $S_{ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}$ | Ratio of sequential to pipelined time | Dimensionless |
| Asymptotic Speedup | $\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{ideal} = k$ | Upper-bound speedup = number of stages | Dimensionless |
| Throughput | $TP = \dfrac{N}{T_{pipe}}$ | Instructions completed per unit time | Inst/sec |
| Pipeline Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{ideal}}{k} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of stages doing useful work per cycle | Percent (%) |
| Speedup with Stalls | $S = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1 + S_{penalty}}$ | Speedup accounting for hazard stalls $S_{penalty}$ | Dimensionless |
| Amdahl's Bound | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced by speedup $k$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for Markdown:** In all KTU answers, never use the vertical pipe `\|` symbol inside math expressions (e.g., for "OR" or absolute value) — it breaks the markdown table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **CPU Design:** All modern superscalar processors (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series) implement multi-stage pipelines (10–20 stages in modern designs) before going superscalar/out-of-order.
* **DSP & GPUs:** Graphics pipelines and digital signal processors use deeply pipelined ALUs to sustain massive data throughput.
* **Networking:** Network-on-chip routers and Ethernet MAC controllers use multi-stage pipelines for packet processing.
* **Automotive & Embedded SoCs:** ARM Cortex-M/A series use 3-stage, 5-stage, 7-stage, and even 13-stage pipelines tailored to power/performance trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Pipelined Execution Time for $N$ Instructions

We want to derive the total time to execute $N$ instructions through a $k$-stage pipeline with cycle time $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Identify the time taken by the first instruction.**
The first instruction must traverse all $k$ stages sequentially because no other instruction is ahead of it.

$$
T_1 = k \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time taken by each subsequent instruction.**
Once the pipeline is "filled," exactly one new instruction enters the pipeline every clock cycle, and one instruction completes every clock cycle.

$$
\text{Time per additional instruction} = 1 \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 3 — Total time for $N$ instructions.**
The remaining $(N - 1)$ instructions each require only one additional cycle beyond the first instruction's $k$ cycles.

$$
T_{pipe} = k \cdot \tau + (N - 1) \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the expression.**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for ideal pipelined execution time.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Speedup

**Step 1 — Sequential (non-pipelined) time.**
Without pipelining, each instruction must complete all $k$ stages before the next one begins.

$$
T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Define speedup as the ratio.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the formulas.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(k + N - 1) \cdot \tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\tau$.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**
Divide numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{ideal} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{1 + \dfrac{k-1}{N}} = k
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** For an infinitely long program, the ideal speedup equals the number of pipeline stages $k$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Pipeline Efficiency

Efficiency is defined as the fraction of stages doing useful work on average over the total stages used.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\text{Average busy stages per cycle}}{\text{Total stages}} = \dfrac{S_{ideal}}{k} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting $S_{ideal}$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{(k + N - 1) \cdot k} \times 100\% = \dfrac{N}{k + N - 1} \times 100\%
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A processor uses a 4-stage pipeline with a clock cycle of 2 ns. Compute: (a) Time to execute 1000 instructions, (b) Speedup over non-pipelined execution, (c) Efficiency.

**Given:** $k = 4$, $\tau = 2 \text{ ns}$, $N = 1000$.

**Part (a) — Pipelined Time:**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau = (4 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 1003 \cdot 2 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pipe} = 2006 \text{ ns} = 2.006 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Part (b) — Speedup:**

$$
S_{ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 4}{1003} = \dfrac{4000}{1003}
$$

$$
S_{ideal} \approx 3.988
$$

**Part (c) — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S_{ideal}}{k} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.988}{4} \times 100\% \approx 99.70\%
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Execution Models

| Metric | Sequential (No Pipeline) | Ideal Pipeline | Pipeline with $s$ Stalls |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Time | $N \cdot k \cdot \tau$ | $(k + N - 1) \cdot \tau$ | $(k + N - 1 + s) \cdot \tau$ |
| Cycles per Instruction (CPI) | $k$ | $1$ | $1 + \dfrac{s}{N}$ |
| Throughput | $\dfrac{1}{k\tau}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | $\dfrac{N}{(k+N-1+s)\tau}$ |
| Speedup | $1$ | $\dfrac{Nk}{k+N-1}$ | $\dfrac{Nk}{k+N-1+s}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 5-Stage Pipelined Datapath (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Program Counter] --> IF1[Instruction Memory]
    IF1 -->|Instruction bits + PCplus4| IFID[(IF ID Reg)]
    IFID --> ID1[Decoder + Register File]
    ID1 -->|ReadData1 + ReadData2 + ImmExt| IDEX[(ID EX Reg)]
    IDEX --> EX1[ALU Unit]
    EX1 -->|ALUResult + WriteData| EXMEM[(EX MEM Reg)]
    EXMEM --> MEM1[Data Memory]
    MEM1 -->|ReadData + ALUResult| MEMWB[(MEM WB Reg)]
    MEMWB --> WB1[Write Back Mux]
    WB1 -->|WriteData| REG[Register File]
    REG -.feedback.-> ID1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram: Pipeline Operation (Instruction Wavefront)

The following Gantt-style chart visualizes how 5 instructions ($I_1$ through $I_5$) overlap across 9 clock cycles in a 5-stage pipeline.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Timing Diagram (5-Stage, 5 Instructions)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instruction 1
    I1-IF    :a1, 1, 1
    I1-ID    :a2, after a1, 1
    I1-EX    :a3, after a2, 1
    I1-MEM   :a4, after a3, 1
    I1-WB    :a5, after a4, 1
    section Instruction 2
    I2-IF    :b1, 2, 1
    I2-ID    :b2, after b1, 1
    I2-EX    :b3, after b2, 1
    I2-MEM   :b4, after b3, 1
    I2-WB    :b5, after b4, 1
    section Instruction 3
    I3-IF    :c1, 3, 1
    I3-ID    :c2, after c1, 1
    I3-EX    :c3, after c2, 1
    I3-MEM   :c4, after c3, 1
    I3-WB    :c5, after c4, 1
    section Instruction 4
    I4-IF    :d1, 4, 1
    I4-ID    :d2, after d1, 1
    I4-EX    :d3, after d2, 1
    I4-MEM   :d4, after d3, 1
    I4-WB    :d5, after d4, 1
    section Instruction 5
    I5-IF    :e1, 5, 1
    I5-ID    :e2, after e1, 1
    I5-EX    :e3, after e2, 1
    I5-MEM   :e4, after e3, 1
    I5-WB    :e5, after e4, 1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Pipeline Hazard Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HAZ[Pipeline Hazards] --> STR[Structural Hazards]
    HAZ --> DAT[Data Hazards]
    HAZ --> CTL[Control Hazards]
    STR --> STR1[Two instructions need same HW resource]
    STR --> STR2[Fix: Duplicate resources or stall]
    DAT --> DAT1[RAW Read After Write]
    DAT --> DAT2[WAR Write After Read]
    DAT --> DAT3[WAW Write After Write]
    CTL --> CTL1[Branches and jumps alter PC flow]
    CTL --> CTL2[Fix: Branch prediction or delay slots]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define pipelining. List any two advantages of a pipelined processor over a non-pipelined processor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by dividing the instruction processing into a series of independent stages. Each stage performs a specific sub-operation on a different instruction simultaneously. *(1.5 marks)*
**Advantages:** *(1.5 marks)*
1. **Increased Throughput:** One instruction completes per cycle (ideally), versus $k$ cycles per instruction non-pipelined.
2. **Lower Average CPI:** CPI approaches 1 instead of $k$.
3. **Better resource utilization** across clock cycles.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a pipeline register? Why is it necessary in a pipelined datapath?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A pipeline register is a set of flip-flops inserted between consecutive pipeline stages to hold the intermediate results, control signals, and operands produced by one stage so that the next stage can use them in the *next* clock cycle. *(1.5 marks)*
**Necessity:**
1. **Isolation of stages:** Prevents the combinational logic of one stage from corrupting another stage's output during the same clock cycle.
2. **Synchronization:** Ensures all stages operate on a common clock edge.
3. **Information forwarding:** Carries control and data signals (like destination register number) along with the instruction. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of pipelining with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of pipeline registers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
Pipelining is the technique of decomposing instruction execution into multiple sub-operations (stages), each performed by a dedicated hardware unit, allowing several instructions to be in different stages of execution simultaneously. This increases instruction throughput without reducing individual instruction latency.

**Functional Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[IF Stage] --> R1[(IF ID Register)] --> S2[ID Stage] --> R2[(ID EX Register)] --> S3[EX Stage] --> R3[(EX MEM Register)] --> S4[MEM Stage] --> R4[(MEM WB Register)] --> S5[WB Stage]
```

**Role of Pipeline Registers:** *(4 marks)*
1. **Data Handoff:** Each register latches the outputs of its preceding stage so the next stage uses stable values.
2. **Control Signal Propagation:** Carry decoded control bits (RegWrite, MemRead, etc.) to later stages that need them.
3. **Decoupling Stages:** Allow each stage to operate at the maximum clock rate determined by its own combinational delay plus the register's setup time.
4. **Hazard Detection Support:** Hold values that may need to be forwarded or stalled via inter-stage signals.

**(b)** A 5-stage pipeline has a clock cycle of 2 ns. Compute the total time to execute 200 instructions, the speedup over a non-pipelined processor, and the efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k = 5$, $\tau = 2$ ns, $N = 200$.

**Step 1 — Pipelined execution time:**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau = (5 + 200 - 1) \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 204 \cdot 2 = 408 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Non-pipelined (sequential) time:**

$$
T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot \tau = 200 \cdot 5 \cdot 2 = 2000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{2000}{408} \approx 4.90
$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4.90}{5} \times 100\% = 98.04\%
$$

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)* The pipeline achieves near-ideal speedup ($S \to k$ as $N \to \infty$) with very high efficiency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a timing diagram how five instructions are processed in a 5-stage pipeline. Assume ideal conditions with no hazards. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I1** | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |   |   |   |   |
| **I2** |   | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |   |   |   |
| **I3** |   |   | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |   |   |
| **I4** |   |   |   | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |   |
| **I5** |   |   |   |   | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |

**Observations:** *(4 marks)*
1. The first instruction takes 5 cycles to complete.
2. After cycle 5, the pipeline is full — one instruction enters per cycle and one completes per cycle.
3. Five instructions require $5 + (5 - 1) = 9$ cycles total.
4. **Throughput** = 5/9 instructions per cycle (for this batch); asymptotically approaches 1 IPC.

**(b)** A processor has a 4-stage pipeline with a clock cycle of 1 ns. Due to data hazards, the pipeline introduces an average of 0.4 stall cycles per instruction. Compute the actual speedup over a non-pipelined processor for 500 instructions and comment on the efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k = 4$, $\tau = 1$ ns, $N = 500$, $s = 0.4$ stalls/instruction $\Rightarrow$ total stalls $S_{total} = 0.4 \times 500 = 200$.

**Step 1 — Total stalls in cycles:**

$$
S_{penalty} = 200 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual pipelined time:**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1 + S_{penalty}) \cdot \tau = (4 + 500 - 1 + 200) \cdot 1 = 703 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-pipelined time:**

$$
T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot \tau = 500 \cdot 4 \cdot 1 = 2000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual speedup:**

$$
S_{actual} = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{2000}{703} \approx 2.845
$$

**Step 5 — Actual efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{k} \times 100\% = \dfrac{2.845}{4} \times 100\% \approx 71.13\%
$$

**Comment:** *(1 mark)* Hazards degrade the efficiency from $\approx 99.7\%$ (ideal) to $\approx 71.1\%$, illustrating why real processors invest heavily in forwarding, hazard detection, and branch prediction units.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using $N \cdot k$ instead of $(N + k - 1)$:** Forgetting to subtract 1 in the pipelined formula is the single most common error. Always write $(k + N - 1)$.
> 2. **Confusing latency with throughput:** Pipelining does **NOT** reduce single-instruction latency; it only improves throughput.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $N$ is dimensionless; $\tau$ is in time units; $T$ comes out in time units. Many students forget to convert ns to µs when required.
> 4. **Ignoring stalls in efficiency problems:** When hazards are mentioned, you MUST add stall cycles to the denominator.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labels:** In KTU valuation, unlabeled blocks = 0 marks. Always label `IF`, `ID`, `EX`, `MEM`, `WB` and the pipeline registers between them.
> 6. **Mixing up Speedup and Efficiency:** Speedup is a *ratio*; Efficiency is a *percentage*. Students often interchange them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Pipelining Goal:** Increase instruction **throughput**, not reduce single-instruction **latency**.
* **Canonical 5-stage MIPS stages:** **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB** — memorize the order; it's a 2-mark question on its own.
* **Pipeline registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB)** are mandatory between stages; without them stages cannot overlap.
* **Ideal pipelined time:** $T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau$
* **Ideal speedup:** $S_{ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}$, asymptotic value $S \to k$ as $N \to \infty$.
* **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{N}{k + N - 1} \times 100\%$ for the ideal case.
* **Hazards degrade performance:** Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), and Control hazards introduce stall cycles that must be added to $S_{penalty}$.
* **Cycle time limit:** $\tau \geq t_{combinational} + t_{register\_setup}$, where $t_{combinational}$ is the slowest stage's logic delay.
* **Clock cycles per instruction (CPI):** Ideal = 1; with $s$ stalls per $N$ instructions, $CPI = 1 + \dfrac{s}{N}$.
* **Amdahl's Law reminder:** Even infinite pipeline depth cannot speed up code that is inherently sequential (e.g., true data dependencies).
* **Always draw timing diagrams** for any "explain pipelining" question — KTU awards 2–3 marks purely for a correct, labeled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipelining** is an *implementation technique* in computer architecture whereby **multiple instructions are overlapped in their execution** by partitioning the instruction-processing function into a series of independent, sequential stages, each stage performing a portion of the complete instruction cycle. While one instruction is being processed in stage $i$, a different instruction is being processed in stage $i+1$, and so on, allowing the processor to approach an instruction completion rate of *one per cycle*.

Formally, a pipeline of depth $k$ processes a stream of $n$ independent instructions in:

$$T_{\text{pipeline}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

cycles, instead of the $n \cdot k$ cycles that a *non-pipelined* (sequential) datapath would require.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Phrasing**: When answering theory questions, always state that pipelining is an *implementation strategy*, **NOT** a new ISA feature. The instruction set architecture (ISA) remains unchanged — only the underlying micro-architecture is enhanced.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Pipeline

To make the concept concrete, consider a classic real-world analogy: washing **4 loads of laundry** through **4 sequential stages**.

| Stage | Operation | Duration (minutes) |
|:-----:|:---------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | Wash | **30** |
| 2 | Dry | **40** |
| 3 | Fold | **20** |
| 4 | Store | **20** |

**Sequential (Non-Pipelined) Execution**

- Load 1: 30 + 40 + 20 + 20 = **110 minutes**
- Load 2: another 110 minutes
- Load 3: another 110 minutes
- Load 4: another 110 minutes
- **Total = 4 × 110 = 440 minutes**

**Pipelined Execution**

- Time 0 → 30: Load 1 in *Wash*
- Time 30 → 70: Load 1 in *Dry*; Load 2 enters *Wash*
- Time 70 → 90: Load 1 in *Fold*; Load 2 in *Dry*; Load 3 enters *Wash*
- Time 90 → 110: Load 1 in *Store*; Load 2 in *Fold*; Load 3 in *Dry*; Load 4 enters *Wash*
- Time 110 → 150: … and so on.
- **Total = 110 + 3 × 40 = 230 minutes**

**Observed Speedup** = $440 / 230 \approx 1.91\times$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for the Student**: Pipelining does **NOT** reduce the latency of a *single* instruction. Each load still takes 110 minutes. What pipelining improves is the **throughput** — the *rate* at which loads finish per unit time.

---

## 1.3 Why Pipelining Matters in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 syllabus for PCITT302 specifically lists pipelining under Module 2 because:

1. It is the **foundational design principle** behind all modern CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex).
2. It bridges Module 1 (basic single-cycle / multi-cycle datapath) with Module 3 (hazards, forwarding, branch prediction).
3. It introduces the *key performance metrics* — **Speedup**, **Throughput**, **Efficiency** — that recur in every computer architecture problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants / Conventions to Memorise**:
> - Standard MIPS-style 5-stage pipeline depth: $k = 5$ (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB).
> - Cycle time $T_{\text{cycle}}$ is set by the **slowest pipeline stage** (the *bottleneck stage*).
> - Pipeline registers separate consecutive stages and add their own propagation delay into $T_{\text{cycle}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Pipeline — The 5-Stage MIPS Pipeline

The canonical MIPS (Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipelined Stages) datapath, which the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly adopts, decomposes every instruction into **five stages**:

| Stage | Mnemonic | Function | Hardware Resources Used |
|:-----:|:--------:|:---------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | **IF** | Instruction Fetch | PC, Instruction Memory, Adder (+4) |
| 2 | **ID** | Instruction Decode / Register Read | Register File, Sign-extend unit |
| 3 | **EX** | Execute / Address Calculation | ALU |
| 4 | **MEM** | Memory Access | Data Memory |
| 5 | **WB** | Write Back | Register File |

Between every consecutive pair of stages sits a **pipeline register** (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) that latches all intermediate values so they are available for the next stage in the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Cue**: Whenever the question says *"draw the MIPS 5-stage pipeline"*, you MUST label the four pipeline registers explicitly. Skipping them is the most common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 2.2 The Three Cardinal Performance Metrics

Pipelining is evaluated by **three derived quantities**, all of which are board-exam favourites.

### 2.2.1 Speedup ($S$)

$$S_n = \frac{T_{\text{sequential}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

- For $n \to \infty$ (very long instruction stream), $S_\infty \to k$.
- The **ideal speedup** of a $k$-stage pipeline is therefore $k$, the *pipeline depth*.

### 2.2.2 Throughput ($TP$)

$$TP = \frac{n}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}} \;\; \text{[instructions/cycle]}$$

- The **peak throughput** is reached asymptotically: $TP_{\max} = 1 / T_{\text{cycle}}$ instructions per cycle, i.e. **one instruction per cycle (IPC = 1)**.

### 2.2.3 Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$\eta = \frac{S_n}{k} = \frac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

- $\eta$ expresses how *fully utilised* the pipeline stages are.
- As $n \to \infty$, $\eta \to 1$ (i.e. **100 %**).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use $\vert$ in formulas below, never $\vert$ inside the table cell, to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Asymptotic Limit ($n \to \infty$) | Unit |
|:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:---------------------------------:|:----:|
| 1 | Sequential Execution Time | $T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | — | seconds |
| 2 | Pipelined Execution Time | $T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | — | seconds |
| 3 | Speedup | $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | $S_\infty = k$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Throughput | $TP_n = \dfrac{n}{(k+n-1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | $TP_\infty = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | instr/cycle |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta_n = \dfrac{n}{k+n-1}$ | $\eta_\infty = 1$ (100 %) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Stage Cycle Time (balanced) | $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{stage}} + T_{\text{reg}}$ | — | seconds |
| 7 | Speedup (imbalanced stages) | $S = \dfrac{n \cdot \sum t_i}{\sum t_i + (n-1) \cdot t_{\max}}$ | $S_\infty = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{t_{\max}}$ | dimensionless |

Where:
- $n$ = number of independent instructions in the stream
- $k$ = number of pipeline stages
- $T_{\text{cycle}}$ = clock period of the pipelined processor
- $t_i$ = delay of stage $i$; $t_{\max}$ = delay of the slowest (bottleneck) stage
- $T_{\text{reg}}$ = delay introduced by the pipeline register (setup time + clock-to-Q + propagation)

---

## 2.4 Why Pipeline Registers Are Non-Negotiable

A pipeline register performs three critical functions:

1. **Isolation** — Decouples combinational logic of stage $i$ from stage $i+1$, preventing signal corruption.
2. **Synchronisation** — All stage outputs are latched on the *same* clock edge, ensuring that the next stage begins with stable inputs.
3. **Forwarding Support (Module 3 preview)** — Modern designs route the register outputs to multiple later stages to mitigate data hazards.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility**: The pipeline-register delay $T_{\text{reg}}$ is *not free*. Deep pipelines (e.g. Intel's 14-stage NetBurst) suffered from high $T_{\text{cycle}}$ penalties precisely because of cascaded register delays. This trade-off is the reason modern CPUs limit pipeline depth to roughly 14–20 stages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mandate**: Every algebraic transition is written out in full. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipelined Execution Time

**Statement to Prove:**
$$T_{\text{pipeline}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

**Reasoning:** A pipeline of $k$ stages needs $k$ cycles to *fill* (one cycle per stage for the *first* instruction to traverse all stages). After that, **one new instruction completes per cycle**, because the pipeline is fully occupied. So for the remaining $n - 1$ instructions, we need $n - 1$ additional cycles.

$$T_{\text{pipeline}} = \underbrace{k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}_{\text{fill time}} + \underbrace{(n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}_{\text{steady-state drain}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speedup Equation

We compare pipelined versus non-pipelined execution on the *same* instruction stream of $n$ instructions, assuming identical per-stage delay $T_{\text{cycle}}$ in both cases.

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined time.** Each instruction must pass through *all* $k$ stages sequentially, and the stages of *different* instructions do **not** overlap. Thus:

$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined time.** From Derivation 1:

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio.** $T_{\text{cycle}}$ cancels:

$$S_n = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot \cancel{T_{\text{cycle}}}}{(k + n - 1) \cdot \cancel{T_{\text{cycle}}}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic behaviour.** Divide numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$S_n = \frac{k}{1 + \frac{k - 1}{n}}$$

Let $n \to \infty$ (long instruction stream):

$$S_\infty = \frac{k}{1 + 0} = k$$

Hence the **maximum theoretical speedup equals the number of pipeline stages**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency

By KTU convention, efficiency is the *fraction of useful work per stage*:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful time per stage}}{\text{Actual time per stage on average}}$$

A stage is "busy" only when it is processing an instruction. The total "busy-stage-cycles" = $n \cdot k$ (each of the $n$ instructions visits $k$ stages). The total available "stage-cycles" in the pipelined execution = $k \cdot (k + n - 1)$ (there are $k$ stages, each running for $k + n - 1$ cycles).

$$\eta = \frac{n \cdot k}{k \cdot (k + n - 1)} = \frac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

Equivalently, $\eta = S_n / k$, which is the standard KTU 2024 textbook definition.

As $n \to \infty$:

$$\eta_\infty = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n}{k + n - 1} = 1 \quad \text{(i.e. 100 %)}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speedup with Imbalanced (Non-Uniform) Stages

In a realistic processor, the stages have *unequal* intrinsic delays $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$. The pipeline cycle time is set by the *slowest* stage:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\max} + T_{\text{reg}} \approx t_{\max}$$

**Sequential time:**

$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$$

**Pipelined time:**

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i \right) + (n - 1) \cdot t_{\max}$$

**Speedup:**

$$S_n = \frac{n \cdot \sum t_i}{\sum t_i + (n - 1) \cdot t_{\max}}$$

**Asymptotic limit:**

$$S_\infty = \frac{\sum t_i}{t_{\max}}$$

This is the celebrated *pipeline bottleneck result*: the speedup is capped by the ratio of the *average* stage delay to the *worst* stage delay. Always balanced designs maximise $S_\infty$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sequential vs Pipelined at a Glance

| Property | Sequential Datapath | Pipelined Datapath |
|:---------|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| Stage overlap | None | Full overlap (IF $\Vert$ ID $\Vert$ EX $\Vert$ MEM $\Vert$ WB) |
| Latency per instruction | $k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | $k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ (unchanged) |
| Total time for $n$ instructions | $n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | $(k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ |
| Throughput (steady state) | $\dfrac{1}{k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}$ instr/cycle | $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ instr/cycle |
| Speedup for large $n$ | 1× | $k$× (ideal) |
| Clock period | Larger (whole instruction) | Smaller (single stage) |
| Hardware cost | Lower (one ALU, one MEM) | Higher (duplicated registers, control) |
| Susceptibility to hazards | None | High (data, control, structural) |
| Ideal efficiency | 100 % | Approaches 100 % as $n \to \infty$ |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style, 7-mark variant]:**
A processor has a 5-stage pipeline with stage delays (in ns) $t_1 = 1, t_2 = 1.5, t_3 = 1, t_4 = 2, t_5 = 1$. Pipeline-register delay = 0.1 ns. Find: (a) clock period, (b) speedup over a non-pipelined equivalent for 100 instructions.

**Solution:**

(a) $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\max} + T_{\text{reg}} = 2 + 0.1 = 2.1$ ns

(b) $\sum t_i = 1 + 1.5 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 6.5$ ns

$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot \sum t_i = 100 \cdot 6.5 = 650 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = \sum t_i + (n-1) \cdot t_{\max} = 6.5 + 99 \cdot 2 = 204.5 \text{ ns}$$

$$S_{100} = \frac{650}{204.5} \approx 3.18\times$$

**Ideal speedup limit** for the *balanced* case: $S_\infty = k = 5$. **Actual limit with imbalance**: $S_\infty = \sum t_i / t_{\max} = 6.5 / 2 = 3.25$. Our $S_{100} = 3.18$ is correctly approaching 3.25. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `s1`, `reg1`) and all labels are plain text (no `**` or HTML inside double quotes), per the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Conceptual Block Diagram of a $k$-Stage Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Stage 1 IF"]:::stage
    reg1["Pipeline Reg 1"]:::reg
    s2["Stage 2 ID"]:::stage
    reg2["Pipeline Reg 2"]:::reg
    s3["Stage 3 EX"]:::stage
    reg3["Pipeline Reg 3"]:::reg
    s4["Stage 4 MEM"]:::stage
    reg4["Pipeline Reg 4"]:::reg
    s5["Stage 5 WB"]:::stage
    in["Instruction In"]:::io
    out["Result Out"]:::io

    in --> s1 --> reg1 --> s2 --> reg2 --> s3 --> reg3 --> s4 --> reg4 --> s5 --> out

    classDef stage fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Blue boxes = combinational logic of each stage.
- Yellow boxes = **pipeline registers** that latch data between stages.
- Green boxes = I/O boundaries.
- Data flows strictly **left to right** within a stage and is **latched** at every yellow box.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Instruction Flow Timing Diagram (Gantt-Style)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Timing Diagram for 5 Instructions Across 5 Stages
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Instr 1
    IF    :a1, 0, 1
    ID    :a2, 1, 1
    EX    :a3, 2, 1
    MEM   :a4, 3, 1
    WB    :a5, 4, 1

    section Instr 2
    IF    :b1, 1, 1
    ID    :b2, 2, 1
    EX    :b3, 3, 1
    MEM   :b4, 4, 1
    WB    :b5, 5, 1

    section Instr 3
    IF    :c1, 2, 1
    ID    :c2, 3, 1
    EX    :c3, 4, 1
    MEM   :c4, 5, 1
    WB    :c5, 6, 1

    section Instr 4
    IF    :d1, 3, 1
    ID    :d2, 4, 1
    EX    :d3, 5, 1
    MEM   :d4, 6, 1
    WB    :d5, 7, 1

    section Instr 5
    IF    :e1, 4, 1
    ID    :e2, 5, 1
    EX    :e3, 6, 1
    MEM   :e4, 7, 1
    WB    :e5, 8, 1
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The *first* instruction (Instr 1) takes cycles 0–4 to traverse all 5 stages (fill phase).
- Starting from cycle 1, a *new* instruction enters the IF stage every cycle (steady-state drain).
- Total cycles for 5 instructions = $k + n - 1 = 5 + 5 - 1 = 9$, consistent with the formula.
- **Critical observation for the exam**: Stage 1 (IF) is busy every cycle once filled — this is the source of the throughput gain.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture Mapping (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 IF]
        pc["PC"]:::io
        imem["Instruction Memory"]:::stage
        adder1["Adder Plus 4"]:::stage
    end

    subgraph S2[Stage 2 ID]
        rf["Register File"]:::stage
        sgn["Sign Extend"]:::stage
    end

    subgraph S3[Stage 3 EX]
        alu["ALU"]:::stage
    end

    subgraph S4[Stage 4 MEM]
        dmem["Data Memory"]:::stage
    end

    subgraph S5[Stage 5 WB]
        wb["Writeback Mux"]:::stage
    end

    if_id[("IF/ID Reg")]:::reg
    id_ex[("ID/EX Reg")]:::reg
    ex_mem[("EX/MEM Reg")]:::reg
    mem_wb[("MEM/WB Reg")]:::reg

    pc --> imem --> if_id --> rf
    if_id --> sgn
    rf --> id_ex
    sgn --> id_ex
    adder1 --> id_ex
    id_ex --> alu --> ex_mem
    ex_mem --> dmem --> mem_wb
    ex_mem --> mem_wb
    mem_wb --> wb

    classDef stage fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The four cylindrical nodes (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) are the **pipeline registers**.
- Notice that the Writeback stage's output loops back to the Register File in Stage 2 — a feedback path required to commit results.
- This is the textbook figure you should reproduce in the KTU 14-mark pipelined-datapath question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Define pipelining. State two advantages.

**Model Answer:**
Pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the instruction-processing function into a sequence of independent stages, with a pipeline register isolating each stage.

*Advantages:*
1. **Higher throughput** — instructions complete at a rate approaching one per cycle (IPC $\to 1$) instead of one every $k$ cycles.
2. **Lower average execution time per instruction** — although single-instruction latency is unchanged, the *amortised* cost per instruction approaches $T_{\text{cycle}}$.
3. *(Bonus point)* Better utilisation of on-chip hardware resources.

> 3 marks = 1 for definition + 1 each for two advantages.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Differentiate between a sequential datapath and a pipelined datapath.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sequential Datapath | Pipelined Datapath |
|:-------|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| Concurrency | One instruction in the datapath at a time | Up to $k$ instructions overlap in $k$ stages |
| Hardware reuse | ALU/MEM reused every cycle | Stage-local hardware (mostly) |
| Cycle time | Long — covers whole instruction | Short — covers one stage |
| Total time for $n$ instructions | $n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | $(k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ |
| Pipeline registers | Absent | Present between every stage |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **5-stage MIPS pipeline** with a neat block diagram. Label all pipeline registers and explain the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipelined processor has **4 stages** with stage delays of 30 ns, 50 ns, 40 ns, and 20 ns respectively. The pipeline register delay is 5 ns. Find: (i) clock period, (ii) speedup for 50 instructions over a non-pipelined equivalent, (iii) efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The 5-stage MIPS pipeline divides the execution of every instruction into the following stages:

1. **IF — Instruction Fetch (1 cycle):** Reads the instruction at the address in the Program Counter (PC) from the instruction memory. The PC is also incremented by 4 using a dedicated adder.
2. **ID — Instruction Decode & Register Read (1 cycle):** Decodes the opcode, reads the source registers from the register file, and sign-extends the 16-bit immediate to 32 bits.
3. **EX — Execute / Effective Address Calculation (1 cycle):** The ALU performs the arithmetic / logic operation, or computes the memory address for loads/stores, or evaluates the branch condition.
4. **MEM — Memory Access (1 cycle):** For loads, reads the data memory; for stores, writes the data memory. ALU-only instructions pass through idle.
5. **WB — Write Back (1 cycle):** Writes the result (ALU output or loaded data) back into the destination register.

**Pipeline registers** separating consecutive stages are: **IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB**. Each latches: the instruction bits, source register values, ALU operands, ALU result, and destination register number, so that downstream stages have stable inputs.

The block diagram is given in Diagram 3 of Section 4.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Clock period:** The clock period is set by the slowest stage plus the register delay:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\max} + T_{\text{reg}} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Speedup for $n = 50$ instructions:**

Sum of stage delays: $\sum t_i = 30 + 50 + 40 + 20 = 140$ ns

Sequential time:
$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot \sum t_i = 50 \cdot 140 = 7000 \text{ ns}$$

Pipelined time:
$$T_{\text{pipe}} = \sum t_i + (n - 1) \cdot t_{\max} = 140 + 49 \cdot 50 = 140 + 2450 = 2590 \text{ ns}$$

Speedup:
$$S_{50} = \frac{7000}{2590} \approx 2.703 \times$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{S_{50}}{k} = \frac{2.703}{4} \approx 0.6758 = 67.58\%$$

(Equivalently, $\eta = n / (k + n - 1) = 50 / 53 \approx 0.9434$ — *check which definition is being asked*. The KTU 2024 standard definition is $\eta = S / k$, giving 67.58 %.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a timing diagram, illustrate the execution of **5 instructions** in a **4-stage pipeline** (IF, ID, EX, WB). Compute the speedup, throughput, and efficiency obtained, assuming equal stage delays of 10 ns. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are pipeline registers? Why are they essential? Mention **two limitations** of pipelining. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Timing diagram (textual, mirroring Diagram 2 style):**

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| I1 | IF | ID | EX | WB | — | — | — | — |
| I2 | — | IF | ID | EX | WB | — | — | — |
| I3 | — | — | IF | ID | EX | WB | — | — |
| I4 | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | WB | — |
| I5 | — | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | WB |

Total cycles = $k + n - 1 = 4 + 5 - 1 = 8$ cycles.

$T_{\text{pipe}} = 8 \times 10 = 80$ ns; $T_{\text{seq}} = 5 \times 4 \times 10 = 200$ ns.

$$S_5 = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5\times$$

$$TP_5 = \frac{5 \text{ instr}}{80 \text{ ns}} = 0.0625 \text{ instr/ns}$$

$$\eta = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Pipeline Registers** are small hardware latch banks inserted between successive pipeline stages to store intermediate results at the end of every clock cycle.

**Why essential:**
1. They **synchronise** data transfer between stages on the clock edge.
2. They **decouple** combinational logic of adjacent stages, preventing glitches and race conditions.
3. They serve as **fan-out points** for forwarding paths (covered in Module 3).

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Data, control, and structural hazards** (covered in Module 3) force the pipeline to *stall* or *flush*, reducing the actual speedup well below the ideal $k\times$.
2. **Imbalanced stage delays** cap the speedup at $\sum t_i / t_{\max} < k$ (the *bottleneck* problem).
3. *(Bonus)* Deeper pipelines incur higher **branch misprediction penalties** because more in-flight instructions must be discarded.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1- to 2-Mark Deductions to Avoid:**
> 1. Writing the speedup formula as $n \cdot k / (k + n)$ — the correct form is $n \cdot k / (k + n - 1)$. The "$-1$" arises because the *fill* and *drain* share one cycle.
> 2. Forgetting to include the **pipeline register delay** $T_{\text{reg}}$ when computing the cycle time of a *non-uniform* pipeline.
> 3. Confusing **throughput** (instructions/cycle) with **latency** (cycles/instruction). In a pipeline, latency of one instruction is unchanged at $k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$; only *amortised* latency decreases.
> 4. For 14-mark design questions, examiners *require* the four pipeline registers labelled in the block diagram. Omitting them costs a clean 2 marks.
> 5. Efficiency formula: use $\eta = S / k$, not $\eta = S / n$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Introduction to Pipelining"**

- **Definition:** Pipelining overlaps instruction execution by partitioning the datapath into $k$ sequential stages separated by pipeline registers.
- **Laundry Analogy:** 4 stages, 4 loads, sequential = 440 min, pipelined = 230 min → speedup ≈ 1.91×.
- **5-Stage MIPS Pipeline:** **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**.
- **Four Pipeline Registers:** IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB — *always label them*.
- **Cycle time:** $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\max} + T_{\text{reg}}$ (slowest stage + register).
- **Pipelined time:** $T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$.
- **Sequential time:** $T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$.
- **Speedup:** $S_n = n \cdot k / (k + n - 1)$; **ideal max** = $k$.
- **Throughput:** $TP_n = n / (T_{\text{pipe}})$; **ideal max** = $1/T_{\text{cycle}}$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = S / k = n / (k + n - 1)$; **ideal max** = 100 %.
- **Imbalanced speedup limit:** $S_\infty = \sum t_i / t_{\max} < k$.
- **Latency** of a single instruction is *unchanged*; only **throughput** improves.
- **Limitations:** Hazards (data/control/structural), imbalance, branch penalties, hardware cost.
- **Forward-looking cue:** Module 3 covers hazards, stalling, forwarding, and branch prediction in this same 5-stage MIPS framework.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipeline Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pipeline Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of pipelined processor design, **Pipeline Hazards** are defined as any condition or event within the instruction pipeline that prevents the next instruction in the instruction stream from executing during its designated clock cycle. Hazards disrupt the smooth, lockstep flow of instructions through the pipeline stages, thereby degrading the theoretical ideal throughput of one instruction per cycle (IPC = 1).

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT302 - Module 2), hazards are the **primary limiting factor** of pipeline performance, and their detection and resolution constitute one of the most critical aspects of advanced computer architecture. The ideal pipeline cycle time $T_{ideal}$ is often compromised to $T_{eff}$ due to stalls introduced by hazards.

Mathematically, a hazard exists when the **Availability Condition** for a stage is violated:

$$A_i(t) = \text{Resource}_i \text{ is free} \land \text{Inputs}_i \text{ are valid at cycle } t$$

If $A_i(t) = 0$, a hazard occurs and the instruction must be stalled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pipeline hazard is NOT a programming error or a software bug. It is a *hardware-level contention* arising from the overlapping execution of instructions. The three classical classes are **Structural**, **Data**, and **Control** hazards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Laundry Pipeline"

To understand hazards intuitively, let us extend the classic **Laundry Pipeline Analogy** introduced by Hennessy and Patterson (the foundational reference for KTU architecture courses).

Imagine a washing machine with **4 stages**: *Wash → Dry → Fold → Store*. Four people (A, B, C, D) need their clothes processed.

* **Ideal Pipeline Flow:** While A's clothes are drying, B's are washing, leaving no idle resources. The pipeline achieves 1 batch per stage per cycle.
* **The Hazard Problem:** Now, what if:
  * **Structural Hazard** → The dryer breaks down, so B must WAIT for A to finish drying before B can start drying. The pipeline has *resource conflicts*.
  * **Data Hazard** → C needs A's *special detergent* (a "data dependency") before C can start washing. Even though the washing machine is free, C is forced to stall.
  * **Control Hazard** → D suddenly says, "Wait, I changed my mind—I don't want red shirts washed." The pipeline must *flush* the current load and restart with the new instruction. This is a *branch misprediction*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hazards are *inevitable* in any non-trivial pipeline. The art of processor design is to **detect** them early, **minimize** their penalty, and **recover** gracefully.

## 1.3 The Three Canonical Classes of Hazards

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes hazards into three families. A summary taxonomy:

| Hazard Class | Root Cause | Frequency in Real CPUs |
|--------------|------------|------------------------|
| **Structural Hazard** | Hardware resource conflict | Low (modern CPUs avoid) |
| **Data Hazard** | Operand dependency between instructions | Very High |
| **Control Hazard** | Branch/jump instruction uncertainty | Very High |

**Performance Impact Metric:** The **Hazard Penalty** is the number of stall cycles introduced per hazard, denoted $P_H$. The actual CPI is given by:

$$CPI_{actual} = CPI_{ideal} + \sum_{i} (\text{Hazard}_i \times P_{H,i})$$

where the sum is taken over all hazard types $i$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hazards (Resource Conflicts)

### 2.1.1 Definition and Mechanism

A **Structural Hazard** occurs when the hardware cannot support all possible combinations of overlapping instructions in the pipeline. In simpler terms, two (or more) instructions competing for the **same physical resource** in the **same clock cycle** create a collision.

**Common causes in KTU-mandated MIPS-style pipelines:**
