```mermaid
    classDef logic fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:2px
    classDef alu fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px

    class ID_RS,ID_RT,EXMEM_RD,MEMWB_RD reg
    class MATCH1,MUX logic
    class EXMEM_ALU,MEMWB_ALU,REGF,ALU_IN_A,ALU_IN_B alu
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define data hazard in a pipelined processor. Differentiate between RAW, WAR, and WAW hazards with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **data hazard** in a pipelined processor is a situation where an instruction in the pipeline depends on the result of another instruction that has not yet completed, leading to incorrect data being used.

**(1 Mark for each correct differentiation)**

| Type | Full Name | Example | Order Issue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RAW** | Read After Write (True) | `ADD R1,R2,R3`; `SUB R4,R1,R5` | Read happens before Write |
| **WAR** | Write After Read (Anti) | `ADD R1,R2,R3`; `MUL R4,R1,R5`; `SUB R2,R6,R7` | Write happens before Read |
| **WAW** | Write After Write (Output) | `ADD R1,R2,R3`; `MUL R1,R4,R5` | Writes must follow specific order |

*Only RAW is a true data dependency; WAR and WAW are name dependencies removed by register renaming.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is operand forwarding? How does it resolve a RAW hazard in a 5-stage MIPS pipeline?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Operand forwarding (bypassing)** is a hardware technique that routes the result of an ALU operation from a later pipeline stage (EX, MEM, or WB) back to the ALU input of an earlier stage in the next instruction, **without waiting for the result to be written to the register file**. (2 Marks)

**Resolution mechanism:** When instruction I1 completes its EX stage, its result is forwarded via a multiplexor directly into the EX stage of I2 in the very next cycle. This eliminates the 2-cycle stall that would otherwise be required, maintaining $CPI = 1.0$ for ALU-ALU dependencies. (1 Mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Consider the following MIPS instruction sequence. Identify all data hazards, classify them, and show how they are resolved using **operand forwarding only** (no stalls). Draw the pipeline timing diagram. **(7 Marks)**

```
I1:  ADD  R1, R2, R3
I2:  SUB  R4, R1, R5
I3:  AND  R6, R1, R7
I4:  OR   R8, R1, R9
I5:  XOR  R10, R4, R11
```

**(b)** A 5-stage pipelined processor executes a program of 5000 instructions. 25% of the instructions cause an ALU RAW hazard that requires 1 stall cycle (with forwarding for the other hazards). Calculate: (i) Effective CPI, (ii) Total execution time for a 2 GHz clock, (iii) Speedup over a non-pipelined processor. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Hazard Identification and Forwarding Diagram (7 Marks):**

**Hazard analysis:**

- **I1 → I2:** I2 reads R1; I1 writes R1 → **RAW hazard** (forwarded from EX/MEM of I1 to EX of I2).
- **I1 → I3:** I3 reads R1; I1 writes R1 → **RAW hazard** (forwarded from MEM/WB of I1 to EX of I3).
- **I1 → I4:** I4 reads R1; I1 writes R1 → **RAW hazard** (forwarded from MEM/WB of I1 to EX of I4).
- **I2 → I5:** I5 reads R4; I2 writes R4 → **RAW hazard** (forwarded from EX/MEM of I2 to EX of I5).

**Pipeline Timing Diagram (with forwarding):**

| Instr. | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 | C6 | C7 | C8 | C9 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I1: ADD | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | | | | |
| I2: SUB | | IF | ID | **EX** ←fwd | MEM | WB | | | |
| I3: AND | | | IF | ID | **EX** ←fwd | MEM | WB | | |
| I4: OR | | | | IF | ID | **EX** ←fwd | MEM | WB | |
| I5: XOR | | | | | IF | ID | **EX** ←fwd | MEM | WB |

(2 Marks — all 4 hazards identified; 3 Marks — timing diagram correct; 2 Marks — forwarding paths labeled)

**Total cycles: 9 (4 hazards resolved with NO stalls).**

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $N = 5000$, $f = 0.25$, $s = 1$ stall per hazard, $f_{clock} = 2$ GHz, $k = 5$.

**(i) Effective CPI:**

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + f \cdot s = 1 + (0.25)(1) = 1.25
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Total execution time:**

Clock period: $T_{clock} = 1 / 2\text{ GHz} = 0.5$ ns

$$
T_{total} = N \cdot CPI_{eff} \cdot T_{clock} = 5000 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 0.5\text{ ns} = 3125\text{ ns}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**(iii) Speedup over non-pipelined:**

Non-pipelined time: $T_{nonpipe} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{clock} = 5000 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.5\text{ ns} = 12500\text{ ns}$

$$
S = \frac{T_{nonpipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{12500}{3125} = 4.0
$$
**(2 Marks)**

Ideal speedup: $k = 5$. Achieved: 4.0. **Performance loss = 20%.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the three types of data hazards with examples. Why are WAR and WAW hazards NOT present in a basic 5-stage MIPS pipeline? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stage pipelined processor has forwarding. If 40% of instructions involve a Load-Use hazard requiring 1 stall cycle, and the program has 2000 instructions at 100 MHz clock, compute: (i) CPI with stalls, (ii) Total execution time, (iii) Speedup compared to the same processor *without* forwarding (which would have 2 stalls per ALU hazard AND 1 stall per Load-Use). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Three Types of Data Hazards (7 Marks):**

**1. Read After Write (RAW) — True Data Dependency:**
- *Definition:* An instruction tries to read a register before a previous instruction has finished writing to it. **(1 Mark)**
- *Example:*
  ```
  ADD R1, R2, R3   ; writes R1
  SUB R4, R1, R5   ; reads R1 — must wait
  ```
- *Resolution:* Forwarding (EX→EX bypass). **(0.5 Mark)**

**2. Write After Read (WAR) — Anti-dependency:**
- *Definition:* An instruction writes to a register that a previous instruction still needs to read. **(1 Mark)**
- *Example:*
  ```
  MUL R3, R1, R2   ; reads R1, R2
  ADD R1, R4, R5   ; writes R1 — would overwrite
  ```
- *Resolution:* Register renaming (in OoO processors). **(0.5 Mark)**

**3. Write After Write (WAW) — Output Dependency:**
- *Definition:* Two instructions write to the same destination; their order must be preserved. **(1 Mark)**
- *Example:*
  ```
  ADD R1, R2, R3
  MUL R1, R4, R5   ; final value must be MUL's result
  ```
- *Resolution:* Stalling or register renaming. **(0.5 Mark)**

**Why WAR/WAW do not occur in a basic 5-stage MIPS pipeline:** **(2 Marks)**

- Instructions are **fetched, decoded, executed, and written back in strict program order (in-order issue and in-order completion)**.
- The register file is read in ID and written in WB — the **write always happens strictly after the read** in clock-cycle terms.
- Therefore, even if I2 nominally overwrites R1 after I1 reads it, by the time I2 reaches WB (cycle 5), I1 has already completed its read (cycle 2). No reordering occurs.
- WAR/WAW only emerge in **out-of-order execution** (e.g., Intel's Tomasulo algorithm) where instructions complete and write back in *non-program* order.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $f_{clock} = 100$ MHz, $T_{clock} = 10$ ns, $k = 4$, Load-Use fraction $= 0.40$ (1 stall each), ALU RAW fraction $= 0.30$ (0 stalls with forwarding).

**(i) CPI with forwarding:**

$$
CPI_{forward} = 1 + (0.40 \cdot 1) + (0.30 \cdot 0) = 1.40
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Total execution time:**

$$
T_{fwd} = N \cdot CPI_{forward} \cdot T_{clock} = 2000 \cdot 1.40 \cdot 10\text{ ns} = 28000\text{ ns} = 28\ \mu\text{s}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**(iii) CPI without forwarding:**

- ALU RAW: 2 stalls each, fraction 0.30 → $0.30 \cdot 2 = 0.60$
- Load-Use: 1 stall each, fraction 0.40 → $0.40 \cdot 1 = 0.40$

$$
CPI_{nofwd} = 1 + 0.60 + 0.40 = 2.00
$$

Execution time: $T_{nofwd} = 2000 \cdot 2.00 \cdot 10\text{ ns} = 40000\text{ ns} = 40\ \mu\text{s}$

$$
\text{Speedup}_{nofwd \to fwd} = \frac{T_{nofwd}}{T_{fwd}} = \frac{40\ \mu\text{s}}{28\ \mu\text{s}} = \frac{10}{7} \approx 1.43
$$
**(3 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing WAR with RAW:** Many students write WAR examples as `ADD R1,R2,R3` followed by `SUB R1,R4,R5`. That is **WAW**, not WAR. WAR requires a *read* of the register *before* the *write*. Lose 1 mark per wrong classification.
> 2. **Forgetting the Load-Use 1-stall rule:** Even with forwarding, the LOAD→USE pattern *always* incurs 1 stall. Students often claim "forwarding eliminates ALL RAW hazards." — **Wrong.** Lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Pipeline timing diagram off-by-one:** When drawing the timing chart, ensure each instruction occupies **exactly one column per cycle**, and stalls are explicitly shaded. A misaligned diagram is 0 marks.
> 4. **Speedup formula misuse:** Use the correct $S = T_{nonpipe}/T_{pipe}$ — do not write $S = k / CPI_{eff}$ unless explicitly deriving it (it's only valid for large $N$).
> 5. **In numerical problems, units matter:** Always state the clock period in ns/µs and final time in matching units. KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Data hazards** occur when pipeline instructions have register/memory dependencies; they corrupt correctness if unresolved.
- ✅ Three types: **RAW (True)**, **WAR (Anti)**, **WAW (Output)** — only RAW is a *true* data dependency.
- ✅ **WAR and WAW do NOT exist** in the basic 5-stage MIPS pipeline (in-order issue, in-order completion) — they appear only in **out-of-order** processors.
- ✅ **Pipeline interlocking (stalls)** is the simplest resolution but degrades CPI.
- ✅ **Operand forwarding (bypassing)** routes data from EX/MEM/WB pipeline registers back to ALU inputs — eliminates ALU-ALU RAW stalls completely.
- ✅ **Load-Use hazards** require **1 stall even with forwarding** (data is ready at end of MEM, too late for next EX).
- ✅ **Compiler scheduling** can hide stall cycles by inserting independent instructions.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize:
  - $CPI_{eff} = 1 + f \cdot s$
  - $S_\infty = k / (1 + f \cdot s)$
  - $T_{total} = N \cdot CPI_{eff} \cdot T_{clock}$
- ✅ **MIPS 5 stages:** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB.
- ✅ Real-world: Forwarding is a *hardware* solution; software reordering is a *compiler* solution. Modern CPUs use both.
- ✅ For KTU, always draw a **pipeline timing diagram** for any hazard problem — it is worth at least 3-4 marks.
- ✅ Examiner's hot topics: (i) timing diagram with/without forwarding, (ii) Load-Use 1-stall exception, (iii) numerical CPI/speedup calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Control Hazard (Branch Hazard):** A *control hazard* in a pipelined processor occurs when the next instruction to execute depends on the outcome of a branch instruction that has not yet been resolved. Because the pipeline must fetch the next instruction *before* the branch target is known, a **Control Hazard** creates uncertainty in the Instruction Fetch (IF) stage, leading to potential incorrect instruction fetch and execution.

In the KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, a control hazard is fundamentally a **dependency in the program flow (control dependency)**, as opposed to a **data dependency** (data hazard) or **resource conflict** (structural hazard). The branch instruction's outcome — *Taken* or *Not Taken* — determines the next Program Counter (PC) value, and this decision is not available until late in the pipeline (typically the EX or MEM stage of a classic 5-stage pipeline).

**Classification of Branch Outcomes:**
- **Taken Branch (BT):** The PC is updated to the Branch Target Address (BTA), causing a *non-sequential* fetch.
- **Not-Taken Branch (BNT):** The PC increments normally (PC + 4), causing *sequential* fetch continuation.

**Standard Pipeline Penalty Metric:** A control hazard typically incurs a penalty of **N − 1 clock cycles**, where **N** is the depth (number of stages) of the pipeline. For the canonical **5-stage pipeline**, this penalty is **4 clock cycles** in the worst case (if no mitigation is applied).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy: The GPS Navigation Dilemma**

Imagine you are driving on a highway at 100 km/h. You see a road sign that says, *"In 2 km, take a detour — turn left OR continue straight."* Until you actually arrive at the junction and read the sign clearly, you do not know which way to go.

However, a *fully pipelined* driver (an expert who is already mentally planning the next move) starts **shifting gears for the left turn while still 2 km away** — based on a *guess* that the road will turn left.

- If the guess is **correct** (taken, left turn): No time is wasted.
- If the guess is **wrong** (not-taken, straight road): The driver has wasted time shifting gears and must **spend 4 extra seconds** to reset the car, brake, and continue straight.

The **4 seconds lost** is exactly analogous to the **pipeline bubble** (stall) introduced by a control hazard. The "guess" is what computer architects call **Branch Prediction**, and the "4-second recovery" is the **branch penalty**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard KTU 2024 reference values** for the canonical 5-stage MIPS pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB):

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pipeline Depth | N | **5 stages** |
| Maximum Control Hazard Penalty (no mitigation) | P_max | **4 clock cycles** |
| Branch Resolution Stage | — | **EX stage (for branches)** |
| Target Address Computation Stage | — | **EX stage** |
| Branch Condition Evaluation Stage | — | **EX stage** |
| Branch Instruction Frequency | f_branch | **~15%–25%** of dynamic instructions |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classic MIPS 5-stage pipeline assumes branch conditions are resolved in the **EX stage**, making the worst-case branch penalty **3 cycles** (because IF and ID of the *next* instruction are the ones that fetched speculatively). Always use the textbook value of **3-cycle** stall if the syllabus specifies "branch resolved in EX," or **4-cycle** stall if the branch target is resolved in MEM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Control Hazards Occur

A control hazard arises due to the **temporal mismatch** between:

1. **The PC update logic** (which is combinational and immediate at the end of IF stage).
2. **The branch condition evaluation** (which requires ALU inputs, computed in EX).

In a **non-pipelined** processor, this is not a problem: the next instruction is fetched *only after* the branch is resolved. In a **pipelined** processor, however, the hardware fetches the **next sequential instruction** during the same clock cycle the branch is being decoded — this is the source of the hazard.

---

### 2.2 Pipeline Behavior — Detailed Step-by-Step

Consider a sequence:
```
I1:  BEQ  R1, R2, LOOP      ; Branch instruction
I2:  ADD  R3, R4, R5         ; Instruction fetched BEFORE branch is resolved
I3:  SUB  R6, R7, R8         ; Same as above
...
```

The table below (using CC = Clock Cycle) shows the pipeline state assuming **no mitigation**:

| CC | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BEQ** | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | **I2** (wrong fetch) | BEQ | — | — | — |
| 3 | **I3** (wrong fetch) | I2 | BEQ | — | — |
| 4 | **I4** (wrong fetch) | I3 | I2 | BEQ | — |
| 5 | **(Target)** | I4 | I3 | I2 | BEQ |
| 6 | Target+1 | — | I4 | I3 | I2 |
| 7 | — | Target+1 | — | I4 | I3 |

**Cycle 4 → 5:** The branch is resolved in EX (CC 3). The PC is corrected to the branch target. **I2, I3, and I4 must be flushed** (turned into NOPs/bubbles). This is the **branch penalty**.

---

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The KTU Cheat Sheet for Control Hazard Calculations**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Branch Penalty (P) | $P = N - B$ | N = pipeline depth, B = branch resolution stage index |
| 2 | Stall Cycles (no mitigation) | $S = P$ | Bubble count inserted into pipeline |
| 3 | CPI with stalls | $CPI = CPI_{ideal} + P \times f_{branch}$ | f_branch = branch frequency |
| 4 | Speedup (stalled vs ideal) | $Speedup = \dfrac{CPI_{ideal} \times N}{CPI_{ideal} \times N + S \times f_{branch} \times N}$ | Amdahl's law application |
| 5 | Branch Target Address | $BTA = PC + 4 + (sign\_ext \times 4)$ | For PC-relative addressing |
| 6 | Branch Taken Penalty | $P_{taken} = P$ | Full penalty applies if branch is taken |
| 7 | Branch Not-Taken Penalty | $P_{NT} = 0$ (if predicted NT) | Zero penalty if NT and predictor said NT |
| 8 | Static Prediction Accuracy | $Acc = \max(f_{taken},\ 1 - f_{taken})$ | Best static guess is the more frequent outcome |
| 9 | Dynamic Predictor Hit Rate | $HR = \dfrac{Correct\ Predictions}{Total\ Branches}$ | Measured over workload |
| 10 | Effective CPI (with predictor) | $CPI = CPI_{ideal} + (1 - Acc) \times P \times f_{branch}$ | P only paid on misprediction |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Utility:** Control hazard mitigation techniques (branch prediction, speculation, delay slots) are foundational to modern superscalar CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series). The Intel Pentium's infamous FDIV bug, while a *data* issue, was symptomatic of the same broader problem of speculative execution — the entire industry standard of *branch prediction + speculation* exists because of the control hazard.

---

### 2.4 The Six Classical Mitigation Strategies (KTU-Favored)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The 6 strategies KTU loves to test (in increasing complexity order):**

1. **Stall (Freeze) the Pipeline** — Simplest; insert NOPs until branch resolved.
2. **Assume Branch is Not Taken** — Predict NT; flush only if actually taken.
3. **Assume Branch is Always Taken** — Predict T; flush only if actually not taken.
4. **Delayed Branch** — Compiler rearranges instructions to fill the "delay slot."
5. **Static Branch Prediction** — Hardware uses fixed prediction (e.g., backward-taken, forward-not-taken).
6. **Dynamic Branch Prediction** — Hardware uses runtime history (1-bit, 2-bit saturating counters, BHT, BTB, Gshare, Tournament).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Branch Penalty in a 5-Stage Pipeline

We derive the **branch penalty** for a 5-stage MIPS pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) where the branch condition is evaluated in the **EX stage** and the branch target address is computed in the **EX stage**.

**Step 1 — Identify the branch resolution stage:**
For a BEQ instruction, the operands (R1 and R2) must be read from the register file. In the **classic MIPS pipeline**, register reads happen in the **ID stage**, but the comparator used to test equality is part of the ALU, which is in the **EX stage**. Therefore, the branch is resolved in EX.

**Step 2 — Count the wrongly fetched instructions:**
At the start of the cycle where the branch is in EX, the following instructions are *already* in the pipeline:
- One instruction in **ID** (fetched in CC = branch_CC + 1)
- One instruction in **IF** (fetched in CC = branch_CC + 2)

**Step 3 — Determine the total penalty:**
By the time the branch is resolved, **2 instructions have been fetched incorrectly**. After resolution, the PC is updated, but the next correct instruction cannot fetch until the IF stage is free. The penalty is therefore:

$$
P = \text{Number of wrongly fetched instructions} = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the canonical formula:**
Using $P = N - B$ where $N = 5$ and $B = 3$ (EX is stage 3):

$$
P = 5 - 3 = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on KTU convention:** Many KTU textbooks (Patterson & Hennessy) state the *full* worst-case penalty is **3 cycles** for a 5-stage pipeline, because the branch instruction itself occupies IF, ID, and EX — meaning 3 fetch cycles are "wasted" if the target is fetched in the same cycle the branch enters EX. The KTU paper typically uses **P = 3 cycles** for the classic MIPS 5-stage pipeline. Always verify the textbook reference used by your professor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective CPI with Control Hazards

**Given:**
- Ideal CPI (no hazards): $CPI_{ideal} = 1.0$
- Pipeline depth: $N = 5$
- Branch resolution stage: $B = 3$ (EX)
- Branch frequency: $f_{branch} = 0.20$ (20% of instructions are branches)
- Static prediction: Assume Branch Not Taken (NT), with prediction accuracy = 70%

**Step 1 — Calculate the base penalty (no mitigation):**

$$
P_{base} = N - B = 5 - 3 = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the prediction error rate:**

$$
\text{Error Rate} = 1 - Acc = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30
$$

**Step 3 — Effective branch penalty (only on misprediction):**

$$
P_{effective} = P_{base} \times \text{Error Rate} = 2 \times 0.30 = 0.6\ \text{cycles per branch}
$$

**Step 4 — CPI contribution from branches:**

$$
\Delta CPI = P_{effective} \times f_{branch} = 0.6 \times 0.20 = 0.12
$$

**Step 5 — Total effective CPI:**

$$
CPI_{total} = CPI_{ideal} + \Delta CPI = 1.0 + 0.12 = 1.12
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
The processor achieves only $\frac{1.0}{1.12} \approx 89.3\%$ of the ideal throughput, demonstrating the significant performance impact of unmitigated control hazards.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Speedup with Delayed Branching

**Given:**
- 5-stage pipeline, $N = 5$
- 1 delay slot (1 cycle stall per branch)
- Branch frequency: $f_{branch} = 0.25$
- Compiler can fill 70% of delay slots with useful instructions

**Step 1 — Effective stall per branch:**

$$
P_{DB} = (1 - \text{fill\_ratio}) \times 1 = (1 - 0.70) \times 1 = 0.3\ \text{cycles per branch}
$$

**Step 2 — CPI with delayed branch:**

$$
CPI_{total} = 1.0 + 0.3 \times 0.25 = 1.075
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup over unmitigated (full 2-cycle penalty):**

$$
Speedup = \frac{CPI_{unmitigated}}{CPI_{DB}} = \frac{1.0 + 2.0 \times 0.25}{1.075} = \frac{1.50}{1.075} \approx 1.395
$$

$$
\boxed{Speedup \approx 1.40\times}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mitigation Strategies

| Strategy | Implementation Cost | Penalty Reduction | Hardware Complexity | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pipeline Stall (NOPs) | **0** | 0% | None | Trivial / Educational |
| Static "Assume Not Taken" | **Low** | ~50% (typical) | 1 mux + flush logic | DLX, early RISC |
| Static "Always Taken" | **Low** | ~40% | Flush logic | Loop-heavy code |
| Delayed Branch (1 slot) | **Compiler** | Up to 100% (filled) | None | MIPS, SPARC |
| 1-bit Dynamic Predictor | **Low** | 70–80% | 1-bit BHT | Early Pentiums |
| 2-bit Saturating Counter | **Medium** | 85–95% | 2-bit BHT | Modern CPUs |
| 2-level Adaptive (Local/Global) | **Medium** | 95%+ | Larger BHT | AMD K7, K8 |
| BTB + Target Cache | **High** | 95–98% | BTB CAM/RAM | All modern CPUs |
| Tournament Predictor | **Very High** | 98%+ | Multiple tables | Alpha 21264, modern x86 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Control Hazard Detection and Resolution Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC[Program Counter PC] --> IFStage[IF Stage: Instruction Fetch from I-Cache]
    IFStage --> IDStage[ID Stage: Decode and Read Registers]
    IDStage --> EXStage[EX Stage: ALU Operation and Branch Compare]
    EXStage --> MEMStage[MEM Stage: Data Memory Access]
    MEMStage --> WBStage[WB Stage: Write Back to Register File]
    
    EXStage --> BranchUnit{Branch Unit}
    BranchUnit -->|Branch Taken| TargetAddr[Compute BTA = PC + 4 + Offset x 4]
    TargetAddr --> Mux[PC Source MUX]
    
    BranchUnit -->|Branch NOT Taken| SeqAddr[Sequential PC + 4]
    SeqAddr --> Mux
    
    Mux -->|Update PC| PC
    Mux -->|Invalidate IF and ID| FlushLogic[Flush IF and ID: Insert Bubbles]
    FlushLogic --> IFStage
    FlushLogic --> IDStage
    
    EXStage --> FlushSignal[Send Flush Signal to IF and ID stages on Branch Resolution]
    FlushSignal --> FlushLogic
    
    style PC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EXStage fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BranchUnit fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FlushLogic fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Mux fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 State Diagram — 2-Bit Saturating Counter (KTU-Favorite Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StronglyNotTaken
    StronglyNotTaken --> WeaklyNotTaken : Branch Taken Once
    WeaklyNotTaken --> WeaklyNotTaken : Branch Not Taken
    WeaklyNotTaken --> StronglyNotTaken : Branch Not Taken Again
    WeaklyNotTaken --> WeaklyTaken : Branch Taken Once
    StronglyTaken --> WeaklyTaken : Branch Not Taken Once
    WeaklyTaken --> WeaklyTaken : Branch Taken
    WeaklyTaken --> StronglyTaken : Branch Taken Again
    StronglyTaken --> StronglyTaken : Branch Taken
    StronglyNotTaken --> StronglyNotTaken : Branch Not Taken
    WeaklyTaken --> StronglyNotTaken : Two Consecutive Not Taken
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** The 2-bit saturating counter has 4 states: **Strongly Not Taken (SNT)**, **Weakly Not Taken (WNT)**, **Weakly Taken (WT)**, **Strongly Taken (ST)**. Two consecutive mispredictions are required to change the prediction — this hysteresis prevents thrashing on alternating branch patterns.

---

### 4.3 Pipeline Timing Diagram — Branch with Stall (Penalty Visualization)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Behavior with Unmitigated Branch Hazard (5-Stage)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section BEQ Instruction
    Branch_IF       :a1, 0, 1
    Branch_ID       :a2, 1, 1
    Branch_EX_Resolve :a3, 2, 1
    Branch_MEM      :a4, 3, 1
    Branch_WB       :a5, 4, 1
    
    section Wrongly Fetched I2
    I2_IF_Wrong     :b1, 1, 1
    I2_ID_Wrong     :b2, 2, 1
    I2_EX_Bubble    :crit, b3, 3, 1
    I2_MEM_Bubble   :crit, b4, 4, 1
    
    section Wrongly Fetched I3
    I3_IF_Wrong     :c1, 2, 1
    I3_ID_Bubble    :crit, c2, 3, 1
    I3_EX_Bubble    :crit, c3, 4, 1
    
    section Target Instruction
    Target_IF       :d1, 3, 1
    Target_ID       :d2, 4, 1
    Target_EX       :d3, 5, 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Visualizing the 2-cycle penalty:** Notice that the wrongly fetched instructions (I2, I3) remain in the pipeline as **bubbles** (marked in red as critical) for 2 additional cycles after the branch resolves, before being discarded. This represents the **2-cycle branch penalty** for a 5-stage MIPS pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**  
*Define a control hazard. Why does it occur in a pipelined processor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **control hazard** is a type of pipeline hazard that arises when the flow of instruction execution depends on the outcome of a branch instruction whose result is not yet known. It occurs in pipelined processors because the **Instruction Fetch (IF) stage continuously fetches the next sequential instruction** based on PC + 4, while the **branch condition is evaluated only in the EX stage**. This 2-stage (or 3-stage, depending on pipeline depth) delay between fetching the branch and knowing its outcome causes **1 to 3 wrong instructions to be fetched speculatively**, which must be flushed when the branch resolves, resulting in a **branch penalty** of 2–3 clock cycles.

*(Self-scoring: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation of timing, 1 mark for penalty concept.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Differentiate between static and dynamic branch prediction. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Prediction | Dynamic Prediction |
|---|---|---|
| When decided | **At compile time** | **At runtime** |
| Adaptability | **Fixed rule, cannot change** | **Adapts to program behavior** |
| Hardware cost | **Zero / minimal** | **Requires BHT/BTB** |
| Accuracy | **~50–65%** | **85–98%** |
| Example | "Backward branches taken, forward not taken" | **2-bit saturating counter in BHT** |
| Example contd. | "Assume always not taken" | **Tournament predictor (Alpha 21264)** |

*(Self-scoring: 1 mark for static, 1 mark for dynamic, 1 mark for the differentiating feature.)*

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **control hazards** in a pipelined processor with a neat pipeline timing diagram. Show the branch penalty for a classic 5-stage MIPS pipeline. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **"Assume Branch is Not Taken"** strategy and the **Delayed Branch** technique as methods to mitigate control hazards. Illustrate with a code example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **control hazard** occurs when the pipeline cannot determine the next instruction to fetch in time, because the outcome of a branch instruction is pending. The classic 5-stage MIPS pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) resolves branches in the **EX stage**, which is 2 cycles after the branch enters the IF stage.

**Pipeline Timing Diagram (assuming branch is taken):**

| CC | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BEQ | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | I2 | BEQ | — | — | — |
| 3 | I3 | I2 | BEQ | — | — |
| 4 | Target | I3 | I2 | BEQ | — |
| 5 | Target+1 | **—** | I3 | I2 | BEQ |
| 6 | Target+2 | **—** | **—** | I3 | I2 |

**Key Observations:**
- At CC 2, **I2 is wrongly fetched** (assumes sequential flow).
- At CC 3, **I3 is wrongly fetched** (assumes sequential flow).
- At CC 3 (end of EX stage), the branch is resolved. The PC is updated to the target.
- At CC 4, the **target instruction** is correctly fetched.
- **I2 and I3 are flushed** (replaced with NOPs/bubbles).

**Branch Penalty Calculation:**

$$
P = N - B = 5 - 3 = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

> The branch instruction itself is not "wasted" — it executes correctly. The penalty is the **time spent fetching and discarding 2 wrong instructions** before the target can be fetched.

*(Self-scoring: 2 marks for concept, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for penalty, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(1) Assume Branch is Not Taken (Static Prediction - NT):**

In this strategy, the hardware **always predicts that the branch will NOT be taken**. The pipeline continues to fetch instructions sequentially. If the prediction is correct, no penalty is incurred. If the branch is actually taken, the instructions in the IF and ID stages are flushed.

**Code Example (MIPS):**
```mips
LOOP:
    BEQ  R1, R2, EXIT    ; Branch to EXIT if R1 == R2
    ADD  R3, R4, R5      ; Assume not taken: fetch & execute this
    SUB  R6, R7, R8      ; ... and this
    J    LOOP
EXIT:
    ...
```

If R1 ≠ R2 (branch not taken), the pipeline runs at full speed. If R1 == R2, the ADD and SUB instructions must be **flushed**, causing a 2-cycle penalty. This strategy works well for **forward branches** (which are usually not taken in loops).

**(2) Delayed Branch Technique:**

The compiler (or hardware) **explicitly defines a "delay slot"** — the instruction(s) immediately following the branch are *always* executed, regardless of whether the branch is taken. The compiler's job is to **fill the delay slot with a useful instruction** from before the branch (or from the branch target, depending on scheduling).

**Code Example (MIPS) with 1 Delay Slot:**
```mips
LOOP:
    BEQ  R1, R2, EXIT    ; Branch
    ADD  R3, R4, R5      ; <-- DELAY SLOT: always executed
    SUB  R6, R7, R8      ; This runs ONLY if branch is not taken
    J    LOOP
EXIT:
    ...
```

**Compiler Scheduling Options for the Delay Slot:**

1. **From before the branch** (most common): Move an instruction that is *always needed* into the delay slot.
2. **From the branch target** (only safe if branch is taken): Move an instruction from the target.
3. **Fill with NOP** (worst case): If no useful instruction can be found, the slot becomes a no-op.

**Limitation:** This technique requires compiler support and becomes ineffective if the delay slot is more than 1–2 cycles. Modern deep-pipelines (10+ stages) use **dynamic branch prediction** instead.

*(Self-scoring: 2 marks for NT strategy, 2 marks for delayed branch concept, 2 marks for example, 1 mark for limitations.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **2-bit saturating counter-based dynamic branch predictor**. Show the state transition for a sequence of branch outcomes: T, T, NT, NT, T, T, NT. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 5-stage MIPS pipeline has 25% branch instructions. The branch is resolved in the EX stage. Calculate the **effective CPI** if:
> - **(i)** No branch prediction is used (always stall). **(3 marks)**
> - **(ii)** Static "Not Taken" prediction is used with 70% accuracy. **(4 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **2-bit saturating counter** uses a 2-bit state variable (0, 1, 2, 3) per branch instruction, stored in the **Branch History Table (BHT)** indexed by the branch's PC address.

**State Definitions:**

| State | Value | Prediction |
|---|---|---|
| Strongly Not Taken (SNT) | 0 | Not Taken |
| Weakly Not Taken (WNT) | 1 | Not Taken |
| Weakly Taken (WT) | 2 | Taken |
| Strongly Taken (ST) | 3 | Taken |

**State Diagram:**
The counter **increments** on a Taken outcome and **decrements** on a Not Taken outcome. It saturates at 0 (SNT) and 3 (ST) — it cannot go below 0 or above 3.

**Sequence Analysis: T, T, NT, NT, T, T, NT**

Assume the initial state is **Weakly Not Taken (WNT = 1)**.

| Step | Actual Outcome | Current State | New State | Prediction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T | WNT (1) | WT (2) | NT (correct? **NO** — mispredict) |
| 2 | T | WT (2) | ST (3) | T (correct) |
| 3 | NT | ST (3) | WT (2) | T (mispredict) |
| 4 | NT | WT (2) | WNT (1) | T (mispredict) |
| 5 | T | WNT (1) | WT (2) | NT (mispredict) |
| 6 | T | WT (2) | ST (3) | T (correct) |
| 7 | NT | ST (3) | WT (2) | T (mispredict) |

**Final State after 7 outcomes: WT (2)**
**Mispredictions: 5 out of 7 ≈ 71.4% accuracy**

> **Key insight:** A single "noisy" outcome does not flip the prediction — the predictor requires **two consecutive contrary outcomes** to change its mind, providing **hysteresis** that prevents thrashing.

*(Self-scoring: 2 marks for state diagram, 2 marks for sequence analysis, 2 marks for table, 1 mark for key insight.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $N = 5$ (5-stage pipeline)
- $B = 3$ (branch resolved in EX)
- $f_{branch} = 0.25$
- $CPI_{ideal} = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Base branch penalty:**

$$
P_{base} = N - B = 5 - 3 = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

---

**(i) No branch prediction — always stall (3 marks):**

Every branch causes a full 2-cycle stall.

$$
P_{effective} = P_{base} = 2\ \text{cycles}
$$

$$
CPI_{total} = CPI_{ideal} + P_{effective} \times f_{branch}
$$

$$
CPI_{total} = 1.0 + 2 \times 0.25 = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5
$$

$$
\boxed{CPI_{total} = 1.5}
$$

---

**(ii) Static "Not Taken" prediction with 70% accuracy (4 marks):**

Only **mispredictions** incur the penalty. Misprediction rate = $1 - 0.70 = 0.30$.

$$
P_{effective} = P_{base} \times \text{Misprediction Rate} = 2 \times 0.30 = 0.6\ \text{cycles per branch}
$$

$$
CPI_{total} = 1.0 + 0.6 \times 0.25 = 1.0 + 0.15 = 1.15
$$

$$
\boxed{CPI_{total} = 1.15}
$$

**Comparison:** The predictor reduces CPI from **1.5 → 1.15**, a **~23% performance improvement** for the same branch frequency.

*(Self-scoring: (i) 1 mark for penalty formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer. (ii) 1 mark for misprediction rate, 1 mark for effective penalty, 1 mark for CPI formula, 1 mark for final answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Branch Penalty Confusion:** Students often write the branch penalty as 4 cycles for a 5-stage pipeline. The correct value is **2 cycles** (since $P = N - B = 5 - 3 = 2$). The penalty is the number of *wrongly fetched* instructions, not the number of stages.
> 
> 2. **CPI Formula Misuse:** $CPI_{total} = CPI_{ideal} + P \times f_{branch}$ is for *every branch incurring the full penalty*. When using a predictor, you must use $P \times (1 - Acc) \times f_{branch}$. Many students forget the accuracy factor.
> 
> 3. **2-bit vs 1-bit Predictor:** If the question asks for a 1-bit predictor, the state diagram has only 2 states (T/NT) and flips on a single contrary outcome. The 2-bit version has 4 states.
> 
> 4. **Delayed Branch Limitation:** Many students assume delayed branching *eliminates* the penalty completely. In reality, the compiler can fill the delay slot only **~60–80% of the time**; the rest become NOPs.
> 
> 5. **Pipeline Depth vs Branch Resolution:** Always state *both* values explicitly in the answer. KTU examiners deduct marks if only one is given.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Control Hazard** = branch outcome unknown when next instruction is fetched.
- ✅ **Branch Penalty (5-stage MIPS, EX resolution)** = **2 cycles** (using $P = N - B$).
- ✅ **Three primary causes** of control hazard: (1) PC update timing, (2) condition evaluation delay, (3) target address computation delay.
- ✅ **6 mitigation strategies** (in order of complexity): Stall → Assume NT → Assume T → Delayed Branch → Static Prediction → Dynamic Prediction.
- ✅ **Static NT** = 50% baseline accuracy; good for forward branches.
- ✅ **Delayed Branch** = compiler fills "delay slot" with a useful instruction; max 1–2 cycles deep.
- ✅ **1-bit predictor** = flips on single contrary outcome (thrashes easily).
- ✅ **2-bit saturating counter** = 4 states (SNT, WNT, WT, ST); requires 2 mispredictions to flip.
- ✅ **BHT** (Branch History Table) = indexed by PC low bits; stores 2-bit counter.
- ✅ **BTB** (Branch Target Buffer) = stores target address for fast target fetch.
- ✅ **CPI formula (stall):** $CPI = 1 + P \times f_{branch}$.
- ✅ **CPI formula (predictor):** $CPI = 1 + P \times (1 - Acc) \times f_{branch}$.
- ✅ **Branch Target Address:** $BTA = PC + 4 + (sign\_ext\_immediate \times 4)$.
- ✅ **Modern CPUs (Intel, AMD, Apple M-series)** all use 2-level adaptive + tournament predictors with >95% accuracy.
- ✅ **Bimodal / 2-level / Gshare / Tournament** are advanced predictor names — know the hierarchy.
- ✅ **Branch Target Buffer (BTB) caches BOTH the branch PC AND the target**, enabling **zero-cycle branch** when BTB hits.
- ✅ **Speculative execution** in modern CPUs can lead to security vulnerabilities (Spectre, Meltdown) — bonus point awareness.
- ✅ **Always state pipeline depth, branch resolution stage, and branch frequency** explicitly in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pipelining Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution. A pipeline is a set of data-processing elements (stages) connected in series, where the output of one stage is the input of the next. The execution of each instruction is decomposed into a sequence of sub-operations (stages), and successive instructions are processed through these stages in a staggered, overlapping manner.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, pipelining is treated as the **fundamental parallelism technique** applied at the **Instruction Level Parallelism (ILP)** granularity. The most commonly implemented model is the classic **5-stage RISC pipeline** consisting of:
- **IF** — Instruction Fetch
- **ID** — Instruction Decode & Register Fetch
- **EX** — Execute / Effective Address Calculation
- **MEM** — Memory Access
- **WB** — Write Back

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim tone):** A pipeline of depth *k* allows up to *k* instructions to be in different stages of execution simultaneously during steady-state operation, achieving a theoretical maximum throughput of **one instruction per clock cycle (IPC = 1)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Analogy

Imagine a household laundry system with **4 stages**: Wash → Dry → Fold → Iron.
- **Non-pipelined (sequential) approach:** You wash 4 loads one after another. Total time = 4 × 4 stages = 16 time units. Each load completes entirely before the next begins.
- **Pipelined approach:** As soon as Load 1 moves from Wash to Dry, you put Load 2 into the Wash stage. After an initial fill-up period of 4 time units, **a new load finishes every single time unit**.

**Translation to CPU:** The "loads" are **instructions**, the "stages" are **pipeline stages (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB)**, and the "time unit" is the **clock cycle**. The pipeline is *empty* only at the start (drain) and *empty* only at the very end.

### 1.3 Key Physical/Architectural Metrics

- **Clock Cycle Time (T):** Time taken by the *slowest* stage. For a balanced pipeline, all stages have equal delay.
- **Pipeline Depth (k):** Number of stages.
- **Pipeline Throughput:** Number of instructions completed per unit time, measured in **Instructions Per Cycle (IPC)** or **MIPS** (Millions of Instructions Per Second).
- **Latency:** Time taken to complete **one** instruction from start to finish = **k × T**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "PIPE":**
> **P**arallelism (instruction-level)
> **I**nstruction overlap
> **P**rocessing stages connected in series
> **E**ach stage does a portion of the work

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How of Pipelining

Pipelining works because of three enabling properties of instruction execution:

1. **Independence of Sub-operations:** A single instruction's execution can be *decomposed* into independent sub-tasks (e.g., fetching the next instruction does not depend on the ALU result of the current one).
2. **Uniform Stage Timing:** Each sub-task can be completed in a bounded time slot, and we insert **pipeline registers (latches)** between stages to hold intermediate results.
3. **Resource Replication (where required):** Functional units (e.g., separate instruction memory and data memory, as in the Harvard-style RISC pipeline) must be available so that consecutive stages do not collide on the same hardware.

**Why pipeline registers?** Without latches, the output of stage 1 would race directly into stage 2 within the same cycle, destroying the clock-by-clock discipline. Pipeline registers:
- Hold the intermediate result stable for the next stage's input.
- Add a small **overhead delay (τ)** to the clock period, so the effective clock period is:
  $$T_{clock} = T_{stage} + \tau$$

**How hazards emerge:** The ideal pipeline breaks down when:
- Two instructions need the **same hardware resource** in the same cycle (Structural Hazard).
- An instruction needs a value that a **previous instruction has not yet produced** (Data Hazard).
- The next instruction to fetch is **not yet known** because of a branch/jump (Control Hazard).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all derivable formulas** that appear in KTU ESE and module test questions for Pipelining. Memorize these and the boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Total time **without** pipeline | $T_{np} = n \cdot k \cdot T$ | n = instructions, k = stages, T = clock period |
| 2 | Total time **with** pipeline | $T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$ | First instruction takes k cycles; each subsequent one takes 1 cycle |
| 3 | Speedup factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | As $n \rightarrow \infty$, $S \rightarrow k$ |
| 4 | Throughput (inst/cycle) | $TP = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Maximum value = 1 (one instruction per cycle) |
| 5 | Pipeline efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k(k + n - 1)}$ | Maximum value = 100% (or 1) |
| 6 | Effective clock period (with latches) | $T_{clock} = T_{stage} + \tau$ | τ = latch setup + hold + propagation delay |
| 7 | Speedup accounting for latch overhead | $S_{real} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{orig}}{k + n - 1)(T_{stage} + \tau)}$ | The price of pipelining |
| 8 | CPI (ideal) | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | Achieved only with no stalls and no hazards |
| 9 | CPI (with stalls) | $CPI_{actual} = 1 + \text{stalls per instruction}$ | Each hazard inserts bubble cycles |
| 10 | Branch penalty (unconditional) | $P = b \cdot c$ | b = branch frequency, c = cycles wasted per branch |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pipelining is **not just an academic concept** — it is the heartbeat of every modern processor:
- **Intel/AMD x86 cores** use 14–20+ stage pipelines (e.g., Intel Golden Cove ~14 stages, AMD Zen 4 ~14 stages).
- **ARM Cortex-A series** uses 8–15 stage pipelines balancing power and performance.
- **GPU shader cores (NVIDIA, AMD)** use *deep* pipelines (20+ stages) to maximize throughput at the cost of latency — ideal for graphics workloads.
- **DSP chips (TI TMS320, Analog Devices SHARC)** use very deep pipelines (50+ stages) for signal processing.

The **trade-off** in engineering: deeper pipelines (larger *k*) ⇒ higher theoretical throughput but **higher branch penalty** and **higher latch overhead**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Speedup Equation for an Ideal k-Stage Pipeline

We want to prove that for *n* instructions executed on a *k*-stage pipeline with clock period *T*:

$$S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 1 — Time without pipelining (sequential).**
Each instruction must pass through all *k* stages sequentially without overlap. Therefore the total time for *n* instructions is:

$$T_{np} = n \cdot k \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Time with pipelining.**
The first instruction must traverse all *k* stages, taking *k* cycles. Every subsequent instruction "emerges" from the pipeline 1 cycle after the previous one. So the total cycles to complete all *n* instructions is:

$$T_{p} = k + (n - 1) = k + n - 1 \text{ cycles}$$

Converting to time units, multiply by *T*:

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Form the speedup ratio.**
By definition, Speedup is the ratio of the un-pipelined time to the pipelined time:

$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$S = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T}$$

The *T* cancels:

$$S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Examine the limit.**
As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the constant *k* becomes negligible:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{1 + \frac{k-1}{n}} = k$$

Hence, the **theoretical maximum speedup equals the number of pipeline stages** *k*, but this is approached only asymptotically as the instruction stream grows.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pure Speedup and Throughput (Classic KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** Consider a 5-stage pipeline (k = 5) with a clock period of 2 ns. Execute n = 8 instructions. Calculate (a) total time without pipelining, (b) total time with pipelining, (c) speedup, (d) throughput, (e) efficiency.

**Solution — Part (a): Total time without pipelining.**

$$T_{np} = n \cdot k \cdot T = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80 \text{ ns}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Total time with pipelining.**

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T = (5 + 8 - 1) \times 2 = 12 \times 2 = 24 \text{ ns}$$

**Solution — Part (c): Speedup.**

$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{80}{24} = 3.33$$

Cross-check using the formula:

$$S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \frac{8 \times 5}{5 + 8 - 1} = \frac{40}{12} = 3.33 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Solution — Part (d): Throughput.**

$$TP = \frac{n}{T_{p}/T} = \frac{8}{12} = 0.667 \text{ instructions/cycle}$$

Equivalently, in millions of instructions per second:

$$TP = \frac{1 \text{ inst}}{24 \text{ ns}} = \frac{1}{24 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 41.67 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Solution — Part (e): Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.33}{5} = 0.666 = 66.67\%$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Speedup Accounting for Pipeline Register Overhead

> **Problem:** A 6-stage pipeline has a stage delay of 8 ns. Each pipeline register introduces a 1 ns overhead. Compare the speedup with and without latch overhead for n = 100 instructions.

**Step 1 — Ideal (no overhead) time per stage:**

$$T_{ideal} = 8 \text{ ns}, \quad T_{np} = 100 \times 6 \times 8 = 4800 \text{ ns}, \quad T_{p} = (6 + 99) \times 8 = 840 \text{ ns}$$

$$S_{ideal} = \frac{4800}{840} \approx 5.71$$

**Step 2 — With latch overhead, the *clock period* becomes:**

$$T_{real} = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ ns}$$

The non-pipelined version does *not* add the latch (no inter-stage transfer for one instruction), so we use the original 8 ns for the serial comparison:

$$T_{p,real} = (6 + 99) \times 9 = 105 \times 9 = 945 \text{ ns}$$

$$S_{real} = \frac{4800}{945} \approx 5.08$$

**Step 3 — Compute the *overhead penalty*:**

$$\Delta S = 5.71 - 5.08 = 0.63$$

This demonstrates why modern deep pipelines use **very small, low-latency flip-flops** — every nanosecond of latch overhead directly erodes achievable speedup.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Data Hazard Detection (RAW)

> **Problem:** Given the following instruction sequence on a 5-stage pipeline without forwarding:
> ```
> I1: ADD  R1, R2, R3     ; R1 = R2 + R3
> I2: SUB  R4, R1, R5     ; R4 = R1 - R5
> ```
> How many stall cycles are required, and in which stage?

**Analysis:** I2 needs R1, but R1 is **written** by I1 in the **WB stage** (cycle 5) and is **read** by I2 in the **ID stage** (cycle 3 of I2).

If I1 enters IF at cycle 1, it reaches WB at cycle 5 and writes R1 at the *end* of cycle 5. I2, if it entered IF at cycle 2 without stalls, would be in ID at cycle 3 — too early.

**Bubbles needed:** I2's ID must be delayed until cycle 6. That means we must insert **2 stall (NOP) bubbles** (cycles 4 and 5 of I2's perspective).

**With forwarding (EX-to-EX bypass):** The result of I1's ALU is available at the *end* of its EX stage (cycle 3). I2 reaches its EX stage at cycle 4. With a forwarding path from EX/MEM register to the ALU input, the bubble count drops to **zero** — illustrating the value of forwarding hardware.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Types and Standard Solutions

| Hazard Type | Definition | Example | Standard KTU Solution | Hardware / Software Cost |
|-------------|-----------|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Structural** | Two instructions need same HW in same cycle | Single memory for IF and MEM | Duplicate resources (Harvard: separate I-cache and D-cache) | Area |
| **Data (RAW)** | Read After Write — true dependency | `ADD R1,R2,R3` followed by `SUB R4,R1,R5` | Forwarding (bypassing) + pipeline interlocking | Bypass muxes, control logic |
| **Data (WAR)** | Write After Read — anti-dependency | Limited in in-order pipelines; arises in OoO | Register renaming | Rename table |
| **Data (WAW)** | Write After Write — output dependency | Same as WAR; in-order pipelines avoid it | Register renaming | Rename table |
| **Control** | PC decision not known early | Conditional branch | Branch prediction, delay slot, early branch resolution | BTB, predictor tables |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-Stage Pipeline Block Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the structural flow of a classic RISC 5-stage pipeline. Each rectangular block represents a combinational stage; the latched registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) sit between stages and hold intermediate state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IF: INSTRUCTION FETCH\nPC, IMEM, +4"] --> B["IF-ID LATCH\nNPC, IR"]
    B --> C["ID: INSTRUCTION DECODE\nREGISTER READ, SIGN EXTEND"]
    C --> D["ID-EX LATCH\nA, B, IMM, RD, OPCODE"]
    D --> E["EX: EXECUTE / ADDRESS CALC\nALU, ADDER, FORWARD MUX"]
    E --> F["EX-MEM LATCH\nALUOUT, B, RD"]
    F --> G["MEM: MEMORY ACCESS\nDMEM READ/WRITE"]
    G --> H["MEM-WB LATCH\nALUOUT, LMD, RD"]
    H --> I["WB: WRITE BACK\nREGFILE WRITE"]

    MUX["FORWARDING MUX A and B"] --> E
    F --> MUX
    H --> MUX

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style I fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
    style MUX fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#d63384
```

### 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (Gantt-style) — 8 Instructions Through 5-Stage Pipeline

The next diagram shows **instruction-level timing** for 8 instructions (I1 through I8) on a 5-stage pipeline. Notice that during cycles 6 through 12, **all 5 stages are simultaneously active** — this is the *steady state* of the pipeline, where one instruction completes every cycle.

```mermaid
gantt
    title 5-Stage Pipeline Timing for 8 Instructions
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat C%S
    section I1
    IF1 :a1, 1, 1
    ID1 :a2, 2, 1
    EX1 :a3, 3, 1
    MEM1 :a4, 4, 1
    WB1 :a5, 5, 1
    section I2
    IF2 :b1, 2, 1
    ID2 :b2, 3, 1
    EX2 :b3, 4, 1
    MEM2 :b4, 5, 1
    WB2 :b5, 6, 1
    section I3
    IF3 :c1, 3, 1
    ID3 :c2, 4, 1
    EX3 :c3, 5, 1
    MEM3 :c4, 6, 1
    WB3 :c5, 7, 1
    section I4
    IF4 :d1, 4, 1
    ID4 :d2, 5, 1
    EX4 :d3, 6, 1
    MEM4 :d4, 7, 1
    WB4 :d5, 8, 1
    section I5
    IF5 :e1, 5, 1
    ID5 :e2, 6, 1
    EX5 :e3, 7, 1
    MEM5 :e4, 8, 1
    WB5 :e5, 9, 1
    section I6
    IF6 :f1, 6, 1
    ID6 :f2, 7, 1
    EX6 :f3, 8, 1
    MEM6 :f4, 9, 1
    WB6 :f5, 10, 1
    section I7
    IF7 :g1, 7, 1
    ID7 :g2, 8, 1
    EX7 :g3, 9, 1
    MEM7 :g4, 10, 1
    WB7 :g5, 11, 1
    section I8
    IF8 :h1, 8, 1
    ID8 :h2, 9, 1
    EX8 :h3, 10, 1
    MEM8 :h4, 11, 1
    WB8 :h5, 12, 1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Cycle **1 to 5** = *fill phase* (pipeline drains in).
- Cycle **5 to 12** = *drain phase* (pipeline flushes out, with 5 instructions in flight).
- **Total cycles** = 5 + 8 − 1 = **12** ✓ (matches the formula).

### 4.3 Data Hazard Resolution Flow (Forwarding Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Instruction Entering EX"] --> Q1{"Operand source?\nForward or Register"}
    Q1 -- "EX hazard" --> R1["Forward from EX/MEM latch\n(previously in MEM)"]
    Q1 -- "MEM hazard" --> R2["Forward from MEM/WB latch\n(previously in WB)"]
    Q1 -- "No hazard" --> R3["Read from Register File"]
    R1 --> S1["Use forwarded value as ALU input"]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> T1["Proceed to MEM stage"]
    T1 --> U1{"Stall needed?\nLoad-use or multi-cycle latency"}
    U1 -- "Yes" --> V1["Insert bubble / NOP cycle"]
    U1 -- "No" --> W1["Continue normal flow"]
    V1 --> W1
```

### 4.4 Branch Handling Strategies — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Branch instruction\nreaches ID stage"] --> B2{"Branch outcome\nknown at ID?\n(computed early)"}
    B2 -- "Yes" --> B3["1-cycle branch penalty\nor zero with prediction"]
    B2 -- "No - resolved at EX" --> B4["2-cycle branch penalty\n(fetch is speculative)"]
    B2 -- "Resolved at MEM" --> B5["3-cycle branch penalty"]
    B4 --> B6["Mitigation: Branch Prediction"]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7["Static prediction\n(Always Taken / BTFN)"]
    B6 --> B8["Dynamic prediction\n(1-bit, 2-bit, BTB, Gshare)"]
    B6 --> B9["Delayed Branch\n(compiler reorders)"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define pipelining. Mention any two advantages of a pipelined processor over a non-pipelined one.

**Model Answer:**
Pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the instruction processing into a sequence of independent stages separated by pipeline registers. Each stage performs a portion of the work, and a new instruction enters the pipeline each clock cycle once it is filled.

*Advantages:*
1. **Higher throughput** — once the pipeline is full, one instruction completes per clock cycle, dramatically increasing instructions-per-second.
2. **Lower average execution time per instruction** — the latency of a single instruction may remain the same, but the *amortized* time is much smaller, improving speedup proportional to the number of stages.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is a pipeline hazard? List the three principal types of hazards with a one-line definition of each.

**Model Answer:**
A pipeline hazard is any condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction from executing during its designated clock cycle, thereby stalling the pipeline.

*Three principal types:*
1. **Structural hazard** — two instructions in the pipeline require the same hardware resource in the same cycle.
2. **Data hazard** — an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that has not yet completed (RAW, WAR, or WAW).
3. **Control hazard** — caused by branch and jump instructions, where the next PC is not known until late in the pipeline.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE, Module 2 questions appear in **Part B** as either Q11 or Q12 (internal choice between them). Either Q11 *or* Q12 will be on Pipelining. The marks split is typically **7 + 7** across sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 4-stage pipeline has stage delays of 5 ns, 7 ns, 6 ns, and 4 ns respectively. Calculate the clock period, total time to execute 20 instructions, speedup over a non-pipelined implementation, throughput, and efficiency. Assume a latch overhead of 1 ns per stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the data forwarding (bypassing) technique used to mitigate RAW data hazards in a 5-stage pipeline. Show clearly how the ALU result of one instruction is forwarded to the ALU input of a dependent instruction.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Determine the clock period.**
The clock period is set by the **slowest stage plus the latch overhead**.

$$T_{stage,max} = \max(5, 7, 6, 4) = 7 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{clock} = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Total time for 20 instructions in the pipeline.**

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clock} = (4 + 20 - 1) \times 8 = 23 \times 8 = 184 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Time without pipelining (sequential, no latch overhead, same stage delays).**
Each instruction takes the sum of all stage delays:

$$T_{inst} = 5 + 7 + 6 + 4 = 22 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{np} = n \cdot T_{inst} = 20 \times 22 = 440 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup.**

$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{440}{184} \approx 2.39$$

**Step 5 — Throughput.**

$$TP = \frac{n}{k + n - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{T_{clock}} = \frac{20}{23} \cdot \frac{1}{8 \text{ ns}} \approx 0.1087 \text{ inst/ns} \approx 108.7 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{2.39}{4} \approx 0.598 = 59.8\%$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Data Forwarding (Bypassing) — Explanation:**

In a 5-stage RISC pipeline, an instruction such as `ADD R1, R2, R3` produces its result at the **end of the EX stage (cycle 3)**. Without forwarding, a dependent instruction like `SUB R4, R1, R5` could not read R1 from the register file until **cycle 5** (after the WB stage of `ADD`), forcing **2 stall cycles**.

**With forwarding**, a hardware path is added that **bypasses the register file** and routes the freshly-computed ALU output back to the ALU input of the next instruction. Two forwarding paths are used:

1. **EX-to-EX forwarding:** The ALU output from the EX/MEM pipeline register (containing the result of an instruction that completed EX one cycle earlier) is fed directly to the ALU input multiplexers of the instruction currently in the EX stage.
2. **MEM-to-EX forwarding:** The value held in the MEM/WB pipeline register (the result of an instruction in WB) is also fed to the same forwarding muxes, for cases where the producer completed EX two cycles ago.

**Working with the canonical example:**

```
Cycle:    1    2    3    4    5    6
I1: ADD   IF   ID   EX   MEM  WB
I2: SUB        IF   ID   EX   MEM  WB
```

At **cycle 4**, I2 is in EX. I1's ALU result is sitting in the **EX/MEM latch** (produced at the end of cycle 3). The forwarding unit detects that I2's source register `R1` matches the destination of I1, and selects the forwarded value through the ALU input mux — **zero stalls**.

**Condition code for forwarding:**

```
if (EX/MEM.RegWrite
    and EX/MEM.RegisterRd != 0
    and EX/MEM.RegisterRd == ID/EX.RegisterRs)
    ForwardA = 10  // from EX/MEM
```

Similar conditions handle the MEM/WB source and the `RegisterRt` operand.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a 6-stage pipeline processing 100 instructions. The pipeline is *unbalanced* — the slowest stage takes 6 ns and the average stage takes 4 ns. The latch overhead is 1 ns. Compute (i) the effective clock period, (ii) total execution time with pipelining, (iii) speedup over a single-cycle implementation that takes 24 ns per instruction, and (iv) the throughput in MIPS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three techniques for handling control hazards in a pipeline: **static branch prediction, dynamic branch prediction, and delayed branch**. Compare their relative effectiveness and hardware cost.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Step (i) — Effective clock period.**
The clock period is determined by the *slowest* stage plus the latch overhead:

$$T_{clock} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ ns}$$

(Note: the average stage time of 4 ns is irrelevant for clock period — pipelining uses the *bottleneck* stage.)

**Step (ii) — Total time with pipelining (k = 6, n = 100).**

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clock} = (6 + 100 - 1) \times 7 = 105 \times 7 = 735 \text{ ns}$$

**Step (iii) — Speedup over single-cycle.**
Single-cycle per-instruction time = 24 ns. Total non-pipelined time:

$$T_{np} = 100 \times 24 = 2400 \text{ ns}$$

$$S = \frac{2400}{735} \approx 3.27$$

**Step (iv) — Throughput.**

$$TP = \frac{100}{735 \text{ ns}} = 0.1361 \text{ inst/ns} = 136.1 \text{ MIPS}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Technique 1 — Static Branch Prediction:**
The compiler or hardware uses a *fixed* rule to predict every branch. Common rules include:
- **Predict Taken:** assume the branch will be taken.
- **Predict Not-Taken (NT):** assume the branch falls through.
- **Backward Taken, Forward Not-Taken (BTFN):** predict loop-back branches as taken, forward branches as not taken.

*Hardware cost:* minimal — just a comparator and a multiplexer.
*Effectiveness:* around 60–70% accuracy on typical programs; can reach 85% with BTFN for loops.

**Technique 2 — Dynamic Branch Prediction:**
A **Branch History Table (BHT)** stores recent outcomes of each branch. A **2-bit saturating counter** is associated with each branch:
- States: Strongly Taken (ST), Weakly Taken (WT), Weakly Not-Taken (WNT), Strongly Not-Taken (SNT).
- On a misprediction, the counter is decremented; on a correct prediction, incremented (with saturation).

More advanced schemes use **two-level adaptive predictors** and **branch target buffers (BTB)** that cache target addresses.

*Hardware cost:* significant — kilobytes of SRAM, update logic, and indexing hardware.
*Effectiveness:* 90–95% accuracy on real workloads.

**Technique 3 — Delayed Branch:**
A *compiler* technique where the instruction immediately following a branch is always executed (it is the *delay slot*). The compiler tries to fill the delay slot with:
1. An instruction from *before* the branch (always safe).
2. An instruction from the branch *target* (safe only if branch is taken).
3. A **NOP** if neither is possible.

*Hardware cost:* zero — purely a compiler technique.
*Effectiveness:* eliminates branch penalty only if the delay slot is filled, which modern compilers achieve ~50–70% of the time.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Technique | Hardware Cost | Prediction Accuracy | Penalty Eliminated |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Static (NT/Taken) | Minimal | 60–85% | Partial |
| Dynamic (BHT) | High | 90–95% | Most |
| Delayed Branch | Zero (compiler only) | 100% for filled slot | When fillable |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in pipelining numericals (verified against KTU 2024 scheme answer key patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting latch overhead** — Many students compute clock period using only the stage delay and lose 1–2 marks. *Always* add the latch (pipeline register) delay.
> 2. **Confusing `T_p` formula variants** — The correct formula is $(k + n - 1) \cdot T$, not $k \cdot n \cdot T$. Writing $T_p = k \cdot n \cdot T$ is the *non-pipelined* formula and earns **zero** credit in KTU ESE.
> 3. **Ignoring that throughput is `1 / T_clock`, not `1 / T_p`** — The throughput *per cycle* is $\frac{n}{k + n - 1}$ inst/cycle; the *time-based* throughput (MIPS) requires dividing by the actual clock period.
> 4. **Mixing up speedup limits** — Writing "speedup = k" without the limit $n \rightarrow \infty$ qualifier is incomplete.
> 5. **Forgetting the +1 (or −1) in the speedup formula** — A very common arithmetic slip. Cross-check by plugging n = 1: speedup should be 1.
> 6. **Miscounting pipeline stages** — In a 5-stage pipeline, *k = 5*, not 4. Count the latches correctly: IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB = 4 latches, **5 stages**.
> 7. **Data hazard → control hazard confusion** — RAW is a *data* hazard, not a control hazard. Examiners deduct full marks for this swap.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for Pipelining Implementation (KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2):**

- ☐ Pipelining is an *implementation technique*, not a new ISA — same instruction set, different execution strategy.
- ☐ **Total pipelined cycles** = $k + n - 1$ (first instruction takes *k* cycles; each subsequent one takes 1 cycle).
- ☐ **Speedup** = $\frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$; approaches *k* only when $n \gg k$.
- ☐ **Efficiency** = speedup / k = $\frac{n}{k(k + n - 1)}$; approaches 1 (100%) asymptotically.
- ☐ **Clock period** = max stage delay + latch overhead; *unbalanced* stages hurt pipeline efficiency because the slowest stage sets the pace.
- ☐ **Throughput** in MIPS = $\frac{1}{T_{clock}} \times 10^{-6}$ (for ideal pipeline with CPI = 1).
- ☐ **Hazards** — Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control.
- ☐ **Solutions** — Forwarding (bypassing) for RAW, resource duplication for structural, branch prediction / delay slots for control.
- ☐ **Pipeline registers (latches)** are *not free* — they introduce delay and area overhead.
- ☐ **Deeper pipeline** = higher theoretical throughput, but higher branch penalty and latch overhead — there is an *optimal* depth (typically 8–15 stages in commercial CPUs).
- ☐ For KTU problems, **always state the formula you are using** before substituting values — partial credit is given for the formula, even if arithmetic errors follow.
- ☐ Practice at least **3 numericals** covering: speedup calculation, unbalanced pipeline with latch overhead, and data-hazard stall counting.
- ☐ Memorize the **5-stage RISC pipeline (IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB)** names and their functions — this is the most-frequently-asked diagram.
- ☐ Know the **two forwarding paths** (EX-to-EX and MEM-to-EX) and the **detection condition** using pipeline register fields (`RegisterRd`, `RegWrite`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RISC V Integer Pipeline to Handle Multicycle Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RISC V Integer Pipeline to Handle Multicycle Operations**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multicycle Operations in a RISC-V Integer Pipeline** are arithmetic/logic operations whose latency exceeds **one clock cycle** in the Execute (EX) stage, thereby forcing the pipeline to **stall subsequent dependent instructions** until the operation completes. The standard RISC-V approach (Patterson & Hennessy) handles such operations by **holding the issuing instruction in the EX stage for multiple cycles**, inserting pipeline bubbles (NOPs) via the **Hazard Detection and Forwarding Unit (HDF)**, and forwarding the final result to dependent instructions in the cycle immediately preceding **Write-Back (WB)**.

**Standard RISC-V 5-Stage Pipeline (Baseline):**

| Stage | Full Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IF** | Instruction Fetch | Fetch instruction from I-cache using PC |
| **ID** | Instruction Decode / Register Read | Decode opcode, read source registers |
| **EX** | Execute / Address Calculation | ALU operation, branch target calculation |
| **MEM** | Memory Access | Load/Store data from/to D-cache |
| **WB** | Write Back | Write result back to register file |

In the **baseline pipeline**, every instruction (arithmetic, logical, memory, branch) completes the EX stage in **exactly one cycle**. However, RISC-V includes instructions whose integer realization requires multiple internal iterations (e.g., `MUL`, `MULH`, `DIV`, `REM`, `DIVU`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "One-Door-at-a-Time" Analogy**
> Imagine a **5-station assembly line** (IF→ID→EX→MEM→WB) where every product (instruction) normally spends exactly **1 minute** at each station, finishing in 5 minutes. Now introduce a **"delicate" product** — say, a custom-painted car — that needs **20 minutes** in the painting station (EX). You have two choices:
> 1. **Stop the whole line** (pipeline stall) — the next 19 cars wait, but the assembly line logic remains trivial.
> 2. **Build a separate side-bay** (out-of-order execution, complex) — faster, but needs extra coordination.
>
> RISC-V's textbook approach uses **Choice 1 (Stall-and-Forward)**: the painting station is "occupied" for many cycles, the upstream station (ID) keeps producing new items, but they **park in a holding zone** (the ID/EX pipeline register is held) until EX frees up.

**Why does this matter?**
- A naive pipeline would produce **wrong results** if a `MUL` issued in cycle 1 and a dependent `ADD` issued in cycle 2 both tried to write the EX stage simultaneously (a **structural hazard**).
- RISC-V solves this by **freezing** the IF/ID and ID/EX registers, effectively inserting **bubbles (NOPs)** into the pipeline.

## 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Latencies (RISC-V Integer RV64IM, Reference textbook)**
> - **Standard ALU op (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, SLL, SRL, SRA):** **1 cycle** in EX
> - **Integer Multiply (MUL, MULH, MULHU, MULHSU):** typically **3 to 5 cycles**
> - **Integer Divide (DIV, DIVU, REM, REMU):** typically **20 to 40 cycles** (radix-2 non-restoring division)
> - **Load Word (LD):** 1 cycle EX + 1 cycle MEM = effective 2-cycle latency
> - **Store Word (SD):** 1 cycle EX + 1 cycle MEM
> - **Branch (BEQ, BNE, etc.):** 1 cycle EX (resolved in EX, not MEM)

**Pipeline Depth:** $D = 5$ stages.
**Ideal CPI (no hazards):** $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Multicycle Operations Break the Simple Pipeline

The base 5-stage RISC-V pipeline assumes **every instruction is fully complete in the EX stage by the rising clock edge** of the cycle following its entry. When this assumption is violated (multicycle operation), three problems emerge:

1. **Structural Hazard**: The EX stage is "busy" for multiple cycles. A new instruction entering EX would overwrite the unfinished operation.
2. **Write-Back Timing**: The result of the multicycle operation is known only at the **end of the last EX cycle**, not the first. The WB stage must be delayed accordingly.
3. **Forwarding Timing**: Forwarding must occur from the **correct intermediate cycle** of the multicycle operation, not just from the EX/MEM or MEM/WB boundary as in the simple pipeline.

## 2.2 Operational Strategy: Stall-and-Forward (RISC-V Textbook Approach)

The RISC-V textbook (Patterson & Hennessy, RISC-V Edition, Chapter 4, Section 4.10) adopts a **conservative but hardware-simple** strategy:

### Logic Steps (Bulleted, Exam-Ready):

1. **Freeze the pipeline upstream of EX** when a multicycle instruction occupies EX.
   - The **Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)** asserts the PCWrite and IF/ID-Write enable signals LOW, preventing the IF and ID stages from advancing.
   - The **ID/EX register is cleared to all zeros** (a `nop` / `0000000...00000` instruction) in every "stall cycle", effectively injecting a **bubble** into the EX stage.

2. **Hold the multicycle instruction in EX** for the required number of cycles.
   - The EX stage contains a **dedicated Multiplication/Division Unit (MDU)** that performs the iterative computation.
   - The instruction's opcode and operands remain latched in the EX-stage internal registers.

3. **Forward the result** to dependent instructions in the cycle just before the multicycle unit writes back.
   - The **Forwarding Unit** is extended with additional multiplexers to read the result from the **output of the MDU**, not from the EX/MEM pipeline register.
   - In the **last cycle of EX**, the result is simultaneously: (a) forwarded to any waiting instruction in EX, and (b) routed to MEM/WB for the WB stage.

4. **Perform Write-Back** in the cycle immediately following the final EX cycle.
   - The destination register write occurs one cycle later than for a single-cycle ALU op.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Pipeline Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}}$ | Instructions completed per second |
| 2 | **Ideal Speedup** | $S_{\text{ideal}} = D = 5$ | For a 5-stage pipeline vs. non-pipelined |
| 3 | **Actual Speedup** | $S = \dfrac{D \times \text{CPI}_{\text{non-pipe}}}{1 + \text{Stall Cycles}}$ | Includes stall penalty |
| 4 | **Effective CPI** | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \sum_{i} (\text{Freq}_i \times \text{Stall}_i)$ | Weighted average of stalls |
| 5 | **Stall Cycles for Multicycle Op** | $\text{Stall}_{\text{dep}} = L_{\text{op}} - 1$ | Per dependent instruction (if no forwarding) |
| 6 | **Stall Cycles for Multicycle Op (with forwarding)** | $\text{Stall}_{\text{dep}} = L_{\text{op}} - 2$ | Per dependent instruction, when result is forwarded from the penultimate cycle |
| 7 | **Total Stall Bubbles** | $N_{\text{bubbles}} = (L_{\text{op}} - 1) \times N_{\text{dep}}$ | $L_{\text{op}}$ = latency of multicycle op, $N_{\text{dep}}$ = # of dependent instructions |
| 8 | **Pipeline Drain Time** | $T_{\text{drain}} = D \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | After last instruction enters, 5 more cycles to drain |
| 9 | **Total Execution Time** | $T_{\text{exec}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | For $N_{\text{inst}}$ instructions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latency Definitions (KTU Convention)**
> - **Latency ($L_{\text{op}}$):** Number of clock cycles from the moment the instruction enters EX until the result is available for forwarding.
> - **Repeat Interval / Throughput Latency:** Minimum number of cycles between two **independent** uses of the same multicycle unit (usually 1 cycle, since the unit is non-blocking once the first op enters).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real silicon (e.g., SiFive cores, BOOM, Rocket Chip, RISC-V cores from Andes, Ventana), the textbook's simple stall-and-forward is rarely used for high-performance cores. Instead, designers use:

- **Pipelined Multipliers** (latency = 1–2 cycles, throughput = 1 op/cycle).
- **Iterative Dividers** with reservation station semantics.
- **Out-of-Order Issue** to hide long latencies (BOOM, XiangShan).

However, the **stall-and-forward model is the cornerstone of all KTU/University exams** because it teaches the **fundamental hazard mechanics** without requiring Tomasulo's algorithm or reorder buffers.

**Industries/Applications:** Embedded controllers (PULPino, SiFive E2-series), IoT edge nodes, real-time MCUs, and educational cores all use variations of this approach.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Stall Cycle Calculation for a Single DIV followed by a dependent ADD

> **Problem:** Consider the following RISC-V instruction sequence. The DIV instruction has a latency of **20 cycles** in EX. Calculate (a) the number of stall bubbles inserted, and (b) the total cycles to execute this 4-instruction sequence from IF of the first instruction to WB of the last.

```
I1:  DIV  x5, x10, x11        # 5 = 10 / 11   (latency 20 cycles)
I2:  ADD  x6, x5, x12         # 6 = 5 + 12   (depends on x5 from I1)
I3:  SUB  x7, x6, x13         # 7 = 6 - 13   (depends on x6 from I2)
I4:  AND  x8, x7, x14         # 8 = 7 & 14   (depends on x7 from I3)
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Forwarding Path: EX-to-EX, last-but-one cycle):

**Step 1 — Establish baseline cycles without any stall:**

For a 4-instruction sequence in a 5-stage pipeline, the **last instruction (I4) completes WB at cycle**:

$$T_{\text{no-stall}} = N_{\text{inst}} + D - 1 = 4 + 5 - 1 = 8 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Compute stall cycles for I2 (depends on I1, multicycle DIV):**

DIV latency $L_{\text{DIV}} = 20$ cycles. The result of DIV is available at the **end of the 20th EX cycle** of I1. With forwarding from the penultimate cycle:

$$\text{Stall}_{I2} = L_{\text{DIV}} - 2 = 20 - 2 = 18 \text{ bubbles}$$

(I1's EX starts in cycle 3; I1's EX ends in cycle 22; I2 can enter EX in cycle 23, but its ID needs x5 forwarded in cycle 22, so 18 NOPs in EX between I1's entry and I2's entry.)

**Step 3 — Compute stall cycles for I3 (depends on I2, regular ADD with 1-cycle latency):**

ADD latency $L_{\text{ADD}} = 1$ cycle. With standard EX-to-EX forwarding from MEM/WB boundary or EX/MEM:

$$\text{Stall}_{I3} = L_{\text{ADD}} - 2 = 1 - 2 = -1 \Rightarrow 0 \text{ bubbles}$$

(Negative stall means no stall needed — the dependent ADD gets the result via forwarding from the MEM/WB register of the previous instruction.)

**Step 4 — Compute stall cycles for I4 (depends on I3, regular SUB):**
