**Step 4 — Required average inter-refresh interval.**

$$
t_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{REF}}{N_{rows}}
$$

> **Worked example:** A chip with $N_{rows} = 8192$ rows and $t_{RFC} = 0.5\ \mu s$:
> $$
> t_{refresh,total} = 8192 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 4.096\ \text{ms}
> $$
> $$
> OH_{\%} = \dfrac{4.096}{64} \times 100 = 6.4\ \%
> $$
> $$
> t_{avg} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{8192} = 7.8125\ \mu s
> $$

So the controller must issue one refresh command every **7.81 µs** on average; the DRAM spends **~6.4 %** of its time refreshing.

## 3.4 Derivation — Row Cycle Time and Pipeline Cost

**Step 1 — Row access begins when RAS goes active.**
Data from a column appears after $t_{RAC}$ (row-access time, asynchronous) or $t_{CL}$ clocks (CAS latency, synchronous).

**Step 2 — Before the same bank can be re-accessed, the open row must be closed (precharged).**
The minimum time RAS must stay active is $t_{RAS}$; the minimum time between precharge and next activate is $t_{RP}$.

**Step 3 — Total row cycle time.**

$$
t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput on one bank (back-to-back accesses).**

$$
\text{Throughput}_{bank} = \dfrac{D}{t_{RC}} \quad \text{(bits/s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput aggregated across $B$ banks** (if controller can pipeline them):

$$
\text{Throughput}_{total} = \dfrac{B \times D}{t_{RC}}
$$

> **Worked example:** $D=64$ bits, $t_{RAS}=35$ ns, $t_{RP}=15$ ns, $B=8$:
> $$
> t_{RC} = 35 + 15 = 50\ \text{ns}
> $$
> $$
> \text{Throughput}_{total} = \dfrac{8 \times 64}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.024 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bit/s} = 10.24\ \text{Gbit/s}
> $$

## 3.5 Derivation — Synchronous (DDR) Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Single-beat transfer.**
On a clock of frequency $f_{CLK}$, a synchronous DRAM transfers one data beat (D bits) per clock cycle. DDR transfers on both edges, so two beats per clock.

**Step 2 — Bandwidth formula.**

$$
BW = f_{CLK} \times D \times 2 \quad \text{(DDR, bits/s)}
$$

For **quad-pumped (QDR)** the multiplier becomes 4.

> **Worked example:** DDR4-3200, $D=64$ bits, $f_{CLK}=1600$ MHz:
> $$
> BW = 1600 \times 10^{6} \times 64 \times 2 = 204.8 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bit/s} = 25.6\ \text{GB/s}
> $$

## 3.6 Derivation — Average Memory Access with Cache

If the CPU first looks in a cache with hit ratio $h$ and access time $t_{cache}$, falling through to DRAM with $t_{DRAM}$ on a miss:

$$
t_{avg} = h \cdot t_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot t_{DRAM}
$$

> **Worked example:** $h=0.95$, $t_{cache}=5$ ns, $t_{DRAM}=60$ ns:
> $$
> t_{avg} = 0.95 \times 5 + 0.05 \times 60 = 4.75 + 3.00 = 7.75\ \text{ns}
> $$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Why DRAM Dominates Main Memory

| Engineering Pressure | DRAM's Response | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Maximise density (bits / mm²) | 1T-1C cell, 8F² footprint | Slower than SRAM |
| Minimise cost per bit | Single transistor + trench/stacked capacitor | Refresh controller needed |
| Reduce I/O pin count | Address multiplexing (RAS/CAS) | Sequential addressing, slight latency |
| Reduce row-access time | Page-mode / burst / SDRAM pipeline | More complex controller |
| Reduce refresh penalty | Distributed refresh + auto-refresh command | Refresh commands consume bandwidth (~5–7 %) |
| Lower power (mobile) | Low-power DDR + partial-array self-refresh | Slightly slower than desktop DDR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DRAM Chip

The figure below maps the **signal flow** of a complete DRAM subsystem, from the CPU's address bus down to the sense amplifiers. It is rendered with Mermaid's flowchart engine (safe alphanumeric node IDs only).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Address Bus] --> B[Memory Controller MCH]
    A2[CPU Data Bus] --> B
    C[DRAM Controller] --> B
    B --> D[DRAM Chip Package]
    D --> E[Address Latch and Multiplexer]
    D --> F[Row Address Buffer]
    D --> G[Column Address Buffer]
    F --> H[Row Decoder X DEC]
    G --> I[Column Decoder Y DEC]
    E --> F
    E --> G
    H --> J[Memory Bank 0 Array 1T 1C cells]
    H --> K[Memory Bank 1 Array 1T 1C cells]
    H --> L[Memory Bank 2 Array 1T 1C cells]
    H --> M[Memory Bank 3 Array 1T 1C cells]
    J --> N[Sense Amplifier Block]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> I
    I --> O[Output Latch and Data Buffer]
    O --> P[Data Pins DQ 0 to DQ M-1]
    Q[Refresh Counter in Controller] --> C
    C --> D
    style B fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading guide:**
- **B** (Memory Controller Hub) takes the CPU's full address, splits it into row + column halves, and schedules bank + rank.
- **C** (DRAM Controller) issues ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, and REFRESH commands.
- **Q** (Refresh Counter) tracks which row is due for refresh next.
- **N** (Sense Amplifiers) perform the read and the implicit **restore** step that refreshes the row.

## 4.2 DRAM Read Cycle — Asynchronous Timing (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> RowActive: RAS goes LOW (row address latched)
    RowActive --> ColActive: CAS goes LOW (column address latched)
    ColActive --> SenseRestore: sense amplifier detects and amplifies differential
    SenseRestore --> DataOut: tRAC elapsed, data driven on DQ
    DataOut --> Precharge: RAS and CAS return HIGH
    Precharge --> Idle: tRP elapsed
    Idle --> Refresh: REFRESH command CBR
    Refresh --> Idle: tRFC elapsed
    Precharge --> Refresh: optional
    note right of SenseRestore: Destructive read. Sense amplifier rewrites cell.
    note left of Refresh: All banks precharged. Controller walks every row.
```

**Key timing parameters visualised on the timeline below.**

```mermaid
gantt
    title Asynchronous DRAM Read Cycle Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s ns
    section Control
    RAS LOW (tRAS window)        :a1, 0, 50
    CAS LOW (data on DQ)         :a2, 20, 30
    section Data
    Valid data on DQ bus         :crit, a3, 25, 25
    section Recovery
    Precharge window tRP         :a4, 50, 15
    Next RAS LOW tRC = 65 ns     :a5, 65, 50
```

> [!NOTE]
> On the gantt chart, $t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$. Two back-to-back reads to the **same bank** must be spaced by at least $t_{RC}$. Two reads to **different banks** can overlap — this is the principle of **bank-level parallelism**.

## 4.3 Refresh-Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> AutoRefresh: REF command from controller
    AutoRefresh --> AddressStep: internal counter increments row
    AddressStep --> AutoRefresh: more rows remain
    AutoRefresh --> SelfRefresh: idle for long period, enter low power
    SelfRefresh --> AutoRefresh: wake event, exit low power
    AutoRefresh --> Active: all rows refreshed in 64 ms
    note right of SelfRefresh: Used in laptops and phones to save power.
```

## 4.4 Memory-Controller Data Flow (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU Core] --> B[L1 Cache]
    B -- hit --> A
    B -- miss --> C[L2 Cache]
    C -- hit --> B
    C -- miss --> D[L3 Cache]
    D -- hit --> C
    D -- miss --> E[Memory Controller]
    E -- read command --> F[DRAM Rank 0]
    E -- read command --> G[DRAM Rank 1]
    F -- data --> E
    G -- data --> E
    E -- data --> D
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
```

> **Insight:** When the controller interleaves requests across **two ranks** on the same channel, the **effective bandwidth nearly doubles** because each rank can be precharging while the other is being read. This is the **memory-interleaving** technique you will meet in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write**
> 1. **Don't write "DRAM is volatile."** Every RAM is volatile. State *why* it is *dynamic* — the charge leaks and **refresh is required every 64 ms**.
> 2. **Always mention "destructive read + sense-amplifier restore"** when explaining a DRAM read. Marks are specifically awarded for this phrase.
> 3. **Address-multiplexed question trap:** students often forget the "+2" pins for RAS, CAS, WE, OE. Mention them.
> 4. **Refresh-overhead numerical:** the question will give $T_{REF}=64$ ms — use it; do not assume 16 ms.
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** refresh cycle time is in **µs**, memory clock period in **ns**. Convert before comparing.
> 6. **Timing-parameter numbers** ($t_{RAC}$, $t_{CAC}$, $t_{RCD}$, $t_{RP}$) are easy to confuse. Memorise that $t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain why DRAM is called *dynamic* memory. Why does it need periodic refresh? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> DRAM stores each bit as **electric charge on a tiny capacitor** inside a 1T-1C cell. Two physical phenomena cause this charge to leak away: (i) the **sub-threshold leakage current** of the access MOSFET even when it is OFF, and (ii) the **dielectric leakage** of the storage capacitor itself. After a few tens of milliseconds the cell voltage decays to an indeterminate level and the stored bit is lost.
>
> To retain the data, the memory controller must therefore **periodically read every row and rewrite it** (sense-amplifier restore). The JEDEC standard mandates that **every row must be refreshed at least once every 64 ms**. This periodic restoration is what gives the technology its name — **Dynamic** RAM.

**(3 marks — distribution: 1 mark "capacitor stores charge", 1 mark "leakage causes loss", 1 mark "refresh every 64 ms restores charge".)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the functions of a DRAM controller. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The DRAM controller is a dedicated hardware unit that:
> 1. **Multiplexes the address bus**, sending the row half on RAS and the column half on CAS.
> 2. **Issues timing commands** — ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE — at the correct intervals ($t_{RCD}$, $t_{RP}$, $t_{RAS}$, $t_{CL}$).
> 3. **Schedules refresh cycles** (auto-refresh commands) so that every row is refreshed at least once every 64 ms, often using a dedicated counter.
> 4. **Manages bank, rank, and channel interleaving** to maximise bandwidth.
> 5. **Performs read-write arbitration** and may queue requests (re-ordering them to minimise page misses).
> 6. **Performs error correction** (ECC) and reports correctable / uncorrectable errors to the OS.

**(3 marks — any three functions, 1 mark each.)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **14 marks**, split as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### (a) [7 marks]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the internal organisation of a DRAM chip. How is the address multiplexed using RAS and CAS lines? Why is this done? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Internal Organisation (4 marks)**
A modern DRAM chip contains the following blocks (refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1):

- **Address latch and multiplexer** — accepts half-width external address.
- **Row-address buffer** + **Row decoder (X-decoder)** — selects one of $2^R$ rows.
- **Column-address buffer** + **Column decoder (Y-decoder)** — selects one of $2^{C_{col}}$ columns.
- **Memory array** split into **multiple banks** (typically 4, 8, 16), each holding rows × columns of 1T-1C cells.
- **Sense amplifiers** — detect and amplify the small differential voltage on the bit lines.
- **I/O gating + data buffer** — route the selected column's bit to the external DQ pins.
- **Control logic** — interprets RAS, CAS, WE, CS, CKE and generates internal timing.
- **Refresh counter** — generates the next row address for auto-refresh.

**2. Address Multiplexing (2 marks)**
- External address pins = $n_{addr}/2$ (e.g., 13 pins for 26 address bits).
- Cycle begins: controller drives the **row address** onto the bus and pulses **RAS LOW** → row latched.
- After $t_{RCD}$, controller drives the **column address** and pulses **CAS LOW** → column latched.
- For a refresh-only cycle, the controller pulses **RAS LOW with CAS held HIGH** (or uses CBR auto-refresh in modern SDRAMs).

**3. Why Multiplex? (1 mark)**
Multiplexing **halves the number of address pins**, which are the most expensive part of an IC package. The cost is a slightly longer access time because the two address halves are sequential.

---

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A 256 Mbit DRAM chip is organised as 4 banks, each with 8192 rows and 8192 columns. The refresh cycle time is 0.5 µs per row and the standard refresh interval is 64 ms. Calculate: (i) the capacity per bank in MB, (ii) the total refresh overhead in %, and (iii) the average inter-refresh interval in µs. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total capacity $C = 256$ Mbit $= 256 \times 2^{20}$ bits.
- Banks $B = 4$, rows $R = 8192$, columns $C_{col} = 8192$.
- $t_{RFC} = 0.5\ \mu s$ per row, $T_{REF} = 64\ \text{ms}$.

**(i) Capacity per bank in MB:**

$$
C_{bank} = \dfrac{C}{B} = \dfrac{256 \times 2^{20}}{4}\ \text{bits} = 64 \times 2^{20}\ \text{bits} = 8\ \text{MB}
$$

**(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer with units.)**

**(ii) Total refresh overhead in %:**

$$
t_{refresh,total} = N_{rows} \times t_{RFC} = 8192 \times 0.5\ \mu s = 4096\ \mu s = 4.096\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
OH_{\%} = \dfrac{4.096\ \text{ms}}{64\ \text{ms}} \times 100 = 6.4\ \%
$$

**(2 marks — 1 mark computation, 1 mark final %.)**

**(iii) Average inter-refresh interval in µs:**

$$
t_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{REF}}{N_{rows}} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^{3}\ \mu s}{8192} = 7.8125\ \mu s
$$

**(2 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark numerical answer.)**

> **Verification (dimensional check):** $8192 \times 7.8125\ \mu s = 64\,000\ \mu s = 64\ \text{ms} = T_{REF}$. ✓

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Explain the read and write operations of a DRAM cell in detail, with a timing diagram. What is meant by "destructive read"? How is data integrity preserved? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Read Operation (3 marks):**
1. **Precharge** — bit lines BL and BL_bar are equalised to $V_{dd}/2$ to remove residual charge.
2. **Row activate (RAS LOW)** — the selected word line rises, connecting each cell's capacitor to its bit line. Charge sharing creates a small ΔV (positive for a stored **1**, negative for a stored **0**).
3. **Sense amplification** — the sense amplifier cross-coupled latch fires, amplifying ΔV to a full-rail logic level. The amplified value is also **driven back into the cell capacitor**, performing the implicit **restore** (this is what refreshes the row).
4. **Column select (CAS LOW)** — the Y-decoder connects the selected bit to the output buffer; data appears on the DQ pins after $t_{RAC}$.

**Write Operation (2 marks):**
1. Row is activated (same as read).
2. WE is asserted LOW together with CAS; the data to be written is driven onto the DQ pins and the I/O gating forces the new value onto the open bit line, **overpowering** the cell's stored charge and rewriting the capacitor.

**Destructive Read & Integrity (2 marks):**
- A read disturbs the cell's charge (charge sharing). The original value is **destroyed** if not restored.
- The sense amplifier's **automatic rewrite** (sense-amp restore) restores the value during every read, **so a read also serves as a refresh of the accessed row**.
- For rows that are never read, the controller must still issue **explicit refresh commands** (CBR auto-refresh) within the 64 ms window.

**Timing diagram (drawn in text):**

```
         ___      ___              ___
RAS  ____| |_____| |________________| |______
         ___                       ___
CAS  ________| |__________________| |________
                       ___         ___
DQ   __________________|X|_________|X|______
       ^precharge      ^data1     ^data2
       tRP             tRAC       tRC-tRAC
```

> Students are expected to label $t_{RAS}$, $t_{RP}$, $t_{RAC}$, and the data-valid window. Mentioning these four parameters in any order earns full marks.

---

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A computer system uses a DDR4-2400 memory module. The data-bus width is 64 bits and the memory clock is 1200 MHz (DDR transfers on both clock edges). Calculate: (i) the peak transfer rate in GB/s, and (ii) the time to transfer a 4 KB page. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 64$ bits, $f_{CLK} = 1200$ MHz, DDR = 2 transfers per clock.

**(i) Peak transfer rate in GB/s:**

$$
BW = f_{CLK} \times D \times 2 = 1200 \times 10^{6} \times 64 \times 2\ \text{bit/s}
$$

$$
BW = 153\,600 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bit/s} = 153.6 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bit/s}
$$

Converting to GB/s (divide by 8):

$$
BW = \dfrac{153.6}{8} = 19.2\ \text{GB/s}
$$

**(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark arithmetic, 1 mark correct unit GB/s.)**

**(ii) Time to transfer a 4 KB page:**

$$
\text{Data size} = 4\ \text{KB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 8 = 32\,768\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
t_{transfer} = \dfrac{\text{Data size}}{BW} = \dfrac{32\,768}{153.6 \times 10^{9}} = 2.133 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} \approx 213.3\ \text{ns}
$$

**(4 marks — 2 marks size conversion + formula, 2 marks numerical answer with units.)**

> **Examiner note:** the wording "peak transfer rate" is a cue that we ignore refresh, command, and address-bus overheads — give the **ideal theoretical** number.

---

## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — DRAM Memory System Organization**

- **Cell:** 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor, footprint ≈ **8 F²**.
- **Dynamic = leaky** → must refresh every **64 ms (JEDEC)**.
- **Read is destructive** → **sense-amplifier auto-restore** fixes it; same read also refreshes the open row.
- **Address multiplexed** in two halves via **RAS (row)** and **CAS (column)** → pin count halved.
- **DRAM controller** issues ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, REFRESH commands; also handles **bank interleaving** and **refresh scheduling**.
- **Three refresh strategies:** **Burst** (long block, CPU stalled), **Distributed** (one row every ~7.8 µs, **most common**), **Transparent** (during CPU cache-hit cycles).
- **CBR (CAS-Before-RAS) refresh** = standard auto-refresh command in SDRAMs.
- **Timing parameters to memorise:** $t_{RAC}$ (row-access), $t_{CAC}$ (column-access), $t_{RCD}$ (RAS-to-CAS delay), $t_{RP}$ (precharge), $t_{RAS}$ (row-active), $t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$.
- **Capacity formula:** $C = B \times 2^{R} \times 2^{C_{col}} \times D$ bits.
- **Refresh overhead %:** $\dfrac{N_{rows} \times t_{RFC}}{T_{REF}} \times 100 \approx 5\text{–}7\,\%$ for modern DDR4.
- **Average inter-refresh interval:** $T_{REF}/N_{rows}$ (e.g., 8 K rows → 7.8125 µs).
- **DDR transfers on both clock edges** → effective bandwidth $= 2 \times D \times f_{CLK}$.
- **Banks enable parallelism** — precharge one while reading another.
- **Why DRAM and not SRAM for main memory?** 4–6× higher density, lower cost/bit; cache uses SRAM because speed dominates.
- **Why not just refresh with software?** Refresh timing is sub-microsecond and must happen in a fixed 64 ms budget — only a hardware controller can do it reliably.
- **Memory wall concept** (Amdahl): even a 10× DRAM speedup yields only a small system speedup if most cycles hit cache — drives the modern **cache-centric** CPU design.

> **Mnemonic — "DRAM CAR RPS":** **D**estructive read, **R**efresh 64 ms, **A**ddress multiplex, **M**ultibank parallel, **C**ontroller-driven, **A**uto restore, **R**AS/CAS strobes, **R**ow cycle $t_{RC}=t_{RAS}+t_{RP}$, **P**recharge, **S**ense amplifier. (Keep this line in your exam-sheet margin!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Computer Organisation and Architecture (PCITT302)**, the term **"Basic Nomenclature"** refers to the standardized set of symbols, terms, and structural vocabulary used to describe the functional building blocks of a digital computer system. It defines how the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory Unit**, and **Input/Output (I/O) subsystems** interact through buses, registers, and control signals.

The primary nomenclature of a computer system consists of three structural pillars:

1. **Hardware Nomenclature** – The physical and logical components (Registers, ALU, Buses, Memory, Multiplexers, Decoders).
2. **Data Nomenclature** – The classification of data types (Operands, Instructions, Addresses, Control Words).
3. **Control Nomenclature** – The vocabulary of control signals and timing logic (Clock, T-states, Micro-operations, Bus Arbitration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The nomenclature forms the **linguistic foundation** required to understand Register Transfer Language (RTL), Micro-operations, and Bus System design. All higher-level topics in Module 3 are built upon these definitions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **computer system as a large modern kitchen**:

| Computer Term | Kitchen Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | The Head Chef | Makes all the decisions and performs calculations. |
| **Registers** | The Chef's Hands | Fastest, smallest workspace — holds items currently in use. |
| **ALU** | The Cutting Knife & Stove | Performs the actual "cooking" (arithmetic/logic operations). |
| **Main Memory (RAM)** | The Refrigerator | Stores ingredients (data) that the chef is working with today. |
| **Buses** | The Serving Counter / Conveyor Belt | The pathway for moving ingredients between chef, fridge, and stove. |
| **Control Unit** | The Recipe Book / Timer | Directs the chef on *what* to do and *when* to do it. |
| **Clock** | The Kitchen Metronome | Synchronizes every action to a steady beat. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nomenclature Matters:**
> Without a universal vocabulary, engineers cannot communicate hardware behavior. Every "register," "bus," and "micro-operation" has a *precise* meaning — misnaming them leads to design errors.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Logical Metrics

The following standardized metrics are used throughout Module 3:

- **Word Length** $\rightarrow$ Typically **16, 32, or 64 bits** in modern systems.
- **Address Space** $\rightarrow$ Determined by address bus width (e.g., $n$ address lines $\rightarrow 2^n$ locations).
- **Memory Capacity** $\rightarrow$ Measured in **Bytes (B), Kilobytes (KB), Megabytes (MB), Gigabytes (GB)**.
- **Bus Width** $\rightarrow$ Number of parallel data/address lines.
- **Clock Frequency** $\rightarrow$ Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — typically **GHz** in modern processors.
- **T-State / Clock Cycle Time** $\rightarrow$ The fundamental unit of timing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Nomenclature Components

### 2.1.1 Processor Nomenclature
The **CPU** itself is composed of sub-units, each with a strict nomenclature:

- **Accumulator (AC / ACC):** A special-purpose register that implicitly holds one of the operands and the result of ALU operations.
- **Program Counter (PC):** A register that holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched.
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the **currently executing instruction** after it is fetched from memory.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR):** A register that holds the **memory address** for a read/write operation.
- **Memory Data Register (MDR) / Memory Buffer Register (MBR):** Holds the **data** being transferred to/from memory.
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** The combinational circuit that executes arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logic (AND, OR, NOT) operations.
- **Control Unit (CU):** Generates the **control signals** that orchestrate the entire system.
- **General Purpose Registers (GPRs):** A small, fast on-chip memory (e.g., R0, R1, ..., R7) used for temporary storage.

### 2.1.2 Bus Nomenclature
A **bus** is a shared communication pathway. There are three primary bus types:

- **Data Bus:** Bi-directional; carries the **actual data/operands** between CPU, memory, and I/O. Width $\equiv$ word length.
- **Address Bus:** Uni-directional (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O); carries the **address** of the memory location. Width $\equiv$ address space.
- **Control Bus:** Carries **command and timing signals** (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock, Reset).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Bus System:** A standardized architecture where all registers are connected to a *single* shared bus through tri-state buffers. This minimizes wiring but requires sequential access.

### 2.1.3 Micro-operation Nomenclature
A **micro-operation** is the most basic, indivisible operation that can be performed by the CPU in a single clock cycle.

- **Register Transfer Micro-operation:** $\text{R2} \leftarrow \text{R1}$ (moves data between registers).
- **Arithmetic Micro-operation:** $\text{R3} \leftarrow \text{R1} + \text{R2}$.
- **Logic Micro-operation:** $\text{R4} \leftarrow \text{R1} \wedge \text{R2}$ (bitwise AND).
- **Shift Micro-operation:** $\text{R2} \leftarrow \text{shr R2}$ (shift right).
- **Memory Micro-operation:** $\text{M} [\text{MAR}] \leftarrow \text{MDR}$ (write to memory).

### 2.1.4 Memory Nomenclature
- **Word:** A group of bits (typically 8, 16, 32, or 64) treated as a single unit.
- **Byte:** Universally **8 bits** (standardized).
- **Nibble:** **4 bits** (half a byte).
- **Memory Address:** A unique number assigned to each memory location.
- **Memory Capacity Formula:** $\text{Capacity} = 2^n \times W$ where $n$ is the number of address lines and $W$ is the word length in bits.

### 2.1.5 Instruction Format Nomenclature
An **instruction** is divided into fields:
- **Opcode (Operation Code):** Specifies the operation to be performed.
- **Address Field:** Specifies the operand location.
- **Mode Field:** Specifies the addressing mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-, Two-, One-, and Zero-Address Instructions:** Classified by the number of explicit operand addresses in the instruction. Stack-based architectures use **zero-address** instructions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory Capacity (bits)** | $C = 2^n \times W$ | $n$ = address lines, $W$ = word length (bits) |
| 2 | **Memory Capacity (bytes)** | $C_{B} = \dfrac{2^n \times W}{8}$ | Bytes |
| 3 | **Address Space Size** | $\text{AS} = 2^n$ | Unique addresses |
| 4 | **Clock Cycle Time** | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Seconds ($f$ in Hz) |
| 5 | **Instruction Cycle Time** | $T_{\text{inst}} = T_{F} + T_{D} + T_{E}$ | Fetch + Decode + Execute |
| 6 | **Bus Bandwidth** | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{W \times f}{N_{\text{cycles}}}$ | Bytes/sec |
| 7 | **Byte Size** | $1 \text{ Byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$ | Universal standard |
| 8 | **Nibble Size** | $1 \text{ Nibble} = 4 \text{ bits}$ | Half-byte |
| 9 | **Word Length** | Typically $8/16/32/64$ bits | System-defined |
| 10 | **Decimal to Binary (MSB)** | $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Positional notation |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The nomenclature of computer architecture directly enables:

- **Processor Design (Intel, AMD, ARM):** Every block diagram uses this standardized vocabulary.
- **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M, AVR):** Engineers communicate using the same register/bus/micro-operation vocabulary.
- **FPGA/ASIC Design:** Register Transfer Level (RTL) design with Verilog/VHDL uses these exact terms.
- **Compiler Design:** Instruction formats and addressing modes directly determine code generation strategies.
- **OS Design:** Memory addressing nomenclature underpins virtual memory and paging systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Address Space from Address Lines

### Problem Context
A computer system has a **16-bit address bus**. Determine:
1. The total number of unique memory locations.
2. The total memory capacity in **bytes** if the word length is 8 bits.
3. The total memory capacity in **KB**.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values
Let $n$ = number of address lines = **16**
Let $W$ = word length = **8 bits**

### Step 2: Apply Address Space Formula
The number of unique memory locations is given by:

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^n
$$

Substituting $n = 16$:

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{16}
$$

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 65536 \text{ unique addresses}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Capacity in Bits
Total capacity in bits:

$$
C_{\text{bits}} = 2^n \times W
$$

$$
C_{\text{bits}} = 2^{16} \times 8
$$

$$
C_{\text{bits}} = 65536 \times 8
$$

$$
C_{\text{bits}} = 524288 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Bytes
Using the universal conversion $1 \text{ Byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$:

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{C_{\text{bits}}}{8}
$$

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{524288}{8}
$$

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = 65536 \text{ Bytes}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Kilobytes
Using $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ Bytes}$:

$$
C_{\text{KB}} = \frac{65536}{1024}
$$

$$
C_{\text{KB}} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 6: Final Result Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Address Lines ($n$) | 16 |
| Word Length ($W$) | 8 bits |
| Unique Addresses | 65,536 |
| Total Capacity | 65,536 Bytes = 64 KB |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bus Bandwidth Calculation

### Problem Context
A system has a **32-bit data bus** and a **clock frequency of 200 MHz**. Each data transfer requires **4 clock cycles**. Calculate the **bus bandwidth**.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values
- Data bus width $W = 32$ bits
- Clock frequency $f = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Clock cycles per transfer $N_{\text{cycles}} = 4$

### Step 2: Apply Bandwidth Formula
The bus bandwidth in bits per second is:

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bits}} = \frac{W \times f}{N_{\text{cycles}}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bits}} = \frac{32 \times 200 \times 10^6}{4}
$$

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bits}} = \frac{6400 \times 10^6}{4}
$$

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bits}} = 1600 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}
$$

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bits}} = 1.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/sec} = 1.6 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Bytes per Second

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^9}{8} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Bytes/sec} = 200 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### Step 5: Final Result

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Bandwidth (bits/s) | 1.6 Gbps |
| Bandwidth (Bytes/s) | 200 MB/s |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Nomenclature Quick-Reference

| Nomenclature Term | Symbol | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| PC | Program Counter | Holds next instruction address |
| MAR | Memory Address Register | Holds address for memory access |
| MDR / MBR | Memory Data / Buffer Register | Holds data for memory access |
| IR | Instruction Register | Holds current instruction |
| AC / ACC | Accumulator | Implicit operand & result holder |
| GPR | General Purpose Register | User-visible temporary storage |
| ALU | Arithmetic Logic Unit | Performs arithmetic & logic |
| CU | Control Unit | Generates control signals |
| DB | Data Bus | Carries data (bidirectional) |
| AB | Address Bus | Carries addresses (unidirectional) |
| CB | Control Bus | Carries control/timing signals |
| MUX | Multiplexer | Selects one of many inputs |
| DEC | Decoder | Activates one output based on input code |
| RTL | Register Transfer Language | Symbolic notation for data transfer |
| μ-op | Micro-operation | Smallest CPU action per clock |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Instruction Format

### Problem
A computer has a **16-bit instruction format** divided as follows:
- Opcode = 4 bits
- Mode = 2 bits
- Address = 10 bits

### Step 1: Verify Total Instruction Length

$$
\text{Opcode} + \text{Mode} + \text{Address} = 4 + 2 + 10 = 16 \text{ bits} \;\; \checkmark
$$

### Step 2: Number of Distinct Operations

$$
N_{\text{op}} = 2^4 = 16 \text{ distinct opcodes}
$$

### Step 3: Number of Addressing Modes

$$
N_{\text{mode}} = 2^2 = 4 \text{ addressing modes}
$$

### Step 4: Addressable Memory Locations

$$
N_{\text{addr}} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ locations} = 1 \text{ K words}
$$

### Step 5: Number of Possible Unique Instructions

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^4 \times 2^2 \times 2^{10} = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ unique instructions}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: A Basic Computer System Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the standard nomenclature-based view of a computer's interaction with memory and I/O, including all major buses and registers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT CPU"]
        subgraph CU["Control Unit CU"]
            CTRL[Control Signals Generator]
        end
        subgraph ALU["ALU"]
            ARITH[Arithmetic Logic Circuit]
        end
        subgraph REG["Register File"]
            PC1[PC - Program Counter]
            AR1[AR - Address Register]
            DR1[DR - Data Register]
            AC1[AC - Accumulator]
            IR1[IR - Instruction Register]
            TR1[TR - Temporary Register]
            INPR1[INPR - Input Register]
            OUTR1[OUTR - Output Register]
            E1[E - Carry Flag]
        end
    end
    MEM[Main Memory RAM ROM]
    IO[Input Output Devices]
    subgraph BUSes["System Buses"]
        DBUS[Data Bus - Bidirectional]
        ABUS[Address Bus - Unidirectional]
        CBUS[Control Bus - Read Write Clock Interrupt]
    end
    PC1 --> ABUS
    AR1 --> ABUS
    ABUS --> MEM
    ABUS --> IO
    DR1 <--> DBUS
    AC1 <--> DBUS
    IR1 <--> DBUS
    MEM <--> DBUS
    IO <--> DBUS
    CTRL --> CBUS
    CBUS --> MEM
    CBUS --> IO
    AC1 --> ARITH
    DR1 --> ARITH
    TR1 --> ARITH
    ARITH --> AC1
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Common Bus System (Standard KTU Diagram)

This diagram shows how **multiple registers** are connected to a **single common bus** using tri-state buffers and a decoder-based selection mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGISTERS["Register Bank"]
        R0[Register R0]
        R1[Register R1]
        R2[Register R2]
        R3[Register R3]
        R4[Register R4]
        R5[Register R5]
        R6[Register R6]
        R7[Register R7]
    end
    subgraph MUX["Bus Selection Unit"]
        MUX2[4 to 1 Multiplexer MUX]
        SEL[S1 S0 Select Lines]
    end
    subgraph BUFFERS["Tri-State Output Buffers"]
        B0[Buffer 0]
        B1[Buffer 1]
        B2[Buffer 2]
        B3[Buffer 3]
        B4[Buffer 4]
        B5[Buffer 5]
        B6[Buffer 6]
        B7[Buffer 7]
    end
    COMMON[Common Bus - Single Shared Data Line]
    R0 --> B0
    R1 --> B1
    R2 --> B2
    R3 --> B3
    R4 --> B4
    R5 --> B5
    R6 --> B6
    R7 --> B7
    B0 --> COMMON
    B1 --> COMMON
    B2 --> COMMON
    B3 --> COMMON
    B4 --> COMMON
    B5 --> COMMON
    B6 --> COMMON
    B7 --> COMMON
    SEL --> MUX2
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B0
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B1
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B2
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B3
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B4
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B5
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B6
    MUX2 -.controls.-> B7
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Instruction Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode: T0 T1 T2
    Decode --> Execute: T3
    Execute --> Store: T4
    Store --> Fetch: Next Instruction
    Execute --> Interrupt: Interrupt Detected
    Interrupt --> Service: ISR Entry
    Service --> Fetch: IRET Return
    Fetch --> Halt: HLT Opcode
    Halt --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Nomenclature Mapping Table (Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Computer System Nomenclature] --> B[Hardware Nomenclature]
    A --> C[Data Nomenclature]
    A --> D[Control Nomenclature]
    B --> B1[CPU: ALU CU Registers]
    B --> B2[Memory: RAM ROM Cache]
    B --> B3[Buses: Data Address Control]
    B --> B4[I/O: Keyboard Display Disk]
    C --> C1[Instruction: Opcode Operand]
    C --> C2[Data: Word Byte Nibble]
    C --> C3[Address: Physical Logical]
    C --> C4[Control Word: Microinstruction]
    D --> D1[Signals: Read Write Clock]
    D --> D2[Micro-operations: RTL]
    D --> D3[Flags: Carry Zero Sign Overflow]
    D --> D4[Timing: T-State Machine Cycle]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the following with respect to computer architecture:**
**(a)** Accumulator  
**(b)** Program Counter  
**(c)** Memory Address Register

### Model Answer:

**(a) Accumulator (AC):** The accumulator is a special-purpose register inside the CPU that implicitly holds one of the operands of an ALU operation and subsequently stores the result. It is the default register for arithmetic and logic operations in single-address instruction formats. For example, in the operation `ADD X`, the operand at memory location `X` is added to the existing value in the AC, and the result is stored back into the AC.

**(b) Program Counter (PC):** The program counter is a dedicated CPU register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched and executed. After each instruction fetch, the PC is automatically incremented (PC ← PC + 1) to point to the following instruction, unless a branch/jump instruction modifies its value.

**(c) Memory Address Register (MAR):** The MAR is a register that holds the **address of the memory location** that the CPU wants to read from or write to. It is directly connected to the address bus, and its contents are used by the memory subsystem to locate the correct cell. It acts as the interface between the CPU and the address bus.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the three types of system buses with their direction of data flow.**

### Model Answer:

A computer system uses three fundamental types of buses to transfer information between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices:

1. **Data Bus:** It is **bi-directional** in nature. The data bus carries the actual data (operands, instructions, or results) between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. The width of the data bus determines the word length of the processor (e.g., a 32-bit data bus can transfer 32 bits in parallel).

2. **Address Bus:** It is **uni-directional** (signals flow only from the CPU to memory/I/O). The address bus carries the memory address of the location that the CPU wants to access. The number of address lines $n$ determines the addressable memory space as $2^n$ unique locations.

3. **Control Bus:** It is used to carry **command and timing signals** such as Read ($\overline{\text{RD}}$), Write ($\overline{\text{WR}}$), Memory/IO select, Interrupt Request (IRQ), Clock, and Reset. The direction depends on the specific signal (e.g., Read is output from CPU, Interrupt is input to CPU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** The data bus is *bidirectional* (data flows both ways), but the address bus is *unidirectional* (only CPU sends out addresses).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic structure of a computer system**, clearly identifying the CPU, Memory, I/O, and the three types of buses. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A computer uses a memory of **4096 words**, with each word being **16 bits** long. Determine:
- (i) The number of address lines required.
- (ii) The total memory capacity in **bits, bytes, and KB**.
- (iii) The size of the MAR and MDR registers. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

The basic structure of a computer system consists of three major subsystems connected by three types of buses:

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1):**

- **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the ALU, Control Unit, and Register File. The ALU performs arithmetic and logic operations, while the Control Unit generates timing and control signals. The registers (PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, TR) provide high-speed temporary storage.
- **Memory Unit:** Stores both data and instructions. Includes RAM (volatile, read/write) and ROM (non-volatile, read-only).
- **Input/Output Devices:** Allow the system to communicate with the external world (keyboard, monitor, disk, etc.).

**The Three Buses:**
- **Data Bus** (bi-directional): Carries the data being read/written.
- **Address Bus** (uni-directional): Carries the destination/source memory address.
- **Control Bus** (mixed direction): Carries control signals like Read, Write, Interrupt, and Clock.

**Operation:** When the CPU needs to execute an instruction, it places the address on the address bus, activates the appropriate control signals (e.g., Read), and data is transferred to/from memory or I/O via the data bus.

---

### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Number of memory words: $N = 4096$
- Word length: $W = 16$ bits

**(i) Number of address lines ($n$):**

The relationship between memory words and address lines is:

$$
N = 2^n
$$

Substituting $N = 4096$:

$$
4096 = 2^n
$$

$$
2^{12} = 2^n
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 12 \text{ address lines}}
$$

**(ii) Total memory capacity:**

Capacity in bits:

$$
C_{\text{bits}} = 2^n \times W = 4096 \times 16 = 65536 \text{ bits}
$$

Capacity in bytes:

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{65536}{8} = 8192 \text{ Bytes}
$$

Capacity in KB:

$$
C_{\text{KB}} = \frac{8192}{1024} = 8 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iii) Size of MAR and MDR:**

- **MAR** must be able to hold the memory address → it must be **$n = 12$ bits** wide.
- **MDR** must be able to hold the data word → it must be **$W = 16$ bits** wide.

| Register | Size |
|---|---|
| MAR | 12 bits |
| MDR | 16 bits |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define **micro-operation**. List and explain the four functional categories of micro-operations with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **common bus system** for **four registers** (R0, R1, R2, R3). Show how the bus selection logic works using a 2-to-4 decoder and tri-state buffers. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A *micro-operation* is the most elementary, indivisible operation that can be performed by the CPU on data stored in its registers during a single clock pulse (T-state). Micro-operations form the basis of all instruction execution.

**The Four Functional Categories:**

1. **Register Transfer Micro-operations:**
   These move data from one register to another without changing its content.
   *Example:* $\text{R2} \leftarrow \text{R1}$ (Copy contents of R1 into R2)

2. **Arithmetic Micro-operations:**
   These perform arithmetic operations on numeric data in registers.
   *Example:* $\text{R3} \leftarrow \text{R1} + \text{R2}$ (Add R1 and R2, store in R3)

3. **Logic Micro-operations:**
   These perform bit-wise logical operations on data in registers.
   *Example:* $\text{R4} \leftarrow \text{R1} \oplus \text{R2}$ (XOR operation)

4. **Shift Micro-operations:**
   These shift the bits of a register left or right (with or without carry linkage).
   *Example:* $\text{R2} \leftarrow \text{shl R2}$ (Shift R2 left by 1 bit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory micro-operations** are sometimes listed as a fifth category: `Read: DR ← M[AR]` and `Write: M[AR] ← DR`.

---

### Model Solution for (b):

**Concept:** In a *common bus system*, all registers share a single set of data lines. Only one register can place data on the bus at a time, controlled by tri-state buffers. A decoder selects which register is "active" on the bus.

**Block Diagram Description (for 4 registers R0–R3):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGS["4 Registers"]
        R0A[Register R0]
        R1A[Register R1]
        R2A[Register R2]
        R3A[Register R3]
    end
    subgraph DEC["2 to 4 Decoder"]
        D0[Output 0]
        D1[Output 1]
        D2[Output 2]
        D3[Output 3]
    end
    subgraph BUF["Tri-State Output Buffers"]
        TB0[Buffer R0]
        TB1[Buffer R1]
        TB2[Buffer R2]
        TB3[Buffer R3]
    end
    CBUS[Common Bus]
    R0A --> TB0
    R1A --> TB1
    R2A --> TB2
    R3A --> TB3
    TB0 --> CBUS
    TB1 --> CBUS
    TB2 --> CBUS
    TB3 --> CBUS
    D0 -.Enable.-> TB0
    D1 -.Enable.-> TB1
    D2 -.Enable.-> TB2
    D3 -.Enable.-> TB3
    S1[Select S1] --> DEC
    S0[Select S0] --> DEC
```

**Working Principle:**

- The 2-to-4 decoder has 2 select inputs $S_1, S_0$ and 4 outputs $D_0$ to $D_3$.
- Each decoder output is connected to the **enable** pin of one tri-state buffer.
- Only the selected buffer is enabled, allowing that register's data to flow onto the common bus.
- All other buffers are in **high-impedance (Hi-Z)** state, isolating their registers.

**Truth Table:**

| $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Active Register | Bus Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | R0 | R0 contents |
| 0 | 1 | R1 | R1 contents |
| 1 | 0 | R2 | R2 contents |
| 1 | 1 | R3 | R3 contents |

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Reduces wiring complexity. **Disadvantage:** Only one register can communicate with the bus at any given time, which slows down parallel operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and where you lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MAR and MDR sizes:** Many students incorrectly equate MAR to the word length. **MAR = address lines; MDR = word length.** This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Direction of Buses:** Forgetting that the **address bus is unidirectional**. If the question asks for direction, write it explicitly — don't just say "carries addresses."
>
> 3. **Units Confusion:** Writing memory capacity in **bits** when the question asks for **bytes/KB**. Always show the unit conversion steps.
>
> 4. **Misnaming Registers:** "Data Register" vs. "Data Bus" — they are *not* the same thing. The DR is an internal CPU register; the data bus is the external pathway.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In 7-mark questions, a neatly labeled diagram is **mandatory**. Skipping it can cost 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong Base in KB:** Use $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ Bytes}$ in computer science (not 1000).
>
> 7. **Tri-State Buffer Logic:** Students often forget to mention the **Hi-Z (high impedance) state** when explaining common bus systems — this is a key valuation point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Nomenclature**

### **A. Core Definitions (Memorize these verbatim)**
- **Computer:** An electronic device that accepts input, processes data, and produces output.
- **CPU:** The "brain" — executes instructions.
- **Memory:** Stores data and instructions (RAM, ROM).
- **Bus:** A shared communication pathway.
- **Register:** A high-speed storage cell inside the CPU.
- **Micro-operation:** The smallest CPU action per clock cycle.

### **B. Key Registers (Don't mix them up)**
- **PC** → Next instruction address
- **AR (MAR)** → Memory address for read/write
- **DR (MDR)** → Data being transferred
- **AC** → Accumulator (operand + result)
- **IR** → Current instruction
- **TR** → Temporary register

### **C. The Three Buses**
- **Data Bus:** Bi-directional, width = word length
- **Address Bus:** Uni-directional, width = $n$ (address lines)
- **Control Bus:** Mixed direction, carries Read/Write/Clock/IRQ

### **D. Essential Formulas (Write these first in any numerical answer)**
- Memory locations: $N = 2^n$
- Capacity (bits): $C = 2^n \times W$
- Capacity (bytes): $C_B = \dfrac{2^n \times W}{8}$
- Clock period: $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$
- Address bus size = $n$ bits; Data bus size = $W$ bits

### **E. Micro-operation Categories (4 + 1)**
1. Register Transfer
2. Arithmetic
3. Logic
4. Shift
5. *(Memory access — Read/Write)*

### **F. Instruction Format**
- Total bits = Opcode bits + Mode bits + Address bits
- Number of opcodes = $2^{(\text{opcode bits})}$
- Addressable memory = $2^{(\text{address bits})}$

### **G. Common Bus System Key Points**
- Uses tri-state buffers (Hi-Z state when disabled)
- Selection done via 2-to-4 decoder (for 4 registers)
- Only **one** register drives the bus at a time
- Reduces hardware complexity

### **H. Exam Strategy Tips**
- Always draw a **neatly labeled block diagram** for 7-mark questions.
- **Show unit conversions** explicitly in numerical problems.
- Use **symbols** (PC, AR, DR, AC) consistently — examiners mark for correct usage.
- Mention **direction of data flow** when discussing buses.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Memory Module

A **Memory Module** is a physical hardware sub-system designed to store, retain, and retrieve binary information (data and instructions) for immediate or future use by a computer system's processor. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Computer Organisation and Architecture, a memory module is the logical and physical realization of a **storage array of bistable cells** organized as an $n \times m$ matrix, where $n$ is the number of words and $m$ is the word length in bits.

The **Module** perspective encompasses not just the raw storage cells, but the complete architecture including:
- The **storage cell array** (SRAM/DRAM/ROM cells)
- The **address decoder** (selects a unique word location)
- The **read/write control circuitry**
- The **input/output buffers** and **sense amplifiers**
- The **refresh logic** (for volatile dynamic memories)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A memory module is a combinational unit of storage cells, address selection logic, and data I/O circuitry that interfaces with the CPU through a parallel bus. The term *module* distinguishes the complete functional block from an individual *memory chip*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Library of Alexandria 🏛️

Imagine a massive library with millions of books, but with a unique rule — **every book has a specific shelf number and row number**. To find a particular book, you don't browse; you just tell the librarian the **address** (e.g., Row 14, Shelf 7, Book 3), and the librarian instantly fetches it.

**Mapping the Analogy to Memory Modules:**

| Library Concept | Memory Module Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Each book | A single bit stored in a memory cell |
| A row of identical books | A memory **word** (e.g., 8 bits, 16 bits) |
| The whole library shelf system | The **memory chip** |
| The librarian | The **address decoder** |
| The catalog system | **Address bus** |
| The reading desk | **Output buffer / Data bus** |
| Re-stocking torn pages | **Refresh operation** (DRAM) |
| Restricted-access section | **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** |
| Special index that searches content | **Associative Memory (CAM)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a library's efficiency depends on organization, indexing, and access speed, a computer's overall performance is heavily dictated by how its memory modules are organized, how fast they can be addressed, and how much data they can hold per access cycle.

## 1.3 The Triad of Memory Module Characteristics

Every memory module in a KTU-grade computer architecture is evaluated on three fundamental axes:

**1. Capacity ($C$)** — The total amount of data it can hold, measured in **bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB, TB**.

**2. Access Time ($t_A$)** — The time delay between presenting a valid address and the data becoming available on the output bus. Expressed in **nanoseconds (ns)** for SRAM and **tens of ns** for DRAM.

**3. Cost per bit** — The economic metric, measured in **₹/bit** or **$/bit**. There is an inherent trade-off: **Faster memory costs more per bit**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hierarchy Mantra:** *"Smaller + Faster + Costlier"* as you go UP the hierarchy (Registers → Cache → Main Memory → Disk). A memory module sits in the **Main Memory** tier — fast enough to talk to the CPU directly, but large enough to hold running programs.

## 1.4 The Classification of Memory Modules

Based on access pattern, volatility, and write capability, memory modules are classified as:

### A. By Access Mechanism
- **Random Access Memory (RAM):** Any location can be accessed in *equal* time. Includes **SRAM** and **DRAM**.
- **Sequential Access Memory:** Data accessed in a fixed order (e.g., magnetic tape). Not commonly a Module-3 focus.
- **Direct Access Memory:** Hybrid (e.g., hard disk). Out of scope for Module 3.
- **Associative Access Memory (CAM):** Access by *content*, not by address. High-speed search.

### B. By Volatility
- **Volatile:** Loses data on power-off (SRAM, DRAM).
- **Non-Volatile:** Retains data without power (ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash).

### C. By Write Capability
- **Read/Write Memory:** Both operations allowed.
- **Read-Only Memory:** Only read operation is supported in normal operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** Despite its name, **RAM (Random Access Memory) is *not* always "read-and-write" memory**. The "Random Access" refers to the addressing scheme, not the read/write capability. ROM is *also* random access — you can randomly address any location to read it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static RAM (SRAM) Module

### 2.1.1 The Bistable SRAM Cell (6-Transistor CMOS Cell)

The fundamental SRAM cell is built from **6 MOSFETs** arranged as two cross-coupled CMOS inverters with two access transistors — forming a **bistable flip-flop**. This is the cornerstone of Module 3.

**Operational Logic:**
- The two inverters (T1-T2, T3-T4) form a **latch** that holds one of two stable states: '0' or '1'.
- **T5 and T6** are the access transistors, gated by the **Word Line (WL)**.
- To **READ**: WL is activated; the stored value drives the **Bit Line (BL)** and its complement $\overline{BL}$ through T5 and T6. A **sense amplifier** detects the small voltage difference.
- To **WRITE**: WL is activated; BL and $\overline{BL}$ are forced to the desired value, overwriting the cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** SRAM uses **6 transistors per bit**. This makes it fast (access time ~ 1–10 ns) but physically large and expensive. It does **NOT** require refreshing.

### 2.1.2 SRAM Module Architecture

An SRAM module chip has the following structure:

1. **Memory Cell Array** — organized in a 2D matrix of rows × columns.
2. **Row Decoder** — selects the row based on the upper address bits.
3. **Column Decoder / Multiplexer** — selects the column bits to route to the output.
4. **Sense Amplifiers** — one per column, amplify the small differential signal.
5. **Read/Write Control** — based on $\overline{CS}$ (Chip Select), $\overline{WE}$ (Write Enable), $\overline{OE}$ (Output Enable).
6. **I/O Buffers** — tri-state buffers for the data bus.

## 2.2 Dynamic RAM (DRAM) Module

### 2.2.1 The 1T-1C DRAM Cell

A DRAM cell stores a single bit as a **charge on a tiny capacitor** (typically 30–50 fF), accessed through a single **access transistor**. This is the **1T-1C** configuration.

**Operational Logic:**
- **WRITE:** The Word Line (WL) activates the access transistor, and the bit value (charged or discharged) is forced onto the capacitor.
- **READ:** The charge is shared with the **bit line capacitance**, producing a small voltage swing (~ 200 mV) detected by a sense amplifier.
- **REFRESH:** Because the capacitor leaks charge (leakage current in the p-n junctions), the cell must be **rewritten (refreshed)** every **2 to 64 ms**, typically every **64 ms** in modern DRAM. This is why it's called *Dynamic*.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Why DRAM is Destructive on Read:** Reading a DRAM cell discharges the capacitor to the bit line capacitance. The sense amplifier reads it, but then the cell must be **rewritten** (a refresh-after-read). This is built into the chip's internal logic and is invisible to the CPU.

### 2.2.2 DRAM Module Organization

DRAM modules historically evolved through several packaging forms — a **KTU-favorite** topic:

| Type | Full Form | Pins | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FPM DRAM** | Fast Page Mode | 30-pin SIMM | Accesses a whole row (page) quickly |
| **EDO DRAM** | Extended Data Out | 72-pin SIMM | Overlaps data-out with next address |
| **SDRAM** | Synchronous DRAM | 168-pin DIMM | Synchronized to system clock |
| **DDR SDRAM** | Double Data Rate | 184-pin DIMM | Transfers on both clock edges |
| **DDR2/DDR3/DDR4/DDR5** | Successive generations | 240/288-pin | Higher clock, lower voltage, prefetch buffers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite:** A common question is to **compare SRAM and DRAM** across cost, speed, density, power, and use case. Memorize the table in Section 5.

### 2.2.3 DRAM Refresh Methods

A **mandatory KTU topic**. The three refresh methods are:

1. **Burst Refresh:** All rows are refreshed consecutively in one "burst" — CPU is blocked for the full refresh time.
2. **Distributed Refresh:** Refresh cycles are spread evenly between memory accesses — CPU blocked briefly each refresh.
3. **Transparent Refresh:** Refresh performed during CPU cycles when the memory is not being accessed — CPU is *not* blocked.

## 2.3 Read-Only Memory (ROM) Modules

A ROM is a memory module where data is **permanently or semi-permanently written** and only read in normal operation.

| ROM Type | Programming | Erasing | Re-writable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROM (Mask ROM)** | At fabrication (mask) | Not possible | No |
| **PROM** | Once by user (fuse/antifuse) | Not possible | No |
| **EPROM** | Electrical | UV light (20–30 min) | Yes (limited) |
| **EEPROM** | Electrical | Electrical (byte-level) | Yes (slow) |
| **Flash Memory** | Electrical | Electrical (block-level) | Yes (fast) |

## 2.4 Associative Memory (CAM — Content Addressable Memory)

A CAM performs a **parallel search** over all stored words for a matching *key* (the search argument) within a single clock cycle. It is the "search-by-content" memory.

**Architecture:**
- Each cell stores a bit AND has a **comparison circuit**.
- The **match line** (one per word) is pulled LOW if all bits of that word match the search argument.
- Multiple matches are possible; a **priority encoder** resolves them.

**Applications:** TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer), network routers, CPU caches, database accelerators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The CAM is *the* fastest lookup structure known. Its cost is **O(n) hardware per word**, making it expensive. It is **NOT** a substitute for RAM — it complements it for fast searches.

## 2.5 Memory Interleaving

Memory interleaving is a technique to **partition the main memory into multiple banks (modules)** such that consecutive addresses lie in *different* banks. This allows **pipelined/pipelined access** — while one bank is performing a refresh/cycle, the next can begin its access.

- **Low-order interleaving:** Consecutive addresses in different banks (e.g., Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3, Bank 0, ...). Best for **sequential** access.
- **High-order interleaving:** Contiguous block of addresses in one bank. Best for **random** access.

## 2.6 Cache Memory (Brief Module-3 Introduction)

A **cache** is a small, fast memory module placed between the CPU and main memory. It exploits **temporal locality** (recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again) and **spatial locality** (data near a recently accessed address is likely to be accessed next).

**Mapping Techniques** (covered in detail in Module 3 of KTU syllabus):
- **Direct Mapping** — each memory block maps to exactly one cache line.
- **Associative Mapping** — a memory block can map to *any* cache line (fully associative).
- **Set-Associative Mapping** — a hybrid; the cache is divided into *sets*, each containing *k* lines (k-way set associative).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> This table summarizes **every formula** you need to solve KTU Module-3 problems. Memorize this — it covers 90% of numerical questions.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory Capacity | $C = 2^n \times m$ | $n$ = address lines, $m$ = word size | bits |
| 2 | Memory Capacity (bytes) | $C_{bytes} = 2^n \times (m \div 8)$ | Same as above | Bytes |
| 3 | Number of chips for word expansion | $N_{WE} = \text{Desired word size} \div \text{Chip word size}$ | — | chips |
| 4 | Number of chips for capacity expansion | $N_{CE} = \text{Desired words} \div \text{Chip words}$ | — | chips |
| 5 | Total chips for combined expansion | $N_{total} = N_{WE} \times N_{CE}$ | — | chips |
| 6 | Cache Hit Rate | $H = \dfrac{Hits}{Hits + Misses}$ | $H \in [0, 1]$ | ratio / % |
| 7 | Cache Miss Rate | $M = 1 - H$ | — | ratio / % |
| 8 | Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) | $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{main}$ | $t_{cache}$, $t_{main}$ in ns | ns |
| 9 | AMAT with cache miss penalty | $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H)(t_{cache} + t_{main})$ | Penalty = full main memory access | ns |
| 10 | AMAT with multi-level | $t_{avg} = t_{L1} + M_1 \cdot (t_{L2} + M_2 \cdot t_{main})$ | Standard hierarchy | ns |
| 11 | Number of cache sets | $S = \dfrac{\text{Total cache lines}}{k}$ | $k$ = associativity | sets |
| 12 | Set index bits | $\text{Set index bits} = \log_2 S$ | — | bits |
| 13 | Tag bits | $\text{Tag bits} = \text{Addr bits} - \text{Set index} - \text{Block offset}$ | Standard | bits |
| 14 | Block offset bits | $\text{Offset bits} = \log_2(\text{Block size in words})$ | — | bits |
| 15 | Number of refresh cycles per ms | $N_{refresh} = \dfrac{\text{Rows}}{\text{Refresh period (ms)}}$ | For DRAM | cycles |
| 16 | Refresh overhead % | $\text{Overhead} = \dfrac{N_{refresh} \cdot t_{cycle}}{\text{Refresh period}} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 17 | Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data transferred (bytes)}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ | — | bytes/s |
| 18 | Interleaving factor | $k$ (number of banks) | Even number usually | banks |
| 19 | Effective access time (interleaved) | $t_{eff} = t_{access} + (k-1) \cdot t_{cycle}$ | Approx. for pipelined | ns |
| 20 | Cost per bit | $c = \dfrac{\text{Total cost}}{C_{bits}}$ | — | ₹/bit or $/bit |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In LaTeX, **never use the pipe character `|` inside tables** (it breaks the markdown table parser). For absolute value or conditional probability, write $\vert x \vert$ or use the \mid symbol instead.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Memory modules are the **backbone of every computing device**:

- **SRAM:** CPU L1/L2/L3 caches, router packet buffers, automotive ECUs, register files.
- **DRAM:** Main memory in PCs, laptops, servers, smartphones (LPDDR variants), GPUs (GDDR/HBM).
- **ROM:** BIOS/UEFI firmware, embedded bootloaders, printer cartridges, IoT device firmware.
- **Flash:** SSDs, USB drives, SD cards, smartphone storage, eMMC, UFS.
- **CAM:** Intel/AMD CPU TLBs, Cisco router route lookup tables, Google search accelerators, AI inference accelerators.
- **Interleaving:** All modern multi-channel memory controllers (dual-channel, quad-channel DDR).

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section exhaustively solves **typical KTU 2024 Scheme numerical and analytical problems** on Memory Modules. Each derivation is fully expanded with **no skipped steps**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: SRAM vs DRAM — Cell Density and Power

### Problem Setup
Compare the silicon area and refresh power of SRAM (6T cell) and DRAM (1T-1C cell) for a **1 Mbit** memory.

### Step 1: Define Cell Area (Assume 90 nm process)
- **SRAM cell area** $\approx 100 F^2$ where $F$ = feature size. For $F = 90$ nm: $A_{SRAM} \approx 100 \times (90 \times 10^{-9})^2 = 8.1 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2 = 0.81 \mu m^2$.
- **DRAM cell area** $\approx 6 F^2$ (for the capacitor + access transistor). $A_{DRAM} \approx 6 \times 0.0081 = 0.0486 \mu m^2$.

### Step 2: Compute Total Die Area
- Total bits $N = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bits.
- $A_{SRAM,total} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 0.81 \mu m^2 = 849{,}346 \mu m^2 \approx 0.85 \text{ mm}^2$.
- $A_{DRAM,total} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 0.0486 \mu m^2 = 50{,}961 \mu m^2 \approx 0.051 \text{ mm}^2$.

### Step 3: Density Ratio
$$
\text{Density ratio} = \frac{A_{SRAM}}{A_{DRAM}} = \frac{0.85}{0.051} \approx 16.7
$$
This means **DRAM is ~17× denser** than SRAM. This is the *fundamental physical reason* DRAM is used for main memory and SRAM is used for cache.

### Step 4: Refresh Power for DRAM
Assume capacitor $C = 30$ fF, supply $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, refresh every $T_{ref} = 64$ ms, all rows must be refreshed.

$$
E_{refresh,row} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 10^{-15} \times 1^2 = 15 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 15 \text{ fJ}
$$

$$
P_{refresh} = \frac{N_{rows} \cdot E_{refresh,row}}{T_{ref}} = \frac{1024 \times 15 \times 10^{-15}}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} = 240 \text{ nW}
$$

This is small but non-zero — and across billions of cells, total refresh power is significant.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Memory Capacity and Address Decoding

### Problem
A memory module has **16 address lines** and a **word size of 32 bits**. Compute:
(a) Total memory capacity in bits, bytes, KB, MB.
(b) Number of addressable locations.
(c) Address of the **last** location.

### Step (a): Capacity in bits
$$
C = 2^{16} \times 32 = 65{,}536 \times 32 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step (b): Convert to Bytes
$$
C_{bytes} = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{8} = 262{,}144 \text{ Bytes}
$$

### Step (c): Convert to KB and MB
$$
C_{KB} = \frac{262{,}144}{1024} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
C_{MB} = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25 \text{ MB} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step (d): Number of Addressable Locations
$$
N_{locations} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ words}
$$

### Step (e): Address of the Last Location
Since addresses start at 0, the last address is:
$$
A_{last} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535 = \text{FFFF}_{16}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Memory Expansion

### Problem
A computer designer has **8 K × 8** memory chips. Design a memory of:
(a) **64 K × 8** capacity (capacity expansion only)
(b) **8 K × 32** word length (word expansion only)
(c) **64 K × 32** combined expansion

Show the number of chips, the address lines, and the decoding logic.

### Part (a): 64 K × 8
$$
N_{CE} = \frac{64\text{K}}{8\text{K}} = 8 \text{ chips}
$$

Address lines: 64K needs $\log_2(65536) = 16$ address bits.
- The lower **13 bits** $\left(A_{12} \text{ to } A_0\right)$ go to each chip's address pins (since each chip is 8K = $2^{13}$).
- The upper **3 bits** $\left(A_{15} \text{ to } A_{13}\right)$ go to a **3-to-8 decoder** to select one of 8 chips via their $\overline{CS}$ pin.

### Part (b): 8 K × 32
$$
N_{WE} = \frac{32}{8} = 4 \text{ chips}
$$

All 4 chips are enabled *simultaneously* (all $\overline{CS}$ tied to ground or to the same enable). Each chip handles **8 bits** of the 32-bit word. Lower 13 address lines feed all 4 chips in parallel. The data bus of the CPU is 32 bits; the 4 chips each contribute 8 bits.

### Part (c): 64 K × 32
$$
N_{CE} = 8, \quad N_{WE} = 4, \quad N_{total} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ chips}
$$

- 4 chips (one "group" of 4) form a 8K × 32 bank.
- 8 such banks (8 groups) form the 64K × 32 memory.
- 3-to-8 decoder selects one of 8 groups; within a group, all 4 chips are active in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always state the **purpose of the decoder**, the **number of address bits** to the chip, and the **number of address bits to the decoder** clearly. The 3-to-8 decoder is implemented using a 74LS138 or similar.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Cache Hit Rate and AMAT

### Problem
A system has a **cache with access time 5 ns** and a **main memory with access time 50 ns**. The cache hit rate is **90%**.

(a) Compute the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**.
(b) What is the AMAT if the hit rate improves to **95%**?
(c) What is the AMAT if the hit rate drops to **80%**?

### Part (a): AMAT at H = 90%
$$
t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot t_{main}
$$

$$
t_{avg} = 0.90 \times 5 + 0.10 \times 50 = 4.5 + 5.0 = 9.5 \text{ ns}
$$

### Part (b): AMAT at H = 95%
$$
t_{avg} = 0.95 \times 5 + 0.05 \times 50 = 4.75 + 2.5 = 7.25 \text{ ns}
$$

### Part (c): AMAT at H = 80%
$$
t_{avg} = 0.80 \times 5 + 0.20 \times 50 = 4.0 + 10.0 = 14.0 \text{ ns}
$$

### Analysis
Notice the **non-linear** effect: dropping from 90% to 80% increases AMAT by 47%, while improving to 95% only reduces it by 24%. **Cache misses are expensive** — even a small change in hit rate has a large impact on performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In the AMAT formula, the cache access time is **not** repeated in the miss penalty unless specified. Some KTU questions include "miss penalty" as a separate term. Read carefully.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Set-Associative Cache Address Breakdown

### Problem
A CPU has a **32-bit address bus**. The cache is **16 KB, 4-way set associative**, with a **block size of 4 words** (word = 4 bytes).

(a) Find the number of sets, the tag bits, the set index bits, and the block offset bits.
(b) Draw the cache line structure.

### Step 1: Block Offset Bits
Block size = 4 words × 4 bytes/word = **16 bytes**.
$$
\text{Offset bits} = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2: Number of Cache Lines
$$
\text{Total lines} = \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size}} = \frac{16{,}384 \text{ bytes}}{16 \text{ bytes}} = 1024 \text{ lines}
$$

### Step 3: Number of Sets
$$
\text{Number of sets} = \frac{\text{Total lines}}{k} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ sets}
$$

### Step 4: Set Index Bits
$$
\text{Set index bits} = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 5: Tag Bits
$$
\text{Tag bits} = 32 - 8 - 4 = 20 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 6: Verification
$$
\text{Total} = 20 (\text{tag}) + 8 (\text{index}) + 4 (\text{offset}) = 32 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: DRAM Refresh Overhead

### Problem
A DRAM has **4096 rows**, refresh period **64 ms**, and a memory cycle time of **200 ns**. Compute:
(a) The number of refresh cycles per row.
(b) The total time spent on refresh per refresh period.
(c) The refresh overhead as a percentage of total time.

### Part (a): Number of Refresh Cycles
Each row must be refreshed once per 64 ms period. So **1 refresh cycle per row per 64 ms**, total **4096 refresh cycles** per 64 ms.

### Part (b): Total Refresh Time
$$
t_{refresh,total} = 4096 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 819{,}200 \text{ ns} \approx 0.819 \text{ ms}
$$

### Part (c): Refresh Overhead
$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{0.819 \text{ ms}}{64 \text{ ms}} \times 100 \approx 1.28\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only about 1.28% of the memory's time is spent on refresh — DRAM is still highly available, but the overhead is non-zero and must be accounted for in real-time systems.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Memory Module

| **Design Constraint** | **Best Choice** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Small, fastest possible (few KB) | **SRAM** | No refresh, ~1 ns access |
| Large main memory (GB scale) | **DRAM (DDR4/5)** | Highest density, lowest cost/bit |
| Firmware that never changes | **Mask ROM** | Cheapest per bit, very secure |
| Field-updatable firmware | **Flash (NOR)** | Random read access, in-system write |
| High-density data storage (SSD) | **Flash (NAND)** | Block-oriented, very dense |
| Content-based search at line-rate | **CAM / TCAM** | Single-cycle parallel compare |
| Sequential block access with no latency tolerance | **SRAM FIFO** | Deterministic, no refresh |
| Embedded boot code | **Mask ROM or NOR Flash** | Non-volatile, byte-addressable |
| Real-time control system with tight timing | **SRAM (with ECC)** | Predictable latency |

## 4.2 Diagram: SRAM 6T Cell Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[BIT LINE BL] --> B[ACCESS TRANSISTOR T5]
    C[WORD LINE WL] --> B
    B --> D[STORAGE NODE S]
    D --> E[INVERTER 1 T1 T2]
    E --> F[STORAGE NODE SBAR]
    F --> G[INVERTER 2 T3 T4]
    G --> D
    A2[BIT LINE BLCOMPLEMENT] --> H[ACCESS TRANSISTOR T6]
    C --> H
    H --> F

    style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The 6T SRAM cell uses two cross-coupled inverters (T1+T2 and T3+T4) to form a bistable latch. The two access transistors (T5, T6) are controlled by the Word Line (WL). Bit Lines BL and $\overline{BL}$ carry the data and its complement.


**Interpretation:** In a CAM, the search argument is broadcast in parallel to every word's comparator logic. Each word's match line indicates a hit or miss. The priority encoder resolves multiple simultaneous matches to a single address output.

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on **actual KTU University Exam patterns** with precise mark allocation as per the **2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** regulations.

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to construction, speed, density, power consumption, and typical applications.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| **Parameter** | **SRAM** | **DRAM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Cell** | 6 transistors (flip-flop) | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor |
| **Refresh Required?** | No | Yes (every ~64 ms) |
| **Access Time** | 1–10 ns (faster) | 50–100 ns (slower) |
| **Density** | Low (6T per cell) | High (1T+1C per cell) |
| **Cost per bit** | High | Low |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (continuous) | Lower (except refresh) |
| **Typical Use** | Cache memory (L1/L2/L3) | Main memory |
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Volatile |

**Conclusion:** SRAM trades **density for speed**, making it ideal for caches. DRAM trades **speed and complexity for density**, making it ideal for main memory.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - December 2023]
**Explain the concept of Associative Memory. How does it differ from Random Access Memory in terms of search mechanism and hardware complexity?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition:** Associative Memory (CAM — Content Addressable Memory) is a special memory module that performs a **parallel search** over all stored words for a *content match* with a search argument, in a **single clock cycle**.

**Key Differences from RAM:**

| **Aspect** | **RAM** | **Associative Memory (CAM)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Method** | By address (location) | By content (data value) |
| **Search Time** | O(1) for known address, O(n) for content search | O(1) — parallel compare |
| **Hardware per Cell** | 1 bit of storage only | Storage + comparison circuit |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low | Very high (often 10× area of RAM) |
| **Match Output** | The data at the address | The address of the matching data |
| **Applications** | Main memory, cache | TLB, network routers, AI accelerators |

**Working Principle:** Each CAM cell has a storage element AND a comparator. When the search argument is broadcast on the search lines, all words are compared *simultaneously*. A match pulls the corresponding match line LOW, signaling a hit. A priority encoder resolves multiple matches to a single address.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question offers an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal organization of a 2K × 8 static RAM chip**. Show the address decoding logic, data I/O, and control signals. Compute the number of address lines required.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** A computer system uses **16K × 1** memory chips to build a **64K × 8** memory module. Show the **memory expansion** with proper address decoding. Calculate the number of chips, address lines, and explain the role of the decoder.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute address lines for 2K × 8 chip.**
$$
\text{2K words} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \text{ words} = 2^{11}
$$
So **11 address lines** $\left(A_{10} \text{ to } A_0\right)$ are required. Word size is **8 bits**, so 8 data lines $\left(D_7 \text{ to } D_0\right)$.

**Step 2: Internal Organization of 2K × 8 SRAM.**
The chip consists of:
- A **2D array** of $2048 \times 8$ cells = **16,384 cells** total.
- The 11 address lines are split: **6 lines** for **row decoding** $\left(A_{10} \text{ to } A_5\right)$ and **5 lines** for **column decoding** $\left(A_4 \text{ to } A_0\right)$. The 6-to-64 row decoder selects one of 64 rows, and the 5-to-32 column decoder selects one of 32 column groups, each containing 8 bits. Total cells: $64 \times 32 \times 8 = 16{,}384$ ✓.
- **Sense amplifiers** detect the small differential output of each column.
- **Control signals:** $\overline{CS}$ (Chip Select), $\overline{WE}$ (Write Enable), $\overline{OE}$ (Output Enable).
- **Bidirectional data bus** $D_7 \text{ to } D_0$ with tri-state buffers.

**Diagram (Textual Block Layout):**
```
                +--------------------+
   A10--A5 ---> | ROW DECODER (6:64) |
   A4--A0 --->  | COL DECODER (5:32) |
                +--------------------+
                         |
                  +--------------+
                  | 64 x 32 x 8  |
                  | SRAM ARRAY   |
                  +--------------+
                         |
                  [SENSE AMPS]
                         |
   CS, WE, OE --> [I/O CONTROL]
                         |
                  D7 ---- D0
```

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of chips for capacity expansion.**
$$
N_{CE} = \frac{64\text{K words}}{16\text{K words/chip}} = 4 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 2: Number of chips for word expansion.**
$$
N_{WE} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{1 \text{ bit/chip}} = 8 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 3: Total chips for 64K × 8.**
$$
N_{total} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 4: Address Lines.**
- 64K memory requires $\log_2(65536) = 16$ address lines.
- Each 16K chip requires $\log_2(16384) = 14$ address lines.
- So the lower 14 bits $\left(A_{13} \text{ to } A_0\right)$ go to each chip.
- The upper 2 bits $\left(A_{15}, A_{14}\right)$ go to a **2-to-4 decoder**.

**Step 5: Decoder Logic.**
The 2-to-4 decoder takes $A_{15}A_{14}$ and produces 4 active-LOW outputs:

| **$A_{15}A_{14}$** | **Selected Decoder Output** | **Chip Group** |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | $\overline{Y_0}$ | Group 0 (addresses 0–16K) |
| 01 | $\overline{Y_1}$ | Group 1 (addresses 16K–32K) |
| 10 | $\overline{Y_2}$ | Group 2 (addresses 32K–48K) |
