| 11 | $\overline{Y_3}$ | Group 3 (addresses 48K–64K) |

Each group contains **8 chips** (one for each bit of the 8-bit word), all selected in parallel. The 4 groups are mutually exclusive — only one group is active at a time.

**Step 6: Total chips and address map.**
- **Total chips:** 32.
- **Address range:** 0000H to FFFFH (64K locations).
- **Data bus width:** 8 bits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - December 2023] — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** Explain the **three cache mapping techniques**: Direct, Associative, and Set-Associative mapping. Compare them with formulas and a clear diagram of address partitioning.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** A system has a **2-level cache hierarchy**. L1 cache has access time **3 ns** with a **miss rate of 8%**. L2 cache has access time **15 ns** with a **miss rate of 30%**. Main memory access time is **100 ns**. Compute the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**1. Direct Mapping:**
- Each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line.
- Mapping function: $\text{Cache line} = \text{(Memory block number)} \mod \text{(Number of cache lines)}$
- **Address Partition:** $\text{Tag} \mid \text{Line Index} \mid \text{Block Offset}$
- **Pros:** Simple, fast hardware.
- **Cons:** High conflict misses if two hot blocks map to the same line.

**2. Fully Associative Mapping:**
- A memory block can map to **any** cache line.
- **Address Partition:** $\text{Tag} \mid \text{Block Offset}$ (no line index).
- **Pros:** Lowest conflict misses, highest hit rate.
- **Cons:** Tag must be compared with **every** line's tag — expensive parallel comparators.

**3. Set-Associative Mapping (k-way):**
- Cache is divided into **sets** of $k$ lines each.
- A memory block maps to a specific **set**, but can occupy any of the $k$ lines within that set.
- Mapping: $\text{Set number} = \text{(Block number)} \mod \text{(Number of sets)}$
- **Address Partition:** $\text{Tag} \mid \text{Set Index} \mid \text{Block Offset}$
- **Pros:** Compromise between speed and flexibility.
- **Cons:** Replacement policy (LRU, FIFO, Random) needed within a set.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Direct** | **Associative** | **Set-Associative** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware complexity | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Hit rate | Lowest | Highest | Moderate-High |
| Replacement logic | Not needed | Needed | Needed |
| Search | 1 tag compare | $N$ tag compares (parallel) | $k$ tag compares |
| Conflict misses | High | None | Low |

**Diagram of Address Partitioning (4-Way Set Associative):**

```
[----- TAG -----][--- SET INDEX ---][- OFFSET -]
   20 bits          8 bits            4 bits
```

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t_{L1} = 3$ ns
- Miss rate of L1, $M_1 = 8\% = 0.08$
- $t_{L2} = 15$ ns
- Miss rate of L2, $M_2 = 30\% = 0.30$
- $t_{main} = 100$ ns

**AMAT formula for 2-level cache:**
$$
t_{avg} = t_{L1} + M_1 \cdot \left( t_{L2} + M_2 \cdot t_{main} \right)
$$

**Step 1: Compute effective L2 access time (including main memory miss).**
$$
t_{L2,eff} = t_{L2} + M_2 \cdot t_{main} = 15 + 0.30 \times 100 = 15 + 30 = 45 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Compute final AMAT.**
$$
t_{avg} = t_{L1} + M_1 \cdot t_{L2,eff} = 3 + 0.08 \times 45 = 3 + 3.6 = 6.6 \text{ ns}
$$

**Interpretation:** The system's effective average access time is **6.6 ns** — about 15× faster than going to main memory (100 ns) for every access. The L1 cache is doing the heavy lifting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often add $t_{L1}$ to the L2 access time even on a hit, but the L1 access is **already included in the formula** — the formula starts with $t_{L1}$.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *miss rate* with *miss penalty*. The miss penalty is the *time cost* of a miss, while the miss rate is the *probability* of a miss. The formula uses **miss rate** as a multiplier.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Treating the L1 miss rate as the global miss rate. The L1 miss rate (8%) is the probability of *escaping L1*; the L2 miss rate (30%) is the probability of *escaping L2 to main memory* — these are *conditional* probabilities.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to convert percentages to decimals. 8% = 0.08, NOT 8.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In mapping questions, students often forget to draw the address partition diagram. The KTU board examiner allocates at least **2 marks** for the partition diagram alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

Here is your **rapid-revision checklist** for KTU Module 3 — Memory Modules:

- [ ] **Memory Module Definition:** A complete functional unit of storage cells + address decoder + I/O + control logic, interfacing with the CPU via a parallel bus.

- [ ] **SRAM cell:** 6 transistors, bistable flip-flop, **no refresh**, access time ~1–10 ns, used for **cache**.

- [ ] **DRAM cell:** 1 transistor + 1 capacitor, **requires refresh every ~64 ms**, destructive read followed by auto-rewrite, access time ~50–100 ns, used for **main memory**.

- [ ] **DRAM is ~5–17× denser** than SRAM (depending on process node).

- [ ] **DRAM refresh methods:** Burst, Distributed, Transparent. Trade-off: CPU blocking vs. refresh complexity.

- [ ] **ROM Types in order of flexibility:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash (NOR/NAND).

- [ ] **Flash NOR:** Byte-addressable, used for code storage. **Flash NAND:** Block-addressable, used for SSDs.

- [ ] **Associative Memory (CAM):** Searches by content in a single cycle. Used in TLB and network routers. Very high hardware cost.

- [ ] **Memory Expansion:** Word expansion increases bus width; capacity expansion increases address space. **Both** require decoders (3-to-8, 2-to-4, etc.) using the upper address bits.

- [ ] **Interleaving:** Distributes consecutive addresses across multiple banks. Reduces effective access time for sequential streams.

- [ ] **Cache AMAT:** $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{main}$. Multi-level: $t_{avg} = t_{L1} + M_1 \cdot (t_{L2} + M_2 \cdot t_{main})$.

- [ ] **Cache Mapping:** Direct (1 location), Associative (any location), Set-Associative (any of $k$ locations per set).

- [ ] **Address Partition (set-associative):** $\text{Tag} \mid \text{Set Index} \mid \text{Block Offset}$. Sum must equal total address bits.

- [ ] **Hit Rate vs. Miss Rate:** $H + M = 1$. Hit rate is the most critical performance parameter for caches.

- [ ] **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers → Cache (SRAM) → Main Memory (DRAM) → SSD/HDD → Archive. **Faster = Smaller + Costlier.**

- [ ] **Memory Capacity Formula:** $C = 2^n \times m$ where $n$ = address lines, $m$ = word size in bits. Convert to bytes by dividing by 8, to KB by dividing by 1024, to MB by dividing by 1024².

- [ ] **KTU Board Trick:** In numerical problems, always show **each step of the computation**. Do not write only the final answer — you lose up to **50% of marks** for skipped steps.

- [ ] **KTU Board Trick:** When asked to "explain with diagram," you MUST include a labeled diagram. Block diagrams with proper labels earn full marks.

- [ ] **Common KTU Pitfall:** Confusing **ROM's "Random Access"** with **RAM's**. ROM is also random access! The term refers to the *addressing scheme*, not the *write capability*.

- [ ] **Modern Trend:** All modern systems use **SDRAM** (Synchronous DRAM), with DDR (Double Data Rate) variants being standard. LPDDR for mobile, GDDR for graphics, HBM for AI accelerators.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU ESE, the Module-3 question is almost always a **numerical + diagram combination**. Practice the formulas in Section 2.7 until they are second nature, and always draw the **address partition diagram** for any cache-related question. 💯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic DRAM Memory Access Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic DRAM Memory Access Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BASIC DRAM MEMORY ACCESS PROTOCOL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM)** is a volatile semiconductor memory technology in which each storage cell consists of **one access transistor and one storage capacitor**. Because the capacitor leaks charge, the cell must be **periodically refreshed** (hence the term *dynamic*). The *Basic DRAM Memory Access Protocol* refers to the standardized sequence of control signals — primarily **Row Address Strobe (RAS), Column Address Strobe (CAS), Write Enable (WE), and Chip Select (CS)** — used by the memory controller to perform Read, Write, and Refresh operations on the DRAM array.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Students must master the *signal-by-signal handshake* between the CPU/memory controller and a DRAM chip, including the multiplexed address bus, the timing constraints between RAS, CAS, and data valid windows, and the difference between **Random Access** (any location, equal time) and the two-step **Row-then-Column** addressing inherent to DRAM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Storey Library"

Imagine a massive library where:
- **Books (data bits)** are stored on shelves arranged as **rows × columns**.
- You cannot fetch a single book directly. First, the **librarian activates an entire row** (all books on that shelf come down to a "review desk"), then you **point to one specific book on that desk by its column number**.
- The "review desk" is the **sense amplifier / row buffer**.
- The librarian resets the desk (precharge) before fetching a new shelf.

This is exactly how DRAM works: **activate row → latch entire row into sense amps → select column → read/write that single bit** → precharge to prepare for the next operation.

### 1.3 The Four Standard Control Signals

| Signal | Symbol | Function |
|--------|--------|----------|
| **Row Address Strobe** | RAS# | Latches the **row address**; activates that row into the sense amplifiers |
| **Column Address Strobe** | CAS# | Latches the **column address**; selects the bit inside the active row |
| **Write Enable** | WE# | Logic **0 = WRITE**, Logic **1 = READ** |
| **Chip Select** | CS# | Enables a particular DRAM chip on the bus |

> [!NOTE]
> The hash symbol (#) denotes **active-low** signals (asserted when LOW). This is standard JEDEC DRAM convention.

### 1.4 Why DRAM Uses a Multiplexed Address Bus

To reduce pin count, DRAM chips share the same physical address pins (A0–An) for **both** row and column addresses. The protocol decides *which* address is currently being interpreted by the state of **RAS#** and **CAS#**:

- **RAS# asserted first** → pins carry the **row address**
- **CAS# asserted next** → pins carry the **column address**

This is called **address multiplexing** and is the defining hallmark of asynchronous DRAM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a DRAM Chip

A DRAM chip is organized as a **2D array of cells** (rows × columns). Surrounding this array are:

- **Row Decoder** — selects one of $2^R$ rows based on the row address.
- **Column Decoder / Mux** — selects one of $2^C$ columns.
- **Sense Amplifiers (Row Buffer)** — detect the tiny charge stored in each cell of the activated row and amplify it to a full logic level.
- **Refresh Logic** — periodically recharges cell capacitors.

The total memory capacity is given by:

$$
\text{Capacity (bits)} = 2^R \times 2^C \times N_{\text{banks}}
$$

where $R$ = number of row address bits, $C$ = number of column address bits, $N_{\text{banks}}$ = number of internal banks.

> [!NOTE]
> A **bank** is an independent 2D array within the same chip. Modern SDRAM/DDR chips have 4 or 8 banks, allowing **interleaved** operations for higher throughput.

## 2.2 The DRAM Read Protocol — Step-by-Step Logic

The **asynchronous DRAM read cycle** follows this canonical sequence:

1. **Idle State** — All control signals are HIGH (de-asserted). Address bus is in a don't-care state.
2. **Row Address Placement** — The memory controller drives the **row address** onto the multiplexed address pins A0–An.
3. **Row Activation (RAS# LOW)** — The controller pulls **RAS# LOW**. The row address is latched into the row decoder; the entire selected row is transferred to the sense amplifiers (this is *destructive read* — the original charge is automatically rewritten).
4. **Address Hold + RAS# HIGH** — After the row address hold time $t_{RAH}$, the controller drives **RAS# HIGH** to begin internal operations.
5. **Column Address Placement** — The controller now drives the **column address** onto the same A0–An pins.
6. **Column Selection (CAS# LOW)** — The controller pulls **CAS# LOW**, latching the column address. The selected bit is routed to the **Data Output buffer**.
7. **Data Valid on Bus** — After the **CAS access time $t_{CAC}$**, valid data appears on DQ pins. (For a full random access, the relevant metric is $t_{RAC}$ — the access time measured from the falling edge of RAS#.)
8. **CAS# HIGH (Precharge begins)** — After data hold time $t_{DHO}$, the controller raises CAS#. Internally, the bank enters **precharge**, readying for the next row access.
9. **Precharge Complete** — After **$t_{RP}$ (RAS Precharge Time)**, a new RAS# assertion is allowed.

## 2.3 The DRAM Write Protocol — Step-by-Step Logic

A write cycle is nearly identical to a read, except:

- **WE# is asserted LOW** at the same time CAS# is asserted LOW (or slightly before).
- The **CPU/Controller drives the data** onto the DQ bus coincident with CAS# going LOW.
- The sense amplifier value for the selected column is overwritten.
- WE# HIGH after $t_{W}$ (write pulse width) ends the write operation.

## 2.4 DRAM Refresh Protocol

Because capacitors leak, every cell must be refreshed within a **refresh window**, typically **64 ms** at room temperature (JEDEC standard). Two common refresh schemes:

- **RAS-Only Refresh (ROR)**: Hold WE# HIGH and CAS# HIGH; strobe RAS# with successive row addresses. No data transfer occurs.
- **CAS-Before-RAS (CBR) Refresh**: Assert CAS# LOW before RAS# LOW. The chip's **internal refresh counter** auto-increments the row to be refreshed. This is the dominant scheme in modern SDRAM.

The required refresh frequency is:

$$
f_{\text{refresh}} = \frac{N_{\text{rows}}}{T_{\text{refresh\_window}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Refresh operations **steal time** from normal read/write accesses — typically 1–5% of total bandwidth. This overhead is accounted for in any realistic performance analysis.

## 2.5 Key Timing Parameters (KTU Exam Favourites)

| Symbol | Name | Typical Value | Meaning |
|--------|------|---------------|---------|
| $t_{RAC}$ | Random Access Time | **60–100 ns** | Time from RAS# falling edge to data valid |
| $t_{CAC}$ | CAS Access Time | **15–25 ns** | Time from CAS# falling edge to data valid |
| $t_{RCD}$ | RAS-to-CAS Delay | **20–30 ns** | Minimum gap between RAS# and CAS# assertions |
| $t_{RP}$ | Precharge Time | **20–40 ns** | Time required to close a row before opening another |
| $t_{RAS}$ | RAS Active Time | **60–100 ns** | Minimum duration RAS# must stay LOW |
| $t_{RC}$ | Random Cycle Time | **110–150 ns** | Minimum time between two successive accesses to **different rows** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students frequently confuse $t_{RC}$ with $t_{RAS}$. Remember:
> $$t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$$
> The cycle time includes **both** the active phase and the precharge phase.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Memory Capacity | $\text{Cap} = 2^R \times 2^C \times \text{Banks} \times \text{BusWidth}$ | bits |
| 2 | Refresh Frequency | $f_{\text{ref}} = N_{\text{rows}} / T_{\text{retention}}$ | rows/sec |
| 3 | Random Cycle Time | $t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$ | ns |
| 4 | Page-Mode Cycle Time | $t_{PC} \approx t_{CAS} + t_{PNP}$ | ns (much faster than $t_{RC}$) |
| 5 | Peak Bandwidth (asynchronous) | $BW = W / t_{RC}$ | bits/sec |
| 6 | Peak Bandwidth (page mode) | $BW_{\text{page}} = W / t_{PC}$ | bits/sec |
| 7 | Burst Throughput (SDRAM) | $T = B \times t_{\text{clk}}$ | ns for burst of length $B$ |
| 8 | Effective Access Time (cache + main mem) | $t_{\text{eff}} = H \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot t_{\text{mem}}$ | s |

> [!NOTE]
> $W$ = data bus width in bits; $H$ = cache hit ratio; $B$ = burst length (e.g., 8 for DDR); $t_{\text{clk}}$ = SDRAM clock period.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

The DRAM access protocol is the **lingua franca of all computing** — every CPU from an 8051 microcontroller to a server-grade AMD EPYC must speak some variant of it. Mastering this protocol is essential for:

- Designing **memory controllers** in SoCs (e.g., DDR4/DDR5 PHYs).
- **Embedded systems engineers** selecting the right SDRAM (SDR, DDR, LPDDR) for power/performance budgets.
- **Performance engineers** optimizing memory access patterns to exploit *row-buffer hits* (when consecutive accesses hit the same open row → burst mode saves massive time).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Random Access Time Calculation

**Problem:** A DRAM chip has $t_{RAC} = 70$ ns, $t_{CAC} = 20$ ns, $t_{RCD} = 25$ ns, $t_{RP} = 30$ ns. The memory controller issues back-to-back accesses to two **different** rows. Compute the minimum time between the start of the first access and the start of the second.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant timing chain.**  
For random (different-row) accesses, the bottleneck is the **random cycle time $t_{RC}$**, which is governed by the sequence:

$$
\text{Activate Row 1} \rightarrow \text{Read/Write} \rightarrow \text{Precharge Row 1} \rightarrow \text{Activate Row 2}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the minimum $t_{RAS}$.**  
The row must remain active at least long enough for the data to be read out after CAS#:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RAS_{\min}} &= t_{RCD} + t_{CAC} \\
              &= 25 \text{ ns} + 20 \text{ ns} \\
              &= 45 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

But the chip's datasheet specifies $t_{RAS} \geq 60$ ns (typical DRAM). Therefore the *binding* constraint is the datasheet value, not the algebraic minimum. We use $t_{RAS} = 60$ ns for this calculation.

**Step 3 — Compute $t_{RC}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RC} &= t_{RAS} + t_{RP} \\
       &= 60 \text{ ns} + 30 \text{ ns} \\
       &= 90 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State the answer.** The minimum gap between two consecutive different-row accesses is **90 ns**, yielding a peak throughput of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= \frac{W}{t_{RC}} = \frac{64 \text{ bits}}{90 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \\
   &= 7.11 \times 10^8 \text{ bits/s} \\
   &= 711 \text{ Mbits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Refresh Overhead

**Problem:** A DRAM has $N_{\text{rows}} = 8192$ rows and must be fully refreshed every $T_{\text{ret}} = 64$ ms. Each refresh operation takes $t_{\text{ref}} = 200$ ns. Compute the refresh frequency and the **percentage of total memory bandwidth** lost to refresh.

**Step 1 — Compute refresh frequency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{refresh}} &= \frac{N_{\text{rows}}}{T_{\text{retention}}} \\
                    &= \frac{8192}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \\
                    &= 128{,}000 \text{ refreshes/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total time spent refreshing per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ref/sec}} &= f_{\text{refresh}} \times t_{\text{ref}} \\
                   &= 128{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} \\
                   &= 0.0256 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute refresh overhead percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{T_{\text{ref/sec}}}{1 \text{ s}} \times 100 \\
                  &= 0.0256 \times 100 \\
                  &= 2.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** About **2.56%** of memory bandwidth is permanently consumed by refresh — a non-trivial cost in high-performance systems.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Effective Access Time with a Cache

**Problem:** A system has an L1 cache with hit ratio $H = 0.95$, cache access time $t_{\text{cache}} = 5$ ns, and DRAM access time $t_{\text{mem}} = 70$ ns (including controller overhead). Compute the **average memory access time (AMAT)**.

**Step 1 — Apply the AMAT formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= H \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot (t_{\text{cache}} + t_{\text{mem}}) \\
              &= H \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot t_{\text{mem}} \\
              &= t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot t_{\text{mem}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 5 \text{ ns} + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 70 \text{ ns} \\
              &= 5 \text{ ns} + 0.05 \cdot 70 \text{ ns} \\
              &= 5 \text{ ns} + 3.5 \text{ ns} \\
              &= 8.5 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Caching reduces average access time from **70 ns** down to **8.5 ns** — an **8.2× speedup**, demonstrating why hierarchical memory is fundamental.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Read vs. Write vs. Refresh Comparison

| Feature | Read Cycle | Write Cycle | Refresh Cycle |
|---------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| WE# state | **HIGH** | **LOW** | **HIGH** |
| CAS# asserted? | Yes (with column addr) | Yes (with column addr) | No (CBR uses CAS-before-RAS) |
| Data direction | DRAM → CPU | CPU → DRAM | None |
| Destructive to cell? | Yes (auto-restored) | Yes (overwritten) | No (charge topped up) |
| Time consumed | $t_{RAC}$ typical | Similar to read | $t_{\text{RFC}}$ (typically ~75 ns) |
| Address source | External (RAS + CAS) | External (RAS + CAS) | Internal counter (CBR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Compare the signal sequence of a DRAM read, write, and refresh."* This table is your one-stop revision sheet.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DRAM Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["Memory Controller / CPU"]
        cpu1["CPU Core"]
        ctrl1["DRAM Controller FSM"]
        cpu1 --> ctrl1
    end

    subgraph ADDR["Address Bus Multiplexer"]
        a1["Row Address Latch"]
        a2["Column Address Latch"]
        ctrl1 -- "Row Addr" --> a1
        ctrl1 -- "Col Addr" --> a2
    end

    subgraph SIG["Control Signal Generator"]
        s1["RAS# Driver"]
        s2["CAS# Driver"]
        s3["WE# Driver"]
        s4["CS# Driver"]
    end

    subgraph CHIP["DRAM Chip"]
        rowdec["Row Decoder"]
        coldec["Column Decoder / Mux"]
        sense["Sense Amplifiers / Row Buffer"]
        cellbank["Memory Cell Array\nRows x Columns\n1T + 1C per cell"]
        refresh["Refresh Counter\nand Logic"]
        rowdec --> cellbank
        coldec --> sense
        cellbank --> sense
        refresh --> cellbank
    end

    a1 --> rowdec
    a2 --> coldec
    s1 --> rowdec
    s2 --> coldec
    s3 --> sense
    s4 --> CHIP
    sense --> DQ["DQ Data Bus"]

    ctrl1 -. "DRAM Protocol:\nRAS-CAS-WE" .- s1
    ctrl1 -. "DRAM Protocol:\nRAS-CAS-WE" .- s2
    ctrl1 -. "DRAM Protocol:\nRAS-CAS-WE" .- s3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DRAM Read Protocol Sequence (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["Cycle Start"]) --> idle1["IDLE: All signals HIGH"]
    idle1 --> placeRow["Place ROW address on Address Bus"]
    placeRow --> rasLow["Assert RAS# LOW\nRow latched into Row Decoder"]
    rasLow --> senseAct["Entire row loaded into Sense Amplifiers\nDestructive read auto-restored"]
    senseAct --> waitRCD["Wait t_RCD minimum"]
    waitRCD --> placeCol["Place COLUMN address on Address Bus"]
    placeCol --> casLow["Assert CAS# LOW\nColumn latched into Column Decoder"]
    casLow --> waitCAC["Wait t_CAC"]
    waitCAC --> dataValid["Data appears on DQ bus"]
    dataValid --> casHigh["Raise CAS# HIGH"]
    casHigh --> rasHigh["Raise RAS# HIGH\nBegin PRECHARGE"]
    rasHigh --> waitRP["Wait t_RP for precharge to complete"]
    waitRP --> idle2["Return to IDLE"]
    idle2 --> startA

    style startA fill:#FFD700
    style dataValid fill:#90EE90
    style idle2 fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DRAM State Machine (Read / Write / Refresh)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> ROW_ACTIVE : RAS# LOW\nRow Address Valid
    ROW_ACTIVE --> COLUMN_ACTIVE : CAS# LOW\nColumn Address Valid
    COLUMN_ACTIVE --> DATA_OUT : t_CAC elapsed\nWE# = HIGH (Read)
    COLUMN_ACTIVE --> DATA_IN : t_CAS elapsed\nWE# = LOW (Write)
    DATA_OUT --> PRECHARGE : CAS# HIGH then RAS# HIGH
    DATA_IN --> PRECHARGE : WE# HIGH then CAS# HIGH
    PRECHARGE --> IDLE : t_RP elapsed

    IDLE --> REFRESH_MODE : CAS# LOW before RAS# LOW (CBR)
    REFRESH_MODE --> IDLE : Internal counter auto-refreshes\none row per RAS# strobe

    note right of REFRESH_MODE
        No data transfer.
        Refresh counter auto-increments.
    end note

    note left of COLUMN_ACTIVE
        Sense amp holds row.
        Burst/page mode possible
        if CAS# toggled repeatedly.
    end note
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Refresh Cycle Sequence (CBR Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1["Step 1: CAS# LOW first"] --> r2["Step 2: Wait t_CSR"]
    r2 --> r3["Step 3: RAS# LOW\nInternal counter selects next row"]
    r3 --> r4["Step 4: Both RAS# and CAS# HIGH\nRow auto-refreshed internally"]
    r4 --> r5["Step 5: Repeat for all N_rows\nwithin 64 ms window"]
    r5 --> r1

    style r3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style r4 fill:#FFB6C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the role of RAS# and CAS# signals in a DRAM access protocol. Why is the address bus multiplexed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RAS# (Row Address Strobe):** An active-low control signal asserted by the memory controller to **latch the row address** into the DRAM's row decoder. It triggers the activation of the selected row, copying the contents of all cells in that row into the sense amplifiers.

**CAS# (Column Address Strobe):** An active-low control signal asserted next to **latch the column address** into the column decoder/mux. It selects the specific bit within the already-activated row to be routed to the data bus.

**Why Multiplexed Address Bus:** To **minimize pin count** on the DRAM package. The same physical pins (A0–An) carry the row address first (during RAS#) and then the column address (during CAS#). This reduces package cost, silicon area, and bus complexity — critical for cost-sensitive commodity DRAM. The protocol guarantees correct interpretation via the strict RAS#-then-CAS# timing order. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state "RAS selects column and CAS selects row" — this is **backwards** and will cost full marks. RAS = **R**ow, CAS = **C**olumn.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is DRAM called "dynamic"? Explain any one refresh technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DRAM is called **dynamic** because each storage cell uses a **capacitor to store charge**, and this charge **leaks away over time** (typically within a few milliseconds). If not replenished, the stored bit will be lost. Therefore, every cell must be **periodically rewritten** — this dynamic maintenance is the origin of the name. *(1.5 marks)*

**CAS-Before-RAS (CBR) Refresh Technique:** The memory controller asserts **CAS# LOW first**, followed by **RAS# LOW**. This specific signal ordering is interpreted by the DRAM chip as a refresh command. The chip's **internal refresh counter** automatically generates the next row address, refreshes that row, and increments. The controller must repeat this for all $2^R$ rows within the refresh window (64 ms for standard JEDEC). No external address is required, and no data transfer takes place. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Pattern**, students answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b) carrying 14 marks. Two alternative choices are provided below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step sequence of a **DRAM READ cycle**. Draw the corresponding **timing diagram** showing the relationship between RAS#, CAS#, Address bus, and Data bus signals. Mention the key timing parameters $t_{RAC}$, $t_{CAC}$, $t_{RCD}$, and $t_{RP}$.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Idle State:** All control signals (RAS#, CAS#, WE#, CS#) are HIGH. Address and data buses are in a high-impedance/idle state.

**Step 2 — Row Address Placement:** The memory controller drives the **row address** onto the multiplexed address bus A0–An.

**Step 3 — Row Activation (RAS# LOW):** The controller asserts **RAS# LOW**. The row address is latched into the row decoder. After $t_{RAH}$ (row address hold time), the address bus can be changed.

**Step 4 — Sense Amplification:** Internally, the entire selected row is read destructively into the sense amplifiers and the original charge is automatically restored to each cell (this refresh-by-read is a free benefit).

**Step 5 — Column Address Placement:** After at least $t_{RCD}$ (RAS-to-CAS delay), the controller drives the **column address** onto the address bus.

**Step 6 — Column Selection (CAS# LOW):** The controller asserts **CAS# LOW**. The column decoder selects the specific bit from the sense amplifier row buffer.

**Step 7 — Data Valid:** After $t_{CAC}$ (CAS access time), valid data appears on the DQ bus. The **total random access time** measured from RAS# falling edge to data valid is $t_{RAC} \approx t_{RCD} + t_{CAC}$.

**Step 8 — De-assertion:** CAS# goes HIGH, then RAS# goes HIGH. The bank begins **precharge**.

**Step 9 — Precharge Complete:** After $t_{RP}$ (precharge time), the DRAM is ready for the next access.

**ASCII Timing Diagram:**

```
              ___                          _____________________________
RAS#   ______/   \________________________/                             \_____
              |<- t_RCD ->|
              ___                    ___                       ___
CAS#   ______________/            \___/                      \________________
                                  |<-t_CAC->|
Addr   [ROW_ADD]   [X]            [COL_ADD]   [X]            [X]
                                  |          |
Data   ----------- (high-Z) ----------------| VALID |---- (high-Z) -----
                                              |<- t_DHO ->|
              |<----------- t_RAS ----------->|
                                                |<-- t_RP -->|
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DRAM has $t_{RAS} = 60$ ns, $t_{RP} = 30$ ns, $t_{RCD} = 25$ ns, $t_{CAC} = 20$ ns, and a 64-bit data bus. Compute:  
(i) The random cycle time $t_{RC}$ and peak bandwidth for sequential accesses to **different rows**.  
(ii) The same metrics for **page-mode (burst)** accesses where $t_{PC} = 25$ ns (column access only, no row re-activation).

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Different-Row Accesses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RC} &= t_{RAS} + t_{RP} \\
       &= 60 + 30 \\
       &= 90 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Peak bandwidth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{random}} &= \frac{W}{t_{RC}} = \frac{64 \text{ bits}}{90 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \\
                   &= 7.11 \times 10^8 \text{ bits/s} \\
                   &= 711 \text{ Mbits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Page-Mode (Same-Row Burst) Accesses:**

In page mode, the row is already activated — only CAS# toggling is needed for subsequent columns. Thus $t_{RC}$ is replaced by the **page-mode cycle time $t_{PC}$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{page}} &= \frac{W}{t_{PC}} = \frac{64 \text{ bits}}{25 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \\
                &= 2.56 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \\
                &= 2560 \text{ Mbits/s} = 2.56 \text{ Gbits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{BW_{\text{page}}}{BW_{\text{random}}} = \frac{90}{25} = 3.6\times
$$

**Conclusion:** Hitting the same open row (row-buffer hit) yields a **3.6× bandwidth improvement** — this is why modern programs are optimized for *spatial locality*.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal organization of a DRAM chip** and describe how a memory cell stores a bit. Why is a read operation "destructive"?

#### Model Solution:

**The DRAM Memory Cell — 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor (1T1C):**

Each cell consists of one **MOSFET access transistor** and one **storage capacitor**:

```
       Word Line (Row Select)
            |
            Drain
   Bit Line ──[ T ]── Gate connected to Word Line
            |
       [ C ]  ← Storage capacitor (stores '1' as charge, '0' as no charge)
            |
           GND
```

- **Write '1':** Word line is raised, transistor turns ON, charge flows from bit line onto capacitor.
- **Write '0':** Word line is raised, capacitor is discharged through the bit line.
- **Read:** Word line is raised, the charge on the capacitor perturbs the bit line voltage, which is detected by a **sense amplifier**.

**Why Read is Destructive:** When the sense amplifier reads the tiny charge, the act of detection **drains** the capacitor's charge to a level that no longer reliably represents the original bit. To preserve data, the sense amplifier **immediately rewrites the detected value back into the cell** — this is called *read-with-restore* or *destructive-read-with-auto-restore*. *(2 marks)*

**Internal Organization:**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                DRAM CHIP                     │
   │                                              │
   │  ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐            │
   │  │ Row Decoder│    │ Row Decoder│  (Banks)   │
   │  └─────┬──────┘    └─────┬──────┘            │
   │        │                 │                   │
   │  ┌─────▼──────┐    ┌─────▼──────┐            │
   │  │ Cell Array │    │ Cell Array │            │
   │  │  R × C     │    │  R × C     │            │
   │  └─────┬──────┘    └─────┬──────┘            │
   │        │                 │                   │
   │  ┌─────▼──────┐    ┌─────▼──────┐            │
   │  │Sense Amps  │    │Sense Amps  │            │
   │  │(Row Buffer)│    │(Row Buffer)│            │
   │  └─────┬──────┘    └─────┬──────┘            │
   │        └────────┬────────┘                   │
   │           ┌─────▼──────┐                     │
   │           │  Column    │                     │
   │           │  Mux/Dec   │                     │
   │           └─────┬──────┘                     │
   │           ┌─────▼──────┐                     │
   │           │  DQ Buffer │───► Data Bus        │
   │           └────────────┘                     │
   │                                              │
   │  ┌──────────────┐                            │
   │  │ Refresh      │                            │
   │  │ Controller   │                            │
   │  └──────────────┘                            │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Key blocks: **Row Decoder, Memory Cell Array, Sense Amplifiers (Row Buffer), Column Multiplexer, Refresh Controller, Data Output Buffer**. *(2 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **RAS-Only Refresh** and **CAS-Before-RAS Refresh** schemes. A 1 Gbit DRAM has $2^{14}$ = 16,384 rows and must be refreshed within 64 ms. Each refresh takes 100 ns. Calculate the **refresh overhead percentage**.

#### Model Solution:

**RAS-Only Refresh (ROR):**  
- WE# = HIGH, CAS# = HIGH throughout.  
- The controller supplies successive **row addresses** on the address bus.  
- It strobes **RAS# LOW** for each row, one at a time.  
- After all rows are refreshed, the cycle repeats.  
- **Disadvantage:** Consumes address bus bandwidth because the controller must generate every row address. *(2 marks)*

**CAS-Before-RAS Refresh (CBR):**  
- The controller asserts **CAS# LOW first**, then **RAS# LOW**.  
- The DRAM chip recognizes this specific ordering as a refresh command.  
- The chip's **internal refresh counter** automatically supplies the row address, refreshes that row, and increments.  
- The controller simply repeats the CAS#-then-RAS# sequence $2^R$ times.  
- **Advantage:** Frees the external address bus; faster; widely used in modern SDRAM. *(2 marks)*

**Refresh Overhead Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Refresh frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{refresh}} &= \frac{N_{\text{rows}}}{T_{\text{retention}}} \\
                   &= \frac{16{,}384}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \\
                   &= 256{,}000 \text{ refreshes/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total refresh time per second:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ref/sec}} &= f_{\text{refresh}} \times t_{\text{ref}} \\
                   &= 256{,}000 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} \\
                   &= 0.0256 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overhead percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{0.0256}{1} \times 100 = 2.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The DRAM loses **2.56%** of its available time to refresh operations — an unavoidable cost of capacitor-based storage. *(3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on DRAM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Signal Polarity:** RAS#, CAS#, WE#, CS# are **active-LOW**. Drawing them HIGH during assertion loses marks.
> 2. **Address Multiplexing Order:** Always **RAS# first, then CAS#**. Reversing the order is a fatal error.
> 3. **Units:** $t_{RAC}$, $t_{CAC}$, etc. are in **nanoseconds**. Converting to bandwidth requires careful $10^{-9}$ handling.
> 4. **Refresh Overhead:** Some students forget to multiply $f_{\text{refresh}}$ by $t_{\text{ref}}$ and report only the frequency — partial credit only.
> 5. **Read = Destructive:** Always mention the **auto-restore** mechanism; otherwise the explanation is incomplete.
> 6. **Page Mode:** Students often confuse page-mode cycle time with random cycle time — remember page mode = same row already open = only CAS# toggling needed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **DRAM = Dynamic RAM** = each cell is **1 transistor + 1 capacitor**; charge leaks → must refresh.
- 🔹 The **access protocol** is a strict handshake: **RAS# → CAS# → Data** (or Data-In for writes).
- 🔹 **Address multiplexing** halves pin count — row address first, then column address on the same pins.
- 🔹 **Read is destructive** — sense amplifiers automatically restore the charge.
- 🔹 **WE# LOW = Write, HIGH = Read.**
- 🔹 **Two refresh schemes:** ROR (external addresses) and **CBR (internal counter)** — CBR is dominant.
- 🔹 **Refresh window = 64 ms** (JEDEC); refresh overhead is typically **2–5%**.
- 🔹 **Key timings:** $t_{RAC}$, $t_{CAC}$, $t_{RCD}$, $t_{RP}$, $t_{RAS}$ — must be respected or data corruption occurs.
- 🔹 **Cycle time formula:** $t_{RC} = t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$.
- 🔹 **Page mode** = same-row burst access → ~3–4× faster than random access → exploit spatial locality.
- 🔹 **Bank interleaving** in modern SDRAM hides precharge latency of one bank behind active access of another.
- 🔹 **Bandwidth** $BW = W / t_{\text{cycle}}$; optimize by minimizing $t_{\text{cycle}}$ (use page mode, burst mode, prefetch).
- 🔹 **Cache AMAT** $t_{\text{eff}} = t_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot t_{\text{mem}}$ — DRAM's slowness is precisely why caches exist.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic DRAM Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic DRAM Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Basic DRAM Commands

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic DRAM Commands** are the fundamental low-level control signals issued by the memory controller to the DRAM chips to perform storage operations. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, these commands govern how data is written to, read from, retained in, and managed within a Dynamic RAM array using a combination of control line activations (RAS, CAS, WE, CS) and address multiplexing.

A **DRAM (Dynamic Random-Access Memory)** stores each bit of data in a tiny capacitor within an array of cells. Because the charge in these capacitors leaks away over time, the memory must be periodically **refreshed**. The "Basic DRAM Commands" refer to the standardized set of control instructions (Active, Read, Write, Precharge, Refresh) that the memory controller uses to manipulate this array.

The classical DRAM interface is controlled by **4 primary signals**:

| Signal | Full Form | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RAS#** | Row Address Strobe | Selects/Activates a row of the memory array |
| **CAS#** | Column Address Strobe | Selects a column for read/write |
| **WE#** | Write Enable | Distinguishes Read (1) from Write (0) operation |
| **CS#** | Chip Select | Enables the specific DRAM chip |

> [!NOTE]
> The `#` suffix indicates **active-low** signals (logic 0 means active). KTU examiners frequently test the polarity of these control lines — keep this in mind.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **large multi-storey library building**:
- The **library building** = the entire DRAM chip.
- Each **floor** = a **Row** of the memory array.
- Each **bookshelf slot on a floor** = a **Column** (individual memory cell).
- The **gatekeeper** = the memory controller.

To retrieve a book (data), the librarian must:
1. **First, ring a bell to open the entire floor** (RAS — Row Address Strobe). All books on that floor are brought out to a reading table.
2. **Then, point to the specific shelf** (CAS — Column Address Strobe) to read or write the book.
3. **After finishing**, lock the floor back up to save resources (Precharge).
4. Every few hours, the librarian must **dust and reshelve all books** so they don't decay (Refresh).

This two-step access (Row then Column) is why DRAM is called **multiplexed addressing** — the address pins are reused, first carrying the row address, then the column address.

---

### 1.3 Why DRAM Uses Commands (The Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DRAM is fundamentally different from SRAM.** In SRAM, any cell is accessible in a single cycle. In DRAM, the cell's capacitor must first be **activated** (connecting it to the sense amplifier), then accessed. This is why DRAM commands are *multi-step* and *stateful*.

The basic DRAM commands form a **state machine**:

$$\text{Power-Up} \rightarrow \text{Idle} \rightarrow \text{Active} \rightarrow \text{Read/Write} \rightarrow \text{Precharge} \rightarrow \text{Idle}$$

Each transition is triggered by a specific **command** issued by the memory controller.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Complete Set of Basic DRAM Commands

Modern DRAMs (SDRAM, DDR, DDR2/3/4/5) inherit their command set from asynchronous DRAM. The **core set of basic DRAM commands** is:

| # | Command | RAS# | CAS# | WE# | CS# | Purpose |
| :- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Active (Open Page)** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Activates a row; brings it to sense amplifiers |
| 2 | **Read** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Reads data from a column in the active row |
| 3 | **Write** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Writes data to a column in the active row |
| 4 | **Precharge** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Closes the active row; prepares for new row access |
| 5 | **Refresh (RAS-only)** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Refreshes a row using only RAS# (no external address) |
| 6 | **No Operation (NOP)** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Idle state; no operation performed |
| 7 | **Burst Terminate (BT)** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Stops a burst read/write sequence early |

> [!NOTE]
> In synchronous DRAM (SDRAM/DDR), these commands are latched on the **rising edge of the clock**. In asynchronous DRAM, they are level-triggered. KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on the **functional meaning** of these commands, not the clocking details.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Operation of Each Command

#### (A) **ACTIVE Command (Open Page / Row Activation)**

- **Trigger**: RAS# = 0, CAS# = 1, WE# = 1.
- **Action**: The row address on the address bus is latched. The entire row of memory cells is connected to the **sense amplifiers**.
- **Latency**: **t<sub>RCD</sub>** (Row-to-Column Delay) — typically **2 to 3 clock cycles** in modern DDR.
- **Why it exists**: A DRAM cell stores a tiny charge (~10s of fC). Reading it *destructively* requires amplifying the signal first.

> **Analogy:** Pressing the "Open Floor" button in an elevator — the whole floor becomes available for inspection.

#### (B) **READ Command**

- **Trigger**: RAS# = 1, CAS# = 0, WE# = 1.
- **Action**: The column address is latched. Data from the addressed cell flows out on the **DQ lines** after a delay.
- **Latency**: **CAS Latency (CL)** — number of clock cycles from READ command to first valid data.
- **Variants**: Single read or **Burst read** (e.g., 2, 4, 8, or full-page burst).

#### (C) **WRITE Command**

- **Trigger**: RAS# = 1, CAS# = 0, WE# = 0.
- **Action**: The column address is latched; data on DQ is written to the addressed cell.
- **Latency**: Write latency is generally lower than read latency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** In READ, **WE# = 1** (Write Enable is HIGH, meaning "not writing"). In WRITE, **WE# = 0**. Examiners often ask students to identify the correct signal combination.

#### (D) **PRECHARGE Command**

- **Trigger**: RAS# = 0, CAS# = 1, WE# = 0.
- **Action**: Closes the currently open row and prepares the sense amplifiers for a new row activation. It **equalizes** the bitline voltages to V<sub>DD</sub>/2.
- **Latency**: **t<sub>RP</sub>** (Row Precharge Time).
- **Auto-Precharge**: A read/write command can be issued with **A10 = 1**, which automatically precharges the row when the burst completes.

#### (E) **REFRESH Command**

DRAM capacitors lose charge (leakage current ~ pA per cell). Therefore, every cell must be rewritten within the **refresh interval** (typically **64 ms** for the entire array, or **7.8 µs per row**).

Two classical refresh mechanisms:

1. **RAS-Only Refresh (ROR)**
   - Issue an **ACTIVE** command with only the row address; no column access.
   - Internally, the sense amplifier rewrites the cell contents.
   - Disadvantage: Consumes full t<sub>RCD</sub> + access time per row.

2. **CAS-Before-RAS (CBR) Refresh**
   - CAS# is asserted *before* RAS# (a condition detected by the DRAM).
   - The DRAM uses an **internal counter** to auto-increment the row address.
   - Used in modern SDRAMs; faster and frees the address bus.

> **Modern SDRAMs also implement Self-Refresh (SR)** — the DRAM refreshes itself using an internal timer, allowing the controller to be powered down in low-power states.

#### (F) **NO OPERATION (NOP)**

- **Trigger**: All control lines de-asserted appropriately.
- **Action**: Performs no operation. Used to satisfy timing constraints without changing state.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and metrics are **must-know** for solving numerical problems in the KTU University Exam.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (DDR3) | Unit | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Row-to-Column Delay | t<sub>RCD</sub> | 13.5 - 15 | ns | Time from ACTIVE to READ/WRITE |
| CAS Latency | CL | 9 - 14 | cycles | Cycles from READ to first data |
| Row Precharge Time | t<sub>RP</sub> | 13.5 - 15 | ns | Time to close an open row |
| Row Active Time | t<sub>RAS</sub> | 36 | ns | Min time row must stay open |
| Refresh Interval | t<sub>REF</sub> | **64** | ms | Full-array refresh period |
| Rows per Array | N | 8192 | — | Total rows to refresh in t<sub>REF</sub> |
| Refresh Cycles per Row | t<sub>RFC</sub> | 160 - 350 | ns | Time to refresh one row |

**Key derived formulas:**

1. **Time per row refresh (CBR):**
$$\text{Refresh Period per Row} = \frac{t_{REF}}{N_{rows}}$$

For N = 8192 rows, t<sub>REF</sub> = 64 ms:

$$\text{Refresh Period per Row} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{8192} \approx 7.81 \;\mu s$$

2. **Total access latency for a complete DRAM cycle (read miss, open new row):**
$$t_{access} = t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK} + t_{RP}$$

3. **Average memory access time** (assuming probability *h* of row hit):
$$T_{avg} = h \cdot (t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK}) + (1-h) \cdot (t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK} + t_{RP})$$

4. **Data rate of burst transfer:**
$$\text{Data Rate} = \frac{\text{Burst Length}}{t_{burst}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> When using `$|x|$`-style absolute values in any table cell, always write `\vert x \vert`. **Never use bare `|`** inside markdown table rows — it breaks the column structure.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of DRAM Command Knowledge |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Design** | Memory controller schedulers use these commands to issue *out-of-order* requests. |
| **GPU (GDDR/HBM)** | High-bandwidth memory uses similar commands but with wider buses. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Low-power MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) use *self-refresh* during sleep. |
| **Operating Systems** | The kernel flushes caches before *self-refresh* on suspend. |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Row-Hammer attacks exploit repeated ACTIVATE commands to flip bits. |

---

### 2.5 State Transition Matrix (Command Truth Table)

The following **logic truth table** is the most tested item in KTU exams:

| Command | CS# | RAS# | CAS# | WE# |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **No Operation (NOP)** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Active (Select Row)** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **Read** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Write** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Precharge (Select Bank)** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Precharge All** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 (A10=1) |
| **Auto-Refresh (CBR)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **Self-Refresh Entry** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 (CKE=0) |
| **Burst Terminate** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Mode Register Set (MRS)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> The CAS-Before-RAS (CBR) refresh is detected by the DRAM as the condition where **CAS# is asserted (low) before RAS# transitions low**. The DRAM's internal sequencer then performs the refresh using its built-in row counter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Refresh Command Timing Constraints

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> A DRAM has 8192 rows, requires the full array to be refreshed in 64 ms, and uses CBR refresh. The system clock period is t<sub>CK</sub> = 7.5 ns. Calculate:
> (a) Time allotted per row refresh.
> (b) Number of refresh commands needed.
> (c) Percentage of total memory bandwidth lost to refresh operations if t<sub>RFC</sub> = 350 ns.

---

#### Part (a) — Time per Row

**Step 1:** Identify known quantities.
- Total refresh window: t<sub>REF</sub> = **64 ms = 64 × 10⁻³ s**
- Number of rows: N = **8192**

**Step 2:** Apply the per-row refresh formula.

$$t_{per\_row} = \frac{t_{REF}}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$t_{per\_row} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{8192}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$t_{per\_row} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-6} \; s = \mathbf{7.81 \;\mu s}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Total Refresh Commands in 64 ms

**Step 1:** The DRAM internally auto-increments the row address on each CBR refresh.

**Step 2:** Therefore, **one CBR command refreshes one row**.

**Step 3:** Total commands required in 64 ms.

$$N_{REFRESH} = N = \mathbf{8192 \; commands}$$

---

#### Part (c) — Bandwidth Lost to Refresh

**Step 1:** Time consumed by one refresh operation: t<sub>RFC</sub> = 350 ns = 350 × 10⁻⁹ s.

**Step 2:** Total time lost to refresh in 64 ms.

$$t_{lost} = N_{REFRESH} \times t_{RFC}$$

$$t_{lost} = 8192 \times 350 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$t_{lost} = 2867.2 \times 10^{-6} \; s = \mathbf{2.867 \; ms}$$

**Step 3:** Compute percentage loss.

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{t_{lost}}{t_{REF}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{2.867 \times 10^{-3}}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{2.867}{64} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{4.48\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** Nearly **4.5% of the memory's available time** is consumed by refresh, even when the CPU is idle. This is a fundamental limitation of DRAM and a key KTU design takeaway.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Complete Read Cycle Latency

**Problem:** A DDR3 SDRAM has the following timing parameters:
- t<sub>RCD</sub> = 15 ns
- CL = 9 cycles
- t<sub>CK</sub> = 1.25 ns
- t<sub>RP</sub> = 15 ns
- Burst length = 8

Compute the **time to fetch a full cache line (64 bytes) from a closed row**.

**Step 1:** Activate the row. Wait t<sub>RCD</sub>.

$$t_{1} = t_{RCD} = 15 \; ns$$

**Step 2:** Issue READ command; wait CL cycles for first data bit.

$$t_{2} = CL \times t_{CK} = 9 \times 1.25 = 11.25 \; ns$$

**Step 3:** Burst of 8 transfers at 1 per cycle = 8 × 1.25 ns = 10 ns.

$$t_{3} = BL \times t_{CK} = 8 \times 1.25 = 10 \; ns$$

**Step 4:** Precharge the bank; wait t<sub>RP</sub> before next activation.

$$t_{4} = t_{RP} = 15 \; ns$$

**Step 5:** Total access time.

$$T_{total} = t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK} + BL \cdot t_{CK} + t_{RP}$$

$$T_{total} = 15 + 11.25 + 10 + 15$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate.

$$T_{total} = \mathbf{51.25 \; ns}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **page-hit** access is faster — it skips the t<sub>RCD</sub> and t<sub>RP</sub> overhead, leaving only the CAS latency and burst time.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix: Refresh Methods

| Feature | RAS-Only Refresh (ROR) | CAS-Before-RAS (CBR) | Self-Refresh (SR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **External Address** | Required (row addr) | Not required | Not required |
| **Address Bus** | Occupied | Free | Free |
| **Controller Overhead** | High | Low | None (internal) |
| **Used in SDRAM** | Optional | Yes (primary) | Yes (low-power) |
| **Power Consumption** | Moderate | Moderate | Very low |
| **KTU Frequency** | Moderate | **High** | High |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answers, **always mention that CBR refresh is preferred in modern systems** because it frees the address bus and uses the DRAM's internal counter for auto-incrementing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of DRAM Command Flow

The diagram below maps the complete state machine of DRAM commands, which is the most commonly asked schematic in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerUp
    PowerUp --> Idle : Power stable
    Idle --> Active : ACT command (RAS#=0, CAS#=1, WE#=1)
    Active --> ReadState : READ command (CAS#=0, WE#=1)
    Active --> WriteState : WRITE command (CAS#=0, WE#=0)
    ReadState --> Precharge : t_RP elapsed
    WriteState --> Precharge : t_RP elapsed
    Precharge --> Idle : t_RP complete
    Idle --> RefreshState : CBR (CAS# then RAS#)
    RefreshState --> Idle : t_RFC elapsed
    Idle --> SelfRefresh : CKE=0
    SelfRefresh --> Idle : CKE=1
    ReadState --> Active : Burst continues
    WriteState --> Active : Burst continues
```

---

### 4.2 Command Decoding Architecture (Block-Level)

This block diagram illustrates how a memory controller decodes commands and routes them to the DRAM bank.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU / Memory Controller] --> B[Address Multiplexer]
    A --> C[Command Encoder]
    C --> D[Control Signal Generator]
    D --> E1[RAS Driver]
    D --> E2[CAS Driver]
    D --> E3[WE Driver]
    D --> E4[CS Driver]
    B --> F[DRAM Address Bus]
    E1 --> G[DRAM Chip]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H1[Bank 0]
    G --> H2[Bank 1]
    G --> H3[Bank N]
    H1 --> I1[Sense Amplifier]
    H2 --> I2[Sense Amplifier]
    H3 --> I3[Sense Amplifier]
    I1 --> J[Data Output Buffer DQ]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

---

### 4.3 DRAM Command Timing Diagram (Asynchronous DRAM)

The waveform below shows the precise sequencing of control signals for a complete **read-from-closed-row** cycle.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CTRL as Memory Controller
    participant RAS as RAS# Line
    participant CAS as CAS# Line
    participant WE as WE# Line
    participant ADDR as Address Bus
    participant DQ as Data Bus

    Note over CTRL,DQ: --- T0: Issue ACTIVE command ---
    CTRL ->> RAS: Drive LOW
    CTRL ->> ADDR: Row Address
    Note over CTRL: Wait t_RCD
    Note over CTRL,DQ: --- T1: Issue READ command ---
    CTRL ->> CAS: Drive LOW
    CTRL ->> WE: Drive HIGH (Read=1)
    CTRL ->> ADDR: Column Address
    Note over CTRL: Wait CL cycles
    CTRL ->> DQ: Data appears
    Note over CTRL,DQ: --- T2: Issue PRECHARGE ---
    CTRL ->> RAS: Drive LOW
    CTRL ->> WE: Drive LOW (Precharge=0)
    Note over CTRL: Wait t_RP
    CTRL ->> RAS: Drive HIGH
    CTRL ->> CAS: Drive HIGH
```

---

### 4.4 State Transition Truth Table (Verifiable Matrix)

This table maps the **4 control signals** to the **resulting DRAM command**, which is the KTU examiner's favorite numerical/logic problem.

| CS# | RAS# | CAS# | WE# | Resulting Command |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **NOP / Deselect** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **ACTIVE (Open Row)** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **READ** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **WRITE** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **PRECHARGE** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **AUTO REFRESH (CBR)** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **MODE REGISTER SET** |
| 1 | x | x | x | **Deselect (No Chip)** |

> [!TIP]
> Note that **AUTO REFRESH (CBR)** and **ACTIVE** differ only in **CAS#** (CBR has CAS#=0, ACTIVE has CAS#=1). The DRAM detects CBR by latching CAS# low *before* RAS# goes low.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the ACTIVE command in DRAM. What is its purpose, and what is the typical timing parameter that must be satisfied before issuing a READ or WRITE command after an ACTIVE?**

**Model Answer:**

The **ACTIVE command** in DRAM is issued to open a specific row of the memory array and connect it to the bank's sense amplifiers. It is initiated by asserting **RAS# = 0, CAS# = 1, WE# = 1** with the row address on the address bus. Its purpose is to make the data in the targeted row available for subsequent READ or WRITE operations.

After an ACTIVE command, the controller must wait for the **Row-to-Column Delay (t<sub>RCD</sub>)** before issuing a READ or WRITE command. This delay allows the sense amplifiers to fully amplify the cell charges.

For a typical DDR3 module, **t<sub>RCD</sub> ≈ 13.5 to 15 ns** (corresponding to 9–11 clock cycles at t<sub>CK</sub> = 1.5 ns). *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between RAS-Only Refresh and CAS-Before-RAS Refresh. Why is CBR preferred in modern systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | RAS-Only Refresh | CAS-Before-RAS Refresh |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Address Provided** | External row address required | No external address |
| **Counter** | External controller manages | Internal counter auto-increments |
| **Address Bus** | Occupied during refresh | Free for other operations |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster |

In **RAS-Only Refresh**, the controller must place the row address on the bus and assert RAS#, using the sense amplifiers to rewrite the row. This consumes valuable bandwidth.

In **CBR Refresh**, the controller asserts CAS# first, then RAS#. The DRAM detects this special signal pattern and uses its **internal row counter** to refresh rows sequentially. This frees the external address bus and reduces controller overhead.

CBR is preferred in modern SDRAM/DDR systems because it allows the memory controller to perform other tasks (e.g., issuing commands to other banks) while refresh occurs, and it simplifies controller logic. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): DRAM Command Sequencing & Timing**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Draw the complete state machine diagram showing transitions between Idle, Active, Read, Write, Precharge, and Refresh states. For each transition, state the command that triggers it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DRAM chip has the following specifications:
- Rows = 8192
- t<sub>REF</sub> = 64 ms
- Clock period t<sub>CK</sub> = 7.5 ns
- t<sub>RCD</sub> = 22.5 ns (3 cycles)
- t<sub>RP</sub> = 22.5 ns (3 cycles)
- t<sub>RFC</sub> = 75 ns
- CL = 2 cycles

Compute:
1. Time allotted per row refresh.
2. Total time consumed by refresh in 64 ms.
3. Percentage of system time consumed by refresh.
4. Time to access a cache line of 8 bytes (burst length 8) from a closed row, assuming the data bus is 8 bits wide. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

> [!TIP]
> The state machine is already provided in **Section 4.1**. The student should reproduce the Mermaid state diagram (or a hand-drawn equivalent) with all six states and clearly label each transition with its command name.

**Key transitions to show:**

- **Idle → Active**: ACTIVE command (RAS#=0, CAS#=1, WE#=1, CS#=0).
- **Active → Read/Write**: After t<sub>RCD</sub>, issue READ or WRITE.
- **Read/Write → Precharge**: After burst completes, issue PRECHARGE (RAS#=0, CAS#=1, WE#=0).
- **Idle → Refresh**: CBR (CAS# then RAS#).
- **Refresh → Idle**: After t<sub>RFC</sub>.
- **Any state → Self-Refresh**: When CKE = 0 (low-power).

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram accuracy, 5 for command description and signal definitions)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(1) Time per row refresh:**

$$t_{per\_row} = \frac{t_{REF}}{N} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{8192} = 7.81 \;\mu s$$

**(2) Total refresh time:**

$$t_{total\_refresh} = N \times t_{RFC} = 8192 \times 75 \times 10^{-9} = 614.4 \;\mu s$$

**(3) Percentage of time consumed:**

$$\%\text{Consumed} = \frac{614.4 \times 10^{-6}}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = \frac{0.6144}{64} \times 100 \approx 0.96\%$$

**(4) Time to access 8-byte cache line from closed row:**

For an 8-bit bus and 8 bytes of data, the burst length is 8 transfers.

$$T_{access} = t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK} + BL \cdot t_{CK} + t_{RP}$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$T_{access} = 22.5 + (2 \times 7.5) + (8 \times 7.5) + 22.5$$

$$T_{access} = 22.5 + 15 + 60 + 22.5 = \mathbf{120 \; ns}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for each sub-question (1) and (4), 1.5 each for (2) and (3), plus 1 for proper formula statement)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Refresh & Power Optimization**

**(a)** Explain the three refresh mechanisms in DRAM: RAS-Only Refresh, CAS-Before-RAS Refresh, and Self-Refresh. Compare them in terms of control complexity, power consumption, and use-case scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laptop's DRAM has 16384 rows and a refresh interval of 64 ms. When the laptop enters sleep mode, the controller switches to self-refresh. The DRAM consumes 200 mW in active refresh mode and 5 mW in self-refresh mode.

Calculate:
1. The number of refresh cycles in 1 second of active operation.
2. Power saved in 1 hour of sleep.
3. Comment on why self-refresh is critical for mobile devices. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**1. RAS-Only Refresh (ROR):**
The controller places the row address on the address bus and asserts RAS#. The sense amplifier rewrites that row. **Complexity:** High (controller manages row counter). **Power:** Moderate. **Use case:** Old asynchronous DRAMs.

**2. CAS-Before-RAS Refresh (CBR):**
CAS# is asserted first, then RAS#. The DRAM detects this sequence and uses its **internal counter** to auto-increment the row. **Complexity:** Low. **Power:** Moderate. **Use case:** SDRAM, DDR, DDR2/3/4/5.

**3. Self-Refresh (SR):**
The DRAM enters SR mode by lowering **CKE (Clock Enable)**. An internal oscillator drives the refresh logic without any external command. The memory contents are preserved with no controller intervention. **Complexity:** Very low (controller is idle). **Power:** Very low (micro-watts per chip). **Use case:** Laptops, smartphones, IoT devices in sleep/standby.

**Comparison Table:**

| Mechanism | Controller Load | Power | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| RAS-Only | High | Moderate | Legacy DRAM |
| CBR | Low | Moderate | Active operation |
| Self-Refresh | None | **Lowest** | Sleep / standby |

*(7 marks — 2 for description, 2 for comparison table, 2 for use cases, 1 for clarity)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(1) Number of refresh cycles per second in active mode:**

$$N_{per\_sec} = \frac{N_{rows}}{t_{REF}} = \frac{16384}{64 \times 10^{-3}} = 256000 \;\text{cycles/second}$$

**(2) Power saved in 1 hour of sleep:**

$$\Delta P = P_{active} - P_{self} = 200 \; mW - 5 \; mW = 195 \; mW$$

For 1 hour (3600 s):

$$E_{saved} = \Delta P \times t = 195 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 702 \; J = 0.195 \; Wh$$

**(3) Comment:**

Self-refresh is **critical for mobile and battery-powered devices** because it allows the DRAM to retain data while consuming **40× less power** than active refresh. Without self-refresh, the system would either need to keep the memory controller fully powered (draining battery) or risk losing data during sleep. Modern smartphones, laptops, and IoT devices rely on this command to achieve multi-day standby times.

*(7 marks — 2 for part (1), 2 for part (2), 3 for part (3) with engineering insight)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU University Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing active-high vs active-low signals.** RAS#, CAS#, WE#, CS# are **active-LOW**. Asserting them means driving them to **logic 0**, not 1. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting t<sub>RCD</sub> in latency calculations.** A common error is to compute only `CL × t<sub>CK</sub>` and ignore t<sub>RCD</sub> and t<sub>RP</sub>. Always state the full formula: $T = t_{RCD} + CL \cdot t_{CK} + BL \cdot t_{CK} + t_{RP}$.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up CBR and ROR refresh.** CBR uses **internal counter**; ROR uses **external address**. Examiners check this distinction carefully.
> 
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Always write **ns, µs, ms, or J** explicitly. Naked numbers lose half-marks.
> 
> 5. **Not labeling Mermaid/diagram transitions.** When asked for a state machine, *every arrow* must show the **command name** that triggers it.
> 
> 6. **Incorrect polarity of burst length and clock.** In SDRAM/DDR, the burst length is **transfers per command**, not bytes — and is clock-driven. Do not confuse with asynchronous DRAM.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DRAM = multiplexed addressing**: Row address first, then column address.
- [x] **Four control signals**: RAS#, CAS#, WE#, CS# (all active-LOW).
- [x] **ACTIVE command** opens a row → state stays in "Active".
- [x] **READ** = CAS#=0, WE#=1 | **WRITE** = CAS#=0, WE#=0.
- [x] **PRECHARGE** closes a row (RAS#=0, CAS#=1, WE#=0).
- [x] **t<sub>RCD</sub>** = ACTIVE to READ/WRITE delay.
- [x] **CL (CAS Latency)** = cycles from READ to first data.
- [x] **t<sub>RP</sub>** = Precharge time before next ACTIVE.
- [x] **t<sub>REF</sub>** = **64 ms** for full array refresh.
- [x] **ROR refresh** = external address required.
- [x] **CBR refresh** = internal counter, no external address, **preferred**.
- [x] **Self-Refresh** = lowest power, used in sleep mode.
- [x] **Refresh time per row** = t<sub>REF</sub> / N ≈ 7.81 µs (for 8192 rows).
- [x] **Bandwidth loss to refresh** ≈ 4–5% in modern DDR.
- [x] **Auto-Precharge (A10=1)** = automatic PRECHARGE at end of burst.
- [x] **Burst Terminate** ends a burst early.
- [x] **Mode Register Set (MRS)** configures CAS latency, burst length, etc.
- [x] **A complete read cycle** = t<sub>RCD</sub> + CL·t<sub>CK</sub> + BL·t<sub>CK</sub> + t<sub>RP</sub>.
- [x] **DRAM is destructive-read**: sense amplifier is required to restore charge.
- [x] **Row-Hammer security flaw** exploits repeated ACTIVATE commands to flip adjacent cells.
- [x] **Mobile/IoT devices depend on Self-Refresh** for multi-day standby.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as **(i) Diagram / Table → (ii) Definition of commands → (iii) Numerical derivation → (iv) Real-world significance**. This mirrors the KTU model answer key pattern and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DRAM Controller Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** DRAM Controller Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DRAM Controller Architecture** refers to the hardware subsystem within a computer's memory controller (typically integrated into the chipset or processor's memory controller hub) that manages all communication, timing, and data transfer operations between the CPU/memory bus and Dynamic Random-Access Memory (DRAM) modules. It performs critical functions including **multiplexing of row and column addresses**, **generation of control signals** (RAS, CAS, WE, CS), **refresh coordination**, **bank management**, **command scheduling**, and **adherence to strict DRAM timing parameters** (tRCD, tRP, tCAS, tRAS, tRFC).

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT302 Module 3)**, the DRAM controller is the bridge that translates abstract memory read/write requests from the CPU into the precise sequence of low-level electrical signalling required by the asynchronous/synchronous DRAM chips.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **DRAM controller as a highly disciplined librarian** running a massive, ancient library of books (the DRAM cells). Here is the analogy:

- The **library building** = the DRAM chip itself.
- The **bookshelves (rows)** = DRAM rows, which must be "activated" (opened) before any book can be read.
- The **specific books (columns)** = the actual data you want.
- The **librarian (DRAM Controller)** = the only person allowed to talk to the library. The librarian:
  1. **Translates your request** ("I need book #3,452") into "Row 5, Shelf 2" (multiplexing row and column addresses).
  2. **Enforces strict waiting times** (you cannot browse shelf 2 while shelf 1 is still being opened — this is the *timing parameter* enforcement).
  3. **Periodically walks around the library shaking the books** to keep the ink from fading (this is **refresh**, because DRAM cells leak charge and must be rewritten every ~**64 ms**).
  4. **Manages multiple reading rooms** (banks) so two patrons can read different sections simultaneously without colliding.

Without this librarian, the CPU would speak directly to the chaotic shelves, causing constant collisions and lost data.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **DRAM cell refresh interval:** **64 ms** (JEDEC standard for standard temperature range).
> - **Typical refresh interval (server/extended):** **32 ms**.
> - **DRAM access latency:** **~50–100 ns** (older SDRAM); modern DDR4/DDR5 is in the order of **10–15 ns** (excluding protocol overhead).
> - **tRFC (Refresh Cycle Time):** Typically **160 ns – 350 ns** depending on density.
> - **DRAM is volatile** but has a density advantage of roughly **~6×** over SRAM (6-transistor vs 1-transistor + 1-capacitor cell).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a DRAM Controller is Necessary

DRAM is architecturally **dense but slow and fragile** compared to registers or SRAM. The reasons a dedicated controller exists are:

1. **Address Multiplexing** — DRAM chips have far fewer physical pins than the number of addressable cells. They share a single address bus, requiring the controller to send the **row address first (RAS)**, then the **column address (CAS)**.
2. **Refresh Management** — Each DRAM cell is essentially a leaky capacitor. The controller must periodically read and rewrite every row to prevent data loss.
3. **Timing Enforcement** — DRAM operations have mandatory minimum delays between commands (e.g., cannot issue READ immediately after ACTIVATE). The controller tracks and enforces these.
4. **Bank & Channel Management** — Modern DIMMs contain multiple banks and ranks; the controller optimizes command scheduling to overlap operations across banks.
5. **Bus Arbitration** — Multiple CPU cores and DMA devices may request memory access; the controller arbitrates and serializes requests.
6. **Error Detection/Correction (ECC)** — In server-class systems, the controller generates and checks ECC codes (e.g., SECDED, Chipkill).

### 2.2 Key Components of a DRAM Controller

The controller can be broken down into the following functional units:

- **Address Multiplexer**: Splits the linear address from the CPU into **Rank → Bank → Row → Column** fields.
- **Command Generator**: Produces the standardized DRAM command set: `ACTIVATE`, `READ`, `WRITE`, `PRECHARGE`, `REFRESH`, `MRS` (Mode Register Set).
- **Refresh Logic**: Issues periodic `REFRESH` commands, tracking which rows have been refreshed.
- **Timing Parameter Engine (State Machine)**: Maintains a set of counters for tRCD, tRP, tRAS, tRFC, tWTR, etc.
- **Write/Read Buffers (Queue)**: Holds pending requests to allow command reordering for performance.
- **Data Path / Serializer**: For DDR, performs 2:1 (DDR), 4:1 (DDR2), 8:1 (DDR3/4/5) serialization using a PLL.
- **ZQ Calibration Logic**: Performs periodic impedance calibration via an external precision resistor.

### 2.3 The Address Decoding Sequence (Logical → Physical)

The CPU presents a flat physical address. The controller decodes it hierarchically:

$$
\text{CPU Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Decode}} \Big[ \text{Channel} \mid \text{Rank} \mid \text{Bank} \mid \text{Row} \mid \text{Column} \Big]
$$

For example, on a system with 2 channels, 2 ranks/channel, 8 banks/rank, 16K rows/bank, 1K columns/row:

$$
\text{Address bits} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{Ch}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{Rank}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{Bank}} + \underbrace{14}_{\text{Row}} + \underbrace{10}_{\text{Col}} = 29 \text{ bits} \ (\text{= 512 MiB})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The channel bits are *typically* the **most significant** (to interleave pages across channels for bandwidth), and the column bits are the **least significant** (because cache lines fill sequentially from column 0).

### 2.4 DRAM Operational Phases

A typical DRAM READ sequence consists of **3 mandatory phases**:

1. **Activation (ACT)**: The target row is opened by asserting RAS. Wordline is driven high, capacitors are connected to sense amplifiers. Takes **tRCD** (RAS-to-CAS Delay).
2. **Read/Write (RD/WR)**: Column address is placed on the bus with CAS asserted. Data is transferred after **tCAS** (or **tCL** for SDRAM).
3. **Precharge (PRE)**: Row must be closed (bitlines equalized) before another row can be opened. Takes **tRP**.

Total access time for a closed-row read = **tRCD + tCAS + tRP** (without overlaps).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table summarizes every key formula and timing parameter a KTU student must memorize for Module 3.

| Symbol | Name | Definition / Meaning | Typical Value (DDR4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_{RCD}$ | RAS-to-CAS Delay | Time from ACTIVATE to READ/WRITE command | **14–16 ns** |
| $t_{RP}$ | Precharge Time | Time to close one row and open another | **14–16 ns** |
| $t_{RAS}$ | Row Active Time | Minimum time row must stay open | **32–36 ns** |
| $t_{CAS}$ / $t_{CL}$ | CAS Latency | Time from READ command to data on bus | **14–16 cycles** |
| $t_{RFC}$ | Refresh Cycle Time | Time to complete one full refresh operation | **260–350 ns** |
| $t_{REFI}$ | Refresh Interval | Average time between REFRESH commands | **7.8 µs** (at 4 Gb density) |
| $t_{WR}$ | Write Recovery | Time from last write data to valid precharge | **15 ns** |
| $t_{WTR}$ | Write-to-Read | Delay between WRITE and READ on same bank | **~5–7.5 ns** |
| $T_{ref}$ | Full Refresh Period | Total time to refresh all rows | **64 ms** (≤ 85 °C) |
| $N_{rows}$ | Number of rows to refresh | Total rows per bank | Depends on density |
| $f_{DRAM}$ | DRAM Clock Frequency | e.g., DDR4-2400 → 1200 MHz, with 2400 MT/s | **800–3200 MHz** |
| $B_{peak}$ | Peak Bandwidth | $8 \cdot f_{clock} \cdot \text{Bytes/transfer}$ | **~25.6 GB/s per channel** |

**Bandwidth Formulas:**

$$
B_{peak} = 2 \cdot f_{clock} \cdot W_{bus} \cdot n_{channels}
$$

Where:
- $2$ = data transferred on both clock edges (DDR).
- $W_{bus}$ = bus width (typically **64 bits** = 8 bytes per channel).
- $n_{channels}$ = number of memory channels.

**Refresh command issuance rate:**

$$
f_{REFI} = \frac{N_{rows}}{T_{ref}} \quad \text{[REFRESH commands per µs]}
$$

For a 4 Gb DDR4 chip with 32K rows, the system issues one REFRESH every:

$$
t_{REFI} = \frac{64 \text{ ms}}{32768} \approx 1.95 \text{ µs} \text{ (per rank)}
$$

But JEDEC allocates this at the channel level → typically **7.8 µs** for 8K refreshes spread across ranks.

**Average Row Access Time (closed page):**

$$
T_{access} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} + t_{RP} \quad \text{[ns]}
$$

**Average Row Access Time (open page, page hit):**

$$
T_{access}^{hit} = t_{CAS} \quad \text{[ns or cycles]}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peak Memory Bandwidth of a DDR4-2400 Channel

Given:
- DDR4-2400 → I/O bus clock = 1200 MHz, transfers = 2400 MT/s (Mega-Transfers per second).
- Bus width per channel = **64 bits = 8 bytes**.
- Number of channels = 2 (typical desktop).

$$
B_{peak} = 2 \cdot f_{clock} \cdot W_{bus} \cdot n_{channels}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the frequency (use MT/s directly for accuracy):

$$
B_{peak} = 2400 \times 10^6 \text{ transfers/s} \times 8 \text{ bytes/transfer} \times 2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the per-channel bandwidth:

$$
B_{channel} = 2400 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 1.92 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/s} = 19.2 \text{ GB/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Double for two channels:

$$
B_{total} = 19.2 \times 2 = 38.4 \text{ GB/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** A "transfer" in DDR occurs on both rising and falling edges of the clock, so the *transfer rate* is 2× the *clock rate*. We multiply by the *bus width* in bytes (8) because each transfer moves 64 bits, and finally by the *number of independent channels* because each channel operates in parallel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DRAM Refresh Budget Calculation

Given a DDR4 SDRAM with:
- Density = **4 Gb** (Giga-bits) per chip.
- Internal banks = 16.
- Rows per bank = 16K = $2^{14}$.
- $T_{ref} = 64$ ms (full refresh period).
- $t_{RFC} = 260$ ns.

**Step 1:** Compute the total number of rows per rank:

$$
N_{rows} = N_{banks} \times N_{rows/bank} = 16 \times 16384 = 262{,}144 \text{ rows}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the time available between two REFRESH commands to one rank:

$$
t_{REFI} = \frac{T_{ref}}{N_{rows}} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{262144} \approx 244 \text{ ns}
$$
