However, JEDEC requires that *all* ranks share the bus for refresh, and refreshes are issued at a lower rate per channel. JEDEC standard:

$$
t_{REFI}^{channel} = 7.8 \text{ µs} \quad \text{(for 4 Gb density)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the refresh *overhead* as a percentage of available DRAM time:

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{t_{RFC}}{t_{REFI}^{channel}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{260 \text{ ns}}{7800 \text{ ns}} \times 100\% \approx 3.33\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This means roughly **3.3% of total DRAM bandwidth** is permanently consumed by refresh. The controller must issue REFRESH commands at this rate to maintain data integrity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective Memory Latency under Row-Buffer Conflict

Modern controllers use an **open-page policy** or **closed-page policy**. We compute effective access time assuming 70% page hits, 20% empty (already closed), 10% conflicts.

Given:
- $t_{CAS} = 14$ cycles = 14 × 0.833 ns = **11.66 ns** (at 1.2 GHz clock).
- $t_{RCD} = 14$ cycles = **11.66 ns**.
- $t_{RP} = 14$ cycles = **11.66 ns**.
- $t_{RAS} = 32$ cycles = **26.6 ns**.

**Step 1:** Latency for a *page hit* (row already open):

$$
L_{hit} = t_{CAS} = 11.66 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Latency for an *empty page* (row closed, no conflict):

$$
L_{empty} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 11.66 + 11.66 = 23.32 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Latency for a *page conflict* (must close current, then open new):

$$
L_{conflict} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 11.66 + 11.66 + 11.66 = 34.98 \text{ ns}
$$

(Or more precisely, $t_{RP} + \max(t_{RCD}, t_{RAS_{old}}) + t_{CAS}$, but we use the simplified form.)

**Step 4:** Compute weighted average effective latency:

$$
L_{avg} = P_{hit} \cdot L_{hit} + P_{empty} \cdot L_{empty} + P_{conflict} \cdot L_{conflict}
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 0.7 \times 11.66 + 0.2 \times 23.32 + 0.1 \times 34.98
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 8.162 + 4.664 + 3.498 = 16.324 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This is why **memory access scheduling algorithms** (FR-FCFS — First-Ready, First-Come-First-Serve) are crucial in modern controllers. They prioritize commands to *currently open rows* to maximize page hits and reduce the dominant $L_{conflict}$ cost.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DRAM Command Truth Table

> [!TIP]
> The following matrix is a high-yield exam reference showing the JEDEC-standard command encoding. `H` = Logic High, `L` = Logic Low, `X` = Don't Care, `V` = Valid (either High or Low).

| Operation | $\overline{CS}$ | $\overline{RAS}$ | $\overline{CAS}$ | $\overline{WE}$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **DESELECT (NOP)** | H | X | X | X |
| **NO OPERATION (NOP)** | L | H | H | H |
| **ACTIVE (Open row)** | L | L | H | H |
| **READ** | L | H | L | H |
| **WRITE** | L | H | L | L |
| **PRECHARGE (Close row)** | L | L | H | L |
| **AUTO REFRESH** | L | L | L | H |
| **SELF REFRESH** | L | L | L | L |
| **MODE REGISTER SET (MRS)** | L | L | L | L |
| **BURST TERMINATE** | L | H | H | L |

> [!NOTE]
> The DRAM chip continuously monitors these four lines. The controller must never place the bus in an undefined state. This is the entire "language" the controller speaks to the DRAM.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DRAM Controller Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef bus fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef dram fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    CPU["CPU Core / Last-Level Cache Miss"]:::bus
    SysReq["System Request Queue and Arbiter"]:::ctrl

    subgraph CTRL ["DRAM CONTROLLER ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        AddrMux["Address Multiplexer - Splits into Rank, Bank, Row, Column"]:::ctrl
        Sched["Command Scheduler - FR-FCFS Policy"]:::ctrl
        CmdGen["Command Generator - Encodes RAS CAS WE CS"]:::ctrl
        Refresh["Refresh Logic and Timer - 7.8 us tREFI"]:::ctrl
        Timing["Timing Parameter Engine - tRCD tRP tCAS tRFC Counters"]:::ctrl
        WrBuf["Write Buffer and Read Buffer"]:::ctrl
        EccGen["ECC Generator and Checker - SECDED"]:::ctrl
        ZQ["ZQ Calibration Engine - Impedance Match"]:::ctrl
        PLL["DDR PLL and Serializer - 2:1 or 4:1 Multiplexing"]:::ctrl
    end

    subgraph DRAM ["DRAM MODULE - DIMM"]
        direction TB
        Rank0["Rank 0 - 8 chips x 8 bits"]:::dram
        Rank1["Rank 1 - 8 chips x 8 bits"]:::dram
        BankA["Bank 0 - Sense Amps and Rows"]:::dram
        BankB["Bank 1 - Sense Amps and Rows"]:::dram
        BankC["Bank 2 - Sense Amps and Rows"]:::dram
        BankD["Bank 3 - Sense Amps and Rows"]:::dram
    end

    PLL --> DDRBus["DDR Data Bus - 64 bits - DQ plus DQS strobes"]:::bus
    DDRBus --> Rank0
    DDRBus --> Rank1
    Rank0 --> BankA
    Rank0 --> BankB
    Rank1 --> BankC
    Rank1 --> BankD

    CPU --> SysReq
    SysReq --> Sched
    Sched --> WrBuf
    Sched --> AddrMux
    AddrMux --> CmdGen
    Refresh --> CmdGen
    CmdGen --> Timing
    Timing --> Rank0
    Timing --> Rank1
    WrBuf --> EccGen
    EccGen --> PLL
    ZQ -.-> PLL
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: DRAM READ Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant Ctrl as DRAM Controller
    participant DRAM as DRAM Chip

    Note over CPU,DRAM: Closed Page State - No Row Open

    CPU->>Ctrl: READ Request - Address 0x1234_5678
    activate Ctrl
    Ctrl->>Ctrl: Decode Address into Rank, Bank, Row, Column
    Ctrl->>DRAM: ACTIVATE Command - Row Address on Address Bus
    Note right of DRAM: Wait tRCD - Row to Column Delay
    DRAM-->>Ctrl: Acknowledge - Row now Open in Sense Amps
    Ctrl->>DRAM: READ Command - Column Address on Address Bus
    Note right of DRAM: Wait tCAS - CAS Latency
    DRAM-->>Ctrl: DATA appears on DQ pins - DQS strobe aligned
    deactivate Ctrl
    Ctrl-->>CPU: Data Returned - Cache Line Filled

    Note over Ctrl,DRAM: Optional Precharge after use
    Ctrl->>DRAM: PRECHARGE Command - Bank Address
    Note right of DRAM: Wait tRP before next ACTIVATE
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Machine: DRAM Bank State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Idle - Bank Closed
    Active: Active - Row Open
    Reading: Reading - Burst in Progress
    Writing: Writing - Burst in Progress
    Charging: Precharging - Bitlines Equalizing
    Refreshing: Refreshing - All Banks

    Idle --> Active: ACTIVATE command - tRCD timer starts
    Active --> Reading: READ command - tCAS timer starts
    Active --> Writing: WRITE command - tCWD timer starts
    Reading --> Active: Burst complete - tRTP elapsed
    Writing --> Active: Write Recovery tWR elapsed
    Active --> Charging: PRECHARGE command - tRP timer starts
    Charging --> Idle: tRP timer expires
    Idle --> Refreshing: REFRESH command - tRFC timer starts
    Refreshing --> Idle: tRFC timer expires
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Command Encoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef enc fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    Op["DRAM Operation Type - From Scheduler"]:::op
    Enc["Command Encoder - Combinational Logic"]:::enc
    Sig1["CS - Chip Select"]:::enc
    Sig2["RAS - Row Address Strobe"]:::enc
    Sig3["CAS - Column Address Strobe"]:::enc
    Sig4["WE - Write Enable"]:::enc

    Op --> Enc
    Enc --> Sig1
    Enc --> Sig2
    Enc --> Sig3
    Enc --> Sig4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Define DRAM Controller. List any four of its primary functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DRAM controller is a digital hardware circuit (often integrated into the northbridge or on-die memory controller of modern CPUs) that manages all communication between the processor and DRAM modules. Its primary functions are:

1. **Address multiplexing** – Sequentially sending row and column addresses over a shared address bus.
2. **Refresh management** – Periodically issuing REFRESH commands to preserve data in capacitive cells.
3. **Timing enforcement** – Maintaining minimum inter-command delays (tRCD, tRP, tCAS, tRFC).
4. **Command generation and scheduling** – Encoding ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, REFRESH signals and ordering them to maximize throughput (e.g., FR-FCFS).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> *Explain the necessity of address multiplexing in DRAM interfacing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DRAM chips have a limited number of physical pins, far fewer than the number of addressable memory locations. To economize on pin count, the address bus is shared between row and column addresses. The **DRAM controller first places the row address on the bus and asserts the RAS (Row Address Strobe) signal**, then places the column address on the same bus and asserts the CAS (Column Address Strobe) signal. This **time-multiplexing** allows a smaller pin package to address a much larger memory space, reducing both cost and electrical complexity. Without multiplexing, the address bus width would have to equal the sum of the row and column bits, drastically increasing chip package size and power consumption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a DRAM controller and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the DRAM read operation sequence in detail, clearly specifying the role of **tRCD**, **tRP**, and **tCAS** parameters. Also compute the effective access time if **70% page hits, 20% empty, and 10% conflicts** are observed for a DDR4-2400 system with tCAS = 14 cycles, tRCD = 14 cycles, tRP = 14 cycles at a clock of 1.2 GHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Architecture (7 marks):**

A DRAM controller architecture consists of the following blocks (as illustrated in the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Address Multiplexer (1 mark):** Takes the linear physical address from the CPU and decomposes it hierarchically into **Channel → Rank → Bank → Row → Column** fields, since DRAM requires two-stage addressing.

2. **Command Scheduler (1 mark):** Implements scheduling policies like **FR-FCFS (First-Ready, First-Come-First-Serve)** to reorder pending requests to maximize page hits and bank-level parallelism.

3. **Command Generator (1 mark):** Encodes the operation into the standard DRAM control signals: **CS, RAS, CAS, WE** (the truth table is shown in Section 3.4).

4. **Refresh Logic (1 mark):** Maintains a counter and issues **REFRESH commands** at the JEDEC-specified interval (typically every **7.8 µs per channel** for DDR4), ensuring all rows are refreshed within **64 ms**.

5. **Timing Parameter Engine (1 mark):** Tracks timing constraints through a state machine holding the minimum-count values for all required parameters.

6. **Write/Read Buffers + ECC + Serializer (1 mark):** Buffers requests, computes/checks ECC (SECDED), and serializes data 2:1 (DDR) onto the DQ pins.

7. **ZQ Calibration + PLL (1 mark):** Matches output impedance to the bus and generates the high-frequency DDR clock.

**(b) DRAM Read Sequence + Latency Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – DRAM Read Operation (4 marks):**

The standard asynchronous / synchronous DRAM read sequence consists of three stages:

1. **ACTIVATE command:** The controller asserts **CS = 0, RAS = 0, CAS = 1, WE = 1**, and places the row address on the bus. The DRAM chip connects the addressed row's capacitors to its sense amplifiers. The row remains "open." The minimum delay between ACTIVATE and the next READ is **tRCD** (RAS-to-CAS Delay).

2. **READ command:** The controller asserts **CS = 0, RAS = 1, CAS = 0, WE = 1**, and places the column address on the bus. After a delay of **tCAS** (CAS Latency), the first data word appears on the DQ pins, strobed by DQS.

3. **PRECHARGE command:** To open a different row in the same bank, the controller issues **PRECHARGE** to close the current row, taking **tRP** to complete before the next ACTIVATE.

**Step 2 – Latency Computation (3 marks):**

Given:
- Clock frequency = 1.2 GHz → clock period = 1/1.2 GHz = 0.8333 ns.
- tCAS = tRCD = tRP = 14 cycles = 14 × 0.8333 = **11.66 ns**.

Latencies for each case:

$$
L_{hit} = t_{CAS} = 11.66 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
L_{empty} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 11.66 + 11.66 = 23.32 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
L_{conflict} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 34.98 \text{ ns}
$$

Weighted average:

$$
L_{avg} = 0.7(11.66) + 0.2(23.32) + 0.1(34.98)
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 8.162 + 4.664 + 3.498 = \boxed{16.32 \text{ ns}}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the different DRAM refresh methods. Compute the average REFRESH command interval for a 4 Gb DDR4 chip with 16 banks and 16K rows per bank, given a full refresh period of 64 ms. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Define the DRAM peak bandwidth. Compute the peak bandwidth of a dual-channel DDR4-3200 system with 64-bit bus width. Also explain the role of the ZQ calibration block in the controller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Refresh Methods + Calculation (7 marks):**

**Refresh methods (4 marks):**

1. **Auto Refresh (AR)** — The controller issues an `AUTO REFRESH` command (CS=L, RAS=L, CAS=L, WE=H). The DRAM's internal address counter selects the next row to refresh, and the chip handles all timing. The controller must ensure no other commands execute for **tRFC**.

2. **Self Refresh (SR)** — For low-power (battery-backed, suspend) states, the controller issues `SELF REFRESH`. The DRAM's internal oscillator takes over, allowing the external controller and clock to be disabled. Data is preserved as long as CKE is held low.

3. **Per-Bank Refresh (DDR4+)** — Allows refreshing one bank at a time, hiding the long tRFC by pipelining refreshes across banks.

4. **Fine Granularity Refresh (DDR5)** — Splits a single row refresh into multiple sub-operations to spread power consumption and reduce peak current draw.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

For a 4 Gb DDR4 chip:
- Total rows = 16 banks × 16,384 rows/bank = **262,144 rows**.
- Required REFRESH interval per row to ensure completion in 64 ms:

$$
t_{REFI} = \frac{T_{ref}}{N_{rows}} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{262144} \approx 244 \text{ ns}
$$

JEDEC allocates this across ranks; per-channel interval for a single rank is **7.8 µs** (for 4 Gb density):

$$
f_{REFI} = \frac{1}{7.8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 128{,}205 \text{ REFRESH commands/s}
$$

**(b) Peak Bandwidth + ZQ Calibration (7 marks):**

**Peak Bandwidth (3 marks):**

Peak bandwidth is the theoretical maximum rate at which data can be transferred between DRAM and the controller, assuming a continuously streaming read or write with no idle cycles.

For a **dual-channel DDR4-3200** system:

$$
B_{peak} = 2 \cdot MT/s \cdot W_{bus} \cdot n_{channels}
$$

- DDR4-3200 → 3200 MT/s.
- $W_{bus}$ = 64 bits = 8 bytes.
- $n_{channels} = 2$.

$$
B_{peak} = 3200 \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 2 = 5.12 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/s} = \boxed{51.2 \text{ GB/s}}
$$

**ZQ Calibration (4 marks):**

ZQ calibration is the process by which the DRAM output drivers (and on-die termination, ODT) are tuned to match the precise impedance of the transmission line on the motherboard. The DRAM pin named **ZQ** is connected externally to a precision reference resistor (typically **240 Ω ± 1%**) tied to ground.

**Process:**
1. The controller issues a **ZQ Calibration Long (ZQCL)** command during initialization, and periodic **ZQ Calibration Short (ZQCS)** commands (e.g., every 128 ms) during operation.
2. The DRAM internally adjusts its pull-up and pull-down transistor strengths using parallel switchable legs until the voltage at ZQ matches the internal reference, matching the external resistor.
3. This compensates for **temperature and voltage variations** that affect transistor drive strength.

Without ZQ calibration, signal integrity would degrade, causing reflections, ringing, and ultimately read/write errors, especially at higher frequencies (DDR4-3200, DDR5-6400).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in DRAM Controller Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing tCAS with tRCD** — tCAS is from READ to data; tRCD is from ACTIVATE to READ. Examiners will deduct marks if these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×2 multiplier in bandwidth** — DDR transfers on *both* clock edges. A student writing "1.2 GHz × 8 = 9.6 GB/s" instead of 19.2 GB/s loses full marks on bandwidth problems.
> 3. **Confusing tREFI with tRFC** — tREFI is the *interval between* refresh commands; tRFC is the *duration* of a single refresh. Both are required, and they are very different (µs vs ns).
> 4. **Skipping units** — Always state the unit (ns, µs, GB/s). KTU valuation explicitly awards marks for units.
> 5. **Drawing the bus as a single arrow in block diagrams** — Show **separate unidirectional paths** for Address, Data, and Control; examiners look for this in 7-mark architecture questions.
> 6. **Ignoring the "per-channel" nature of REFI** — At the chip level it's 244 ns, at the channel level it's 7.8 µs. State which one your answer refers to.
> 7. **Writing "DRAM is faster than SRAM"** — this is the opposite; SRAM is faster but less dense. Many students lose marks on the "Compare SRAM vs DRAM" question type.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for DRAM Controller Architecture:**

- ✅ The **DRAM controller** is a digital hardware block that mediates between the CPU and DRAM, multiplexing addresses, generating commands, and enforcing timing.
- ✅ DRAM uses a **two-dimensional addressing scheme** (row + column) because the cells are organized as a 2D array; the row is opened first via ACTIVATE.
- ✅ **Five primary commands:** ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, REFRESH (plus NOP, MRS, DESELECT).
- ✅ **Mandatory timing parameters** to memorize: tRCD, tRP, tRAS, tCAS, tRFC, tREFI, tWR, tWTR.
- ✅ **Total closed-page latency** = tRCD + tCAS + tRP; **page-hit latency** = tCAS only.
- ✅ **Refresh interval** = 64 ms full period → ≈ 7.8 µs per REFRESH command (per channel, DDR4 4 Gb).
- ✅ **Bandwidth formula** = $2 \times MT/s \times W_{bus} \times n_{channels}$ (the "2" accounts for DDR double-edge transfers).
- ✅ The **command truth table** is determined by the four control lines: CS, RAS, CAS, WE.
- ✅ **ZQ calibration** matches DRAM output impedance to an external 240 Ω reference resistor, ensuring signal integrity.
- ✅ **Open-page policy** is favored for sequential accesses; **closed-page policy** is favored for random access patterns. Modern controllers use adaptive policies.
- ✅ Modern DRAMs use **bank-level parallelism** and **schedulers** (e.g., FR-FCFS) to overlap ACTIVATE/PRECHARGE of one bank with READ/WRITE of another.
- ✅ Modern DRAM standards: **DDR → DDR2 → DDR3 → DDR4 → DDR5**, each doubling prefetch (2n) and increasing transfer rate.
- ✅ The controller is **physically inside the CPU die** in modern systems (Integrated Memory Controller, IMC), connected to DIMMs via the memory bus.
- ✅ **ECC (SECDED or Chipkill)** is generated and checked by the controller in server-class systems.
- ✅ **Self Refresh** is used during low-power suspend states; the DRAM refreshes itself internally without controller intervention.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Row Buffer Management Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Row Buffer Management Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row Buffer Management Policy** is the strategy employed by the **DRAM memory controller** to determine when to *close* (precharge) a row in a DRAM bank after an access, or *keep it open* in the sense amplifiers (row buffer) to exploit spatial and temporal locality of memory references.

In modern **DRAM architecture**, each bank contains a **row buffer** — a small, high-speed register composed of *sense amplifiers* — that holds the most recently activated row of memory cells. When the memory controller issues an `ACTIVATE` (RAS) command, an entire row is read from the cell array into the row buffer. Subsequent accesses (reads/writes) to the same row become merely `READ`/`WRITE` commands targeting the row buffer directly, bypassing the slower activation step.

The **row buffer management policy** decides the lifetime of an open row:
- **Close Page Policy** — close the row immediately after the access.
- **Open Page Policy** — keep the row open indefinitely until a conflict requires closure.
- **Adaptive / Hybrid Policy** — dynamically switch based on observed access patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Bank**: An independent 2D array of DRAM cells with its own row buffer.
> - **Row Buffer**: The sense amplifier array holding one open row (~8 KB for DDR4 x8 device).
> - **ACTIVATE (RAS)**: Command that transfers a row from cell array → row buffer. Latency ≈ **t_RCD ≈ 15–18 ns** (DDR4-2400).
> - **PRECHARGE**: Command that writes the row buffer back and prepares the bank for a new row. Latency ≈ **t_RP ≈ 15–18 ns**.
> - **READ/WRITE (CAS)**: Column access to the open row buffer. Latency ≈ **t_CAS ≈ 15–18 ns**.

## Conceptual Analogy — The Library Reading Room

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Library Desk):**
> Imagine you are a librarian (the **memory controller**) and a student (the **CPU**) requests books from a massive warehouse (the **DRAM cell array**).
>
> - **Activating a row** = Walking to the warehouse, fetching a whole **pallet of books (the row)** and placing it on your **front desk (the row buffer)**. This takes time (≈ walking time = **t_RCD**).
> - **CAS (Read/Write)** = Handing individual books from the pallet to the student. This is super fast (≈ reaching into the desk = **t_CAS**).
> - **Precharge** = Returning the entire pallet to the warehouse so a different pallet can be brought up. This takes time (≈ **t_RP**).
>
> **The "Row Buffer Policy"** is the rule you (the librarian) follow:
> - *Close Page*: Always return the pallet to the warehouse after handing out one book. (Safe, predictable.)
> - *Open Page*: Keep the pallet on the desk — if the student asks for the *next* book, you hand it instantly. (Fast for sequential patterns.)
> - *Adaptive*: Watch the pattern — if the student keeps asking for books from the same pallet, keep it open; if they switch to a different topic, return it.

This analogy explains **why locality matters**: keeping a row open only helps when the next access is a **row buffer hit** (same row, different column).

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Exam Recall)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (DDR4-2400) | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Row-to-Column Delay | **t_RCD** | **15.0 ns** | Time to open a row |
| Column Access Strobe | **t_CAS / t_CL** | **15.0 ns** | Time to read from open row |
| Row Precharge Time | **t_RP** | **15.0 ns** | Time to close a row |
| Row Cycle Time | **t_RC** | **46–50 ns** | t_RCD + t_CAS + t_RP (worst case) |
| Row Buffer Size | — | **1024 columns × 8 bits ≈ 1 KB per chip; 8 KB per rank** | Hold capacity |
| Refresh Interval | **t_REFI** | **7.8 µs** | Required to prevent data loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Row buffer management directly affects **DRAM throughput (GB/s)** and **average memory access time (AMAT)**. A poor policy can reduce effective bandwidth by **30–50%** in real workloads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown — The Three Canonical Policies

### A. Close Page Policy (a.k.a. "Auto-Precharge" / Close-after-Use)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Close Page Policy):** The memory controller automatically issues a `PRECHARGE` command **immediately after every READ or WRITE** access completes. The row buffer is *always* empty between consecutive transactions.

**Logic Flow (Bulleted):**
1. CPU issues memory request `(Bank B, Row R, Column C)`.
2. Controller sends `ACTIVATE` → row R loaded into row buffer (cost = **t_RCD**).
3. Controller sends `READ/WRITE` for column C (cost = **t_CAS**).
4. Controller **immediately** sends `PRECHARGE` to close the row (cost = **t_RP**).
5. Row buffer is now empty — next access always pays the full **t_RCD + t_CAS**.

**Why Use It?**
- **Predictable latency**: every access pays the same total cost. Excellent for **real-time systems**, **hard real-time embedded controllers**, and **instruction streaming** where sequential accesses typically move to *different* rows.
- **No row buffer conflicts**: precharge is implicit, simplifying controller logic.

**Why Avoid It?**
- Destroys **temporal locality**. If the CPU re-accesses the same row (e.g., looping over an array, repeated stack operations), every access pays the activation penalty.

### B. Open Page Policy (a.k.a. "Stay-Open" / Keep-Page-Open)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Open Page Policy):** The memory controller **leaves the row open** in the row buffer after a READ/WRITE. The row remains open until a *different row* in the *same bank* is requested, at which point a precharge is issued.

**Logic Flow (Bulleted):**
1. CPU issues memory request `(Bank B, Row R, Column C)`.
2. Controller sends `ACTIVATE` → row R loaded (cost = **t_RCD**).
3. Controller sends `READ/WRITE` for column C (cost = **t_CAS**).
4. **No precharge** — row buffer still holds row R.
5. Next access:
   - If `(Bank B, Row R, …)` → **Row Buffer HIT** → only `READ` needed (cost = **t_CAS**).
   - If `(Bank B, Row R', …)` → **Row Buffer CONFLICT (MISS)** → precharge + activate + read (cost = **t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS**).
   - If `(Bank B', …)` → different bank, no conflict.

**Why Use It?**
- Maximizes benefit of **temporal locality** — repeated access to the same row is served from the high-speed buffer at full bandwidth.
- Ideal for **streaming workloads**: matrix multiplication, sequential scans, hash table probing.

**Why Avoid It?**
- **Worst-case latency** is enormous (`t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS ≈ 45–50 ns`) for conflict misses.
- **Energy cost**: open rows leak charge and require periodic refresh; keeping many rows open increases refresh overhead.
- A row buffer **hit ratio** below ~50% yields *worse* performance than close page.

### C. Adaptive / Hybrid Policy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Adaptive Policy):** The controller uses **runtime counters** (e.g., per-bank hit/miss counters) to dynamically choose between open and close page behaviour. If a bank exhibits a high hit rate, leave its page open; otherwise, precharge aggressively.

**Logic Flow (Bulleted):**
1. Maintain a per-bank **hit counter** `H` and **miss counter** `M` (saturating counters, e.g., 2-bit or 3-bit).
2. On every access, update the counter for that bank.
3. Use a **threshold heuristic**: e.g., if `H > M` over a sliding window, keep page open; else precharge.
4. Modern variants: **FR-FCFS** (First-Ready, First-Come-First-Serve) with row-buffer hit priority.

**Real-World Usage:**
- **AMD Zen**, **Intel Skylake+**, **ARM Cortex memory controllers** all use adaptive open-page with **page-hit-first** scheduling.
- DDR5 controllers (2024+ standard) integrate this in the **Decision Feedback Equalizer + scheduler** logic on-die.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)** | `AMAT = Hit_Time + Miss_Rate × Miss_Penalty` | ns | Memory hierarchy universal formula |
| **Row Buffer Hit Latency** | `t_HIT = t_CAS` | ns | Only CAS, no activate |
| **Row Buffer Miss Latency** | `t_MISS = t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS` | ns | Full pipeline: precharge + activate + read |
| **Empty (Idle) Bank Latency** | `t_EMPTY = t_RCD + t_CAS` | ns | No precharge needed, just activate |
| **Average Access Time (Policy-Aware)** | `t_AVG = h·t_HIT + (1−h)·t_MISS` | ns | `h` = row buffer hit rate, 0 ≤ h ≤ 1 |
| **Bandwidth (Open Page)** | `BW = (Burst_Length × Bus_Width × f_clk) / (cycles_per_access)` | GB/s | Effective depends on hit rate |
| **DRAM Throughput (peak)** | `BW_peak = 2 × f_clk × Bus_Width / 8` | GB/s | DDR = Double Data Rate (factor 2) |
| **Refresh Overhead** | `Ovhd_REFRESH = t_RFC / t_REFI` | % | `t_RFC` ≈ 350 ns, `t_REFI` = 7.8 µs → ~4.5% |
| **Hit Rate Threshold for Open vs Close** | `h_crit = t_RP / (t_RP + t_CAS − t_CAS) = t_RP / t_RP` simplified | — | When `h > 0.5`, open-page generally wins |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Numericals:** Always substitute `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = t` for simplicity when actual values aren't given. Then:
> - `t_HIT = t`
> - `t_MISS = 3t`
> - `t_AVG = h·t + (1−h)·3t = (3 − 2h)·t`

## Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **GPU Memory Systems (GDDR6/HBM):** Use aggressive open-page with **FR-FCFS** because graphics shading has strong row-buffer locality.
2. **Database Engines:** MySQL/PostgreSQL buffer pools cause *random* row access — **close-page** may outperform open-page unless adaptive logic is used.
3. **AI/ML Training (Tensor Cores):** Matrix multiply access patterns are highly row-local → **open-page** boosts effective bandwidth by 2–3×.
4. **Mobile SoCs (LPDDR5):** Aggressive **adaptive** policies needed to balance energy (refresh) vs performance — most critical in battery-powered devices.

## The 'Why' and 'How' Summary

| Policy | Why Chosen | How It Works | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Close Page | Predictability, no conflicts | Precharge after every access | Streaming, low-locality workloads |
| Open Page | Exploit temporal locality | Keep row open until conflict | High-locality loops, streaming |
| Adaptive | Best of both worlds | Per-bank hit/miss counters | Mixed real-world workloads |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Average Access Time Under Open-Page Policy

We need to derive the **average memory access time** as a function of the row buffer **hit rate `h`**.

### Step 1: Define the two possible outcomes

- **Outcome 1 (Row Buffer HIT)**: probability = `h`, latency = `t_HIT = t_CAS`.
- **Outcome 2 (Row Buffer MISS)**: probability = `1 − h`, latency = `t_MISS = t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS`.

### Step 2: Express average latency as expectation

$$
t_{AVG} = P(\text{hit}) \cdot t_{HIT} + P(\text{miss}) \cdot t_{MISS}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the probabilities

$$
t_{AVG} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + (1 - h) \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})
$$

### Step 4: Expand and simplify (distribution of multiplication)

$$
t_{AVG} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) - h \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})
$$

### Step 5: Collect `h` terms

$$
t_{AVG} = (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) + h \cdot \left[ t_{CAS} - (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) \right]
$$

### Step 6: Simplify the bracket

$$
t_{CAS} - (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) = -(t_{RP} + t_{RCD})
$$

### Step 7: Final closed-form expression

$$
t_{AVG} = (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) - h \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - When `h = 0` (no hits): `t_AVG = t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS` (worst case).
> - When `h = 1` (always hit): `t_AVG = t_CAS` (best case).
> - Linear interpolation between extremes.

### Step 8: Special case `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = t` (KTU convention)

$$
t_{AVG} = 3t - h \cdot 2t = t(3 - 2h)
$$

For close-page policy, `h = 0` always:

$$
t_{AVG}^{close} = 3t
$$

For open-page with `h = 1`:

$$
t_{AVG}^{open,perfect} = t
$$

## Derivation 2: Break-Even Hit Rate (Open vs Close Page)

We want to find the hit rate `h*` where **open page** matches **close page** in average latency.

### Step 1: Set the two latencies equal

$$
t_{AVG}^{open}(h^*) = t_{AVG}^{close}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute derived formulas (with `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = t`)

$$
t(3 - 2h^*) = 3t
$$

### Step 3: Solve for `h*`

$$
3 - 2h^* = 3
$$

$$
-2h^* = 0
$$

$$
h^* = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (Counter-Intuitive!):** Under the simplifying assumption `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = t`, open page is **always at least as good as** close page — and better as `h` increases. The reason: open page never pays more than close page for the *first* access, and gains `2t` per subsequent hit.

### Step 4: More realistic break-even (when `t_RP ≠ 0`)

If we model close page more carefully — close page *also* suffers the precharge (in the *next* access), while open page *avoids* it for hits:

$$
t_{AVG}^{open} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + (1-h) \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})
$$

$$
t_{AVG}^{close} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} + t_{RP}
$$

Wait — these are actually the same. The key difference emerges when comparing **consecutive accesses to the same row**:

- **Open page, hit on second access**: total for 2 accesses = `t_RCD + t_CAS + t_CAS`.
- **Close page, always re-activate**: total for 2 accesses = `2·(t_RCD + t_CAS + t_RP)`.

**Speedup of open-page per hit:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{2(t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} + t_{RP})}{t_{RCD} + 2 \cdot t_{CAS}} = \frac{2(3t)}{4t} = 1.5
$$

So **each row-buffer hit saves 1.5× the latency** in this simplified model.

## Derivation 3: AMAT with Cache Hierarchy Integration

The classical KTU formula:

$$
\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + \text{MR}_{L1} \cdot t_{L2\_miss\_penalty}
$$

Expanding the **L2 miss penalty** through DRAM row buffer:

$$
t_{L2\_miss\_penalty} = t_{AVG}^{DRAM} = t(3 - 2h)
$$

Full hierarchy:

$$
\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + \text{MR}_{L1} \cdot \left[ t_{L2} + \text{MR}_{L2} \cdot t(3 - 2h) \right]
$$

For `t_{L1} = 1 ns, t_{L2} = 10 ns, MR_{L1} = 5\%, MR_{L2} = 10\%, h = 0.7, t = 15 ns`:

$$
\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \cdot [10 + 0.10 \cdot 15 \cdot (3 - 1.4)]
$$

$$
= 1 + 0.05 \cdot [10 + 0.10 \cdot 15 \cdot 1.6]
$$

$$
= 1 + 0.05 \cdot [10 + 2.4]
$$

$$
= 1 + 0.05 \cdot 12.4
$$

$$
= 1 + 0.62 = 1.62 \text{ ns}
$$

## Analytical Decision Matrix — KTU Exam Quick Reference

| Workload Type | Access Pattern | Recommended Policy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequential array scan | `A[0], A[1], A[2]…` (same row) | **Open Page** | 100% hit rate possible |
| Pointer chasing (linked list) | Random rows | **Close Page** | No locality; avoid precharge cost |
| Matrix transpose | Access row, then column | **Close Page** | Conflicting rows |
| Hash table with chaining | Mostly random | **Adaptive (lean close)** | Bursts of locality |
| Video frame processing | Sequential rows | **Open Page** | Stream-like |
| Stack operations | Same addresses repeatedly | **Open Page** | High temporal locality |
| Hard real-time control | Predictable timing | **Close Page** | Bounded latency |
| AI/ML matrix multiply | Tile-based | **Open Page + Adaptive** | Tile fits in row buffer |
| Multi-threaded random | Mixed | **Adaptive** | Only viable choice |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Define Row Buffer Management Policy and list its three main variants.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Row Buffer Management Policy is the strategy used by the DRAM memory controller to decide when to keep an activated row open in the sense-amplifier row buffer of a DRAM bank, and when to close (precharge) it. The three main variants are: **(1) Close Page Policy** — precharge the row immediately after every access; **(2) Open Page Policy** — keep the row open until a different row in the same bank is requested; and **(3) Adaptive / Hybrid Policy** — dynamically switch between the two based on observed per-bank hit rate counters.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is a row buffer hit and how does it differ from a row buffer conflict?**
*(Cognitive Level: Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **row buffer hit** occurs when the memory controller issues a READ/WRITE command and the target bank already has the required row open in its row buffer from a prior access — only a CAS (column access) command is needed, costing `t_CAS`. A **row buffer conflict (miss)** occurs when the target bank has a *different* row open — the controller must first issue PRECHARGE (cost `t_RP`) to close it, then ACTIVATE the new row (cost `t_RCD`), and finally issue CAS — total cost `t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS`, roughly 3× higher.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Open Page Policy in Depth

**Q. (a)** Explain the Open Page Policy in detail with a state diagram. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a DRAM system with `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = 15 ns`. The observed row buffer hit rate for a benchmark is `h = 0.6`. Calculate:
  (i) Average memory access time under Open Page Policy.
  (ii) Average memory access time under Close Page Policy.
  (iii) Percentage improvement of Open Page over Close Page. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

Open Page Policy is a row buffer management strategy where the memory controller **leaves a row activated in the row buffer** after a READ or WRITE completes. The row remains open indefinitely until a subsequent request targets a **different row in the same bank**, at which point a PRECHARGE command is issued to close the open row before activating the new one.

**State Transitions:**
- **IDLE → ACTIVATE**: New row requested for first time → `ACT` command sent, taking `t_RCD`.
- **ACTIVATE → READ/WRITE**: After `t_RCD`, CAS command sent, taking `t_CAS`.
- **READ/WRITE → ROW_OPEN (stay)**: Policy keeps row in buffer; no PRECHARGE.
- **ROW_OPEN → READ/WRITE (hit)**: Same row, different column → only CAS, `t_CAS` only.
- **ROW_OPEN → PRECHARGE**: Different row requested → `PRE` command, `t_RP`.
- **PRECHARGE → IDLE**: Bank ready for new activation.

**Advantages:**
1. Exploits **temporal locality** — repeated access to same row served at `t_CAS` only.
2. **High throughput** for sequential/streaming workloads.
3. Reduces energy spent on redundant activations.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Worst-case latency** is high — conflict miss costs `t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS ≈ 3t`.
2. **Energy waste** — open row consumes refresh power even when not accessed.
3. **Unpredictable latency** — unsuitable for hard real-time systems.
4. Performs **worse** than close page if hit rate falls below ~30–50%.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = t = 15 ns`, `h = 0.6`.

**(i) Open Page Average Access Time:**

$$
t_{AVG}^{open} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + (1 - h) \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})
$$

Substitute:

$$
t_{AVG}^{open} = 0.6 \cdot 15 + (1 - 0.6) \cdot (15 + 15 + 15)
$$

$$
= 9.0 + 0.4 \cdot 45
$$

$$
= 9.0 + 18.0
$$

$$
= 27.0 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Close Page Average Access Time:**

Close page always incurs precharge + activate + read:

$$
t_{AVG}^{close} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 15 + 15 + 15 = 45.0 \text{ ns}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Improvement:**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{t_{AVG}^{close} - t_{AVG}^{open}}{t_{AVG}^{close}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{45 - 27}{45} \times 100\% = \frac{18}{45} \times 100\% = 40.0\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Open Page reduces average latency by **40%** for `h = 0.6`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Adaptive Policy Analysis

**Q. (a)** Describe the Adaptive Row Buffer Management Policy. Explain how per-bank hit/miss counters are used to make the open/close decision. (7 marks)

**(b)** A DRAM system has `t_RCD = 12 ns, t_CAS = 12 ns, t_RP = 12 ns`. A workload runs in two phases:
  - **Phase 1 (10,000 accesses)**: 85% row buffer hit rate.
  - **Phase 2 (10,000 accesses)**: 20% row buffer hit rate.
  Calculate the average access time for:
  (i) Pure Open Page Policy applied uniformly.
  (ii) Pure Close Page Policy applied uniformly.
  (iii) An Adaptive Policy that uses Open Page in Phase 1 and Close Page in Phase 2. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Adaptive Row Buffer Management Policy** dynamically selects between open-page and close-page behaviours on a **per-bank basis**, using **runtime monitoring of access patterns**. It maintains a pair of saturating counters per bank — a **hit counter** `H` and a **miss counter** `M` (typically 2-bit or 3-bit saturation to avoid oscillation).

**Decision Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. For each access to a bank, increment `H` (if hit) or `M` (if conflict).
2. Periodically (or on each access) compare `H` vs `M` against a **threshold** `T`.
3. If `H > T` (e.g., `H > 2` in a 3-bit counter) → **Open Page** for that bank.
4. If `H ≤ T` → switch to **Close Page** mode for that bank.
5. Optionally, a **hysteresis margin** is added to prevent flapping between modes.

**Hardware Implementation:**
- Per-bank 6-bit counter pair (≈ 24 bits per channel for an 8-bank DDR4).
- Comparator + threshold register.
- Updated by the controller's **scheduler** (FR-FCFS in modern CPUs).

**Benefits:** Achieves near-optimal latency in mixed workloads; used in **Intel Core i-series**, **AMD Zen 2/3/4**, and **ARM Cortex-A** memory controllers.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = 12 ns`.

**(i) Pure Open Page, blended hit rate:**

Weighted average hit rate:

$$
h_{avg} = \frac{10000 \cdot 0.85 + 10000 \cdot 0.20}{20000} = \frac{8500 + 2000}{20000} = 0.525
$$

$$
t_{AVG}^{open} = 0.525 \cdot 12 + (1 - 0.525) \cdot (12 + 12 + 12)
$$

$$
= 6.3 + 0.475 \cdot 36 = 6.3 + 17.1 = 23.4 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Pure Close Page:**

$$
t_{AVG}^{close} = 12 + 12 + 12 = 36.0 \text{ ns}
$$

**(iii) Adaptive Policy (Open in Phase 1, Close in Phase 2):**

Phase 1 (Open, h = 0.85):

$$
t_1 = 0.85 \cdot 12 + 0.15 \cdot 36 = 10.2 + 5.4 = 15.6 \text{ ns}
$$

Phase 2 (Close):

$$
t_2 = 36.0 \text{ ns}
$$

Weighted average:

$$
t_{AVG}^{adaptive} = \frac{10000 \cdot 15.6 + 10000 \cdot 36}{20000} = \frac{156000 + 360000}{20000} = \frac{516000}{20000} = 25.8 \text{ ns}
$$

**Comparison:**
- Open uniform: **23.4 ns**
- Close uniform: **36.0 ns**
- Adaptive: **25.8 ns**

**Analysis:** Adaptive (25.8 ns) sits between open (23.4) and close (36.0). However, if we kept **Open Page uniform**, it is *better* than Adaptive here because Phase 1's high locality (85%) compensates for Phase 2. Adaptive is best when *phase-specific* cost is high — illustrating that simple averaging can mislead scheduling decisions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `t_RC` with `t_RCD`**: `t_RC = t_RCD + t_CAS + t_RP`. Many students use `t_RC` as the activate-only delay — this is WRONG.
> 2. **Forgetting the precharge cost in close page**: A close page access still pays `t_RP` because precharge must occur (either implicit in `t_RCD` of the next access, or explicit). Always include it in your formula.
> 3. **Sign error in `t_AVG`**: The formula is `t_AVG = (t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS) − h(t_RP + t_RCD)`, **not** `+ h(...)`. Subtraction because each hit *saves* time.
> 4. **Hit rate not weighted by access count**: When given multiple phases, **always weight by access count**, not by phase duration.
> 5. **Confusing "row buffer" with "cache"**: The row buffer is NOT a cache — it is a sense amplifier array. There is no tag, no eviction policy. A row is "open" or "closed" per bank.
> 6. **Missing the `t_RFC` (refresh) component** in real bandwidth calculations — refresh cuts effective bandwidth by ~5–7%.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**
>
> ✅ Row buffer = sense amplifier array inside each DRAM bank; holds **1 active row** (~1–8 KB).
>
> ✅ **Three DRAM commands**: `ACTIVATE` (`t_RCD`), `READ/WRITE` (`t_CAS`), `PRECHARGE` (`t_RP`).
>
> ✅ **Close Page Policy**: Precharge after every access. Predictable, `t_AVG = t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS = 3t`.
>
> ✅ **Open Page Policy**: Keep row open. Latency = `t_CAS` on hit, `3t` on conflict.
>
> ✅ **Adaptive Policy**: Per-bank hit/miss counters (2- or 3-bit saturation) with threshold + hysteresis.
>
> ✅ **Master formula (KTU):** `t_AVG = (t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS) − h(t_RP + t_RCD)` (or `t(3 − 2h)` for symmetric timing).
>
> ✅ **Row Buffer HIT** = same bank + same row. **MISS** = same bank + different row. **EMPTY** = idle bank (no precharge needed).
>
> ✅ **Best for sequential/streams**: Open Page. **Best for hard real-time / random**: Close Page. **Best for mixed real-world**: Adaptive.
>
> ✅ **Performance metric**: AMAT incorporates DRAM hit rate; cache misses × DRAM latency dominate.
>
> ✅ **Modern CPUs** (Zen 4, Raptor Lake) use **FR-FCFS scheduling with row-hit priority** — a hybrid of open page and adaptive logic.
>
> ✅ **Energy trade-off**: Open page saves activation energy but adds refresh energy. Adaptive balances both.
>
> ✅ **DRAM standards (KTU 2024)**: DDR4 typical values: `t_RCD = t_CAS = t_RP = 15 ns`; DDR5: ~14–16 ns; LPDDR5: even lower.
>
> ✅ **Bandwidth formula**: `BW_peak = 2 × f_clk × (Bus_Width / 8)` — DDR (factor 2) is the *Double* Data Rate multiplier.
>
> ✅ **Critical exam trap**: For open page, ALWAYS check if next access is hit or miss — the `h` factor decides everything.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Page Row Buffer Management Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Open Page Row Buffer Management Policy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open Page Policy** is a **Dynamic RAM (DRAM) row buffer management strategy** in which, after a memory access (read or write) to a particular row (page) of a DRAM bank, the **row buffer is intentionally left OPEN** (i.e., the sense amplifiers remain activated and the row data stays latched) so that any subsequent access targeting the **same row** can be serviced with minimal latency. In contrast, in a **Close Page Policy**, the row buffer is **precharged (closed)** immediately after the access, forcing the next access — even to the same row — to incur the full **ACTIVATE → READ/WRITE → PRECHARGE** sequence. The Open Page Policy is also commonly called the **"Page Mode DRAM"** or **"Stay-Open Page Policy"**, and it forms the basis of modern memory controllers in DDR (Double Data Rate) SDRAM systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Library Reading Desk > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Librarian's Desk** > > Imagine a librarian who has just fetched a thick book (the **row**) from the shelves. Two possible policies: > > 1. **Close Page Policy** → The librarian immediately returns the book to the shelf after you finish reading **one page (column)**. If you ask for another page from the same book, the librarian must walk back to the shelf, retrieve the book again, and bring it to the desk — wasting time. > > 2. **Open Page Policy** → The librarian leaves the book **open on the desk** after you finish. If your next request is another page from the *same* book, the librarian simply flips to it — **almost no delay**. The book is only returned to the shelf (precharged) when a request comes for a *different* book (different row). This desk is the **row buffer (sense amplifiers)**, the book is the **DRAM row**, and the pages you read are **columns** within that row. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Box > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical DRAM Terms (MUST memorize for KTU exams):** > - **Bank** — An independent array of DRAM cells with its own row buffer. > - **Row / Page** — A horizontal line of cells sharing a wordline; typically **8 KB** in DDR4/DDR5. > - **Column** — A vertical bit-line intersection; a single burst access reads multiple columns. > - **Row Buffer** — A set of **sense amplifiers** that latch an entire row after activation. > - **ACTIVATE (RAS)** — Opens a row into the row buffer. > - **READ/WRITE (CAS)** — Accesses a column from the open row buffer. > - **PRECHARGE** — Closes the row, writing it back to the cells. > - **Hit** — Request targets the currently open row (low latency). > - **Miss / Conflict** — Request targets a different row (full ACT+PRECHARGE cycle needed). > - **Empty** — Row buffer is closed (idle state); first access is a **miss** but no precharge needed. ## 1.4 Why Open Page Policy Exists Modern programs exhibit **strong spatial and temporal locality**: - **Spatial Locality** → Consecutive memory addresses often map to the same DRAM row. - **Temporal Locality** → The same address (or row) is reused frequently. Open Page Policy exploits this locality by **predicting the next access** will likely hit the open row. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step by Step The Open Page Policy is implemented by the **DRAM memory controller's scheduler**. The state machine is as follows: ### Step 1: Receive Memory Request The controller receives a physical address from the CPU/cache. It decodes the address into: - **Channel → Rank → Bank → Row → Column** ### Step 2: Compare with Currently Open Row The controller maintains a **row-buffer table (open row register)** per bank. It compares the **row bits** of the incoming request against the currently open row. > **Three possible cases arise:** | Case | State of Row Buffer | Action | Effective Latency | |------|--------------------|--------|-------------------| | **Row Hit** | Requested row == Open row | Issue only **CAS (READ/WRITE)** | **Lowest (≈ t_CAS)** | | **Row Miss (Empty)** | Row buffer is closed (idle) | Issue **ACTIVATE** + **CAS** | **Medium (t_RCD + t_CAS)** | | **Row Conflict** | Requested row ≠ Open row | **PRECHARGE** old row + **ACTIVATE** new row + **CAS** | **Highest (t_RP + t_RCD + t_CAS)** | ### Step 3: Issue DRAM Commands Based on the case above, the controller issues the appropriate sequence of JEDEC-standard commands: - **ACT** (Activate) - **RD / WR** (Read / Write) - **PRE** (Precharge) — only on a conflict ### Step 4: Buffer Remains Open After **CAS** completes, the controller **does NOT issue a PRECHARGE**. The row buffer is left active, latched in the sense amplifiers, waiting for the next access. ### Step 5: Timeout / Eviction If no access arrives for a long time, or the controller decides to switch rows, a **PRECHARGE** is issued to close the row. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **timing parameters** you must know for KTU numerical problems. All values are in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **clock cycles (tCK)** and follow **JEDEC DDR4/DDR5** naming conventions. ### Table 2.1 — Essential DRAM Timing Parameters | Symbol | Full Name | Meaning | Typical DDR4 Value | |--------|-----------|---------|--------------------| | $t_{RCD}$ | RAS-to-CAS Delay | Time from ACTIVATE to READ/WRITE | **14–15 ns** | | $t_{CAS} \;\text{or}\; t_{CL}$ | CAS Latency | Time from READ command to data output | **14–15 ns** | | $t_{RP}$ | Row Precharge | Time to close (precharge) a row | **14–15 ns** | | $t_{RAS}$ | Row Access Strobe | Minimum time ACTIVE must stay open | **32–36 ns** | | $t_{RC}$ | Row Cycle Time | $t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$ — full row cycle | **46–50 ns** | | $t_{CCD}$ | Column-to-Column Delay | Back-to-back CAS in same bank | **4–5 ns** | | $t_{RTP}$ | Read-to-Precharge | Minimum time between last RD and PRE | **7–8 ns** | | $t_{Burst}$ | Burst Length Time | Time to transfer one burst (e.g., BL=8) | **5 ns** (DDR4-3200) | ### Table 2.2 — Effective Latency Formulas | Scenario | Latency Formula | Example (DDR4-2400) | |----------|-----------------|---------------------| | **Row Hit** | $L_{hit} = t_{CAS}$ | **13.75 ns** | | **Row Empty** | $L_{empty} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$ | **26.25 ns** | | **Row Conflict** | $L_{conf} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$ | **41.25 ns** | ### Table 2.3 — Hit Rate vs Throughput | Hit Rate ($h$) | Avg. Latency (mixed workload) | Throughput Impact | |----------------|------------------------------|-------------------| | **90%** | $\approx 0.9 \cdot t_{CAS} + 0.1 \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$ | **Excellent** | | **50%** | $\approx 0.5 \cdot t_{CAS} + 0.5 \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$ | **Mediocre** | | **10%** | $\approx 0.1 \cdot t_{CAS} + 0.9 \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$ | **Poor (worse than Close Page)** | The general formula for **average access latency** is: $$L_{avg} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + (1-h) \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$$ where $h$ is the **row-buffer hit rate** (fraction of accesses that hit the open row). ## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Use > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where is Open Page Policy used in practice?** > - **CPU memory controllers** in Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, and ARM Cortex CPUs. > - **GPU memory controllers** (GDDR6, HBM) — variants adapted for graphics workloads. > - **AI accelerators** (TPU, NVIDIA HBM-based GPUs) — critical for batched matrix access. > - **Database systems** — column-store databases benefit massively when scans stay in one row. > **Engineering trade-off:** Open Page policy is **best for workloads with high row-buffer locality** (e.g., sequential scans, matrix multiplication). It performs **poorly for random access patterns** because conflicts become frequent, wasting both the original precharge and the subsequent activation energy. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time under Open Page Policy We model the DRAM as an $M/M/1$-style queue and define: - $h$ = probability of a **row hit** (access targets the currently open row). - $m$ = probability of a **row miss on empty** (row buffer is closed, first access to a new row). - $c$ = probability of a **row conflict** (access targets a *different* row from the one open). These probabilities satisfy the conservation law: $$h + m + c = 1$$ The **expected (average) access latency** is the weighted sum of the three latencies: $$L_{avg} = h \cdot L_{hit} + m \cdot L_{empty} + c \cdot L_{conf}$$ Substituting the latency expressions from Table 2.2: $$L_{avg} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + m \cdot (t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) + c \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$$ Expanding: $$L_{avg} = (h + m + c) \cdot t_{CAS} + (m + c) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP}$$ Since $(h + m + c) = 1$, this simplifies to: $$\boxed{L_{avg} = t_{CAS} + (m + c) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP}}$$ Equivalently, since $(m + c) = (1 - h)$: $$\boxed{L_{avg} = t_{CAS} + (1 - h) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP}}$$ > **Key insight:** The hit rate $h$ **directly reduces the $t_{RCD}$ penalty**, while the conflict rate $c$ **adds the $t_{RP}$ penalty**. Maximizing $h$ and minimizing $c$ is the goal of every memory controller scheduler. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU-style):** A DDR4-2400 system has $t_{RCD} = 13.75$ ns, $t_{CAS} = 13.75$ ns, and $t_{RP} = 13.75$ ns. A workload produces a row hit rate of 80% and a conflict rate of 15%. Compute the average memory access latency. **Step 1 — Identify the three probabilities.** - $h = 0.80$ (hits) - $c = 0.15$ (conflicts) - $m = 1 - h - c = 1 - 0.80 - 0.15 = 0.05$ (empty) **Step 2 — Compute each scenario's latency.** Row hit latency: $$L_{hit} = t_{CAS} = 13.75 \text{ ns}$$ Row empty latency: $$L_{empty} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 13.75 + 13.75 = 27.50 \text{ ns}$$ Row conflict latency: $$L_{conf} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 13.75 + 13.75 + 13.75 = 41.25 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the weighted average.** $$L_{avg} = 0.80 \cdot 13.75 + 0.05 \cdot 27.50 + 0.15 \cdot 41.25$$ Computing each term: $$0.80 \cdot 13.75 = 11.00 \text{ ns}$$ $$0.05 \cdot 27.50 = 1.375 \text{ ns}$$ $$0.15 \cdot 41.25 = 6.1875 \text{ ns}$$ Summing: $$L_{avg} = 11.00 + 1.375 + 6.1875 = 18.5625 \text{ ns}$$ $$\boxed{L_{avg} \approx 18.56 \text{ ns}}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with Close Page Policy.** Under Close Page, *every* access incurs the full conflict cycle (precharge was implicit before each access), giving: $$L_{close} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 41.25 \text{ ns}$$ The speedup obtained by Open Page over Close Page is: $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{L_{close}}{L_{avg}} = \frac{41.25}{18.5625} \approx 2.22\times$$ > **Interpretation:** With 80% hit rate, Open Page policy is **more than twice as fast** as Close Page for this workload. This is why modern memory controllers universally adopt it. ## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Hit Rate Threshold A common KTU question: *"At what hit rate does Open Page beat Close Page?"* Set the average latency equal to the close-page latency: $$t_{CAS} + (1 - h) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$$ Assuming worst-case $c = (1-h)$ (every miss is a conflict): $$(1-h) \cdot t_{RCD} + (1-h) \cdot t_{RP} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD}$$ $$(1-h) \cdot (t_{RCD} + t_{RP}) = t_{RCD} + t_{RP}$$ $$1 - h = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h = 0$$ > **Conclusion:** The crossover point collapses trivially. In practice, the comparison is: > - If the next access is **guaranteed to be the same row**, Close Page wastes time — Open Page wins. > - If the next access is **guaranteed to be a different row**, Open Page wastes time — Close Page wins. > - With **random access**, Open Page and Close Page perform comparably, but Open Page saves the $t_{RP}$ on every hit. A more meaningful metric: **Open Page wins if the hit rate exceeds a workload-dependent threshold**, typically around **40–60%** for real applications. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Open Page Policy State Machine The following Mermaid diagram shows the **per-bank state machine** maintained by the DRAM controller under Open Page Policy. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Memory Request Arrives] --> B[Decode Address: Bank, Row, Column] B --> C{Compare Requested Row
with Open Row Register} C -->|Match: Row Hit| D[Issue CAS only: READ or WRITE] C -->|Mismatch and Buffer Open: Row Conflict| E[Issue PRECHARGE: close old row] C -->|No Row Open: Buffer Empty| G[Skip Precharge, go to ACTIVATE] E --> F[Wait tRP] F --> H[Issue ACTIVATE: open new row] G --> H H --> I[Wait tRCD] I --> D D --> J[Service the Burst] J --> K{Another Request
in Queue?} K -->|Yes same row| L[Hit: Reuse Open Row Buffer] K -->|Yes different row| E K -->|No idle| M[Keep Buffer Open
Wait for next request] L --> D M --> N[Idle State: Row Open
Sense Amps Latched] N --> A ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with a request arriving. The controller checks the **Open Row Register** and branches into one of three paths. The **green path (right)** is the fast hit path — only a CAS is needed. The **red path (left)** is the slow conflict path, requiring a full PRECHARGE → ACTIVATE → CAS cycle. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DRAM Bank with Open Row Buffer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DRAMCHIP["DRAM Chip - Single Bank"] ARR[Memory Cell Array
Rows x Columns of Capacitors] RB[Row Buffer
Sense Amplifiers latching one full row] DEC[Column Decoder and MUX] ARR -->|Wordline ACTIVATE| RB RB -->|Column Select via CAS| DEC end subgraph CTRL["DRAM Memory Controller"] SCHED[Scheduler and Command Queue] ORR[Open Row Register
per bank: stores currently open row ID] MAP[Address Mapper: Physical Address to Bank, Row, Column] TIMER[Timing Engine: enforces tRCD, tRP, tCAS] MAP --> SCHED ORR --> SCHED SCHED --> TIMER TIMER -->|ACT, RD, WR, PRE commands| DRAMCHIP end CPU[CPU Core issuing Load or Store] --> CTRL DRAMCHIP -->|Data burst on DQ lines| CPU ``` > **Block-level interaction map:** The CPU sends a physical address. The address mapper splits it into **bank, row, column** bits. The scheduler consults the **Open Row Register** to decide whether this is a hit, empty, or conflict. The timing engine then issues the precise sequence of JEDEC commands. The DRAM bank activates the row, latches it into the row buffer, and serves column data without re-activation as long as the row stays open. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Command Sequence Timeline (Hit vs Conflict) ```mermaid gantt title DRAM Command Timeline: Hit vs Conflict dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Row Hit ACTIVATE (none) :done, 0, 1 CAS READ :active, 1, 2 Data burst :active, 2, 3 section Row Conflict PRECHARGE old row :crit, 0, 2 ACTIVATE new row :crit, 2, 4 CAS READ :crit, 4, 6 Data burst :crit, 6, 7 section Row Empty ACTIVATE :active, 0, 2 CAS READ :active, 2, 4 Data burst :active, 4, 5 ``` > **Timeline reading:** A row hit takes essentially **one CAS latency** (~$t_{CAS}$). A conflict takes **three full timings** (PRECHARGE + ACTIVATE + CAS ≈ $t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$). An empty-buffer access sits in between, needing only ACTIVATE + CAS. The visual gap between the hit and conflict bars illustrates why maximizing the hit rate is so important. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]** ### Q1. Define Open Page Row Buffer Management Policy. How does it differ from Close Page Policy? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Open Page Policy is a DRAM management strategy in which, after a memory access, the **row buffer is left open** so subsequent accesses to the same row can be serviced with only a CAS command (lowest latency). In contrast, **Close Page Policy** issues a **PRECHARGE immediately** after each access, so every subsequent access — even to the same row — requires a full ACTIVATE → CAS → PRECHARGE sequence. (3 marks) --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q2. List the three possible row-buffer states in an Open Page Policy and the DRAM command sequence triggered by each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Row Hit** → Requested row matches the open row → only **CAS (READ/WRITE)** is issued. (1 mark) 2. **Row Miss on Empty Buffer** → No row is open → **ACTIVATE** + **CAS** are issued. (1 mark) 3. **Row Conflict** → Requested row differs from the open row → **PRECHARGE** (old) + **ACTIVATE** (new) + **CAS** are issued. (1 mark) --- ## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 14 Marks]** ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the Open Page Row Buffer Management Policy with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Open Page Policy is a DRAM row-buffer management technique where, after a memory access, the activated row remains latched in the sense amplifiers (row buffer) and is **not precharged**. The buffer stays open, ready to serve the next request. **Operation (3 marks):** The memory controller maintains a per-bank **Open Row Register**. For each new request: - **Hit** → Issue only CAS → latency = $t_{CAS}$. - **Miss (empty)** → Issue ACTIVATE then CAS → latency = $t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$. - **Conflict** → Issue PRECHARGE, then ACTIVATE, then CAS → latency = $t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$. **Diagram (1 mark):** The state machine shown in Section 4.1 of these notes illustrates the three branches. **Advantages (0.5 marks):** - Lowest possible latency for row hits (just $t_{CAS}$). - Maximizes throughput for workloads with strong row-buffer locality. - Energy-efficient: avoids redundant ACTIVATE/PRECHARGE on hits. **Disadvantages (0.5 marks):** - Performs poorly for random-access workloads. - Conflicts incur the *worst* possible latency (full triple penalty). - Requires additional controller logic (Open Row Register + comparator). --- #### (b) A DDR4-2400 system has $t_{RCD} = 13.75$ ns, $t_{CAS} = 13.75$ ns, $t_{RP} = 13.75$ ns. For a workload with 70% hit rate, 20% conflict rate, and 10% empty-buffer rate, calculate the average access latency. Compare it with Close Page Policy. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Given (1 mark):** - $t_{RCD} = t_{CAS} = t_{RP} = 13.75$ ns - $h = 0.70$, $c = 0.20$, $m = 0.10$ **Step 1 — Latency for each case (2 marks):** $$L_{hit} = t_{CAS} = 13.75 \text{ ns}$$ $$L_{empty} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 13.75 + 13.75 = 27.50 \text{ ns}$$ $$L_{conf} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 13.75 \times 3 = 41.25 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 2 — Average latency (2 marks):** $$L_{avg} = h \cdot L_{hit} + m \cdot L_{empty} + c \cdot L_{conf}$$ $$L_{avg} = 0.70 \cdot 13.75 + 0.10 \cdot 27.50 + 0.20 \cdot 41.25$$ $$L_{avg} = 9.625 + 2.750 + 8.250 = 20.625 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison with Close Page (2 marks):** $$L_{close} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS} = 41.25 \text{ ns}$$ $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{41.25}{20.625} \approx 2.00\times$$ > **Conclusion:** Open Page Policy is **2× faster** for this workload. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) With a state-transition diagram, explain the operation of Open Page Policy. How does the memory controller decide which DRAM command to issue? (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **State Machine (3 marks):** The controller transitions through the following states per bank: **IDLE → ROW_OPEN → SERVING → ROW_OPEN (or PRECHARGING)**. On every memory request, the controller: 1. Decodes the address into **bank, row, column**. 2. Compares the incoming row with the **Open Row Register** for that bank. 3. Issues commands based on the match result. **Decision logic (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Request] --> B{Row in Open Register?} B -->|Yes, same row| C[Issue CAS only] B -->|No, register empty| D[Issue ACTIVATE then CAS] B -->|No, different row| E[Issue PRECHARGE, then ACTIVATE, then CAS] ``` **Command table (1 mark):** Already covered in the answer to Question A part (a). --- #### (b) Discuss the impact of row-buffer hit rate on overall memory system performance. Derive the average access latency formula and plot a qualitative latency vs. hit-rate graph. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Derivation (4 marks):** The general average latency is: $$L_{avg} = t_{CAS} + (1-h) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP}$$ Substituting $c = (1-h)$ in the worst case: $$L_{avg} = t_{CAS} + (1-h) \cdot (t_{RCD} + t_{RP})$$ This is a **linear** function of $(1-h)$. As $h$ grows, $L_{avg}$ drops linearly. **Qualitative graph (2 marks):** The latency vs. hit-rate curve is a **decreasing straight line** that flattens at $h = 1$ (latency = $t_{CAS}$) and rises to $L_{conf}$ at $h = 0$. **Discussion (1 mark):** High hit rates are achievable in workloads like: - Sequential array scans (databases, image processing). - Matrix multiplication (tiled access patterns). - Loop-based numerical code. Low hit rates dominate in: - Pointer-chasing (linked lists, trees). - Hash tables with random probing. > **Engineering insight:** Modern memory controllers combine Open Page Policy with **request reordering and batching** (e.g., FR-FCFS — First-Ready, First-Come-First-Serve) to *artificially boost* the effective hit rate by reordering requests to hit the open row first. --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize:** > 1. **Forgetting $t_{RP}$ in conflict latency** — Students often write $L_{conf} = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$, omitting the precharge. This loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "Row Hit" with "Cache Hit"** — They are unrelated. A row hit means the same DRAM bank row is open, *regardless* of cache state. > 3. **Mixing up bank, rank, and channel** — In the address mapping, a row-buffer hit is checked *per bank*, not per channel or rank. > 4. **Wrong units** — $t_{RCD}$, $t_{CAS}$, $t_{RP}$ are typically in nanoseconds. If the question gives them in clock cycles, you must multiply by $t_{CK}$. > 5. **Not labeling the diagram** — KTU awards marks for labeled state transitions. A bare box-and-arrow diagram without labels may get only 0.5 out of 1 mark for the diagram. > 6. **Omitting the $h + m + c = 1$ check** — If your three probabilities do not sum to 1, the examiner will mark you down and the calculation will be wrong. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minure KTU Prep):** > - [x] **Open Page Policy** = leave the row buffer **open** after an access. > - [x] **Close Page Policy** = precharge (close) the row buffer **immediately** after an access. > - [x] Three row-buffer states: **Hit, Empty, Conflict**. > - [x] **Hit** latency = $t_{CAS}$ (fastest). > - [x] **Empty** latency = $t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$. > - [x] **Conflict** latency = $t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$ (slowest). > - [x] **Average latency formula:** $L_{avg} = h \cdot t_{CAS} + m \cdot (t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}) + c \cdot (t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS})$. > - [x] **Conservation law:** $h + m + c = 1$. > - [x] **Simplified form:** $L_{avg} = t_{CAS} + (1-h) \cdot t_{RCD} + c \cdot t_{RP}$. > - [x] Open Page wins for **locality-friendly** workloads; loses for **random-access** patterns. > - [x] Modern controllers use **FR-FCFS scheduling** to boost the effective hit rate. > - [x] Per-bank **Open Row Register** is the key controller data structure. > - [x] Typical DDR4 values: $t_{RCD} \approx t_{CAS} \approx t_{RP} \approx 13.75$ ns at 2400 MT/s. > - [x] **Energy benefit:** avoiding ACTIVATE/PRECHARGE on hits saves substantial DRAM power. > - [x] **Most common KTU trick:** a question gives a hit rate and asks to compute average latency — always break it into the three weighted cases, never approximate.

topics.title: Close Page Row Buffer Management Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Close Page Row Buffer Management Policy # Module 3: Close Page Row Buffer Management Policy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Close Page Row Buffer Management Policy** is a DRAM controller scheduling strategy in which the row (page) currently residing in a bank's **row buffer (sense amplifiers)** is **immediately precharged (closed)** at the earliest legal time after a column access (READ or WRITE) operation completes. Under this policy, no two consecutive requests can hit (be a "page hit" for) the same row within the same bank, because the row buffer is always returned to the **IDLE / precharged** state after servicing every transaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCITT302 – Module 3):** > Row buffer management policies (Open Page vs. Close Page) belong to the **Memory Organization** unit covering DRAM internals, bank structure, and controller-level scheduling. The Close Page policy is the **safest, most deterministic, and lowest-power** of the two standard policies taught. ### 1.2 Key DRAM Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Bank** | An independent 2-D array of DRAM cells sharing a single row buffer. | | **Row / Page** | A horizontal strip of cells (typically **8 KB** in DDR4/DDR5 for ×8 chips). | | **Row Buffer** | Latch of sense amplifiers that holds the most recently activated row. | | **ACTIVATE** | Command that opens a row: drives wordline high, latches the row into sense amplifiers. | | **READ / WRITE** | Column command that accesses one or more columns from the open row. | | **PRECHARGE** | Command that closes the open row, equalises bitlines, and returns the bank to IDLE. | ### 1.3 Intuition — Plain English Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Library Reading Desk** > Imagine a library where a librarian pulls one *entire shelf* (the **row**) onto a side table (the **row buffer**) every time you ask for a single book. There are two possible rules: > > - **Open Page Rule:** "I'll keep the shelf on the side table. If you ask for another book from the same shelf, I'll hand it to you instantly." > - **Close Page Rule:** "After giving you your book, I'll put the shelf back immediately. Next time, even if you want the very next book on the same shelf, I must pull the shelf out all over again." > > The **Close Page** rule wastes a little effort on every request, but it guarantees the side table is **always free** for the next visitor and **consumes less energy** keeping the shelf open. This trade-off is exactly what the **Close Page Row Buffer Management Policy** does in a DRAM bank. ### 1.4 Why Does This Policy Exist? DRAM cells are destructive — every read requires a sense-and-restore. Keeping a row **open** consumes **standby power** (the sense amplifiers stay energised). In workloads with **poor locality** (e.g., random pointer-chasing, hash tables, hard real-time control loops), keeping a row open is wasteful because the next access is unlikely to hit the same row. **Close Page** sacrifices a small amount of latency for predictable, power-efficient operation. ### 1.5 What "Close Page" Guarantees - After every column access, the controller **issues a PRECHARGE** either explicitly (PRECHARGE command) or implicitly (READ with auto-precharge, denoted `RDA` / `WRA`). - The bank returns to the **IDLE state** before the next ACTIVATE. - The next access to the same bank **must pay the full tRP + tRCD** latency, **regardless of which row it targets**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Steps (End-to-End Sequence) For a single memory transaction under **Close Page**, the DRAM controller issues the following strict command sequence: 1. **Issue `ACTIVATE` to the target Bank & Row.** - Wordline of the target row is raised. - Bitlines are sensed and latched into the row buffer. - Wait at least **tRCD** (RAS-to-CAS Delay) before the next command. 2. **Issue `READ` or `WRITE` to the target Column.** - Data is transferred on the next rising/falling edge of DQS. - Wait at least **tCL** (CAS Latency) for READ data to appear on the DQ bus. - For burst-of-8 transfers (DDR4), wait `tCL + 4` cycles for full burst. 3. **Issue `PRECHARGE` immediately after the column command.** - The row buffer contents are **restored back** into the cell array (refresh sense-amplifier data into capacitors). - Bitlines are equalised to VDD/2. - Wait at least **tRP** (Row Precharge time) before the next ACTIVATE. 4. **Bank is now IDLE** → ready for the next transaction from any thread/requestor. > [!IMPORTANT] > Under **Close Page**, the cycle length for a single random access is: > $$\text{Cycle} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP} \quad (\text{plus transfer time})$$ ## 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — The Underlying Logic - **Why close immediately?** Because the next access is *most likely* to a *different* row in modern multi-core workloads; holding the row open wastes **dynamic power** in the sense amplifiers (≈ 30–40 % of total DRAM power). - **How is closing done?** Either by an explicit `PRE` command *after* the column command, or by setting `A10 = 1` during the column command (this is called **auto-precharge**, an in-DRAM shortcut that fuses precharge into the column cycle). - **Where is the controller?** In the **Memory Controller** of the CPU's System Agent / Uncore — modern Intel/AMD/ARM cores contain a sophisticated scheduler that picks Open vs. Close on a *per-request* basis (adaptive policies). ## 2.3 Detailed Comparison: Close Page vs. Open Page | Parameter | **Close Page Policy** | **Open Page Policy** | |---|---|---| | Row buffer state after access | **Precharged (empty)** | **Held open with last row** | | Consecutive same-row access | Must re-ACTIVATE → **miss latency** | Direct READ → **hit latency** | | Consecutive different-row access | Same as random | Must PRECHARGE + ACTIVATE → **miss latency** | | **Average latency** for **random workload** | **Lower** (no wasted precharges) | Higher (extra precharges on misses) | | **Average latency** for **streaming workload** | Higher (every access full cost) | **Lower** (row stays open, reuse hits) | | **Power consumption** | **Lower** (row buffer de-energised) | Higher (sense amps stay on) | | Predictability / determinism | **High** (constant latency) | Low (variable, locality-dependent) | | Best-suited workload | Random / hard real-time | Streaming / high spatial locality | | Used in | Embedded, real-time, low-power | Desktops, servers, GPUs | | KTU mark weight | 3 – 7 marks (definitions + comparison) | 3 – 7 marks | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) for absolute value / divider bars in tables — never raw `|`.** | Symbol | Name | Typical Value (DDR4-2400) | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | $t_{RCD}$ | RAS-to-CAS Delay | **15 ns** | ACTIVATE → READ/WRITE delay | | $t_{CL}$ | CAS Latency | **15 ns** | READ cmd → first data on DQ | | $t_{RP}$ | Row Precharge | **15 ns** | PRECHARGE → next ACTIVATE delay | | $t_{RAS}$ | Row Active Time | **33 ns** | ACTIVATE → same-bank PRECHARGE | | $t_{RC}$ | Row Cycle Time | **48 ns** | $t_{RAS} + t_{RP}$ — back-to-back ACTs | | $t_{BURST}$ | Burst transfer | **4 ns** (8-beat burst) | Time to move a cache line (64 B) | | $N_{banks}$ | Banks per rank | **8** (DDR4) | Parallelism unit | | $N_{ranks}$ | Ranks per channel | **1 – 2** | Independent DRAMs sharing channel | ### Core Equations **Equation 1 — Close-Page cycle time for a single random access:** $$T_{CP} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{BURST} + t_{RP}$$ **Equation 2 — Open-Page hit cycle time (consecutive same-row):** $$T_{OP\_hit} = t_{CL} + t_{BURST}$$ **Equation 3 — Open-Page miss cycle time (different row):** $$T_{OP\_miss} = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{BURST}$$ **Equation 4 — Total service time for $N$ accesses with hit-ratio $h$:** $$T_{total} = N \cdot \left[ h \cdot T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss} \right]$$ **Equation 5 — Bank-level throughput (Close Page):** $$\text{Throughput}_{CP} = \dfrac{N_{banks}}{T_{CP}} \;\;\text{accesses / second}$$ **Equation 6 — Average memory access time under Close Page:** $$\text{AMAT}_{CP} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{BURST} + t_{RP} + \dfrac{Q \cdot T_{CP}}{N_{banks}}$$ where the last term captures the queuing delay $Q$ amortised over the $N_{banks}$ parallel banks. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded & Automotive (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262):** Determinism is non-negotiable. Close Page gives **WCET-bounded** latencies → the timing analyser can certify worst-case execution time. - **Networking ASICs / NPUs:** Packet buffers are scanned randomly → Close Page wins. - **Mobile SoCs (smartphone AP):** Idle screens & low-utilisation phases use Close Page aggressively to hit **standby current budgets** of <1 mA. - **Database engines:** A row-store database with random B-tree probes benefits from Close Page; a column-store doing sequential scans benefits from Open Page. Modern memory controllers therefore run **adaptive** policies. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Latency of Close Page vs. Open Page **Setup:** A workload issues $N = 1000$ accesses to a single bank in a stream.
