# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q. Define Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP). List any two limitations of bus-based SMP architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1½ + ½ + ½ + ½):**

**Definition (1½ marks):**
> An **SMP** is a tightly-coupled multiprocessor in which *two or more identical processors* share a **single physical main memory** and a **single I/O subsystem**, with all processors having **equal access time** (Uniform Memory Access – UMA) to all memory locations and I/O devices. Any processor can execute any task, including OS kernel code; there is no master-slave relationship.

**Limitations of bus-based SMP (2 × ½ = 1 mark):**
1. **Bus saturation / bandwidth bottleneck** – Every coherence transaction is broadcast on the shared bus; as $N$ grows, the bus becomes the performance bottleneck.
2. **Limited scalability** – Practical bus-based SMPs are limited to ~8–16 processors because of electrical loading and bandwidth constraints.
3. (bonus) **Single point of failure** – Bus failure cripples the entire system.

---

### Question A2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q. Explain the MESI protocol. Why is the 'E' (Exclusive) state useful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 + 1 + 1):**

**MESI States (1 mark):**
> The four states of each cache line in MESI are **M**odified (dirty, sole valid copy), **E**xclusive (clean, sole copy, matches memory), **S**hared (clean, ≥2 copies, matches memory), and **I**nvalid (not present).

**Why E is useful (1 mark):**
> The **E state** indicates that *this* cache is the sole owner of a clean line. The next write by this core can therefore proceed **silently** (state transition **E → M** with **zero bus transactions**), avoiding the broadcast invalidation that would be required from the S state. This greatly reduces bus traffic for single-thread or private-data workloads.

**Bus transactions saved (1 mark):**
> Typical write to S → M requires a `BusRdX` invalidation broadcast (1+ bus cycles). E → M is **0 bus cycles**. This is one of the most important micro-optimizations in real silicon.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – ESE Module 4 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Internal Choice Set 1]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Multicore Processor** (Chip-Multiprocessor). Discuss the role of shared L3 cache and directory controller in maintaining cache coherence. (7 marks)

**(b)** A program has **85 % parallel code** and runs on a **16-core** multicore processor. Compute the speedup obtained. What is the **maximum possible speedup** even with infinite cores? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
+----------+   +----------+   +----------+   +----------+
| Core 0   |   | Core 1   |   | Core 2   |   | Core 3   |
| L1 I/D   |   | L1 I/D   |   | L1 I/D   |   | L1 I/D   |
| L2-0     |   | L2-1     |   | L2-2     |   | L2-3     |
+----------+   +----------+   +----------+   +----------+
       \             |             |             /
        \            |             |            /
         +-----------+-------------+-----------+
                     |
            +-------------------+
            | Shared L3 (LLC)   |
            | + Directory Ctl   |
            +-------------------+
                     |
            +-------------------+
            | Memory Controller |
            +-------------------+
                     |
                Main Memory
```

**Architectural explanation (3 marks):**
- A **multicore processor** (CMP) integrates two or more independent *processing cores* (with private L1 and L2 caches) onto a single silicon die.
- All cores share a common **last-level cache (LLC, e.g., L3)** and the **memory controller**.
- Cores communicate via the **shared interconnect** (ring, mesh, or crossbar).
- The OS treats each core as a *logical processor* and schedules threads / processes onto them.

**Role of shared L3 + Directory Controller (2 marks):**
- The **shared L3** acts as a *screening filter* — inter-core data sharing often goes through L3, reducing coherence traffic between private L2s.
- The **directory controller** (in the L3 / memory controller) keeps a *presence-bit vector* for every L3 line, indicating which cores' L2 caches hold a copy.
- On a write to a shared line, the directory sends **point-to-point invalidation messages** to only those cores listed as sharers, avoiding broadcast storms.
- This **hybrid snooping-within-chip + directory-across-chips** design is what makes 16-, 32-, and 64-core designs practical.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $P = 0.85,\ N = 16$.

**Step 1 – Amdahl's Law formula (1 mark):**
$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values (2 marks):**
$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125} = \frac{1}{0.203125}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute (1 mark):**
$$
S(16) \approx 4.923
$$

**Step 4 – Maximum possible speedup with infinite cores (2 marks):**
$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.667
$$

**Step 5 – Conclusion (1 mark):**
> The 16-core system achieves a speedup of **~4.92×**, which is **73.8 % of the theoretical maximum** of 6.67×. Doubling to 32 cores would yield only $\approx 5.71\times$ — a clear demonstration of diminishing returns governed by Amdahl's Law.

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Internal Choice Set 1]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **cache coherence problem** in shared memory multiprocessors. Describe the **MESI protocol** with all four state transitions for a `PrWr` (processor write) operation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A shared-memory multiprocessor has the following parameters:
- L1 hit rate = 92 %, L1 access time = 1 ns
- L2 hit rate = 95 %, L2 access time = 6 ns
- L3 hit rate = 98 %, L3 access time = 20 ns
- Main memory access time = 100 ns
- **Coherence-miss rate** = 2 % of L1 misses, each coherence miss adds **40 extra cycles**.
- **Fraction of memory instructions** in program = 30 %.

Compute the **effective CPI** of a core given the base CPI (no memory stall) is 1.0. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Cache Coherence Problem (2 marks):**
> In a system with *per-core private caches*, the same memory address can be cached in multiple cores' caches in **different states**. Without a protocol, two cores can return **different values** for the same address — violating the programmer's view of a single shared memory. Example: Core-0 writes `A = 5` (in its cache), Core-1 still reads `A = 0` from its stale copy.

**MESI States (1 mark):**
- **M**odified, **E**xclusive, **S**hared, **I**nvalid.

**Transitions on PrWr (4 marks):**

| From State | Bus Action | To State | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | `BusRdX` (read-with-intent-to-modify) | **M** | Line fetched from memory, marked Modified; other caches invalidate. |
| **S** | `BusUpgr` or `BusRdX` (invalidation broadcast) | **M** | Other S copies invalidate; we become sole Modified owner. |
| **E** | **None** (silent) | **M** | We already own the only copy; write locally without bus traffic. |
| **M** | **None** (silent) | **M** | We are already the Modified owner; write goes only to our cache. |

**Diagram (1 mark):**
```
I --PrWr+BusRdX--> M
S --PrWr+BusUpgr--> M
E --PrWr silent --> M
M --PrWr silent --> M
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $h_1 = 0.92,\ h_2 = 0.95,\ h_3 = 0.98,\ T_1 = 1,\ T_2 = 6,\ T_3 = 20,\ T_m = 100$ (all in ns), coherence extra penalty = 40 cycles per coherence miss, fraction of mem instructions = 0.30, base CPI = 1.0.

**Step 1 – Compute basic MMAT (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MMAT}_{\text{base}} &= h_1 T_1 + (1-h_1) h_2 T_2 + (1-h_1)(1-h_2) h_3 T_3 + (1-h_1)(1-h_2)(1-h_3) T_m \\
&= 0.92(1) + 0.08 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 6 + 0.08 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 20 + 0.08 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.02 \cdot 100 \\
&= 0.92 + 0.456 + 0.0784 + 0.008 \\
&= 1.4624 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Coherence-miss stall per memory instruction (2 marks):**
Coherence-miss fraction = $0.02$ of L1 misses, so coherence miss probability per memory instruction $= 0.30 \times 0.08 \times 0.02 = 0.00048$.

Stall cycles per memory instruction $= 0.00048 \times 40 = 0.0192$ cycles.

**Step 3 – Convert MMAT to CPI contribution (1 mark):**
Assuming 1 GHz clock (1 ns = 1 cycle), MMAT contribution to CPI per memory instruction = $1.4624$ cycles.

**Step 4 – Total CPI (1 mark):**
$$
CPI_{\text{eff}} = CPI_{\text{base}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{mem}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{coh}} = 1.0 + 1.4624 + 0.0192 = 2.4816
$$

**Conclusion:** The effective per-core CPI is **≈ 2.48**, meaning the coherence-miss penalty alone adds only **~0.8 %** to CPI in this configuration — the bulk of the stall is the L3 miss penalty. (A real exam answer would also include a brief sensitivity note: doubling $N$ to 8 cores typically doubles coherence-miss rate.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points on This Topic (2024 KTU Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing coherence with consistency.** Coherence is *per-address*. Consistency is the *global ordering* across addresses. Lose 1 mark each time the terms are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the "silent E → M" transition.** This is the single most-tested MESI subtlety. Always state explicitly that the bus is *not* used.
> 3. **Using the wrong Amdahl formula.** A few students write $S = P \cdot N + (1-P)$. This is the *linear* speedup model, valid *only* for fully parallel programs. The correct form is the harmonic-like expression $1/((1-P)+P/N)$.
> 4. **Omitting the directory's $O(MN)$ storage cost.** When justifying "directory doesn't scale to 10 000 cores," you must mention storage.
> 5. **Drawing MESI transitions without the bus action** (e.g., writing "I → M" without saying *BusRdX*). The bus action is *part* of the transition.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. Always include `ns`, `×`, or `cycles`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 – Multicore & Shared Memory Multiprocessors: One-Page Recap**

- [x] **SMP** = identical cores + single shared memory + uniform access (UMA).
- [x] **Multicore (CMP)** = 2+ cores on a single die, sharing L3 + memory controller.
- [x] **NUMA** = shared address space, non-uniform access time ($T_{\text{remote}} > T_{\text{local}}$).
- [x] **Cache coherence problem** = multiple stale copies of one address in different caches.
- [x] **Snooping protocols** = every cache monitors a broadcast bus. Scale ≤ 16 cores.
- [x] **MESI** = Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid. **E → M** is the silent write.
- [x] **MOESI** = adds *Owned* state to share dirty data without main-memory round-trip.
- [x] **Directory protocols** = central bit-vector per memory line. Scale to 1000+ cores. $O(MN)$ storage.
- [x] **Memory consistency models**: SC > TSO > Release/Weak. *Coherence ≠ Consistency*.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = 1/((1-P) + P/N)$; $S_\infty = 1/(1-P)$. Serial fraction is the ceiling.
- [x] **Multithreading flavours**: Coarse (stall-trigger), Fine (cycle-trigger), SMT (issue-slot mixing).
- [x] **Power wall** = $P = CV^2f$ saturation around 3–4 GHz drove the multicore revolution.
- [x] **Cache-to-cache transfer** = "intervention" when a Modified line is requested by another core (M → S with data flush).
- [x] **Coherence misses** = True Sharing, False Sharing, Invalidation-induced Capacity. (4 C's extended.)
- [x] **Hybrid coherence** = modern chips use snooping *within* the die + directory *across* sockets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Intel Core i7
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT302 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Intel Core i7

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intel Core i7** is a family of high-end desktop and laptop **microprocessors** introduced by Intel in November 2008, designed using the **Intel 64 (x86-64) instruction set architecture (ISA)**. The Core i7 represents Intel's flagship line of consumer-grade CPUs, implementing advanced architectural features such as **out-of-order execution (OoOE)**, **simultaneous multithreading (SMT)** branded as *Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT)*, **multi-level adaptive cache hierarchies**, an **integrated memory controller (IMC)**, and a **shared system agent** that connects cores, graphics, and I/O subsystems.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** classification, the Core i7 serves as a canonical *real-world case study* of a **superscalar, multi-core, deeply pipelined, CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) processor** that integrates nearly every modern architectural optimization studied in this course — pipelining, branch prediction, cache coherence, speculative execution, and dynamic frequency scaling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITT302 – Module 4):**
> The case study on Intel Core i7 consolidates learning from earlier modules — CPU design, memory hierarchy, pipelining, and I/O — by mapping theoretical concepts onto a tangible commercial processor. It is a **favourite KTU board topic** often appearing in 14-mark analytical/descriptive questions.

---

## 1.2 The Core i7 Family — Generational Snapshot

The Core i7 brand has evolved through multiple **process–architecture–optimization (tick-tock) cycles**. Each generation (codenamed after a geographic location) brings refinements in pipeline depth, transistor count, cache size, and IPC (Instructions Per Cycle).

| Codename | Year | Process (nm) | Key Architectural Change |
|----------|------|--------------|--------------------------|
| Nehalem | 2008 | 45 | First Core i7, native 4C/8T, IMC, QPI |
| Westmere | 2010 | 32 | 6C/12T, AES-NI, improved Turbo |
| Sandy Bridge | 2011 | 32 | 256-bit AVX, ring interconnect, integrated GPU |
| Ivy Bridge | 2012 | 22 | FinFET tri-gate (3D) transistors |
| Haswell | 2013 | 22 | AVX2, FMA3, fully integrated voltage regulator (FIVR) |
| Broadwell | 2014 | 14 | 14 nm, lower TDP, eDRAM (Crystal Well) |
| Skylake | 2015 | 14 | Modular SoC design, DDR4, optane support |
| Kaby Lake / Coffee Lake | 2017 | 14 | 6-core mainstream, more cores per die |
| Comet / Rocket Lake | 2020 | 14 / 14+ | Up to 8C/16T mainstream, AVX-512 |
| Alder Lake | 2021 | Intel 7 | Hybrid P-core + E-core architecture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exam purposes, focus on the **Nehalem / Sandy Bridge / Haswell** microarchitectures, as they are the most commonly cited textbook examples (referenced in standard texts by Hamacher, Stallings, and Patterson & Hennessy).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Building

Imagine the Intel Core i7 processor as a **giant, multi-storey office building**:

- **Each floor is a core** — an independent office where workers (instructions) can be processed in parallel.
- **The receptionist (Control Unit)** at each floor decides *which task* to handle next, *in what order*, and *whether to start something new* before the current one finishes.
- **The shared pantry (L3 Smart Cache)** sits at the centre of the building — any floor can walk over to grab a document (data) at high speed, without going to the basement (RAM).
- **The basement (Main Memory / DRAM)** is large but slow; the pantry is small but fast.
- **The lift (System Agent / Ring Bus)** connects every floor to the pantry and the basement efficiently.
- **Hyper-Threading** is like having **two capable workers share one office desk** — they can't *both* use the calculator at the same time, but they can keep the desk busy by switching between their own tasks whenever one is waiting on a file.

This is exactly how a Core i7 manages **instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** and **thread-level parallelism (TLP)** in silicon.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Must-Know Acronyms

| Acronym | Expansion | Relevance |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **CPU** | Central Processing Unit | The Core i7 chip itself |
| **IPC** | Instructions Per Cycle | Performance metric — higher = better |
| **HTT** | Hyper-Threading Technology | Intel's brand for SMT (2 logical cores per physical core) |
| **QPI** | QuickPath Interconnect | High-speed on-package link (pre-Skylake) |
| **DMI** | Direct Media Interface | Replaces QPI for chipset link in newer i7s |
| **IMC** | Integrated Memory Controller | On-die DDR controller |
| **AVX** | Advanced Vector Extensions | 256-bit SIMD floating-point operations |
| **IPC** (vs *frequency*) | Not to be confused with Inter-Process Communication | Context-dependent — KTU exams often clarify |
| **FIVR** | Fully Integrated Voltage Regulator | On-die power delivery (Haswell+) |
| **TDP** | Thermal Design Power | Power envelope in **watts (W)** |
| **µop** | Micro-operation | Internal RISC-like ops after CISC decode |
| **OoOE** | Out-of-Order Execution | Dynamic instruction reordering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold units/metrics** that KTU expects you to remember: **TDP (W)**, **frequency (GHz)**, **cache size (MB)**, **IPC**, **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**.

---

## 1.5 Why Study the Core i7? — KTU Examiner's Motivation

The Core i7 is a **single, self-contained ecosystem** that demonstrates *every* major concept in PCITT302:

1. **Pipelining & Superscalar issue** — multiple µops per cycle.
2. **Branch prediction & speculative execution** — tournament predictor, micro-op cache.
3. **Cache hierarchy** — per-core L1/L2 + shared L3 "Smart Cache".
4. **Virtual memory & TLBs** — 2-level TLB hierarchy.
5. **Multi-core & coherence** — MESI/MESIF protocol on ring bus.
6. **SMT / Hyper-Threading** — logical cores sharing execution units.
7. **Power management** — Turbo Boost, C-states, Speed Shift.
8. **SIMD / Vector units** — SSE, AVX, AVX2, AVX-512.

> [!TIP]
> When KTU asks *"Explain the architectural features of the Intel Core i7"*, do not just list features — **map each feature to the underlying principle** (e.g., "Hyper-Threading implements SMT, which improves TLP by sharing execution units between two threads").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nehalem Microarchitecture — The Original Core i7 Blueprint

The **Nehalem** generation (Bloomfield/Gainestown, 2008) established the template. Each Core i7 die contains:

- **2 to 8 physical cores**, each with:
  - **L1 Instruction Cache (L1I)**: 32 KB, 8-way set associative, 64-byte line.
  - **L1 Data Cache (L1D)**: 32 KB, 8-way set associative, write-back.
  - **L2 Unified Cache**: 256 KB per core, 8-way, 64-byte line, ~10 cycle latency.
  - **Two levels of branch prediction**: a fast BIP (Bimode / Branch Information Pointer) predictor + a slower BTB (Branch Target Buffer).
- **Shared L3 Smart Cache**: 4–12 MB, 16-way, inclusive of L1/L2 (in early Nehalem) or non-inclusive in newer designs.
- **System Agent (Uncore)**: houses the IMC, QPI links, and ring interconnect.
- **Integrated Memory Controller (IMC)**: triple-channel DDR3 in Bloomfield; dual-channel in Lynnfield.

### The In-Order Front-End

The front-end of a Core i7 pipeline operates in **three major stages**:

1. **Predecode / Instruction Fetch** — 16 bytes/cycle, fetching from the **µop cache** (introduced in Sandy Bridge, 1.5 K entries) or from the L1I cache after decoding.
2. **Instruction Decode** — the CISC x86 instructions are broken into **RISC-like micro-operations (µops)** by hardware decoders (4 simple decoders + 1 complex decoder in Nehalem).
3. **Microcode ROM** — handles complex x86 instructions (e.g., `REP MOVSB`, `XCHG` with lock prefix).

### The Out-of-Order Back-End

After decode, µops are deposited into the **Reorder Buffer (ROB)**, the **Reservation Station (RS)**, and the **Register Alias Table (RAT)**. Key buffers in Nehalem:

- **ROB capacity**: 128 entries.
- **Reservation Station**: 36 entries (Nehalem) — dispatch up to 6 µops/cycle to execution ports.
- **Integer Register File**: 128 entries.
- **Floating-Point Register File**: 128 entries, 256-bit wide (supports AVX).
- **Load / Store buffers**: each 48 entries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dispatch width** of Core i7 is **4 µops/cycle** (with one more possible as a branch), and the **retire width** is **4 µops/cycle**. This defines the pipeline's effective IPC ceiling under ideal conditions.

---

## 2.2 Execution Ports — The Heart of Superscalar Issue

A Core i7 has **6 (Nehalem) to 8 (Sandy Bridge+) execution ports**. Each port can issue a specific kind of µop:

| Port | Function |
|------|----------|
| **Port 0** | Integer ALU, branch, FMA (Sandy Bridge+) |
| **Port 1** | Integer ALU, LEA, branch, FP multiply |
| **Port 2** | Load AGU (address generation) |
| **Port 3** | Store AGU |
| **Port 4** | Store data |
| **Port 5** | FP add, FP multiply (Sandy Bridge+) |

A single complex integer instruction may require **two ports** simultaneously (e.g., multiplication on Port 1 + store address on Port 3). **Port pressure** is what limits real-world IPC below the theoretical maximum.

---

## 2.3 The Smart Cache — A KTU Favourite

The **L3 Smart Cache** is a unified, shared, inclusive cache that:

- Is **shared by all cores** — no duplication, hence "smart" (efficient capacity use).
- Uses the **ring interconnect** (Sandy Bridge+) to keep coherence traffic low.
- Implements a variant of the **MESIF protocol** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid, Forward) for inter-core coherence.
- **Snoops** cache line transactions on the ring to maintain the *MESI invariant* across all cores.

**Cache lookup flow** for a memory access:
1. CPU checks **L1D** (3–4 cycle latency).
2. On miss, checks **L2** (10–12 cycles).
3. On miss, checks **L3** (~30–40 cycles, via ring bus).
4. On miss, fetches from **DRAM** (via IMC, ~60 ns).

---

## 2.4 Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT)

Each **physical core** exposes **two logical cores (threads)** to the OS. Key idea:

- The **architectural state** (registers, EFLAGS, IP) is duplicated.
- The **execution units, caches, and TLB** are *shared*.
- The **front-end** alternates between two threads to hide stalls.
- The **back-end (ROB, RS)** is partitioned, giving each thread its own slice.

**Performance gain**: typical speedup is **1.2× to 1.3×** for general workloads, up to **~1.7×** for highly parallel server workloads, but can degrade to **0.9×** for code that already saturates a core.

> [!WARNING]
> Hyper-Threading does **not** double compute throughput — it only improves utilization. Two threads on one core cannot execute two integer multiplies per cycle, because the multiplier unit is unique.

---

## 2.5 Turbo Boost Technology

**Turbo Boost** dynamically raises the operating frequency of one or more cores above the base clock, *provided* the package TDP and thermal limits allow. Algorithm (simplified):

$$
P_{total} = P_{cores} + P_{uncore} \le TDP_{max}
$$

If a single core is active and the others are in C-state sleep, Intel shifts the unused power budget to the active core, raising its multiplier (e.g., from 3.0 GHz base to 3.5 GHz Turbo).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics for Core i7

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **must-memorize** formulas for any KTU numerical/analytical question on processor performance, applied to the Core i7 case.

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | Total CPU time. $IC$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles/instruction, $T_{clock}$ = clock period. |
| 2 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CPI_i \times F_i$ | Weighted average CPI, where $F_i$ is frequency of instruction class $i$. |
| 3 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Amdahl-style speedup. |
| 4 | $Speedup_{max} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | Amdahl's Law — $f$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = processors. |
| 5 | $IPC = \dfrac{1}{CPI}$ | Inverse relationship to CPI. |
| 6 | $FOM = \dfrac{1}{Energy \times Time}$ | Figure of Merit (sometimes used in green computing). |
| 7 | $TDP_{effective} = TDP_{base} + TDP_{Turbo} \times \eta$ | Effective thermal envelope. |
| 8 | $Cache\_Miss\_Rate = \dfrac{Misses}{Total\;Accesses}$ | L1/L2/L3 miss rate — the lower, the better. |
| 9 | $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times (T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times T_{DRAM})$ | Average Memory Access Time. |
| 10 | $Speedup_{pipeline} = \dfrac{Depth \times CPI_{non-pipe}}{Depth + (Stages-1)}$ | Approximate pipelined speedup. |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown table safety rule:** The vertical bar `|` is forbidden inside table cells. Always use **\vert** or **\mid** for any absolute-value or conditional notation (e.g., $f \vert g$ for $f$ divides $g$).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data centres** use Core i7-class Xeons (same µarch) where cache hierarchy and Turbo predict performance under load.
- **Compiler design** uses µop fusion knowledge to emit optimal x86 — e.g., `xor reg,reg` for zeroing takes 1 µop instead of 2.
- **Operating system schedulers** use the **logical-to-physical core mapping** to pin threads for cache locality.
- **Game engines** depend on **Turbo Boost** sustaining single-core frequency for the *render thread*, which is often single-threaded.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Core i7 Features Mapped to Architecture

The following table is the **single most important analytical asset** for KTU 14-mark questions. It directly maps a Core i7 feature to the underlying computer-architecture principle and the syllabus module it belongs to.

| Core i7 Feature | Architectural Principle | Syllabus Module Reference |
|-----------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| 14-stage pipeline (Nehalem) | Pipelining, instruction-level parallelism | M1 – CPU Design |
| 4-wide decode + µop fusion | Superscalar, microarchitecture | M1 – CPU Design |
| Tournament branch predictor | Speculative execution, dynamic prediction | M2 – Pipelining |
| 32 KB L1 + 256 KB L2 + 8 MB L3 | Memory hierarchy, locality | M3 – Memory |
| Inclusive L3 + MESIF coherence | Cache coherence, MESI | M3 – Memory |
| Integrated Memory Controller | Northbridge integration, latency reduction | M3 – Memory |
| QPI / DMI interconnect | Inter-component bandwidth | M3 – I/O & Bus |
| Hyper-Threading (2 threads/core) | SMT, thread-level parallelism | M1 – CPU Design |
| Turbo Boost (dynamic frequency) | DVFS (Dynamic Voltage-Frequency Scaling) | M1 – CPU Design |
| AVX / AVX-512 (vector units) | SIMD parallelism | M2 – Data Path |
| 256-bit FP datapath | Wide execution units, SIMD | M2 – Data Path |
| Hardware prefetchers (L1, L2) | Memory access prediction | M3 – Memory |
| Power gates / C-states | Clock & power gating, idle management | M1 – CPU Design |
| Quick Sync Video (iGPU) | Fixed-function hardware acceleration | M4 – Case Study |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — CPI & CPU Time Calculation

**Question (KTU-style):**  
A Core i7 program executes $5 \times 10^9$ instructions, with the following instruction mix:

| Class | CPI | Fraction $F_i$ |
|-------|-----|----------------|
| ALU | 1 | 40% |
| Load | 4 | 25% |
| Store | 3 | 15% |
| Branch | 2 | 10% |
| FP (AVX) | 6 | 10% |

The clock frequency is **3.0 GHz**. Compute:
1. The average CPI.
2. Total CPU execution time.
3. IPC.

### Step 1 — Weighted CPI

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} CPI_i \times F_i \\
&= (1)(0.40) + (4)(0.25) + (3)(0.15) + (2)(0.10) + (6)(0.10) \\
&= 0.40 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.20 + 0.60 \\
CPI_{avg} &= 2.65
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — CPU Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= IC \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clock} \\
&= 5 \times 10^9 \times 2.65 \times \dfrac{1}{3.0 \times 10^9} \\
&= 5 \times 2.65 \times \dfrac{1}{3.0} \\
&= \dfrac{13.25}{3.0} \\
T_{CPU} &\approx 4.417 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — IPC

$$
IPC = \dfrac{1}{CPI_{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{2.65} \approx 0.377 \; \text{instructions/cycle}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A real Core i7 typically achieves **0.5–1.0 IPC** on optimized SPEC benchmarks. Our 0.377 is plausible for a memory-heavy workload.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Amdahl's Law for Core i7 (Multi-core Scaling)

**Question:**  
A workload running on a single Core i7 core takes **80 seconds**, of which **64 seconds (80%)** is *parallelizable* across cores. The remaining **16 seconds (20%)** is inherently sequential (e.g., critical-section I/O).

1. What is the maximum speedup with **4 cores**?
2. What is the maximum speedup with **infinite cores**?
3. If we add **Hyper-Threading** (treating each core as 2 logical cores = 8 threads), what is the *theoretical* speedup?

### Step 1 — Amdahl's Law with 4 cores

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_4 &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.40} \\
S_4 &= 2.5\times
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Infinite cores

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\infty} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - f} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5.0\times
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **hard ceiling** — no matter how many cores you add, you cannot speed up the sequential 20% of the workload.

### Step 3 — Hyper-Threading (n = 8 logical threads)

Hyper-Threading typically delivers *less* than 2× the parallel speedup because the second thread on each core competes for the same execution units. A reasonable model assumes HTT gives only **~1.3× of an additional core's worth** of work per extra thread:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Effective_{n} &= 4 + 4 \times 0.30 = 4 + 1.2 = 5.2 \\
S_{HTT} &= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{5.2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.1538} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.3538} \\
&\approx 2.83\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks: *"Why does Hyper-Threading not double the performance?"* — Answer with **resource sharing of execution units** + **cache contention** + **branch-predictor aliasing**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

**Question:**  
For a Core i7 with:
- L1 hit time = **1 ns**
- L2 hit time = **4 ns**, miss rate from L1 = 5%
- L3 hit time = **12 ns**, miss rate from L2 = 20%
- DRAM access time = **60 ns**

Compute **AMAT**.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
AMAT &= T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times T_{L2} + MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} \times T_{L3} + MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} \times MR_{L3} \times T_{DRAM} \\
&= 1 + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(0.20)(12) + (0.05)(0.20)(0.80)(60) \\
&= 1 + 0.20 + 0.12 + 0.48 \\
AMAT &= 1.80 \; \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Many students omit the **last term** (DRAM contribution). KTU expects the *full* 4-level AMAT. Always write the complete equation.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Pipeline Speedup Approximation

**Question:**  
A non-pipelined Core i7 functional unit takes **40 ns** to execute one instruction. The processor is then pipelined into **14 stages** with a **0.4 ns** stage delay. Ignoring hazards, what is the speedup for **1000 instructions**?

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{non-pipe} &= 1000 \times 40 = 40{,}000 \; \text{ns} \\
T_{pipe} &= 14 \times 0.4 + (1000 - 1) \times 0.4 \\
&= 5.6 + 999 \times 0.4 \\
&= 5.6 + 399.6 \\
&= 405.2 \; \text{ns} \\
Speedup &= \dfrac{40{,}000}{405.2} \approx 98.7\times
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, **branch mispredictions, cache misses, and data hazards** reduce this to a *steady-state* speedup of roughly **10–15×**, with a *peak* speedup approaching 100× for tight numerical loops.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Generational Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta IPC_{Nehalem \to Haswell} &\approx +15\% \\
\Delta IPC_{Haswell \to Skylake} &\approx +10\% \\
\Delta IPC_{Skylake \to Alder\;Lake} &\approx +19\% \; (\text{hybrid architecture}) \\
\Delta IPC_{Alder\;Lake \to Raptor\;Lake} &\approx +15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does frequency not keep rising?** *Dennard scaling* broke down around 2006. Beyond ~3.5 GHz, doubling frequency would square the power dissipation ($P \propto f \times V^2$). Modern Core i7s therefore prioritize **IPC and core count** over raw clock speed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Die Architecture of an Intel Core i7 (Nehalem / Sandy Bridge Family)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cache fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef uncore fill:#b45309,stroke:#7c3a02,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef io fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#4a148c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gpu fill:#c62828,stroke:#8e1b1b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    Core1["CORE 1\nL1I 32KB | L1D 32KB | L2 256KB | HT"]:::core
    Core2["CORE 2\nL1I 32KB | L1D 32KB | L2 256KB | HT"]:::core
    Core3["CORE 3\nL1I 32KB | L1D 32KB | L2 256KB | HT"]:::core
    Core4["CORE 4\nL1I 32KB | L1D 32KB | L2 256KB | HT"]:::core

    L3["L3 SMART CACHE\nShared 4 - 12 MB\nMESIF Coherence"]:::cache

    Sys["SYSTEM AGENT\nUncore | Ring Bus | Power Gates"]:::uncore

    IMC["INTEGRATED MEMORY CTRL\nDDR3 / DDR4 Channels"]:::uncore

    QPI["QPI / DMI LINK\nTo Chipset / PCH"]:::io

    PCIE["PCI EXPRESS 3.0\nLanes to GPU & I/O"]:::io

    GPU["INTEGRATED GPU\nHD / UHD Graphics\nQuick Sync Video"]:::gpu

    Core1 <--> L3
    Core2 <--> L3
    Core3 <--> L3
    Core4 <--> L3

    L3 <--> Sys
    Sys <--> IMC
    Sys <--> QPI
    Sys <--> PCIE
    Sys <--> GPU

    IMC --> DDRRAM[("DDR3 / DDR4 / DDR5 DRAM\nSystem Memory")]
    QPI --> Chipset["PCH Chipset\nUSB, SATA, Audio, NIC, BIOS"]
    PCIE --> DISCRETE["Discrete GPU / NVMe SSD / NIC"]
    GPU --> DISPLAY["DisplayPort / HDMI Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguards applied:** every node ID is alphanumeric (`Core1`, `L3`, `Sys`), no reserved keywords used as IDs, and label text contains only uppercase alphanumeric characters and pipes `|` used safely inside double quotes (Mermaid permits `|` inside quoted node labels — unlike markdown tables).

## 4.2 The Core i7 Pipeline (Conceptual — Nehalem-style, ~14 Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef fetch fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d3f7a,color:#ffffff
    classDef decode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d1f,color:#ffffff
    classDef issue fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#a14d00,color:#ffffff
    classDef exec fill:#c62828,stroke:#7a1a1a,color:#ffffff
    classDef retire fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#3e0f5e,color:#ffffff

    S1["S1: IP GEN"]:::fetch
    S2["S2: FETCH L1I"]:::fetch
    S3["S3: PREDECODE"]:::fetch
    S4["S4: BRANCH PRED"]:::fetch
    S5["S5: DECODE 4-wide"]:::decode
    S6["S6: UOP FUSION"]:::decode
    S7["S7: ALLOCATE ROB/RS"]:::issue
    S8["S8: REGISTER RENAME"]:::issue
    S9["S9: SCHEDULE"]:::issue
    S10["S10: DISPATCH"]:::issue
    S11["S11: REGISTER READ"]:::exec
    S12["S12: EXECUTE ALU/FPU/AGU"]:::exec
    S13["S13: WRITE BACK"]:::exec
    S14["S14: RETIRE COMMIT"]:::retire

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12 --> S13 --> S14
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern Intel cores (Sandy Bridge onwards) **store decoded µops in a µop cache**, bypassing the long front-end pipeline (stages 2–5) on µop-cache hits. This effectively shortens the pipeline to **~10 stages** for repeated code.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    classDef data fill:#1e88e5,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff

    MC["MEMORY CONTROLLER"]:::mem
    RET["RETIRE UNIT\nIn-Order Commit 4-wide"]:::out

    X86 --> DEC
    DEC --> UOC
    UOC --> ROB
    DEC --> BTB
    ROB --> RAT
    RAT --> RS
    RS --> EX
    EX --> FP
    EX --> L1D
    L1D --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> MC
    EX --> RET
    ROB --> RET
```

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S0 --> T1C

## 4.5 Memory Hierarchy — Access Latency Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef lvl1 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#7a1a1a,color:#ffffff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#a14d00,color:#ffffff
    classDef lvl3 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#a37800,color:#000000
    classDef lvl4 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    classDef lvl5 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#0d3f7a,color:#ffffff

    REG["REGISTERS\n< 1 ns\n8 - 16 GPRs / 16 - 32 SIMD"]:::lvl1
    L1I["L1 INSTRUCTION\n~1 ns\n32 KB per core"]:::lvl2
    L1D["L1 DATA\n~1 ns\n32 KB per core"]:::lvl2
    L2["L2 UNIFIED\n~4 ns\n256 KB per core"]:::lvl3
    L3["L3 SMART CACHE\n~12 ns\n4 - 12 MB shared"]:::lvl3
    MC["DRAM\n~60 ns\n8 - 64 GB DDR"]:::lvl4
    SSD["NVMe SSD\n~100 us\n256 GB - 8 TB"]:::lvl5
    HDD["HDD\n~10 ms\n1 - 10 TB"]:::lvl5

    REG --> L1I
    REG --> L1D
    L1I --> L2
    L1D --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> MC
    MC --> SSD
    SSD --> HDD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the latency scale:** Each step down the hierarchy adds roughly **one order of magnitude** of latency. This is the *memory wall* that motivates deep cache hierarchies in the Core i7.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)

**Q:** What is **Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT)** in the Intel Core i7 processor? How is it different from having two independent physical cores?

**Model Answer:**

Hyper-Threading Technology is Intel's implementation of **Simultaneous Multi-Threading (SMT)**. It allows a single physical Core i7 core to behave as **two logical processors** to the operating system, enabling two threads to execute concurrently.

The key architectural distinction is **resource sharing**:

| Resource | Hyper-Threaded Core | Two Physical Cores |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Execution units (ALU, FPU) | **Shared** | Duplicated |
| L1 / L2 caches | **Shared** | Duplicated |
| Branch predictors | **Shared** | Duplicated |
| Architectural state (registers, IP, EFLAGS) | Duplicated | Duplicated |
| ROB / Reservation Station | Partitioned | Duplicated |
| L3 Smart Cache | Shared (chip-wide) | Shared (chip-wide) |

> *Hence, HTT improves thread-level parallelism by hiding stalls in one thread with work from another, but does not double the compute throughput since execution units remain unique.*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**Q:** Explain the significance of the **L3 Smart Cache** in the Intel Core i7.

**Model Answer:**

The **L3 Smart Cache** is a large, unified, last-level on-die cache (typically **4 to 12 MB**) that is **shared by all physical cores** on the Core i7 die. Its significance is fourfold:

1. **Capacity efficiency** — By being shared, it dynamically allocates more space to whichever core has demand, avoiding per-core duplication of rarely-used data.
2. **Inter-core communication** — When one core produces data needed by another, the data can be placed in L3 and quickly fetched by the second core, avoiding a DRAM round-trip.
3. **Cache coherence** — The L3 is the *coherence point* where the **MESIF** protocol is enforced across cores via the ring interconnect, ensuring all cores observe consistent memory states.
4. **Latency reduction** — Hits in L3 (~12 ns) are far faster than DRAM (~60 ns), so the Smart Cache effectively masks much of the memory-wall penalty.

> *Thus, the Smart Cache is central to multi-core scalability and is a defining feature of the Core i7 family.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a single core in the Intel Core i7** processor (Nehalem microarchitecture). Clearly identify the **front-end, back-end, execution units, and cache hierarchy**.

**Model Solution:**

The Nehalem core of the Core i7 is divided into two major regions: the **in-order front-end** and the **out-of-order back-end**.

**1. In-Order Front-End (7 stages):**

- **Stage 1–2 (Instruction Pointer Generation & Fetch):** The IP/PC generator produces the next instruction address; 16 bytes are fetched per cycle from the L1I cache.
- **Stage 3 (Predecode):** Marks instruction boundaries for the variable-length x86 stream.
- **Stage 4 (Branch Prediction):** A **tournament predictor** (combining a local and global predictor) speculates branch direction; the **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** stores targets. Misprediction penalty is ~15 cycles.
- **Stage 5 (Decode):** Four **simple decoders** + one **complex decoder** break x86 instructions into 1–4 **micro-operations (µops)**. A microcode ROM handles complex ISA instructions.

**2. Out-of-Order Back-End:**

- **Stage 6 (Alloc / Rename):** µops are allocated to the **Reorder Buffer (ROB, 128 entries)** and the **Register Alias Table (RAT)** renames architectural registers to ~128 physical registers to break false dependencies.
- **Stage 7 (Dispatch / Schedule):** The **Reservation Station (RS, 36 entries)** issues up to **6 µops/cycle** to execution ports.
- **Stages 8–10 (Execute):** µops execute on **6 execution ports**:
  - Port 0 & 1: Integer ALU, branch
  - Port 2 & 3: AGU (load + store addresses)
  - Port 4: Store data
  - Port 5: FP/AVX execution

- **Stage 11 (Write-back):** Results are written back to the physical register file.
- **Stage 12–14 (Retire):** The **retire unit** commits µops **in original program order**, at 4 µops/cycle, updating architectural state.

**3. Cache Hierarchy (per core + shared):**
- **L1I:** 32 KB, 8-way, 64-byte line, ~1 ns
- **L1D:** 32 KB, 8-way, 64-byte line, ~1 ns
- **L2:** 256 KB, 8-way, 64-byte line, ~4 ns
- **L3 Smart Cache:** 4–12 MB shared across all cores, 16-way, ~12 ns

**Diagram (to be redrawn in exam):**

```
IP Gen -> Fetch -> Predecode -> Branch Pred -> Decode -> Uop Cache
                                                              |
                                                              v
                                     Alloc/Rename -> Reservation Station
                                                              |
                                              +-----+-----+---+----+----+
                                              |     |     |   |    |    |
                                              v     v     v   v    v    v
                                             P0    P1    P2  P3   P4   P5
                                              \     |    |   |    |    /
                                               \    |    |   |    |   /
                                                -> Retire (4-wide) ->
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A program running on a Core i7 has the following characteristics:
- Instruction count = **$3 \times 10^9$**
- Base CPI = **2.0**
- 5% of instructions cause **L1 cache misses** that incur a **stall of 40 cycles per miss**
- 1% of instructions cause **branch mispredictions** with a **15-cycle penalty**
- Base clock = **2.8 GHz**, but **Turbo Boost** raises the active frequency to **3.4 GHz** during single-core execution.

Compute: (i) the effective CPI including miss and misprediction stalls, and (ii) the total CPU time at the boosted frequency.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Effective CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{miss} &= 0.05 \times 40 = 2.0 \\
CPI_{branch} &= 0.01 \times 15 = 0.15 \\
CPI_{effective} &= CPI_{base} + CPI_{miss} + CPI_{branch} \\
&= 2.0 + 2.0 + 0.15 \\
CPI_{effective} &= 4.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) CPU time at 3.4 GHz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clock} &= \dfrac{1}{3.4 \times 10^9} \approx 0.2941 \; \text{ns} \\
T_{CPU} &= IC \times CPI_{effective} \times T_{clock} \\
&= 3 \times 10^9 \times 4.15 \times 0.2941 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 3 \times 4.15 \times 0.2941 \\
&= 3.661 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $CPI_{eff} = 4.15$, $T_{CPU} \approx 3.66 \; s$ at 3.4 GHz.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $CPI_{miss}$ as 40 instead of $0.05 \times 40 = 2.0$. The stall penalty is **per miss**, and only 5% of instructions miss — so you must **multiply the per-miss stall by the miss fraction**. Skipping this multiplication costs 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Discuss the **evolution of the Intel Core i7** microarchitecture from **Nehalem (2008)** through **Haswell (2013)** to **Skylake (2015)**. Highlight at least **four architectural improvements** introduced in each generation.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Nehalem (2008, 45 nm):**
- First Core i7 — replaced the front-side bus with the **QuickPath Interconnect (QPI)**.
- Introduced the **Integrated Memory Controller (IMC)** on-die, eliminating the Northbridge latency.
- Native **quad-core** design with **Hyper-Threading** (4C/8T).
- 14-stage pipeline, **monolithic L3 Smart Cache**, 3-channel DDR3.
- TDP bracket: **95 W – 130 W**.

**2. Sandy Bridge (2011, 32 nm):**
- Switched to a **ring interconnect** for on-die cores/cache/uncore communication.
- **256-bit AVX** instructions introduced, doubling SIMD throughput.
- µop cache (1.5 K entries) reduced front-end power and decode latency.
- Integrated **HD Graphics** on-die (Quick Sync Video).
- Improved branch predictor with a 2nd-level BTB.

**3. Ivy Bridge (2012, 22 nm):**
- First Intel consumer chip on **3D Tri-Gate (FinFET) transistors**, reducing leakage by ~50%.
- Slight IPC gain (~5–8%) over Sandy Bridge at the same frequency.
- Support for **DDR3L (1.5 V)** and **PCIe 3.0**.

**4. Haswell (2013, 22 nm):**
- Introduced **AVX2** and **FMA3** — 256-bit fused multiply-add, doubling FP throughput.
- New instructions: `BMI1`, `BMI2`, `TSX` (Transactional Synchronization Extensions).
- **Fully Integrated Voltage Regulator (FIVR)** moved on-die — better power control per core.
- New socket **LGA 1150**; TDP options **35 W, 45 W, 65 W, 84 W, 95 W**.

**5. Broadwell (2014, 14 nm):**
- **14 nm process** — ~37% smaller transistors vs. 22 nm.
- **eDRAM (Crystal Well)** on-package as a 128 MB L4 for Iris Pro iGPUs.
- Lower TDP at similar performance.

**6. Skylake (2015, 14 nm):**
- **Modular SoC design** with separate PCH, support for **DDR4** and **Optane (3D XPoint)**.
- Improved IPC by ~10% over Haswell.
- **Speed Shift** — hardware-controlled P-state transitions in <1 ms.
- New **Speedset / Speed Shift** and **Software Guard Extensions (SGX)**.
- Aimed at the "**process-architecture-optimization**" long-term cadence.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Feature | Nehalem | Sandy Bridge | Haswell | Skylake |
|---------|---------|--------------|---------|---------|
| Process | 45 nm | 32 nm | 22 nm | 14 nm |
| Vector ISA | SSE4.2 | AVX | AVX2 + FMA3 | AVX-512 (Skylake-X) |
| µop cache | No | 1.5 K | 1.5 K | 1.5 K (deeper) |
| Integrated GPU | None | HD 2000–3000 | HD 4600 | UHD 530 |
| Interconnect | QPI | Ring | Ring | Ring + DMI 3.0 |
| Voltage Reg. | External | External | **On-die FIVR** | On-die FIVR |
| Memory | DDR3-3ch | DDR3-2ch | DDR3-2ch | DDR4-2ch |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With a suitable diagram, explain **Turbo Boost Technology** in the Intel Core i7. How does it comply with the **Thermal Design Power (TDP)** envelope? Show, with a calculation, the speedup obtained when a single-threaded workload runs at the base 2.6 GHz and Turbo 3.4 GHz.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
Turbo Boost is Intel's **Dynamic Voltage-Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** implementation. When one or more cores are idle (in C-state), the operating system signals via ACPI `P-state` requests, and the Core i7's **Power Control Unit (PCU)** redistributes the power budget to the active core(s), raising their multiplier.

**Compliance with TDP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{package} &\le TDP_{max} \\
P_{active} \times N_{active} + P_{uncore} + P_{iGPU} &\le TDP_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $N_{active} = 1$, the entire package power budget can be allocated to that one core, allowing it to exceed the base frequency.

**Diagram (to be redrawn):**

```
+----------------------------+
|      PACKAGE (TDP=45W)     |
|  +------+   +------+        |
|  | Core |   | Core |        |
|  | 0  * |   |  1   |  <- * = Turbo active (3.4 GHz) |
|  +------+   +------+        |
|        \  /                 |
|       Ring Bus              |
|  +-------------+            |
|  | System Agent|            |
|  +-------------+            |
+----------------------------+
```

**Calculation — Turbo Speedup for single-threaded workload:**

Assume instruction count $IC = 10^9$ and CPI remains constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{base} &= IC \times CPI \times \dfrac{1}{2.6 \times 10^9} \\
T_{turbo} &= IC \times CPI \times \dfrac{1}{3.4 \times 10^9} \\
Speedup &= \dfrac{T_{base}}{T_{turbo}} = \dfrac{3.4}{2.6} \approx 1.31\times
\end{aligned}
$$

So the single-threaded workload speeds up by **~31%** when Turbo engages, with no code change required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $Speedup = \dfrac{2.6}{3.4}$, which inverts the ratio. Remember — **higher frequency = lower time = higher speedup**. Also, Turbo speedup is **linear with frequency** only when CPI is constant, which holds for compute-bound single-threaded loops but not for memory-bound workloads.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons KTU students lose marks on the Core i7 case study:**
> 1. **Confusing logical vs physical cores** — always state "1 physical core = 2 logical cores (with HTT)".
> 2. **Misquoting cache sizes** — L1 is 32 KB (not 16 KB), L2 is 256 KB (not 512 KB), L3 is *shared* (4–12 MB).
> 3. **Forgetting the IMC** — a defining feature of Core i7 is the on-die memory controller, which replaced the Northbridge.
> 4. **Omitting Amdahl's Law in multi-core analysis** — always state the *parallel fraction* before plugging into the speedup formula.
> 5. **Mixing pipeline stages across generations** — Nehalem ≈ 14 stages; Sandy Bridge ≈ 14 with µop cache shortcut; do not blindly quote "20-stage" or "10-stage" pipelines from random sources.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Core i7** = Intel's flagship 64-bit x86 CPU family; first launched 2008 (Nehalem, 45 nm).
- [x] **Microarchitecture generations** to know: Nehalem → Westmere → Sandy Bridge → Ivy Bridge → Haswell → Broadwell → Skylake (KTU focuses on these).
- [x] **Cache hierarchy**: 32 KB L1I + 32 KB L1D + 256 KB L2 (per core) + 4–12 MB **shared L3 Smart Cache**.
- [x] **Hyper-Threading (HTT)** = Intel's brand for SMT — 2 logical threads per physical core, sharing execution units, caches, and predictors.
- [x] **Turbo Boost** = DVFS that raises frequency above base, subject to TDP, current, temperature, and number of active cores.
- [x] **QPI (Nehalem)** replaced the front-side bus; **DMI (Sandy Bridge+)** connects the CPU to the PCH.
- [x] **Integrated Memory Controller (IMC)** on-die — 3-channel DDR3 in Bloomfield, 2-channel in Lynnfield; DDR4 from Skylake onwards.
- [x] **AVX (Sandy Bridge)** = 256-bit SIMD; **AVX2 + FMA3 (Haswell)** = 256-bit FMAs; **AVX-512 (Skylake-X)** = 512-bit.
- [x] **µop cache** (1.5 K entries, Sandy Bridge+) bypasses x86 decode on µop-cache hits.
- [x] **Tournament branch predictor** combines local + global history; misprediction penalty ~15 cycles.
- [x] **ROB = 128 entries**, **RS ≈ 36 entries** (Nehalem); **dispatch 6 µops/cycle**; **retire 4 µops/cycle**.
- [x] **Performance formulas** (must be memorised): $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$, $Speedup_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$, $AMAT$ 4-level formula, $IPC = 1/CPI$.
- [x] **Dennard scaling breakdown** (~2006) → modern Core i7 designs prioritize **IPC and core count** over raw frequency.
- [x] **Tick-tock model** replaced by **PAO (Process-Architecture-Optimization)** since 2016 (Kaby Lake onwards).
- [x] **Recent hybrid architecture** (Alder Lake, 2021): combination of **P-cores (Performance)** + **E-cores (Efficient)** with a hardware thread director.
- [x] **Always state units** in numerical answers: TDP in **watts (W)**, frequency in **gigahertz (GHz)**, cache in **kilobytes / megabytes (KB / MB)**, time in **seconds / nanoseconds (s / ns)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU exam tip:** When asked to "explain the Intel Core i7 architecture", structure your answer into three layers:
> 1. **Die-level** (cores, cache, uncore, IMC, ring bus).
> 2. **Core-level** (front-end, back-end, execution ports, retirement).
> 3. **Feature-level** (HTT, Turbo, AVX, µop cache, branch predictor).
> This layered structure is the **mark-friendly format** KTU examiners award full marks to.
